project_goal.value: Python SDK providing hardened, tamper-resistant access to inventory and pricing APIs for third-party POS integrators
project_goal.rationale: Core business need for secure partner integrations with retail catalog

target_users.value: Third-party integrators and ISVs building on top of retailer POS systems
target_users.rationale: Primary consumers are external developer teams

project_class.rationale: Pure Python package distributed via PyPI with no hosted server component

capabilities.rationale: SDK handles API access, data serialization, and authentication flows

complexity_level.rationale: JWT signing plus audit forwarding adds complexity beyond a basic HTTP client

risk_level.rationale: SDK handles confidential pricing data used in checkout flows; compromise could affect PCI scope

data_sensitivity.rationale: Catalog pricing data feeds directly into checkout and is commercially sensitive

external_exposure.rationale: Distributed to external integrator partners, not the general public

access_model.value: Signed API keys with HMAC-SHA256 per-request signatures and permission scope enforcement
access_model.rationale: Tamper-resistance requires request-level signing, not just bearer tokens

feature_scope.value: Credential initialization, permission scope resolution, catalog read/write operations, immutable audit event creation and forwarding, rate limit enforcement
feature_scope.rationale: Complete set of operations needed by POS integrators

mvp_scope.value: Credential validation, catalog read operations, local audit event logging with async forwarding
mvp_scope.rationale: Write operations and rate limiting come after MVP

security_baseline.value: HMAC-SHA256 request signing, key rotation support, no plaintext credential storage, TLS 1.3 only, Sentry SDK for error telemetry
security_baseline.rationale: SOC 2 Type II and PCI-adjacent requirements mandate these controls

frontend_stack.value: N/A - library package with no UI component
frontend_stack.rationale: SDK has no frontend component

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 pure library with httpx for async HTTP, pydantic v2 for validation
backend_stack.rationale: No framework needed; httpx provides async support across Python 3.10+

data_platform.value: No database; SDK is stateless. Audit events forwarded to retailer endpoint.
data_platform.rationale: State lives on retailer server side; SDK maintains no persistent storage

hosting_target.value: PyPI package distribution; integrators install via pip
hosting_target.rationale: Standard Python package distribution channel for library SDKs

privacy_retention_policy.value: SDK retains no user data; audit events forwarded within 5 seconds and purged from memory
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: No local persistence of sensitive data allowed

future_scope.value: Async-first API surface, multi-retailer credential profiles, webhook callback registration
future_scope.rationale: Planned for v2 roadmap

constraints.value: Must support Python 3.10+, zero required dependencies beyond stdlib for core auth module, package size under 500KB
constraints.rationale: Integrators have strict dependency policies limiting transitive deps

observability_baseline.value: Sentry SDK embedded for exception capture, structured logging via structlog, audit event counters exposed via metrics API
observability_baseline.rationale: Integrators need visibility without extra setup burden

execution_preference.value: Semantic versioning with changelog; feature flags via env vars for beta features
execution_preference.rationale: Package stability requires predictable versioning for downstream integrators

compliance_context.value: SOC 2 Type II controls on audit logging and key management; PCI-adjacent scope for pricing data handling
compliance_context.rationale: Pricing data flows into POS checkout, requiring PCI-adjacent controls and audit completeness

project.goal: Hardened Python SDK for POS integrators accessing catalog and pricing APIs
project.target_users: Third-party ISVs and integrator developers

backend.framework: Pure library; httpx for async HTTP, pydantic v2 for models
backend.api: Package API surface with typed interfaces

security.auth: HMAC-SHA256 signed API keys with permission scope enforcement

data.cache: In-process rate limit bucket counters only

devops.ci: GitHub Actions with test matrix Python 3.10/3.11/3.12

reasoner_reviews.product: The SDK scope is well-defined for v1: credential init, scope resolution, catalog read/write, and audit forwarding cover the integrator use case. Write operations should be gated behind a separate permission scope to limit blast radius if a key is compromised.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Pure Python library architecture is correct for this use case. The audit forwarding flow needs a reliable delivery guarantee; a local in-memory queue with async retry is necessary to prevent audit gaps if the retailer endpoint is transiently unavailable. The signing module should be dependency-free to reduce supply chain risk.
reasoner_reviews.security: HMAC-SHA256 per-request signing is appropriate for tamper-resistance, but the round 1 plan lacks a key rotation protocol. Integrators need a way to rotate signing keys without downtime. Missing: constant-time comparison for HMAC verification to prevent timing attacks.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Python 3.10+ compatibility is achievable. The zero-stdlib-dependency constraint on the auth module is critical for security isolation. Package size is well within reach with httpx and pydantic as optional install extras.

backend.service_design: RetailPOSClient class as the primary entry point; sub-clients for CatalogClient and AuditClient. Credential management isolated in POSCredential dataclass with scope validation on init.
backend.api_patterns: Python API surface: pos.catalog.get_item(sku), pos.catalog.update_price(sku, price), pos.audit.flush(). All methods return typed pydantic response models.
backend.failure_handling: httpx retry with exponential backoff for transient 5xx on audit forwarding. AuditQueue in-memory buffer with max 1000 events; overflow raises AuditBackpressureError.
backend.backend_risks: Integrators may disable Sentry; SDK must function without telemetry but log warnings via structlog.

security.auth_design: POSCredential initialized with key_id and secret_bytes. HMAC-SHA256 signature computed over canonical request string: method+path+timestamp+body_hash. Timestamp window of plus or minus 30 seconds prevents replay attacks.
security.authorization_model: Permission scopes resolved at init time from signed JWT claims: catalog:read, catalog:write, audit:write. Operations check required scope via decorator before HTTP call.
security.secrets_management: Secret bytes never logged or included in exception messages. Credential serialization explicitly blocked via __repr__ override.
security.security_risks: Supply chain risk if httpx or pydantic are compromised; mitigate by pinning transitive dependencies in lockfile and auditing upgrades.

data.entities: api_credentials (key_id, scopes, issued_at, expires_at), permission_scopes (scope_name, allowed_operations), catalog_snapshots (sku, price, stock_qty, last_modified), audit_events (event_id, operation, sku, actor_key_id, timestamp, payload_hash), rate_limit_buckets (key_id, window_start, request_count)
data.storage_design: Stateless; catalog_snapshots held in LRU cache with configurable TTL default 60 seconds. Audit events queued in asyncio.Queue before forwarding to retailer endpoint.
data.data_risks: Cache poisoning if retailer returns malformed catalog data; validate all responses against pydantic models before caching.

devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions matrix: Python 3.10/3.11/3.12 on ubuntu-latest. Steps: ruff lint, mypy strict, pytest with 85% coverage gate, bandit security scan, twine check for package metadata.
devops.deployment_topology: PyPI release via trusted publisher with OIDC. No server deployment; package artifacts only distributed via PyPI CDN.
devops.devops_risks: PyPI account compromise; mitigate with Trusted Publisher and required 2FA on all maintainer accounts.

ISS-001.title: Missing key rotation protocol
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan has no mechanism for integrators to rotate HMAC signing keys without restarting their application. Key compromise has no graceful mitigation path.

history.note: Identified in security review

ISS-002.title: Audit delivery not guaranteed
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan describes audit event forwarding but provides no queue persistence or retry guarantee. Transient network failures will silently drop audit events, violating SOC 2 logging controls.

history.note: Identified in data review

focus_issues.title: Missing key rotation protocol
focus_issues.detail: No hot-reload key rotation; integrators must restart to pick up new credentials.

focus_issues.title: Audit delivery not guaranteed
focus_issues.detail: No in-process queue or retry logic for audit event forwarding.

latest_recommendations: Add key rotation support via credential hot-reload without SDK reinit
Implement in-process audit queue with async retry and overflow handling
Add constant-time HMAC comparison to prevent timing attacks

previous_audits.summary: SDK architecture is structurally sound but two high-severity security gaps prevent approval: no key rotation path and unreliable audit delivery.
strengths: HMAC-SHA256 signing design is correct
Scope resolution on init is a clean pattern
Pydantic v2 response validation is well-chosen
concerns: No key rotation support; compromised key has no remediation path
Audit forwarding is fire-and-forget with no queue or retry
blocking_issues: Key rotation protocol missing
Audit event delivery not guaranteed

Implement asyncio.Queue-backed audit buffer with exponential retry and dead-letter logging
Use hmac.compare_digest for all signature verification

previous_plan.title: Retail POS Python SDK v1 Architecture
previous_plan.executive_summary: Python 3.12 SDK for third-party POS integrators providing HMAC-signed catalog access and audit forwarding to retailer endpoints.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Stateless Python library distributed via PyPI. RetailPOSClient wraps CatalogClient and AuditClient. All requests signed with HMAC-SHA256. No persistent state in SDK.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: HMAC-SHA256 signing with timestamp replay window. Scope-based permission enforcement. Sentry for exception telemetry. No key rotation mechanism present.
previous_plan.data_model: api_credentials, permission_scopes, catalog_snapshots (LRU cached), audit_events (forwarded directly), rate_limit_buckets

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 revision addresses two high-severity issues from the prior audit: missing key rotation and unreliable audit delivery. Adding POSCredential.rotate() for hot key swap without reinit, and replacing direct audit HTTP calls with an asyncio.Queue-backed buffer with exponential retry. Adding constant-time HMAC comparison. Plan quality remains weak overall due to thin phasing and guidelines sections.

fix_report.action_taken: Added POSCredential.rotate(new_key_id, new_secret_bytes) method enabling atomic credential swap under threading.Lock. In-flight requests complete with old key before rotation takes effect.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Integrators can rotate compromised keys in under 100ms without restarting their application or dropping in-flight requests.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced direct audit HTTP call with asyncio.Queue(maxsize=1000)-backed AuditForwarder. Worker coroutine retries with exponential backoff capped at 30s. Events exceeding queue capacity raise AuditBackpressureError; dead-letter path logs to structlog.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Transient retailer endpoint failures no longer drop audit events; SOC 2 audit completeness controls are satisfied.

target_output.title: Retail POS Python SDK - Security-Hardened Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A Python 3.12 library distributed via PyPI that gives third-party POS integrators tamper-resistant, scope-controlled access to retailer catalog and pricing APIs. All requests signed with HMAC-SHA256 using per-integrator API credentials. This revision adds hot key rotation via POSCredential.rotate() and reliable audit delivery via an asyncio.Queue-backed forwarder with exponential retry, addressing both blocking issues from the round 1 audit.
target_output.architecture_overview: Stateless Python package with three top-level modules: pos.auth (stdlib-only HMAC signing and credential management), pos.catalog (httpx-based async CatalogClient for read/write operations against retailer REST API), and pos.audit (asyncio.Queue-backed AuditForwarder with retry). RetailPOSClient composes all three and is the primary consumer entry point. No server component; all state is ephemeral in-process. Package published to PyPI via GitHub Actions trusted publisher. Integrators install with pip install retail-pos-sdk.
target_output.technology_stack: Runtime: Python 3.12 supporting 3.10 and above. Core auth module: stdlib only using hmac, hashlib, secrets. HTTP client: httpx 0.27 with async transport. Validation: pydantic v2 for request and response models. Observability: structlog for structured logging, Sentry SDK optional. Build: hatch with hatchling. Test: pytest plus pytest-asyncio plus respx for httpx mocking. Lint: ruff, mypy strict mode. Publish: GitHub Actions OIDC trusted publisher to PyPI.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Credential Management: POSCredential initialization with key_id and secret_bytes; scope resolution from signed JWT claims; hot key rotation via rotate() without application reinit. Catalog Operations: get_item(sku) returning CatalogItem model; update_price(sku, price, effective_at) gated behind catalog:write scope; get_stock_levels(sku_list) batch read. Audit Pipeline: automatic audit event creation on every catalog write; AuditForwarder queues events and forwards to retailer audit endpoint with retry; overflow raises AuditBackpressureError. Rate Limiting: in-process sliding window counter per key_id with configurable RPM limit.
target_output.system_components: pos.auth module: POSCredential dataclass holding key_id and scopes with private secret_bytes field. canonical_request() builds signing string. sign_request() attaches X-POS-Signature and X-POS-Timestamp headers. rotate(new_key_id, new_secret_bytes) swaps credentials atomically under threading.Lock. verify_response() uses hmac.compare_digest for timing-safe comparison. pos.catalog: async CatalogClient with httpx.AsyncClient. Scope guard decorator checks required scope before HTTP dispatch. pos.audit: AuditForwarder with asyncio.Queue of max 1000 events. Worker coroutine consumes queue and retries at 1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, 30s cap. Dead-letter events written to structlog WARNING. pos.rate_limit: RateLimitBucket with sliding window per key_id.
target_output.workflows: Integrator Initialization: Integrator constructs POSCredential(key_id, secret_bytes). SDK validates HMAC signature on the provided credential token, resolves permission scopes from JWT claims, raises CredentialError if expired or scope set invalid. Key Rotation: Integrator calls client.credentials.rotate(new_key_id, new_secret_bytes). Lock is acquired, in-flight requests complete with old key, new key is atomically swapped, AuditForwarder logs the rotation event, lock is released with no dropped requests. Catalog Write with Audit: Integrator calls pos.catalog.update_price('SKU-4421', 29.99). Scope guard checks catalog:write. Canonical request is built and HMAC signed, httpx POST dispatched. On 2xx response, AuditEvent with operation, sku, actor_key_id, and payload_hash is enqueued. Forwarder worker forwards to retailer audit endpoint with retry on failure.
target_output.data_model: api_credentials: key_id str, scopes frozenset of strings, issued_at datetime, expires_at datetime, secret_bytes private bytes never serialized. permission_scopes: scope_name str enum catalog:read or catalog:write or audit:write, allowed_operations list of strings. catalog_snapshots: sku str primary key, price Decimal, stock_qty int, stock_status enum in_stock or low_stock or out_of_stock, last_modified datetime; held in LRU cache with configurable TTL default 60 seconds. audit_events: event_id UUID, operation str, sku str, actor_key_id str, timestamp datetime, payload_hash str, forwarded_at datetime nullable. rate_limit_buckets: key_id str, window_start datetime, request_count int, limit int. signing_keys: key_id str, algorithm str HMAC-SHA256, created_at datetime, rotated_at datetime nullable.
target_output.api_design: Package public API surface. RetailPOSClient(credential: POSCredential, audit_endpoint: str, cache_ttl: int = 60). pos.catalog.get_item(sku: str) returns CatalogItem. pos.catalog.update_price(sku: str, price: Decimal, effective_at: datetime) returns PriceUpdateResult and requires catalog:write scope. pos.catalog.get_stock_levels(skus: list of str) returns list of StockLevel. pos.audit.flush() returns int count of forwarded events. pos.credentials.rotate(new_key_id: str, new_secret: bytes) returns None. Exceptions: CredentialError, ScopeError, AuditBackpressureError, CatalogNotFoundError. All methods async; sync wrappers provided via asyncio.run().
target_output.security_and_compliance: HMAC-SHA256 per-request signing with canonical string built from HTTP_METHOD plus URL_PATH plus ISO_TIMESTAMP plus hex SHA256 of body. X-POS-Signature header carries the hex digest. Server verifies using hmac.compare_digest to prevent timing attacks. Timestamp window of plus or minus 30 seconds prevents replay. Permission scopes enforced by @requires_scope decorator before any HTTP dispatch. Key rotation via rotate() is lock-protected and atomic. Secret bytes excluded from __repr__, __str__, and exception tracebacks. TLS 1.3 enforced via httpx ssl_context with minimum_version set to TLS 1.3. SOC 2 audit logging: every catalog:write operation generates a forwarded audit event with payload hash. PCI-adjacent compliance: pricing data is never logged; only sku and payload_hash appear in audit events.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: PyPI distribution via GitHub Actions trusted publisher with OIDC requiring no stored PyPI token. Release workflow triggered by tag push: hatch build, twine check, pytest matrix across Python 3.10 through 3.12, bandit security scan, then pypi-publish action with OIDC token. Changelog generated from conventional commits using git-cliff. Semantic versioning: patch releases for bug fixes, minor for new catalog operations, major for breaking API surface changes. Integrators pin with pip install retail-pos-sdk>=1.2 and less than 2.0. Dev environment managed with hatch envs per Python version. No server infrastructure to manage; SDK is purely a distributed package with no deployment topology.
target_output.observability: structlog configured with JSON renderer in production context and console renderer in development. Log levels: DEBUG for cache hits and misses, INFO for credential init and rotation events, WARNING for dead-letter audit events and scope denials, ERROR for HMAC verification failures. Sentry SDK optional; integrators opt in by passing sentry_dsn to RetailPOSClient constructor. Audit event counters exposed via pos.metrics.get_stats() returning dict with queued_count, forwarded_count, failed_count, and dead_letter_count. Rate limit state inspectable via pos.rate_limit.get_bucket_status(key_id).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: No hosting costs; package distributed via PyPI CDN at no charge. Sentry free tier covers exception capture for most integrators. HMAC signing adds approximately 0.1ms per request. AuditForwarder asyncio.Queue adds under 0.05ms enqueue latency. LRU cache reduces catalog API calls by approximately 70 percent for typical read-heavy integrator workloads. Rate limiter prevents integrators from accidentally exhausting retailer API quotas.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (4 weeks): POSCredential with HMAC signing, catalog:read operations, basic audit event logging, pytest matrix across Python 3.10 through 3.12, PyPI trusted publisher pipeline. Phase 2 Security Hardening (2 weeks): key rotation via rotate(), constant-time HMAC verification, scope enforcement decorator, AuditForwarder with asyncio.Queue and exponential retry. Phase 3 Production Polish (2 weeks): rate limit buckets, LRU catalog cache with TTL, Sentry integration, structured logging, developer documentation and changelog.
target_output.development_guidelines: mypy strict mode enforced in CI. ruff lint with E, W, F rule sets. bandit security scan required before release. 85% test coverage gate enforced. All public API methods require docstrings with parameter types and exception documentation. Secret bytes must never appear in logs or exceptions, verified by test asserting __repr__ output. PR requires 2 approvals for changes to the pos.auth module.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: integrators disabling Sentry or structlog; SDK must not fail if observability hooks are absent. Mitigation: all telemetry calls wrapped in try/except. Risk: asyncio event loop not running in integrator context. Mitigation: sync wrappers provided via asyncio.run() with event loop requirement documented. Tradeoff: stdlib-only auth module prevents using cryptography library AEAD features but satisfies zero-dependency constraint. Tradeoff: in-process AuditForwarder queue lost on crash; acceptable for v1 given 1000-event buffer but noted for v2 persistent queue implementation.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Webhook callback registration deferred to v2. Sync vs async API: both provided via asyncio.run() wrappers. Multi-retailer credential profiles deferred to v2. Confirmed httpx over requests for async-native support and HTTP/2 readiness.

metadata.notes: Python POS SDK revision addressing key rotation and audit delivery gaps; phasing and guidelines sections remain thin.

project_goal.value: Automation tool that ingests daily POS transaction logs, runs ML demand forecasting per SKU, and auto-generates replenishment purchase orders for a regional grocery chain
project_goal.rationale: Core business goal is reducing manual buyer intervention for high-velocity SKUs

target_users.value: Procurement buyers and supply chain operations teams at a regional grocery chain
target_users.rationale: Primary users monitor forecasts and approve or override generated purchase orders

project_class.rationale: Scheduled batch automation with ML inference, no end-user UI beyond operational dashboards

capabilities.rationale: Requires batch pipeline, ML inference, EDI integration, and data storage

complexity_level.rationale: ML retraining loop, EDI integration, multi-SKU forecasting at scale, and Prefect orchestration add significant complexity

risk_level.rationale: Incorrect forecasts lead to overstock or stockout; financial impact is recoverable but operationally disruptive

data_sensitivity.rationale: POS transaction and inventory data is confidential to the grocery chain but not regulated

external_exposure.rationale: System operates on internal network; EDI outbound to suppliers is egress-only

access_model.value: IAM roles for ECS tasks; no human-facing login; supplier EDI via authenticated SFTP
access_model.rationale: Fully automated system; human interaction is via Grafana dashboards only

feature_scope.value: Nightly POS ingestion, per-SKU demand model scoring and retraining, 7-day forecast generation, reorder point comparison, purchase order generation, EDI dispatch to suppliers
feature_scope.rationale: Complete pipeline from raw sales data to purchase order dispatch

mvp_scope.value: POS ingestion, forecast scoring for top-500 SKUs, purchase order generation with buyer approval gate before EDI dispatch
mvp_scope.rationale: Full auto-dispatch comes after buyer confidence in forecast accuracy is established

security_baseline.value: IAM role-based access for all AWS resources, encrypted S3 at rest, VPC-isolated Fargate tasks, no public endpoints
security_baseline.rationale: Internal-only system with no external ingress

frontend_stack.value: N/A - no UI; operational visibility via Grafana dashboards
frontend_stack.rationale: Automation tool has no frontend; Grafana provides operational dashboards

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Prefect for orchestration; scikit-learn and statsmodels for demand forecasting models
backend_stack.rationale: Prefect provides flow-level observability and retry management for batch jobs

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for forecast and order data; Redis 7 for job state; S3 for POS log ingestion and model artifacts
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL handles relational forecast data; S3 provides cost-effective raw log storage

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate scheduled tasks in us-east-1 VPC
hosting_target.rationale: Managed containers with no fixed EC2 cost for nightly batch workloads

privacy_retention_policy.value: POS transaction logs retained 3 years in S3 Glacier; forecast data retained 2 years; model artifacts retained indefinitely
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Internal data retention policy for financial analysis and model auditing

future_scope.value: Real-time intraday forecast updates, promotional lift modeling, multi-DC reorder optimization
future_scope.rationale: Year 2 roadmap extensions pending MVP accuracy validation

constraints.value: Nightly batch must complete within 4-hour window (2am-6am); EDI dispatch before supplier cutoff at 7am; no ML framework requiring GPU
constraints.rationale: Supplier EDI systems have morning processing cutoffs

observability_baseline.value: Grafana dashboards for forecast accuracy metrics and job status; Prometheus for pipeline latency; Prefect UI for flow run history
observability_baseline.rationale: Buyers need forecast accuracy trends; ops team needs pipeline health visibility

execution_preference.value: Trunk-based development; nightly automated runs; weekly model retrain; buyer sign-off required for auto-dispatch promotion
execution_preference.rationale: Gradual automation promotion reduces risk of erroneous mass order generation

llm_integration.value: LLM (GPT-4o via OpenAI API) used for anomaly explanation: when a SKU forecast deviates more than 30 percent from baseline, generates a natural-language explanation for buyer review
llm_integration.rationale: Buyers need understandable explanations for forecast outliers to decide on manual overrides

project.goal: Nightly ML demand forecasting pipeline with automated purchase order generation
project.target_users: Grocery chain procurement buyers and supply chain ops
project.timeline: MVP in 12 weeks; full auto-dispatch in 20 weeks

backend.framework: Prefect for orchestration, scikit-learn + statsmodels for forecasting models
backend.api: Internal only; no external API surface

security.auth: IAM roles for ECS tasks; SFTP key auth for EDI

data.cache: Redis 7 for job deduplication and model run state
data.storage: S3 for POS logs and model artifacts

reasoner_reviews.product: The pipeline scope is well-structured from ingestion through EDI dispatch. The buyer approval gate for MVP is the right risk control; full auto-dispatch should only be enabled after forecast MAPE drops below an agreed threshold per SKU category.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Prefect orchestration is appropriate for this pipeline complexity. The round 2 plan still lacks a forecast model versioning strategy; without it, a bad retrain that degrades MAPE has no rollback path. Model artifacts in S3 need versioned prefixes tied to Prefect flow run IDs.
reasoner_reviews.security: IAM role isolation for each Prefect flow task is solid. The EDI SFTP credentials are stored in Secrets Manager which is correct. The round 2 plan was missing encryption of POS log files at rest before S3 upload; this needs to be confirmed as server-side encryption is enabled on the bucket.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 4-hour batch window is feasible for 500 SKUs with CPU-only scikit-learn if parallelized across 4 Fargate vCPUs. For 5000+ SKUs the window tightens; Dask or multiprocessing pool should be evaluated in Phase 2.

backend.service_design: Prefect flows: POS_Ingestion_Flow (S3 trigger), Forecast_Scoring_Flow (per-SKU parallel tasks), PO_Generation_Flow, EDI_Dispatch_Flow. Each flow is an independent ECS Fargate task.
backend.business_modules: demand_forecaster.py: loads model artifact from S3, scores ARIMA or exponential smoothing model per SKU, writes sku_forecasts to PostgreSQL. reorder_engine.py: compares forecast to current inventory_snapshots and supplier_contracts.lead_time_days. po_generator.py: creates replenishment_orders records. edi_client.py: serializes orders to EDI 850 format and SFTP uploads.
backend.background_jobs: Weekly Retrain Job: Prefect flow pulls 90-day sales_transactions from PostgreSQL, retrains per-SKU models, saves versioned artifacts to S3 under run_id prefix. Anomaly Explainer: for SKUs with forecast deviation >30%, calls OpenAI GPT-4o with SKU sales context and deviation delta to generate buyer-readable explanation.
backend.backend_risks: Model retrain producing degraded MAPE with no rollback path; need model versioning and A/B comparison before promotion.

security.auth_design: ECS task roles with least-privilege IAM policies: POS_Ingestion_Flow role reads S3 bucket, Forecast_Scoring_Flow role reads/writes PostgreSQL via RDS IAM auth, EDI_Dispatch_Flow role reads Secrets Manager for SFTP credentials.
security.secrets_management: OpenAI API key and SFTP credentials in AWS Secrets Manager; rotated quarterly. No secrets in environment variables or task definitions.
security.security_risks: Supplier EDI endpoint may reject malformed 850 documents causing missed orders; implement EDI schema validation before dispatch.

data.entities: sales_transactions (id, sku_id, store_id, quantity_sold, transaction_date, pos_source_file), sku_forecasts (id, sku_id, forecast_date, predicted_demand, model_version, model_run_id, mape, created_at), replenishment_orders (id, sku_id, supplier_id, quantity, unit_cost, status enum draft/approved/dispatched/confirmed, created_at), supplier_contracts (id, supplier_id, sku_id, lead_time_days, min_order_qty, price_per_unit), inventory_snapshots (id, sku_id, store_id, on_hand_qty, snapshot_date), model_runs (id, sku_id, run_id, model_type, artifact_s3_key, train_mape, val_mape, promoted_at)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ for transactional data. S3 with server-side AES-256 encryption for raw POS logs and model artifacts. Redis 7 for Prefect task deduplication keys and flow run locks.
data.retention_and_deletion: POS logs archived to S3 Glacier after 90 days, deleted after 3 years. sku_forecasts retained 2 years. model_runs retained indefinitely for audit. replenishment_orders retained 5 years.

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate tasks triggered by Prefect agent on scheduled flows. Each flow runs in isolated task with 2 vCPU and 4GB RAM. VPC private subnets only; NAT Gateway for outbound S3 and OpenAI API calls.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: pytest with 80% coverage, mypy, ruff, then build Docker image, push ECR, update Prefect deployment. Prefect deployment versioned by Git SHA.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scrapes Prefect flow metrics. Grafana dashboards: Forecast MAPE by SKU category, batch job duration, PO generation count, EDI dispatch success rate.
devops.devops_risks: Nightly batch overrun if forecast scoring parallelism is insufficient for SKU count growth; monitor batch duration and alert if >3 hours.

ISS-001.title: No model version rollback path
ISS-001.detail: Rounds 1 and 2 plans have no model versioning strategy. A degraded retrain has no rollback mechanism, risking incorrect purchase orders being dispatched.

history.note: Raised in architect review
Still absent in round 2 plan

ISS-002.title: POS log S3 encryption not confirmed
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 and 2 plans did not explicitly confirm server-side encryption on the S3 bucket receiving POS log files. Now resolved in round 3.

history.note: Raised in security review
Not addressed
AES-256 SSE confirmed in data and deployment sections

ISS-003.title: EDI 850 schema validation missing
ISS-003.detail: No pre-dispatch EDI schema validation exists. Malformed 850 documents sent to suppliers could result in order failures without detection.

history.note: Raised in security review round 2

focus_issues.title: No model version rollback path

previous_plan.title: Grocery Chain Demand Forecasting Pipeline - Round 2
previous_plan.executive_summary: Nightly Prefect pipeline on ECS Fargate ingesting POS logs from S3, running scikit-learn demand models per SKU, generating replenishment orders, and dispatching EDI 850 files to suppliers.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Four Prefect flows: POS_Ingestion_Flow, Forecast_Scoring_Flow, PO_Generation_Flow, EDI_Dispatch_Flow. All tasks run as isolated ECS Fargate containers. PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ for forecast and order data.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: IAM roles per flow task. Secrets Manager for SFTP credentials. S3 SSE-S3 encryption confirmed. No public endpoints.
previous_plan.data_model: sales_transactions, sku_forecasts, replenishment_orders, supplier_contracts, inventory_snapshots, model_runs (without versioned artifact keys)

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 addresses model versioning rollback (ISS-001) by adding versioned S3 artifact paths tied to Prefect run IDs and a MAPE promotion gate that only replaces the active model if validation MAPE improves. ISS-003 EDI validation is addressed by adding an edi_validator task using the pyx12 library for EDI 850 schema checking before dispatch. Plan quality remains weak due to thin guidelines and cost sections.

fix_report.action_taken: Added model versioning: artifacts stored at s3://pos-models/{sku_id}/{run_id}/model.pkl. ModelPromotion task compares new val_mape against current champion stored in model_runs.promoted_at. Promotion only if new val_mape < champion val_mape by more than 0.5 percentage points. Rollback function loads previous artifact from S3 by querying model_runs for last promoted_at.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Degraded retrain cannot automatically replace the champion model; buyers see MAPE regression alert before any promotion.

fix_report.action_taken: Added edi_validator.py task using pyx12 library to validate EDI 850 document against ANSI X12 schema before SFTP upload. Validation failures write to failed_edi_validations table and trigger PagerDuty alert; EDI_Dispatch_Flow is halted for the failing SKUs.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Malformed 850 documents are caught before dispatch; supplier rejection rate drops to near zero.

target_output.title: Grocery Chain Demand Forecasting Pipeline - Round 3
target_output.executive_summary: Nightly Prefect automation pipeline on AWS ECS Fargate that ingests daily POS transaction logs from S3, scores per-SKU ARIMA and exponential smoothing demand models, generates 7-day replenishment forecasts, and auto-creates purchase orders dispatched via EDI 850 to supplier SFTP endpoints. This round adds versioned model artifact management with MAPE-gated promotion and pre-dispatch EDI 850 schema validation, resolving both remaining blocking issues.
target_output.architecture_overview: Five Prefect flows coordinated by a Prefect agent running on ECS Fargate: POS_Ingestion_Flow pulls daily POS logs from S3 and upserts to sales_transactions in PostgreSQL. Forecast_Scoring_Flow parallelizes across up to 4 Fargate vCPUs scoring ARIMA or ETS models per SKU and writing to sku_forecasts. Weekly Retrain_Flow retrains models and saves versioned artifacts to S3 under s3://pos-models/{sku_id}/{run_id}/model.pkl; ModelPromotion task compares val_mape and only promotes if improved. PO_Generation_Flow compares forecasts against inventory_snapshots and supplier_contracts to create replenishment_orders. EDI_Dispatch_Flow validates 850 documents via pyx12 and uploads approved orders to supplier SFTP endpoints.
target_output.technology_stack: Orchestration: Prefect 2.x on ECS Fargate. ML: Python 3.12, scikit-learn, statsmodels for ARIMA and ETS. LLM integration: OpenAI GPT-4o via openai Python SDK for anomaly explanation generation. Data: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ, Redis 7 on ElastiCache, S3 with SSE-AES-256. EDI: pyx12 library for EDI 850 schema validation; SFTP via paramiko. CI/CD: GitHub Actions. Observability: Prometheus plus Grafana. Secrets: AWS Secrets Manager.
target_output.functional_feature_map: POS Ingestion: nightly pull of transaction log files from S3 landing bucket; parse CSV, deduplicate by pos_source_file, upsert to sales_transactions. Demand Forecasting: per-SKU ARIMA or ETS model scored using prior 90-day rolling window; generates 7-day demand forecast in sku_forecasts table. Model Retraining: weekly Prefect flow retrains all SKU models; new artifacts versioned in S3; MAPE promotion gate prevents regression. Anomaly Explanation: for SKUs with forecast deviation greater than 30 percent from 4-week average, calls GPT-4o with sales context to write buyer-readable explanation stored in sku_forecasts.explanation_text. Reorder Engine: compares forecast demand against on-hand inventory and supplier lead times to identify replenishment needs. PO Generation: creates replenishment_orders with draft status; buyer approval required before EDI dispatch in MVP phase. EDI Dispatch: validates 850 documents with pyx12; uploads approved orders to supplier SFTP; updates order status to dispatched.
target_output.system_components: POS_Ingestion_Flow: S3 event trigger via EventBridge Scheduler at 2am; reads CSVs from pos-logs/ prefix; upserts sales_transactions with deduplication on pos_source_file. Forecast_Scoring_Flow: Prefect parallel task map across SKU list; loads model.pkl from S3 champion artifact path; writes sku_forecasts rows with model_version and mape. Retrain_Flow: weekly Sunday 1am; trains ARIMA and ETS per SKU on 90-day window; saves to s3://pos-models/{sku_id}/{run_id}/model.pkl; ModelPromotion task updates model_runs.promoted_at only if val_mape improves. Anomaly_Explainer: Prefect sub-task calling OpenAI GPT-4o with 30-day sales context and deviation magnitude; response stored in sku_forecasts.explanation_text. PO_Generation_Flow: reorder_engine.py computes order quantities from forecast and supplier_contracts; creates replenishment_orders with draft status. EDI_Dispatch_Flow: edi_validator.py runs pyx12 schema check on each 850 document; failures halt dispatch for that supplier batch; paramiko SFTP uploads validated documents.
target_output.workflows: Nightly Forecast Run: EventBridge Scheduler triggers POS_Ingestion_Flow at 2am. POS logs parsed and upserted. Forecast_Scoring_Flow runs parallel tasks across 500 SKUs completing by 3:30am. PO_Generation_Flow creates draft replenishment_orders by 4am. Anomaly_Explainer tasks run for outlier SKUs. Buyer dashboard shows pending orders with GPT-4o explanations by 5am. Buyers approve or override orders before 6am. EDI_Dispatch_Flow validates 850 documents with pyx12, uploads approved orders via SFTP to suppliers before 7am cutoff. Weekly Model Retrain: Retrain_Flow starts Sunday 1am, trains per-SKU ARIMA and ETS models on 90-day sales_transactions window, stores versioned artifacts in S3, ModelPromotion task compares val_mape to champion; promotes only if improved; Grafana alert fires if any SKU MAPE regresses above threshold.
target_output.data_model: sales_transactions: id UUID, sku_id str FK, store_id str, quantity_sold int, transaction_date date, pos_source_file str (dedup key), loaded_at timestamp. sku_forecasts: id UUID, sku_id str, forecast_date date, predicted_demand numeric, model_version str, model_run_id UUID FK, mape numeric, explanation_text text nullable, created_at timestamp. replenishment_orders: id UUID, sku_id str FK, supplier_id str FK, quantity int, unit_cost numeric, status enum draft/approved/dispatched/confirmed/failed, buyer_id str nullable, approved_at timestamp nullable, dispatched_at timestamp nullable, created_at timestamp. supplier_contracts: id UUID, supplier_id str, sku_id str, lead_time_days int, min_order_qty int, price_per_unit numeric, active bool. inventory_snapshots: id UUID, sku_id str, store_id str, on_hand_qty int, snapshot_date date. model_runs: id UUID, sku_id str, run_id UUID, model_type str enum arima/ets, artifact_s3_key str, train_mape numeric, val_mape numeric, promoted_at timestamp nullable, created_at timestamp. failed_edi_validations: id UUID, order_id UUID FK, edi_error_code str, error_detail text, occurred_at timestamp.
target_output.api_design: No external API surface; all interactions are internal Prefect flow tasks. Internal interfaces: reorder_engine.compute_order_qty(sku_id, forecast_demand, on_hand_qty, lead_time_days) returns int. edi_validator.validate_850(document_str) returns ValidationResult with is_valid bool and errors list. anomaly_explainer.explain(sku_id, deviation_pct, sales_context_json) calls OpenAI GPT-4o and returns explanation_text str. Model artifact access: s3_client.get_champion_artifact(sku_id) reads model_runs table for latest promoted_at and fetches from S3.
target_output.security_and_compliance: IAM role per Prefect flow task with least-privilege S3 and RDS access. RDS IAM authentication for PostgreSQL; no embedded DB passwords. S3 bucket with SSE-AES-256 server-side encryption enforced via bucket policy denying uploads without SSE header. Secrets Manager for OpenAI API key and SFTP credentials; rotated quarterly. VPC private subnets; no public-facing endpoints; NAT Gateway for outbound S3, RDS, and OpenAI API calls. EDI SFTP uses RSA key pairs stored in Secrets Manager; host key verification enabled to prevent MITM. pyx12 EDI schema validation prevents malformed 850 dispatch.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate tasks triggered by Prefect agent on EventBridge Scheduled Rules. Each flow runs in isolated task with 2 vCPU and 4GB RAM inside a VPC private subnet. Docker images built and pushed to ECR on every main branch merge via GitHub Actions. Deployment: GitHub Actions builds image, runs pytest with 80% coverage gate, pushes to ECR, registers new Prefect deployment. Prefect deployments versioned by Git SHA for traceability. Retrain_Flow runs on AWS Fargate Spot to reduce weekly batch cost. Database migrations managed via Alembic; run as one-off ECS task before primary flow execution. Rollback: previous Prefect deployment re-activated via Prefect API if new deployment fails health check flow.
target_output.observability: Prometheus scrapes Prefect flow metrics exposed on task sidecar port. Grafana dashboards: Forecast MAPE by SKU category trending over 30 days, batch job duration per flow with 3-hour alert threshold, replenishment_orders count by status, EDI dispatch success rate, model_runs promotion history. PagerDuty alerts on: batch overrun beyond 3 hours, EDI validation failure rate above 5 percent, MAPE regression on promoted model, failed_edi_validations count above zero for a supplier batch. Prefect UI shows complete flow run DAG with task-level logs accessible by ops team.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: ECS Fargate on-demand for nightly batch approximately 2 dollars per night at 2 vCPU for 90 minutes. Retrain_Flow on Fargate Spot reduces weekly retrain cost by 70 percent. RDS Multi-AZ db.t3.medium approximately 80 dollars per month. S3 POS log storage approximately 5 dollars per month. OpenAI GPT-4o calls for anomaly explanations approximately 10 dollars per month at 500 SKUs with 10 percent anomaly rate. Total estimated 150 dollars per month. Scaling beyond 500 SKUs: parallelize Forecast_Scoring_Flow tasks across 8 vCPU Fargate task; evaluate Dask for 5000 plus SKU scale.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (8 weeks): POS ingestion, Forecast_Scoring_Flow for top-500 SKUs, PO_Generation_Flow with buyer approval gate, basic Grafana dashboard, GitHub Actions CI/CD. Phase 2 Security and Quality (4 weeks): model versioning with MAPE promotion gate, EDI 850 pyx12 schema validation, EDI_Dispatch_Flow with SFTP upload, PagerDuty alerts. Phase 3 LLM and Automation (4 weeks): Anomaly_Explainer with GPT-4o integration, weekly Retrain_Flow, auto-dispatch promotion after MAPE threshold met by buyers, full Grafana observability suite.
target_output.development_guidelines: Prefect flow functions typed with pydantic models for task inputs and outputs. mypy strict for all pipeline modules. pytest with 80% coverage gate; Prefect flows tested with mocked S3 and RDS using moto and pytest-postgresql. ruff lint enforced in CI. Model artifact paths follow s3://pos-models/{sku_id}/{run_id}/model.pkl convention enforced in constants.py.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: ARIMA models underperform for seasonal SKUs with promotional spikes; mitigation: track MAPE per SKU category in Grafana and flag categories where MAPE exceeds 20 percent for model type review. Risk: EDI 850 format varies by supplier; mitigation: pyx12 supports standard ANSI X12 but supplier-specific segments require per-supplier mapping validation. Tradeoff: CPU-only scikit-learn models satisfy no-GPU constraint but limit model complexity; acceptable for MVP accuracy targets of MAPE under 15 percent for top-500 SKUs.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Model versioning strategy confirmed as S3-versioned artifacts with Prefect run_id. Auto-dispatch promotion gate confirmed as buyer-controlled MAPE threshold per category. Intraday forecasting deferred to v2. Dask parallelism evaluation deferred to Phase 3.

metadata.notes: Grocery demand forecasting automation round 3; addresses model versioning and EDI validation but guidelines and cost sections remain thin.

project_goal.value: Data pipeline synchronizing product catalog, pricing, and stock availability across retailer ERP, e-commerce platform, and third-party marketplace integrations via a unified API layer with sub-minute latency
project_goal.rationale: All downstream channels must reflect real-time inventory state to prevent overselling and price discrepancies

target_users.value: Internal merchandising team, third-party marketplace partners (Amazon, eBay), and e-commerce platform integration team
target_users.rationale: Multiple consumer types require both internal admin and partner-facing API access

project_class.rationale: Core value is data movement and transformation, not UI or application logic

capabilities.rationale: Requires real-time data fan-out, external marketplace integrations, nightly reconciliation, and partner-facing API

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-channel fan-out with sub-minute SLA, marketplace API integration, DynamoDB streams, and reconciliation logic

risk_level.rationale: Sync failures cause price discrepancies and overselling; financial impact is recoverable but reputational risk is meaningful

data_sensitivity.rationale: Catalog pricing is commercially sensitive but not regulated data

external_exposure.rationale: Marketplace partners access sync status and catalog data via authenticated API

access_model.value: JWT-based API authentication for partners; IAM roles for internal Lambda functions; DynamoDB streams for event fan-out
access_model.rationale: Partners need authenticated API access; internal pipeline components use IAM only

feature_scope.value: ERP webhook ingestion, catalog transformation and validation, real-time fan-out to all registered channels, per-channel adapter for marketplace APIs, nightly reconciliation, sync event audit log, partner API for sync status and catalog query
feature_scope.rationale: Complete pipeline from ERP change event to all downstream channels

mvp_scope.value: ERP webhook ingestion, catalog fan-out to e-commerce platform and one marketplace, sync_events audit log, partner sync status API
mvp_scope.rationale: Two-channel sync proves the architecture before adding more marketplace adapters

security_baseline.value: JWT authentication for partner API, HMAC webhook verification for ERP ingestion, IAM least-privilege for Lambda, DynamoDB encryption at rest
security_baseline.rationale: Standard security controls for partner-facing API and internal pipeline

frontend_stack.value: N/A - data pipeline with no UI; partner API is RESTful
frontend_stack.rationale: No frontend component; API Gateway serves partner requests

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with Gin for API Gateway handlers; Go Lambda functions for catalog transformation and channel adapters
backend_stack.rationale: Go provides high throughput for fan-out with low cold start latency on Lambda

data_platform.value: DynamoDB for canonical catalog store with streams; Redis 7 for channel sync state cache; S3 for reconciliation snapshots
data_platform.rationale: DynamoDB Streams enable event-driven fan-out without polling; low-latency reads for API responses

hosting_target.value: AWS Lambda plus API Gateway; us-east-1 primary
hosting_target.rationale: Serverless scales to burst sync events without provisioned capacity

privacy_retention_policy.value: sync_events audit log retained 1 year; catalog_items retained indefinitely; reconciliation S3 snapshots retained 90 days
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Marketplace partner SLAs require 1-year dispute resolution window on sync events

future_scope.value: Additional marketplace adapters (Walmart, Target+), real-time pricing rules engine, catalog enrichment with AI-generated descriptions
future_scope.rationale: Year 2 roadmap extensions after MVP validates pipeline reliability

constraints.value: Sub-minute fan-out SLA for stock changes; API rate limits per marketplace (Amazon: 2 req/s, eBay: 5000 req/day); DynamoDB pay-per-request billing
constraints.rationale: Marketplace API quotas shape the throughput design of channel adapters

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM for Lambda invocations; custom metrics for sync latency per channel, fan-out success rate, and reconciliation discrepancy count
observability_baseline.rationale: Merchandising team needs channel-level sync health visibility

execution_preference.value: Event-driven architecture; changes propagate within 30 seconds; nightly reconciliation catches any missed updates
execution_preference.rationale: Event-driven fan-out is the only approach meeting sub-minute SLA

project.goal: Sub-minute catalog sync pipeline from ERP to all downstream channels
project.target_users: Merchandising team and marketplace partners

backend.framework: Gin for API handlers; Lambda functions for adapters
backend.api: RESTful partner API via API Gateway

security.auth: JWT for partner API; HMAC webhook verification for ERP ingestion

data.cache: Redis 7 for channel sync state

reasoner_reviews.product: The pipeline scope covers the critical path from ERP to all channels. The nightly reconciliation job is essential insurance for any fan-out gaps. Marketplace adapter extensibility should be designed as a plugin interface from day one so adding Walmart or Target Plus does not require core changes.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: DynamoDB Streams plus Lambda fan-out is the right pattern for sub-minute SLA at this scale. The round 2 plan added per-channel DLQ which is correct. The remaining gap is the channel_mappings configuration: hardcoded channel configs in Lambda environment variables do not scale beyond 3 channels. A channel registry in DynamoDB allows runtime channel registration without redeployment.
reasoner_reviews.security: JWT partner authentication is solid. The ERP webhook HMAC verification was added in round 2 which is correct. The nightly reconciliation Lambda needs its own IAM role with read-only DynamoDB access; the round 2 plan reuses the fan-out role which is overly permissive.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Amazon 2 req/s and eBay 5000 req/day limits are handled by the per-channel rate limiter token bucket in Redis. The reconciliation job running against the full catalog in DynamoDB needs a paginated Scan with consistent reads; at 1M catalog items this takes approximately 20 minutes which fits the nightly window.

backend.service_design: Catalog fan-out via DynamoDB Streams trigger to CatalogFanout Lambda. Per-channel adapters as separate Lambda functions: AmazonAdapter, eBayAdapter, EcommerceAdapter. Partner API via API Gateway with Go Gin handlers deployed as Lambda.
backend.api_patterns: RESTful partner API: GET /v1/sync-status/{channel_id}, GET /v1/catalog/{sku_id}, POST /v1/channels (admin only). ERP ingestion: POST /v1/ingest/erp-webhook with HMAC-SHA256 verification.
backend.failure_handling: Per-channel SQS DLQ for failed fan-out events. Adapter retries with exponential backoff. Reconciliation job auto-corrects discrepancies from DLQ-captured missed updates.
backend.backend_risks: Amazon Marketplace API throttling during bulk catalog updates; implement adaptive throttling in AmazonAdapter using token bucket keyed by API credential in Redis.

security.auth_design: Partner API: JWT signed with RS256, issued by internal auth service, validated at API Gateway Lambda authorizer. ERP webhook: HMAC-SHA256 signature on payload validated in IngestHandler. IAM: separate roles for CatalogFanout, each ChannelAdapter, ReconciliationJob, and PartnerAPI Lambda.
security.authorization_model: Partners access only their channel sync data; JWT claims include channel_id list. Admin endpoints require admin:catalog scope.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: All sync_events written to DynamoDB with TTL-based expiry at 1 year. Partner API calls logged to CloudTrail. DLQ messages logged to CloudWatch for failure analysis.
security.security_risks: ERP webhook spoofing if HMAC key is leaked; rotate HMAC key quarterly via Secrets Manager rotation.

data.entities: catalog_items (sku_id, name, description, price, stock_qty, status, category, last_modified), price_rules (rule_id, sku_id, channel_id, price_override, effective_from, effective_to), stock_levels (sku_id, warehouse_id, on_hand_qty, reserved_qty, updated_at), channel_mappings (channel_id, channel_type, adapter_function, rate_limit_rpm, active), sync_events (event_id, sku_id, channel_id, event_type, status, latency_ms, error_detail, occurred_at), marketplace_listings (listing_id, sku_id, channel_id, marketplace_item_id, status, last_synced_at)
data.storage_design: DynamoDB on-demand billing; catalog_items table with Streams enabled for fan-out. GSI on channel_id for marketplace_listings. Redis 7 for per-channel rate limit token buckets and last-synced-at cache.
data.consistency_notes: DynamoDB eventual consistency acceptable for catalog reads; consistent reads required only in reconciliation Scan.
data.migration_notes: Initial catalog load from ERP via bulk import Lambda reading from S3 CSV export; DynamoDB BatchWrite with 25-item limit batching.

devops.deployment_topology: Lambda functions deployed via SAM or Serverless Framework. API Gateway Regional endpoint. DynamoDB in us-east-1 with point-in-time recovery enabled. S3 reconciliation bucket with versioning.
devops.ci_cd_design: CircleCI pipeline: go test with race detector, go vet, golangci-lint, SAM build, SAM deploy to staging, integration test suite against staging, promote to production. Lambda alias-based deployments with 10% canary traffic before full cutover.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog Lambda extension on all functions. Custom metrics: sync_latency_ms per channel, fan_out_success_rate, reconciliation_discrepancy_count. Datadog monitors alert on p99 latency above 45 seconds or fan-out error rate above 1 percent.
devops.devops_risks: Lambda cold starts on CatalogFanout during burst ERP bulk updates; use provisioned concurrency for CatalogFanout Lambda during known bulk import windows.

target_output.title: Retail Catalog Synchronization Pipeline - Round 3
target_output.executive_summary: An event-driven Go Lambda pipeline on AWS that synchronizes product catalog, pricing, and stock availability from the retailer ERP to e-commerce and marketplace channels within 30 seconds of any ERP change. DynamoDB Streams trigger per-channel adapter Lambdas with per-channel SQS DLQs, Redis-backed rate limiting, and a nightly reconciliation job. A partner-facing REST API provides sync status and catalog queries. This revision adds a channel_mappings DynamoDB registry and a dedicated read-only ReconciliationJob IAM role.
target_output.architecture_overview: ERP system sends stock and catalog change events to the IngestHandler Lambda via API Gateway webhook endpoint. IngestHandler validates HMAC-SHA256 signature, transforms the ERP payload to canonical CatalogItem format, and writes to DynamoDB catalog_items table. DynamoDB Streams triggers CatalogFanout Lambda which reads channel_mappings table to discover active channels and fans out to per-channel adapter Lambda functions (AmazonAdapter, eBayAdapter, EcommerceAdapter) via SNS fan-out. Each adapter enforces its channel-specific rate limit using a Redis token bucket and calls the downstream marketplace API. Failed adapter invocations are routed to per-channel SQS DLQs. Nightly reconciliation Lambda scans DynamoDB catalog_items, compares against marketplace_listings, and auto-corrects discrepancies. Partner API served by API Gateway with Lambda authorizer validating RS256 JWTs.
target_output.technology_stack: Runtime: Go 1.22 Lambda functions with Gin for API Gateway handlers. Canonical store: DynamoDB on-demand with Streams. Event fan-out: DynamoDB Streams to Lambda, SNS for parallel adapter invocation. Rate limiting: Redis 7 on ElastiCache for per-channel token buckets. DLQ: SQS per channel. Reconciliation storage: S3 with versioning. CI/CD: CircleCI with Lambda alias canary deployment. Observability: Datadog Lambda extension with custom metrics. Secrets: AWS Secrets Manager for JWT signing key and marketplace API credentials.
target_output.functional_feature_map: ERP Ingestion: POST /v1/ingest/erp-webhook receives ERP change events; IngestHandler validates HMAC-SHA256 signature from Secrets Manager key, transforms to CatalogItem, writes to DynamoDB. Catalog Fan-out: DynamoDB Streams record triggers CatalogFanout; reads channel_mappings for active channels; publishes to SNS topic; per-channel adapter Lambda subscribes. Per-Channel Adapters: AmazonAdapter maps CatalogItem to Amazon Product API format, enforces 2 req/s token bucket; eBayAdapter maps to eBay Inventory API, enforces daily quota counter; EcommerceAdapter posts to internal e-commerce platform REST API. Channel Registry: channel_mappings table allows adding new marketplace channels without Lambda redeployment by inserting a new row with adapter_function ARN. Reconciliation: nightly Lambda scans catalog_items and marketplace_listings, identifies stale listings, triggers re-sync for discrepancies, uploads snapshot CSV to S3. Partner API: GET /v1/sync-status/{channel_id} returns latest sync_events for the JWT-authorized channel.
target_output.system_components: IngestHandler Lambda: verifies ERP webhook HMAC, deserializes to CatalogItem struct, writes DynamoDB PutItem. CatalogFanout Lambda: triggered by DynamoDB Stream NEW_IMAGE events; reads channel_mappings (cached in process memory TTL 60s); publishes to SNS catalog-sync-topic. AmazonAdapter Lambda: subscribes to SNS; maps CatalogItem to Amazon Listings API payload; Redis token bucket at 2 req/s; on throttle retries with backoff; on final failure sends to sqs://amazon-dlq. eBayAdapter Lambda: daily quota counter in Redis; maps to eBay UpdateInventory payload; DLQ at sqs://ebay-dlq. EcommerceAdapter Lambda: internal HTTP POST to e-commerce platform; circuit breaker in Redis. ReconciliationJob Lambda: nightly EventBridge trigger; scans catalog_items with consistent read; joins against marketplace_listings; for each discrepancy triggers re-sync via CatalogFanout; uploads discrepancy CSV to S3. PartnerAPI Lambda: Gin router; Lambda authorizer extracts channel_id claims from JWT; GET /v1/sync-status queries sync_events GSI.
target_output.workflows: Real-Time Stock Change Fan-out: ERP sends POST /v1/ingest/erp-webhook with HMAC signature. IngestHandler verifies signature, maps ERP SKU to catalog_items, writes DynamoDB PutItem with stock_qty updated. DynamoDB Stream delivers NEW_IMAGE to CatalogFanout within 1 second. CatalogFanout reads active channels from channel_mappings, publishes to SNS. AmazonAdapter and eBayAdapter invoked in parallel; each applies rate limit, calls marketplace API, writes sync_event with status and latency_ms. Total fan-out completes within 20-30 seconds for typical single-SKU change. Nightly Reconciliation: EventBridge triggers ReconciliationJob at 1am UTC. Lambda scans entire catalog_items table with consistent reads in paginated batches of 100. For each catalog_item, queries marketplace_listings by sku_id GSI. Compares last_modified against last_synced_at; if stale by more than 24 hours, publishes re-sync event to CatalogFanout. Uploads discrepancy CSV to S3 reconciliation-snapshots bucket. Datadog metric incremented for each corrected discrepancy.
target_output.data_model: catalog_items: sku_id str PK, name str, description str, price Decimal, stock_qty int, status enum active/inactive/discontinued, category str, last_modified ISO8601 timestamp, version int (for optimistic locking). price_rules: rule_id UUID, sku_id str, channel_id str, price_override Decimal, effective_from date, effective_to date nullable. stock_levels: sku_id str PK, warehouse_id str SK, on_hand_qty int, reserved_qty int, updated_at timestamp. channel_mappings: channel_id str PK, channel_type str enum amazon/ebay/ecommerce, adapter_function_arn str, rate_limit_rpm int, daily_quota int nullable, active bool, registered_at timestamp. sync_events: event_id UUID, sku_id str, channel_id str, event_type str enum stock_update/price_update/listing_create/listing_delete, status enum success/failed/dlq, latency_ms int, error_detail str nullable, occurred_at timestamp; TTL at 1 year. marketplace_listings: listing_id UUID, sku_id str, channel_id str, marketplace_item_id str, status enum active/pending/error, last_synced_at timestamp.
target_output.api_design: Partner-facing REST API at /v1 via API Gateway Regional endpoint. GET /v1/sync-status/{channel_id}?sku_id={optional}&limit=50 returns paginated sync_events for the authenticated channel. GET /v1/catalog/{sku_id} returns CatalogItem with current price and stock_qty. POST /v1/channels (admin:catalog scope required) registers a new channel_mapping. POST /v1/ingest/erp-webhook (HMAC-verified, not JWT) for ERP ingestion. All partner endpoints require Authorization: Bearer {JWT} with RS256 signature. Rate limit: 100 req/min per partner JWT. Responses: application/json; error shape includes error_code and detail.
target_output.security_and_compliance: ERP webhook authentication: HMAC-SHA256 over request body with shared secret from Secrets Manager; verified in IngestHandler with hmac.Equal to prevent timing attacks. Partner API authentication: RS256 JWT validated at API Gateway Lambda authorizer; claims include channel_id list and scope. IAM roles: CatalogFanout role allows DynamoDB:GetItem on channel_mappings and SNS:Publish only. AmazonAdapter role allows Secrets Manager:GetSecretValue for Amazon credentials only. ReconciliationJob role allows DynamoDB:Scan and DynamoDB:GetItem on catalog_items and marketplace_listings plus S3:PutObject on reconciliation-snapshots; no write access to catalog tables. JWT signing key and marketplace API credentials in Secrets Manager with 90-day rotation. DynamoDB encryption at rest via AWS-managed key. TLS 1.3 enforced at API Gateway.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Infrastructure as code via AWS SAM templates. CircleCI pipeline: go test with race detector, go vet, golangci-lint, SAM build, SAM deploy to staging environment, integration test suite against staging (covers ingest, fan-out, and API endpoints), promote to production via SAM deploy with Lambda alias canary at 10 percent traffic for 10 minutes, shift 100 percent on health check pass. Environments: staging (single-region, reduced DynamoDB capacity), production (us-east-1, DynamoDB on-demand, DLQ alarms enabled). DynamoDB point-in-time recovery enabled in production. EventBridge Scheduler for ReconciliationJob at 1am UTC daily. Lambda provisioned concurrency for CatalogFanout during bulk import windows to eliminate cold starts. Rollback: Lambda alias rollback to previous version via SAM parameter override in CircleCI on failed canary.
target_output.observability: Datadog Lambda extension on all functions capturing invocation metrics. Custom Datadog metrics: catalog.sync_latency_ms tagged by channel_id (p50, p95, p99), catalog.fan_out_success_rate per channel, catalog.reconciliation_discrepancy_count, catalog.dlq_depth per channel SQS queue. Datadog monitors: alert on p99 sync_latency above 45 seconds (SLA breach), fan_out_success_rate below 99 percent for any channel, DLQ depth above 0 for more than 5 minutes. CloudWatch Logs for all Lambda invocations with structured JSON including sku_id, channel_id, and correlation event_id. Datadog APM trace spans across CatalogFanout through ChannelAdapter to capture end-to-end fan-out latency.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Lambda: approximately 5 million invocations per month at average 200ms duration equals approximately 50 dollars per month. DynamoDB on-demand: approximately 30 dollars per month for 1M catalog items with moderate write throughput. Redis ElastiCache cache.t3.micro approximately 25 dollars per month. API Gateway: approximately 5 dollars per month for partner API traffic. Total approximately 110 dollars per month at current scale. Scaling: DynamoDB auto-scales read and write capacity on demand; Lambda concurrency scales automatically with SNS subscription. Amazon rate limit of 2 req/s is the binding constraint for large bulk catalog updates; batch adapter with exponential backoff handles throttle gracefully.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): ERP IngestHandler with HMAC verification, CatalogFanout with DynamoDB Streams, EcommerceAdapter and AmazonAdapter, channel_mappings DynamoDB registry, sync_events audit log, partner GET /v1/sync-status API, CircleCI pipeline with staging deploy. Phase 2 Resilience (3 weeks): SQS DLQ per channel, per-channel Redis rate limiting, ReconciliationJob with dedicated IAM role, S3 reconciliation snapshots, Datadog monitors and SLA alerts. Phase 3 Expansion (3 weeks): eBayAdapter, Lambda alias canary deployment, price_rules support in fan-out, admin POST /v1/channels endpoint, integration test suite expansion.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go modules with pinned dependency versions in go.sum. golangci-lint with errcheck, staticcheck, and gosec linters enforced in CI. Lambda handlers use structured zerolog for JSON logging with sku_id and channel_id in every log entry. Unit tests use mockery for DynamoDB and SNS clients; integration tests use LocalStack. PR requires 2 approvals for changes to IngestHandler or CatalogFanout. SAM templates version-controlled alongside application code.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: DynamoDB Streams shard iterator expiry during Lambda downtime causes missed fan-out events; mitigated by nightly reconciliation which catches all missed updates within 24 hours. Risk: Amazon Marketplace API changes breaking AmazonAdapter; mitigated by adapter contract tests in CI that stub Amazon API responses. Tradeoff: DynamoDB eventual consistency for catalog reads versus strong consistency; strong consistency only required in reconciliation Scan and explicitly requested there. Tradeoff: Lambda cold start latency for CatalogFanout; provisioned concurrency during known bulk windows trades cost for latency SLA guarantee.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Channel configuration approach resolved as DynamoDB channel_mappings table enabling runtime registration. Reconciliation IAM role separation confirmed. Pricing rules engine deferred to v2. Walmart and Target Plus adapters deferred to v2. Confirmed Lambda alias canary deployment for production safety.

metadata.notes: Retail catalog sync pipeline round 3; strong domain-specific content with resolved channel registry and IAM separation.

project_goal.value: Windows desktop application for warehouse managers providing real-time bin-level inventory visibility, pick-and-pack workflow management, and barcode scanning integration to replace paper-based picking sheets
project_goal.rationale: Core goal is eliminating paper picking sheets and giving workers real-time bin guidance

target_users.value: Warehouse floor workers and shift managers at a mid-size electronics distributor
target_users.rationale: Primary users are non-technical warehouse staff using USB barcode scanners and touchscreen workstations

project_class.rationale: On-premise Windows workstation application with local data layer syncing to central PostgreSQL

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, realtime, integrations
capabilities.rationale: Requires local UI, local Express API, SQLite local DB, AD auth, real-time barcode events, and central PostgreSQL sync

complexity_level.rationale: Local-first with central sync, barcode scanner hardware integration, and optimistic UI updates adds moderate complexity

risk_level.rationale: Incorrect bin assignments or missed scans cause mis-picks leading to wrong items shipped; financially recoverable but operationally disruptive

data_sensitivity.rationale: Inventory and order data is internal business data; not regulated or customer-facing

external_exposure.rationale: Application runs entirely on-premise; central PostgreSQL is on internal LAN only

access_model.value: Active Directory LDAP authentication; RBAC with warehouse_worker and shift_manager roles
access_model.rationale: AD integration required by IT policy for all on-premise applications

feature_scope.value: Barcode scan to bin assignment, pick task routing, packing slip generation, real-time inventory updates, cycle count sessions, worker shift management, central sync with PostgreSQL
feature_scope.rationale: Full scope of warehouse operations to replace paper processes

mvp_scope.value: Barcode scanning, pick task creation and routing, bin-level inventory display, central PostgreSQL sync
mvp_scope.rationale: Core pick workflow replaces paper; packing slips and cycle counts come in Phase 2

security_baseline.value: AD LDAP authentication, session tokens in memory only, no credentials stored to disk, internal network only, New Relic agent for crash monitoring
security_baseline.rationale: On-premise app with no external exposure; AD handles identity

frontend_stack.value: Electron 30 with React 18 and TypeScript; Tailwind CSS for styling
frontend_stack.rationale: Electron enables Windows desktop packaging with React UI reuse; TypeScript reduces runtime errors in warehouse-critical flows

backend_stack.value: Node.js 22 with Express running as local API server inside Electron main process
backend_stack.rationale: Local Express API separates data layer from renderer process for security and testability

data_platform.value: SQLite (local, via better-sqlite3) syncing to PostgreSQL 16 on central on-premise server
data_platform.rationale: Local SQLite enables offline operation during network interruptions; central PostgreSQL is the system of record

hosting_target.value: On-premise Windows workstations; installer distributed via internal IT tooling
hosting_target.rationale: No cloud; all infrastructure on distributor LAN

privacy_retention_policy.value: Inventory and pick data retained 2 years on central PostgreSQL; local SQLite cache purged after 30 days
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: 2-year retention for order audit trail per distributor policy

future_scope.value: Mobile handset support for Android barcode scanners, advanced cycle count scheduling, integration with distributor ERP system
future_scope.rationale: Android handsets in Phase 3 after desktop proves the core workflow

constraints.value: Must run on Windows 10 and 11; USB HID barcode scanner input; no cloud dependencies; offline capable for up to 4 hours
constraints.rationale: Warehouse LAN can experience outages; app must remain functional for a full shift without central server

observability_baseline.value: New Relic agent for crash reporting and performance monitoring; Winston structured logging to local log files; sync failure alerts via internal email
observability_baseline.rationale: IT team needs crash visibility; warehouse ops needs sync failure alerts

execution_preference.value: Agile sprints; installer-based releases distributed to workstations via SCCM every 2 weeks
execution_preference.rationale: IT uses SCCM for all workstation software deployment

project.goal: Windows desktop app for warehouse pick-and-pack replacing paper picking sheets
project.target_users: Warehouse floor workers and shift managers

frontend.state: Zustand for local UI state; React Query for Express API calls

backend.framework: Express local API server in Electron main process

security.auth: Active Directory LDAP via ldapts library
security.transport: localhost only; LDAP over TLS to AD

data.db: SQLite via better-sqlite3 for local cache
data.sync: Bidirectional sync to PostgreSQL 16 on central server via pg library

devops.hosting: On-premise Windows workstations via SCCM installer

reasoner_reviews.product: Pick task routing and bin-level display are the core value. The round 1 plan lacked a conflict resolution strategy for inventory_adjustments when a workstation makes local writes during network outage and the central server has diverged. Last-writer-wins is the simplest policy but needs explicit timestamp comparison.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Electron with local Express API is a sound architecture for this use case. The sync mechanism needs a sync_log table in SQLite to track which local changes have been pushed to PostgreSQL; without it, a mid-sync crash could result in duplicate or missing inventory updates. The barcode scanner HID input should be handled in the Electron main process via node-hid, not in the renderer, to avoid focus issues.
reasoner_reviews.security: AD LDAP authentication with ldapts is correct. Session tokens must be stored in memory only, never persisted to localStorage or SQLite. The Express local API should only bind to 127.0.0.1, not 0.0.0.0, to prevent other LAN hosts from accessing the local API.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 4-hour offline capability is achievable with SQLite local cache. The sync scheduler should attempt central sync every 60 seconds and queue failed syncs in the sync_log table. USB HID barcode input latency must be under 100ms from scan to screen update.

frontend.app_structure: Electron BrowserWindow renders React SPA. Main process hosts Express server and node-hid barcode listener. Renderer communicates with main via contextBridge IPC for scanner events and via localhost fetch for data API calls.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Login screen with AD username/password. Pick Queue dashboard showing open pick_tasks sorted by priority. Pick Task Detail: bin location map, scan prompt, progress indicator. Inventory Browser: warehouse_bins grid with stock levels. Shift Manager View: worker shift status, task assignment, cycle_count_sessions. Packing Slip printer preview.
frontend.state_management: Zustand store for scanner session state (current_task_id, last_scanned_barcode). React Query for all Express API calls with 30-second stale time. Offline indicator component watches navigator.onLine and sync_log pending count.
frontend.frontend_risks: Electron security: contextBridge must expose only specific IPC channels; no nodeIntegration in renderer.

backend.service_design: Express server on localhost:3847. Routes: /api/pick-tasks, /api/bins, /api/scans, /api/inventory, /api/sync-status. SQLite database layer via better-sqlite3 with WAL mode for concurrent read/write.
backend.business_modules: pick_router.js: assigns workers to pick_tasks based on bin proximity and worker_shift.active. barcode_validator.js: validates scanned SKU against expected pick_task.sku_id. inventory_adjuster.js: applies bin quantity changes to SQLite and queues to sync_log. sync_manager.js: background interval syncing sync_log entries to PostgreSQL.
backend.failure_handling: Sync failures logged to sync_log with retry_count; exponential backoff up to 5 retries; items exceeding 5 retries flagged for IT review via email alert.

security.auth_design: ldapts binds to AD with service account credentials from Electron keytar OS credential store. Worker authenticates with AD username/password; on success, Express generates JWT stored in memory; JWT required on all API routes via middleware.
security.authorization_model: RBAC: warehouse_worker role can read pick_tasks, submit scans, view own bins. shift_manager role adds task assignment, cycle count initiation, all workers view.
security.secrets_management: AD service account password stored in OS credential store via Electron keytar; never in SQLite or log files. JWT secret generated at app startup, stored in memory.
security.security_risks: Electron nodeIntegration must be disabled in renderer; contextBridge restricts IPC surface to prevent renderer-side code execution.

data.entities: warehouse_bins (bin_id, aisle, shelf, position, sku_id, quantity, last_counted_at), pick_tasks (task_id, sales_order_id, sku_id, required_qty, bin_id, assigned_worker_id, status, created_at, completed_at), packing_slips (slip_id, sales_order_id, worker_id, printed_at, items_json), barcode_scans (scan_id, task_id, scanned_sku, expected_sku, match bool, scanned_at), inventory_adjustments (adj_id, bin_id, delta_qty, reason, worker_id, adjusted_at, synced_at nullable), cycle_count_sessions (session_id, bin_id, counted_qty, counter_id, session_date), worker_shifts (shift_id, worker_id, start_time, end_time, station_id), sync_log (log_id, table_name, record_id, operation, payload_json, synced_at nullable, retry_count)
data.storage_design: SQLite WAL mode for local cache; better-sqlite3 synchronous API for atomicity. PostgreSQL 16 on central server is system of record; sync_log drives replication from local to central.
data.consistency_notes: Last-writer-wins on inventory_adjustments conflicts resolved by comparing adjusted_at timestamp; higher timestamp wins on central server.

devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: ESLint, TypeScript check, Jest unit tests, electron-builder package for Windows installer, upload artifact. IT distributes installer via SCCM to workstations on 2-week cadence.
devops.deployment_topology: Electron app installed on each warehouse workstation. Central PostgreSQL 16 on on-premise Windows Server 2022 with daily pg_dump backup to NAS.
devops.observability_stack: New Relic agent installed in Electron main process. Winston logs to AppData/warehouse-app/logs with 30-day rotation. Sync failure email alerts via nodemailer to IT distribution list.
devops.devops_risks: SCCM deployment failure leaving some workstations on older version; ensure backward-compatible SQLite schema migrations.

ISS-001.title: Sync conflict resolution not specified
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan had no conflict resolution strategy for inventory_adjustments made locally during network outage diverging from central server. Resolved in round 2 with last-writer-wins by adjusted_at timestamp.

history.note: Raised in architect review
