ISS-002.title: Express local API binding to 0.0.0.0
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan did not specify the Express server bind address. Default Express binds to 0.0.0.0 which exposes the local API to other LAN hosts. Must bind to 127.0.0.1 only.

history.note: Raised in security review

focus_issues.detail: Express must bind to 127.0.0.1 only to prevent LAN exposure of local API.

fix_report.action_taken: Explicitly bound Express server to 127.0.0.1 only: app.listen(3847, '127.0.0.1'). Added this requirement to deployment_and_operations and development_guidelines. Added firewall rule note for Windows Firewall to block inbound on port 3847 except localhost.

target_output.title: Warehouse Pick-and-Pack Desktop Application - Round 2
target_output.executive_summary: An Electron 30 and React 18 TypeScript desktop application for Windows warehouse workstations at a mid-size electronics distributor. The app provides real-time bin-level inventory display, pick task routing guided by barcode scanner input, and packing slip generation. A local Express server on port 3847 bound to 127.0.0.1 serves as the data layer, backed by SQLite in WAL mode, with bidirectional sync to central PostgreSQL 16. AD LDAP authenticates workers and shift managers. This revision adds explicit Express localhost binding and sync_log-based conflict resolution.
target_output.architecture_overview: Electron main process hosts three subsystems: Express API server bound to 127.0.0.1:3847, node-hid barcode scanner listener, and sync_manager background service. Renderer process runs a React 18 SPA communicating with main via contextBridge IPC for scanner events and via localhost fetch for all data operations. SQLite database in WAL mode provides offline-capable local storage for warehouse_bins, pick_tasks, inventory_adjustments, and sync_log. sync_manager periodically flushes sync_log entries to central PostgreSQL 16 on the warehouse LAN. Worker authentication via AD LDAP using ldapts; JWT issued on successful bind, stored in memory only. New Relic agent monitors the main process for crashes and performance.
target_output.technology_stack: Desktop framework: Electron 30 with contextBridge security model. Frontend: React 18 with TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, Zustand for scanner session state, React Query for localhost API calls. Local backend: Node.js 22 with Express bound to 127.0.0.1, better-sqlite3 with WAL mode. Central database: PostgreSQL 16 on Windows Server 2022 via pg library. Auth: ldapts for Active Directory LDAP bind. Barcode: node-hid for USB HID scanner input in main process. Observability: New Relic agent, Winston logging. Build: electron-builder for Windows NSIS installer. CI: GitHub Actions.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Barcode Scan Flow: USB HID scanner input captured in main process via node-hid; barcode string emitted to renderer via contextBridge IPC; barcode_validator compares scanned SKU against active pick_task.sku_id and updates task progress. Pick Task Routing: pick_router assigns warehouse_worker to open pick_tasks ordered by bin_id proximity to worker station; renders aisle-shelf-position navigation in Pick Task Detail view. Inventory Adjustment: inventory_adjuster writes delta to inventory_adjustments and updates warehouse_bins.quantity in SQLite; entry added to sync_log for central push. Offline Operation: app operates fully on local SQLite for up to 4 hours; sync_manager queues failed central pushes in sync_log with retry_count; sync status indicator shows pending sync count. Cycle Count Session: shift_manager initiates cycle_count_session for a bin; worker scans and enters counted_qty; discrepancy triggers inventory_adjustment. Shift Management: shift_manager views active worker_shifts, assigns workers to pick_tasks, reviews packing_slips.
target_output.system_components: Electron Main Process: Express server bound exclusively to 127.0.0.1:3847 serving /api routes. node-hid USB HID listener emitting scanner events via contextBridge. sync_manager: setInterval at 60 seconds flushing sync_log to PostgreSQL via pg; on failure increments retry_count with exponential backoff up to 5 retries. Renderer Process: React SPA with React Query fetching from localhost:3847. Zustand store with current_task_id and last_scanned_barcode. Offline indicator reading sync_log pending count. SQLite Database: WAL mode, tables: warehouse_bins, pick_tasks, packing_slips, barcode_scans, inventory_adjustments, cycle_count_sessions, worker_shifts, sync_log. Central PostgreSQL 16: system of record; receives sync_log entries from all workstations; last-writer-wins on adjusted_at for inventory_adjustments conflicts.
target_output.workflows: Incoming Shipment Scan: Worker scans shipment barcode. node-hid emits HID event to main process, contextBridge forwards to renderer. App validates SKU against expected shipment manifest, prompts for bin assignment. Worker confirms bin; inventory_adjuster writes +quantity to warehouse_bins in SQLite and adds sync_log entry. Sync_manager pushes to PostgreSQL within 60 seconds. Pick Task Execution: Shift manager creates pick_task from sales order in PostgreSQL. Sync_manager pulls new pick_tasks to local SQLite. Worker opens Pick Queue, selects task, app navigates to aisle-shelf-position. Worker scans item barcode; barcode_validator checks scanned_sku against pick_task.sku_id. On match, pick_task.status advances to picked; on mismatch, error modal with re-scan prompt. After all items scanned, packing_slip generated and sent to warehouse label printer via Electron shell.openPath.
target_output.data_model: warehouse_bins: bin_id text PK, aisle text, shelf text, position text, sku_id text FK, quantity int, last_counted_at datetime. pick_tasks: task_id text PK, sales_order_id text, sku_id text, required_qty int, picked_qty int default 0, bin_id text FK, assigned_worker_id text, status text enum open/in_progress/picked/packed/cancelled, created_at datetime, completed_at datetime nullable. packing_slips: slip_id text PK, sales_order_id text, worker_id text, printed_at datetime, items_json text. barcode_scans: scan_id text PK, task_id text FK, scanned_sku text, expected_sku text, match int (0 or 1), scanned_at datetime. inventory_adjustments: adj_id text PK, bin_id text FK, delta_qty int, reason text, worker_id text, adjusted_at datetime, synced_at datetime nullable. cycle_count_sessions: session_id text PK, bin_id text FK, counted_qty int, counter_id text, session_date date. worker_shifts: shift_id text PK, worker_id text, start_time datetime, end_time datetime nullable, station_id text. sync_log: log_id text PK, table_name text, record_id text, operation text enum insert/update, payload_json text, synced_at datetime nullable, retry_count int default 0.
target_output.api_design: Internal localhost REST API at 127.0.0.1:3847/api. GET /api/pick-tasks?worker_id={id}&status=open returns pick_tasks assigned to worker. POST /api/scans {task_id, scanned_sku} validates scan and updates pick_task. GET /api/bins/{bin_id} returns warehouse_bin with current quantity. POST /api/inventory/adjust {bin_id, delta_qty, reason} writes to inventory_adjustments and sync_log. GET /api/sync-status returns {pending_count, last_synced_at, retry_errors}. POST /api/login {username, password} triggers AD LDAP bind and returns JWT. All endpoints except /api/login require Authorization: Bearer {JWT} header validated by Express middleware.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Express server bound to 127.0.0.1:3847 only; Windows Firewall rule blocks inbound on port 3847 except loopback, documented in deployment notes. AD LDAP authentication via ldapts: service account credentials stored in OS credential store via Electron keytar. Worker JWT generated on successful AD bind, stored in Electron process memory only, never written to SQLite or localStorage. JWT secret generated fresh at app startup; tokens expire after 8-hour shift duration. Electron security: contextBridge exposes only defined IPC channels to renderer; nodeIntegration disabled in renderer; webSecurity enabled. Session termination on workstation lock via Electron powerMonitor.on('lock-screen') event clears JWT from memory.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: electron-builder packages as Windows NSIS installer (.exe). GitHub Actions CI builds installer on Windows runner, runs Jest unit tests, ESLint, TypeScript compiler check, uploads installer artifact. IT team distributes installer via Microsoft SCCM on 2-week cadence. SQLite database stored in %APPDATA%/warehouse-app/warehouse.db with automated daily backup to NAS via scheduled robocopy task. Central PostgreSQL 16 on Windows Server 2022 with daily pg_dump to NAS. SQLite schema migrations run via better-sqlite3 migration scripts on app startup; migrations must be backward-compatible to support workstations running the previous installer version during phased rollout. New Relic agent configured in main process; crash reports and API latency dashboards available in New Relic dashboard for IT ops team.
target_output.observability: New Relic agent in Electron main process captures crashes, Express route latency, and sync_manager duration. Winston logger writes structured JSON to AppData/warehouse-app/logs/app.log with 30-day rotation via winston-daily-rotate-file. Log levels: DEBUG for scan events and sync operations, INFO for task status changes, WARN for sync retries, ERROR for sync exhaustion and AD bind failures. Sync failure email alert: nodemailer sends to IT distribution list when sync_log retry_count exceeds 5 for any record. New Relic custom events track scan_to_screen_latency metric target under 100ms.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: No cloud costs; all infrastructure on-premise. Central PostgreSQL on existing Windows Server 2022 hardware. Electron app installed on existing workstation fleet. New Relic Essentials tier approximately 25 dollars per month per workstation host. SQLite local cache grows approximately 50MB per workstation per month; manageable on standard warehouse workstation storage. Scaling to additional warehouses requires provisioning additional PostgreSQL instances per site; sync_manager target_host is configurable per installation.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): AD login, pick task queue, barcode scan validation, bin-level inventory display, central PostgreSQL sync with sync_log, GitHub Actions CI. Phase 2 Operations (4 weeks): packing slip generation and print, cycle_count_sessions, shift manager dashboard, offline sync indicator, New Relic observability integration. Phase 3 Extensions (4 weeks): ERP system integration for automatic sales order import, cycle count scheduling, Android barcode handset support investigation.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode in renderer and main process. ESLint with @typescript-eslint recommended rules. Jest for unit tests on Express routes and sync_manager logic; 75% coverage gate. Electron security checklist enforced in PR review: no nodeIntegration, contextBridge required, no remote module. Express bound to 127.0.0.1 verified in integration test. SQLite migrations must be idempotent and backward-compatible.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: network outage exceeding 4-hour offline window causes sync_log to grow beyond memory; mitigation: sync_log max size alert at 10000 pending entries triggers shift manager notification. Risk: AD service account expiry locking all workstations out simultaneously; mitigation: monitor service account expiry in New Relic synthetic check. Tradeoff: SQLite local cache versus direct PostgreSQL connection; SQLite chosen for offline resilience at cost of eventual consistency; acceptable given warehouse operation tolerates 60-second sync lag. Tradeoff: Electron bundle size approximately 180MB versus lighter native Windows app; Electron chosen for React team skill reuse and faster development.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Conflict resolution confirmed as last-writer-wins by adjusted_at timestamp for inventory_adjustments. Express bind address confirmed as 127.0.0.1 only. Sync interval confirmed as 60 seconds. Android handset support deferred to Phase 3. ERP integration deferred to Phase 3.

metadata.notes: Warehouse desktop app round 2; good core architecture with resolved Express bind issue; observability and cost sections slightly thin.

profile.domain: Furniture Retailer Stock Availability Status Page

project_goal.value: Public-facing stock availability status page displaying live in-store and online inventory counts for featured furniture product lines, with an embeddable real-time availability widget for partner dealers
project_goal.rationale: Retailer wants to reduce customer service calls about stock by making availability self-service and embeddable for dealer websites

target_users.value: Retail customers checking furniture availability online, and dealer partner websites embedding the availability widget
target_users.rationale: Dual audience: direct consumers and B2B dealer partners who embed the widget on their own sites

project_class.rationale: Pure static site with client-side API polling; no server-side runtime needed at hosting layer

capabilities.rationale: Static frontend with client-side availability polling and Vercel Analytics for page metrics

complexity_level.rationale: Static site with periodic client-side fetch; no auth, no database, no server runtime

risk_level.rationale: Read-only public data; no transactions, no user data, no authentication

data_sensitivity.rationale: All displayed data is publicly available inventory information; no PII or sensitive data

external_exposure.rationale: Site is publicly accessible and embeddable; no authentication required

access_model.value: No authentication required; public read-only; dealer embed via script tag with SKU scope parameter
access_model.rationale: Public availability data needs no access control

feature_scope.value: Product listing pages with real-time availability badges, in-store vs online stock differentiation, embeddable iframe widget scoped to dealer SKU list, 60-second polling refresh, store location filter
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set for launch

mvp_scope.value: Static product listings with availability badges polling every 60 seconds, embeddable script tag widget
mvp_scope.rationale: Store location filter and store-level breakdown come in v2

security_baseline.value: CSP headers via Vercel headers config; no inline scripts; SRI for third-party assets; no user input accepted
security_baseline.rationale: Static site minimal attack surface; CSP headers prevent XSS via injected content

frontend_stack.value: Astro 4 with vanilla JavaScript for availability polling; no client-side framework
frontend_stack.rationale: Astro generates minimal HTML with zero JS framework overhead; vanilla JS is sufficient for poll-and-update pattern

backend_stack.value: No server-side runtime at hosting layer; site consumes a public read-only inventory API provided by the retailer backend team
backend_stack.rationale: Static hosting on Vercel; all data comes from existing retailer inventory API

data_platform.value: No database; site reads from read-only public inventory API at api.retailer.com/v1/availability
data_platform.rationale: Static site has no persistence layer

hosting_target.value: Vercel Edge Network with automatic CDN; preview deployments per PR
hosting_target.rationale: Vercel provides global CDN, preview URLs, and built-in analytics for static sites

privacy_retention_policy.value: No user data collected or retained; Vercel Analytics collects anonymous page view metrics only
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: No personal data; GDPR impact is minimal with anonymous analytics only

future_scope.value: Store-level availability breakdown with map view, product comparison widget, email availability alerts via API integration
future_scope.rationale: v2 features post-launch based on customer usage patterns

constraints.value: No server-side runtime at Vercel; inventory API at api.retailer.com is read-only with CORS enabled for the site domain; widget must load in under 2 seconds
constraints.rationale: Vercel static hosting has no server functions in scope; existing API team provides CORS headers

observability_baseline.value: Vercel Analytics for page views and Core Web Vitals; no server-side monitoring needed
observability_baseline.rationale: Static site needs only client-side analytics; no server infrastructure to monitor

execution_preference.value: Continuous deployment on every main branch push; PR preview deployments; GitHub Actions for build validation
execution_preference.rationale: Fast feedback loop with Vercel preview URLs for stakeholder review

project.goal: Public furniture stock availability page with embeddable dealer widget
project.target_users: Retail customers and dealer partner websites

frontend.styling: CSS custom properties; no Tailwind framework dependency
frontend.state: No state management framework; vanilla JS DOM updates

security.headers: CSP, X-Frame-Options ALLOWALL for widget iframe, SRI on third-party scripts

data.source: Public read-only inventory API at api.retailer.com/v1/availability

devops.hosting: Vercel Edge Network with continuous deployment

constraints.cors: Inventory API CORS enabled for site domain

reasoner_reviews.product: The scope is tight and appropriate for a static site. The availability badge pattern with 60-second polling is the right UX for furniture inventory which moves slowly. The embeddable script tag widget is a well-scoped feature; dealer partners just need to include one script tag and pass their SKU list as a data attribute.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Astro with vanilla JS is the correct choice here: zero client-side JavaScript overhead for the page structure, only the availability polling script is loaded. The widget embed should be an iframe served from the same Astro build rather than a shared script that runs in dealer DOM context, to avoid CSS and JS conflicts on partner sites.
reasoner_reviews.security: CSP headers in vercel.json prevent injected scripts. Since the widget is embedded via iframe the CSP only needs to allow framing from dealer domains listed in a frame-ancestors policy, not a wildcard. The inventory API endpoint should be proxied through a Vercel Rewrite to avoid exposing the API base URL in client-side code.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Widget load under 2 seconds is achievable: Astro generates minimal HTML, static assets are CDN-cached globally, and the inventory API response is small JSON. Total initial JS weight should be under 15KB uncompressed.

frontend.app_structure: Astro 4 project with src/pages/index.astro (main availability page), src/pages/widget.astro (iframe widget page served to dealer embeds), src/components/AvailabilityBadge.astro, src/components/ProductCard.astro, src/components/StoreFilter.astro. Static assets in public/. Vanilla JS availability poller in src/scripts/availability-poller.js.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Main page: renders ProductCard grid for featured furniture lines with AvailabilityBadge per product. Badge shows in_stock, low_stock, or out_of_stock from API response. Page loads static HTML from Astro build; availability-poller.js fetches /api/availability every 60 seconds and updates badge DOM nodes via data-sku attributes. Widget page: iframe-embeddable page filtered by sku list query param; same polling logic scoped to partner SKUs.
frontend.performance_notes: Astro partial hydration: zero client JS for structural components. availability-poller.js is 4KB minified. No external font imports. Images served from Vercel Image Optimization. Core Web Vitals target: LCP under 1.2s on CDN-cached pages.
frontend.frontend_risks: Inventory API rate limiting could affect rapid badge refreshes on high-traffic pages; implement client-side debounce so rapid visibility-change events do not trigger extra fetches.

security.auth_design: No authentication required. API calls are unauthenticated reads to public endpoint.
security.abuse_prevention: Rate limiting on client-side polling: setInterval at 60 seconds minimum; tab visibility API pauses polling when page is backgrounded to reduce API call volume.
security.privacy_controls: No cookies set. Vercel Analytics uses anonymous fingerprinting with no PII. Cookie consent banner not required under ePrivacy directive for analytics-only cookies that are anonymous.
security.security_risks: XSS via injected content in product descriptions rendered from API response; mitigation: use textContent not innerHTML when updating DOM from API data.

data.entities: product_listings (sku_id, name, image_url, category, featured bool), availability_badges (sku_id, in_store_qty, online_qty, status enum in_stock/low_stock/out_of_stock, updated_at), store_locations (store_id, store_name, city, region), partner_embed_tokens (token_id, dealer_name, allowed_skus list)
data.storage_design: No database on this site. Availability data fetched from api.retailer.com/v1/availability?skus={comma-delimited} via client-side fetch. Product listing metadata (name, image URLs) baked into Astro pages at build time from a static JSON catalog file in src/data/products.json.
data.retention_and_deletion: No data retained by the static site. Vercel Analytics retains anonymous page view data for 90 days.

devops.deployment_topology: Astro build outputs to dist/. Vercel reads dist/ and serves via Edge Network across all regions. No functions, no server runtime. vercel.json configures CSP headers and rewrites for /api/availability to proxy retailer API.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: astro check (TypeScript in Astro), ESLint, astro build, Lighthouse CI run against preview URL, merge to main triggers Vercel production deployment. PR opens Vercel preview deployment automatically via Vercel GitHub integration.
devops.observability_stack: Vercel Analytics dashboard for page views, Web Vitals by URL, and top referrers. Vercel Speed Insights for LCP and CLS tracking. No server-side monitoring needed.
devops.devops_risks: Inventory API downtime shows stale availability badges; implement last-known-good cache in sessionStorage so stale data is shown with a 'as of' timestamp rather than a blank badge.

target_output.thinking_summary: Static Astro site serving public furniture availability data. Key decisions: iframe-based widget embed to avoid CSS/JS conflicts on dealer sites; Vercel Rewrite to proxy inventory API and hide base URL from client code; vanilla JS with textContent DOM updates to prevent XSS from API data; CSP headers blocking inline scripts; sessionStorage for last-known-good badge state during API outages.
target_output.title: Furniture Retailer Stock Availability Status Page
target_output.executive_summary: A public static website built with Astro 4 and vanilla JavaScript, deployed to Vercel Edge Network, displaying real-time in-store and online stock availability for featured furniture product lines. Client-side polling fetches from a proxied inventory API every 60 seconds and updates availability badges without page reload. Dealer partners embed a scoped iframe widget by including a single script tag referencing their allowed SKU list. Vercel Analytics provides anonymous page view and Core Web Vitals tracking.
target_output.architecture_overview: Astro 4 static site generating zero-JavaScript HTML pages for furniture product listings and a separate widget.astro page for dealer iframe embeds. At build time, product metadata is read from src/data/products.json and baked into static HTML. In the browser, availability-poller.js fetches from /api/availability (a Vercel Rewrite proxying api.retailer.com/v1/availability) every 60 seconds and updates AvailabilityBadge DOM nodes by matching data-sku attributes. The widget page accepts a skus query parameter and scopes polling to the dealer partner SKU list. No server-side runtime at Vercel; all page structure is pre-rendered. CSP headers configured in vercel.json block inline scripts and restrict frame-ancestors to the retailer domain and registered dealer origins.
target_output.technology_stack: Static site generator: Astro 4 with TypeScript for component types. Client-side scripting: vanilla JavaScript (availability-poller.js, approximately 4KB minified). Hosting: Vercel Edge Network with global CDN and automatic HTTPS. Deployment: Vercel GitHub integration with preview deployments per PR. CI: GitHub Actions with astro check, ESLint, astro build, and Lighthouse CI against preview URL. Analytics: Vercel Analytics and Speed Insights (anonymous, no cookies). Build tool: Astro built-in Vite bundler. No npm framework dependencies in runtime bundle.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Product Availability Page: static product grid for featured furniture lines; each ProductCard includes an AvailabilityBadge showing in_stock, low_stock, or out_of_stock status fetched from inventory API. Badge updates without page reload via 60-second polling. Availability Polling: availability-poller.js collects all data-sku attributes on page load, batches into a single /api/availability?skus= fetch, parses JSON response, and updates badge text and CSS class via textContent. Backgrounded Tab Optimization: document.addEventListener('visibilitychange') pauses polling when page is hidden, resumes on visible to reduce API traffic. Dealer Widget Embed: dealer adds script tag to their site that renders an iframe pointing to widget.astro?skus=SKU1,SKU2 scoped to their product list. Widget Resilience: sessionStorage caches last successful API response; on API failure, stale badges shown with 'Updated at HH:MM' timestamp.
target_output.system_components: src/pages/index.astro: main availability page rendering ProductCard grid from products.json at build time. src/pages/widget.astro: iframe-embeddable page accepting skus query param, renders filtered ProductCard set, includes same availability-poller.js with param-scoped SKU list. src/components/AvailabilityBadge.astro: renders badge div with data-sku and data-status attributes. src/components/ProductCard.astro: product image, name, category, and AvailabilityBadge. src/scripts/availability-poller.js: vanilla JS module collecting data-sku nodes, batching fetch to /api/availability, updating DOM via textContent, managing 60-second interval and visibility pause. src/data/products.json: static catalog with sku_id, name, category, image_url for all featured products. vercel.json: CSP and security headers, /api/availability rewrite to api.retailer.com/v1/availability.
target_output.workflows: Page Load Availability Display: Browser loads pre-rendered Astro HTML from Vercel CDN. availability-poller.js executes, collects all data-sku values, sends GET /api/availability?skus=SKU001,SKU002,...,SKU024. Vercel Rewrite proxies to api.retailer.com/v1/availability with same query string. API returns JSON array of sku availability objects. Poller updates each badge DOM node: badge.textContent = status label, badge.className = availability-badge plus status enum. setInterval repeats every 60 seconds. Dealer Widget Embed: Dealer includes a script tag. Script creates an iframe pointing to status.retailer.com/widget?skus=SKU005,SKU011. iframe loads widget.astro which reads skus param, renders only those ProductCards, runs same availability-poller.js scoped to the two SKUs. Widget fits in a 320x200px embed box with responsive CSS.
target_output.data_model: product_listings (static, baked at build time): sku_id str, name str, image_url str, category str enum sofa/bed/table/chair/storage, featured bool. Sourced from src/data/products.json; updated by re-running astro build when catalog changes. availability_badges (runtime, from API): sku_id str, in_store_qty int, online_qty int, status str enum in_stock/low_stock/out_of_stock, updated_at ISO8601. store_locations (future v2): store_id str, store_name str, city str, region str. partner_embed_tokens (future v2 for token-scoped widgets): token_id str, dealer_name str, allowed_skus list of str. Current MVP uses open query param for SKU scoping with no token validation.
target_output.api_design: No server-side API routes on this static site. Client-side fetch targets the Vercel Rewrite: GET /api/availability?skus={comma-delimited-skus} proxied to api.retailer.com/v1/availability?skus={same}. Response shape: [{sku_id: 'SKU001', in_store_qty: 14, online_qty: 3, status: 'in_stock', updated_at: '2026-03-24T10:00:00Z'}, ...]. No pagination needed for up to 50 SKUs per batch. Error handling: on non-200 response, poller reads sessionStorage for last successful response and renders with stale timestamp.
target_output.security_and_compliance: vercel.json headers configuration: Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self'; img-src 'self' cdn.retailer.com; frame-ancestors 'self' *.authorizeddealer.com. X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff. Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin. DOM update via textContent only, never innerHTML, to prevent XSS from API response data. Vercel Rewrite hides retailer API base URL from client code. No user inputs accepted on the page. Anonymous analytics only; no GDPR consent banner required. Subresource Integrity applied to any third-party analytics script loaded from CDN.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Continuous deployment via Vercel GitHub integration. Every push to main triggers Vercel production build and global CDN deployment completing in under 60 seconds. Every PR triggers a preview deployment with unique URL for stakeholder review. GitHub Actions pipeline: astro check for TypeScript errors in Astro components, ESLint for availability-poller.js, astro build to verify no build errors, Lighthouse CI run against Vercel preview URL asserting Performance score above 95 and LCP under 1.5 seconds. vercel.json checked into repository alongside source. No database migrations, no server rollbacks; a bad deployment is reverted by re-deploying the previous commit via Vercel dashboard.
target_output.observability: Vercel Analytics: page views, unique visitors, and top referrers per day. Vercel Speed Insights: LCP, CLS, and FID per page with P75 targets. No server-side infrastructure to monitor. Availability API error rate observable via browser DevTools Network tab for debugging; no server-side error tracking needed. If API error rate becomes an operational concern, a lightweight Vercel Edge Function could log API response codes to a logging endpoint, but this is out of scope for MVP.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Vercel Hobby plan is free for this traffic profile. Pro plan at 20 dollars per month supports commercial use with SLA. Vercel Analytics included in Pro. No database, no compute costs. Inventory API calls are made by client browsers directly through Vercel Rewrite; API rate limits at api.retailer.com are shared across all visitor sessions. At 10000 daily page views with 60-second polling per session of 5 minutes average, approximately 50000 API calls per day; well within standard API tiers.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (3 weeks): Astro project setup, static product listings from products.json, availability-poller.js with 60-second interval and sessionStorage fallback, dealer iframe widget page, vercel.json with CSP headers and API rewrite, GitHub Actions CI, Lighthouse CI gate, Vercel production deployment. Phase 2 v2 Features (3 weeks): store location filter with store_id query param, per-store availability breakdown in badge tooltip, partner embed token validation for private dealer SKU lists, Vercel Analytics custom events for badge interaction tracking.
target_output.development_guidelines: Astro TypeScript mode enabled; component props typed via interface definitions. ESLint with astro/recommended and standard JS rules. availability-poller.js uses ES modules; no bundler polyfills needed for modern browser targets. DOM updates exclusively via textContent; innerHTML banned via ESLint no-unsanitized rule. Lighthouse CI score gate of 95 enforced on every PR. products.json schema validated via Zod at Astro build time to catch catalog data errors before deployment.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: inventory API CORS policy change breaking client-side fetches; mitigation: Vercel Rewrite proxies all calls through Vercel so retailer controls origin while CORS is always satisfied. Risk: dealer embedding widget on sites with Content-Security-Policy blocking iframes from retailer domain; mitigation: document frame-ancestors requirement in dealer integration guide. Tradeoff: client-side polling versus server-sent events; polling chosen for simplicity given 60-second update frequency; SSE would add server runtime complexity inconsistent with static site constraint. Tradeoff: iframe embed versus script-injected widget; iframe chosen to isolate CSS and JS from dealer page context at the cost of fixed sizing requirements.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Widget embed mechanism confirmed as iframe, not injected script, to avoid dealer site CSS conflicts. Inventory API proxy confirmed via Vercel Rewrite to hide API base URL. Store-level breakdown deferred to v2. Partner embed token validation deferred to v2. Confirmed Astro over Next.js for zero-JS overhead on static pages.

metadata.notes: Furniture retailer static availability page; strong domain-specific content across all sections; iframe widget and Vercel Rewrite proxy well-reasoned.

project_goal.value: macOS desktop application streaming live sell-through rates, markdown alerts, and reorder signals to fashion retail buyers across hundreds of SKUs
project_goal.rationale: Replaces static spreadsheet-based buying reports with real-time decision support

target_users.value: Retail buyers and merchandising managers at a fashion apparel company
target_users.rationale: Primary end users identified in discovery workshops

project_class.rationale: Tauri-based native macOS app with local sidecar and remote WebSocket server

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, realtime, analytics
capabilities.rationale: Live dashboard requires realtime streaming; analytics drives alert logic; auth gates confidential buying data

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-SKU streaming with alert logic and drill-down views is non-trivial but scoped to internal users

risk_level.rationale: Confidential financial performance data; internal-only but must be access-controlled

data_sensitivity.rationale: Sell-through and markdown data constitutes commercially sensitive buying intelligence

external_exposure.rationale: App is deployed exclusively to buyer workstations; no public endpoints

access_model.value: Okta SSO with role-based access; buyer and manager roles with separate alert subscription permissions
access_model.rationale: Prevents unauthorized access to competitor pricing intelligence within the organization

feature_scope.value: Live sell-through dashboard, per-SKU markdown alert subscriptions, reorder signal feed, store-level drill-down, watchlist management, alert history log
feature_scope.rationale: Core feature set approved by buying team lead

mvp_scope.value: Live sell-through dashboard with markdown alerts and watchlist for top 200 SKUs; store drill-down deferred to phase 2
mvp_scope.rationale: Phase 1 scoped to highest-velocity SKUs for Q2 buying cycle

security_baseline.value: Okta OIDC with token refresh, TLS 1.3 for all WebSocket and REST connections, local config encrypted with macOS Keychain
security_baseline.rationale: Protects confidential merchandising data in transit and at rest on buyer laptops

frontend_stack.rationale: Tauri provides native macOS packaging with Rust sidecar; Vue 3 for reactive dashboard components

backend_stack.value: Rust with Axum (local sidecar) + remote WebSocket server on GKE Autopilot
backend_stack.rationale: Rust sidecar handles OS-level events; Axum WebSocket server streams analytics from ClickHouse

data_platform.value: ClickHouse for analytics queries with Redis 7 for alert state and session caching
data_platform.rationale: ClickHouse sub-second aggregation over millions of sell-through rows; Redis for fast alert threshold evaluation

hosting_target.value: GKE Autopilot (WebSocket server and analytics API); macOS app distributed via internal MDM
hosting_target.rationale: GKE Autopilot reduces node management; MDM ensures controlled rollout to buyer workstations

privacy_retention_policy.value: Sell-through data retained 3 years for trend analysis; alert logs purged after 90 days
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Buying cycle analysis requires multi-season history; alert logs are operational only

future_scope.value: AI-driven markdown recommendation engine; mobile companion app for buyers on buying trips
future_scope.rationale: Year 2 roadmap items pending Q1 ROI review

constraints.value: macOS 13+ only; WebSocket server budget under $800/month on GCP; app binary under 50MB
constraints.rationale: Buyer fleet standardized on macOS 13; cost constraint from IT budget allocation

observability_baseline.value: Grafana dashboards with Prometheus metrics on GKE; Tauri crash reports via Sentry; WebSocket connection health tracked per client
observability_baseline.rationale: GKE Prometheus integration standard; Sentry for desktop crash visibility

execution_preference.value: 2-week sprints; app releases via MDM every sprint end
execution_preference.rationale: Aligns with buying team planning cadence

compliance_context.value: SOC 2 Type II controls for confidential financial performance data; access logs required for data access audits
compliance_context.rationale: Internal compliance policy treats sell-through data as confidential financial intelligence

project.goal: Stream live SKU sell-through rates and markdown alerts to buyer macOS desktops
project.target_users: Fashion retail buyers and merchandising managers

frontend.state: Pinia for buyer watchlists and alert subscriptions
frontend.approach: Reactive dashboard with WebSocket-driven data binding

backend.api: WebSocket streams + REST for watchlist CRUD

security.secrets: macOS Keychain for local credential storage

data.cache: Redis 7 for alert state and session tokens

devops.hosting: GKE Autopilot + MDM for macOS distribution

reasoner_reviews.product: The real-time sell-through dashboard directly addresses the buying team's need to act on markdown signals during the selling season. The watchlist model for top-200 SKUs is the right MVP scope -- buyers care most about high-velocity styles where days-of-supply decisions are time-sensitive. Alert subscription at SKU level is the correct granularity.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Tauri + Rust sidecar is a good choice for macOS-native performance with a small binary footprint. The ClickHouse analytics backend can serve sub-second sell-through aggregations across store dimensions. The WebSocket streaming model avoids polling overhead. Alert threshold evaluation in Redis keeps latency predictable and decoupled from ClickHouse query load.
reasoner_reviews.security: Okta OIDC is appropriate for internal SSO. The macOS Keychain for token storage prevents credential exposure in plaintext config files. TLS 1.3 for WebSocket connections is mandatory given confidential buying data. Access audit logging must capture which buyer viewed which SKU drill-down for SOC 2 evidence.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: GCP budget of $800/month is tight for GKE Autopilot; recommend ClickHouse on a single e2-standard-8 node with Autopilot for the WebSocket tier to stay within budget. MDM distribution avoids the App Store review cycle. Binary size constraint of 50MB is achievable with Tauri.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 1 plan lacked specificity on alert threshold persistence -- it was unclear whether thresholds were stored per-buyer in ClickHouse or Redis. The WebSocket reconnection logic was not described. These gaps need addressing in round 2.

frontend.app_structure: Tauri 2 app with Vue 3 SPA inside webview; Rust sidecar handles Okta token refresh and Keychain I/O; main process manages WebSocket lifecycle
frontend.pages_and_flows: Dashboard (live SKU grid with sell-through sparklines), SKU Detail (store-level drill-down), Watchlist Editor (add/remove SKUs with threshold config), Alert History (paginated log), Settings (Okta re-auth, notification preferences)
frontend.state_management: Pinia stores: watchlistStore (buyer SKU subscriptions and thresholds), alertStore (in-memory alert ring buffer), sessionStore (Okta token and buyer profile)
frontend.performance_notes: Vue virtual scroll for SKU grid (500+ SKUs); ClickHouse queries pre-aggregated to 5-min buckets server-side; WebSocket delta updates only changed SKU rows to minimize DOM churn

backend.service_design: Two tiers: Rust Axum WebSocket server on GKE (streams analytics deltas); Rust sidecar in Tauri app (token management, local IPC bridge between webview and OS Keychain)
backend.api_patterns: WebSocket subscription protocol: client sends JSON subscribe message with SKU list and threshold config; server streams delta events with sell_through_rate, markdown_flag, reorder_signal fields
backend.scaling_notes: GKE Autopilot scales WebSocket pods on CPU 60% target; Redis Pub/Sub fan-out to all subscribed buyer sessions for the same SKU group; ClickHouse handles up to 10M sell-through rows per query under 500ms

security.auth_design: Okta OIDC authorization code flow with PKCE; access token stored in macOS Keychain via Tauri Rust sidecar; 1-hour token expiry with silent refresh
security.authorization_model: Buyer role: read-only access to all SKUs in assigned category hierarchy. Manager role: read access plus cross-category visibility and markdown approval actions. Scopes enforced on WebSocket server via JWT claims.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Every SKU drill-down view emitted as audit_event to PostgreSQL audit table with buyer_id, sku_id, store_filter, and timestamp; retained 90 days for SOC 2 access review evidence

data.entities: sku_performance_metrics (sku_id, store_id, sell_through_rate, units_sold, units_on_hand, week_ending -- ClickHouse MergeTree partitioned by week_ending), markdown_events (sku_id, store_id, old_price, new_price, effective_date -- ClickHouse), reorder_signals (sku_id, store_id, days_of_supply, reorder_qty, generated_at -- ClickHouse), buyer_watchlists (buyer_id, sku_id, sell_through_threshold, reorder_threshold_days -- PostgreSQL), alert_subscriptions (buyer_id, sku_id, alert_type ENUM markdown/reorder/low_stock, active -- PostgreSQL)
data.storage_design: ClickHouse MergeTree for immutable analytics events; PostgreSQL 16 for buyer configuration (watchlists, thresholds, alert subscriptions); Redis sorted sets for real-time alert state evaluation
data.retention_and_deletion: ClickHouse sell-through data TTL 3 years; PostgreSQL alert_history purged after 90 days via pg_cron; buyer_watchlists retained until account deletion

devops.deployment_topology: GKE Autopilot cluster in us-central1 running WebSocket server pods and REST API pods behind GCP Internal Load Balancer; ClickHouse on dedicated e2-standard-8 node; PostgreSQL on Cloud SQL; Redis on Memorystore
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI: clippy lint + Rust unit tests + Vue tsc on every MR; Tauri universal binary build on macOS runner on tag; distribute via Jamf MDM API on release tag
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scrapes WebSocket pod metrics (active_connections, message_rate, alert_fanout_latency); Grafana dashboards; Sentry for Tauri crash reports and Rust sidecar panics
