previous_plan.title: Fashion Buyer Real-Time Analytics Desktop App
previous_plan.executive_summary: Tauri 2 macOS application streaming SKU sell-through rates and markdown alerts from a ClickHouse analytics backend via WebSocket. Buyers manage watchlists and receive real-time alerts when configured thresholds are breached.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Tauri 2 app with Vue 3 webview communicates with a Rust Axum WebSocket server on GKE Autopilot. ClickHouse serves sell-through aggregations. Redis holds session state. Okta provides SSO.
previous_plan.data_model: sku_performance_metrics, markdown_events, reorder_signals in ClickHouse. buyer_watchlists in PostgreSQL. Alert threshold storage not specified.
previous_plan.workflows: Buyer subscribes to SKU set; WebSocket streams delta updates. Markdown alert fires when threshold breached. Reconnection logic not described.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 addresses two blocking issues: (1) alert threshold persistence -- thresholds belong in PostgreSQL as buyer configuration, with a Redis Pub/Sub propagation path to update active WebSocket subscriptions without dropping connections; (2) WebSocket reconnection -- exponential backoff in the Tauri Rust sidecar with automatic re-subscription on reconnect. ClickHouse remains the analytics store; PostgreSQL owns all mutable buyer configuration.

fix_report.action_taken: Added alert_subscriptions entity to data_model with PostgreSQL as the authoritative store. Described propagation path: buyer updates threshold via REST PATCH /watchlists/:id; API writes to PostgreSQL and publishes to Redis channel threshold_updates; WebSocket server subscribes to Redis channel and pushes updated threshold config to the buyer's active connection without disconnect.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Auditor can trace full lifecycle from buyer threshold change to active subscription update; ISS-001 resolved.

fix_report.action_taken: Added WebSocket reconnection strategy to deployment_and_operations and workflows: Tauri Rust sidecar implements exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, max 30s) on disconnect. On reconnect, sidecar re-sends subscribe message with full watchlist payload so server reinstates all SKU subscriptions. GKE rolling updates drain connections with 30s graceful termination period.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Buyers do not silently lose alerts during GKE pod replacements; ISS-002 resolved.

target_output.title: Fashion Buyer Real-Time Sell-Through Analytics -- macOS Desktop
target_output.executive_summary: A Tauri 2 macOS desktop application for fashion retail buyers that streams live sell-through rates, markdown alerts, and reorder signals from a ClickHouse analytics backend via authenticated WebSocket connections. Buyers configure per-SKU alert thresholds stored in PostgreSQL and manage watchlists of up to 500 SKUs. The app provides store-level drill-down views and an alert history log. Deployed to buyer workstations via Jamf MDM with Okta SSO authentication and SOC 2 Type II audit logging.
target_output.architecture_overview: Two-tier architecture: a Tauri 2 macOS app (Vue 3 webview + Rust sidecar) connecting over TLS 1.3 WebSocket to a Rust Axum WebSocket server cluster on GKE Autopilot in us-central1. The WebSocket server queries ClickHouse for sell-through aggregations and evaluates alert thresholds against Redis state. Buyer configuration (watchlists, alert subscriptions, thresholds) lives in Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16. Threshold changes propagate from REST API to active WebSocket subscriptions via Redis Pub/Sub without dropping buyer connections. The Rust Tauri sidecar manages Okta OIDC token refresh via macOS Keychain and implements exponential backoff reconnection with automatic subscription re-registration after pod replacement events.
target_output.technology_stack: Desktop: Tauri 2.0, Vue 3 with TypeScript, Pinia, Vite. Sidecar: Rust with tokio and keyring crate for macOS Keychain. WebSocket server: Rust with Axum, tokio-tungstenite on GKE Autopilot. Analytics DB: ClickHouse 24 (MergeTree tables, e2-standard-8 node). Config DB: Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16. Cache and Pub/Sub: Redis 7 on Memorystore. Auth: Okta OIDC with PKCE. CI/CD: GitLab CI with Tauri universal binary builds. MDM: Jamf Pro. Observability: Prometheus, Grafana, Sentry.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Live SKU Dashboard: Vue virtual scroll grid showing sell_through_rate, units_on_hand, markdown_flag, and reorder_signal for up to 500 watched SKUs updated via WebSocket deltas every 30 seconds. Watchlist Manager: buyers add/remove SKUs and configure markdown threshold (e.g., sell-through below 40% at day 14) and reorder threshold (days_of_supply below 7). SKU Drill-Down (Phase 2): store-level sell-through breakdown with hourly bars from ClickHouse. Alert History: paginated log of fired markdown_events and reorder_signals with acknowledged flag. Markdown Alert Push: desktop notification via Tauri API when threshold breached, with one-click drill-down link.
target_output.system_components: Tauri 2 app shell (macOS native window, notification API, Keychain bridge), Vue 3 webview (dashboard, watchlist, alert pages), Rust sidecar (Okta token refresh, reconnect loop, IPC relay to webview), GKE Autopilot WebSocket server pods (Axum, subscription registry, Redis Pub/Sub listener), ClickHouse node (sell-through MergeTree tables with 5-min materialized views), Cloud SQL PostgreSQL (buyer_watchlists, alert_subscriptions, audit_events), Redis Memorystore (alert state sorted sets, threshold_updates Pub/Sub channel), Jamf MDM (app distribution and update enforcement).
target_output.workflows: Threshold Update Propagation: Buyer adjusts sell-through alert threshold in Watchlist Manager; Vue calls REST PATCH /api/v1/watchlists/:id; Axum REST handler validates Okta JWT, writes new threshold to PostgreSQL alert_subscriptions row, publishes JSON event to Redis channel threshold_updates:buyer_id; WebSocket server process subscribes to that channel and pushes updated subscription config to the buyer's active WebSocket connection; next ClickHouse evaluation cycle uses the new threshold without connection drop. WebSocket Reconnection: Rust sidecar detects TCP close on WebSocket connection; initiates reconnect with exponential backoff starting at 1s doubling to max 30s; on successful reconnect sends subscribe message with full current watchlist from Pinia store snapshot; server reinstates all SKU subscriptions and resumes streaming deltas; GKE rolling updates send connection close frame 30s before pod termination to trigger orderly reconnect cycle.
target_output.data_model: buyer_watchlists (id UUID PK, buyer_id FK, sku_id VARCHAR, sell_through_threshold DECIMAL, reorder_threshold_days INT, created_at, updated_at -- PostgreSQL). alert_subscriptions (id UUID PK, buyer_id FK, sku_id, alert_type ENUM markdown/reorder/low_stock, active BOOL, last_fired_at -- PostgreSQL). alert_history (id UUID PK, buyer_id, sku_id, store_id, alert_type, fired_at, acknowledged_at -- PostgreSQL, purged after 90 days). audit_events (id UUID PK, buyer_id, action, sku_id, store_filter, ip_address, ts -- PostgreSQL, retained 90 days). sku_performance_metrics (sku_id, store_id, sell_through_rate Float64, units_sold UInt32, units_on_hand UInt32, week_ending Date -- ClickHouse MergeTree partitioned by week_ending, TTL 3 years). markdown_events (sku_id, store_id, old_price, new_price, effective_date, triggered_by -- ClickHouse). reorder_signals (sku_id, store_id, days_of_supply Float64, reorder_qty UInt32, generated_at DateTime -- ClickHouse).
target_output.api_design: WebSocket wss://analytics.internal/ws: client sends {type:'subscribe', buyer_id, skus:[{sku_id, sell_through_threshold, reorder_threshold_days}]}; server streams {type:'delta', sku_id, sell_through_rate, markdown_flag, reorder_signal, ts} or {type:'alert', sku_id, alert_type, value, ts}. REST: GET /api/v1/watchlists (buyer's watchlist), POST /api/v1/watchlists (add SKU), PATCH /api/v1/watchlists/:id (update threshold -- triggers Redis Pub/Sub propagation), DELETE /api/v1/watchlists/:id, GET /api/v1/alerts/history?page=1 (paginated), PATCH /api/v1/alerts/:id/acknowledge. All endpoints require Okta JWT Bearer token validated by Axum middleware.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Okta OIDC authorization code + PKCE; access tokens stored in macOS Keychain via Tauri keyring crate; 1-hour expiry with silent refresh. WebSocket and REST over TLS 1.3 only; certificate pinned in Tauri app bundle. JWT validated on every WebSocket subscribe message and REST request. RBAC: buyer role reads SKUs in assigned category; manager role has cross-category visibility. SOC 2 Type II: audit_events table captures buyer_id, action, sku_id, store_filter, ip_address, and timestamp for every drill-down and threshold change; exported monthly to GCS for compliance review. GKE workload identity for ClickHouse and Cloud SQL access; no static service account keys in container images.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GKE Autopilot in us-central1: WebSocket server deployment (2-5 pods, HPA targeting 200 active_connections per pod), REST API deployment (1-3 pods, HPA on CPU 60%). ClickHouse single e2-standard-8 node with persistent SSD disk. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 with daily automated backups and point-in-time recovery. Redis Memorystore 2GB standard tier. GitLab CI pipeline: clippy (Rust), vue-tsc (TypeScript), unit tests, integration tests against local ClickHouse Docker, Tauri universal binary build on macOS runner on tag, Jamf MDM API push on release tag. Rollback: Jamf reverts to previous version artifact; GKE rollback via deployment revision. Rolling updates: maxUnavailable=0, terminationGracePeriodSeconds=30 to allow sidecar reconnect before pod death.
target_output.observability: Prometheus scrapes GKE WebSocket pods: active_connections gauge, subscription_updates_total counter, alert_fanout_latency_seconds histogram, clickhouse_query_duration_seconds histogram. Grafana dashboard: active buyer sessions, alert fire rate by alert_type, WebSocket reconnection rate per hour, ClickHouse p99 query latency. Sentry: Tauri crash reports and Rust sidecar panics enriched with buyer_id context tag. PagerDuty alerts on: reconnection_rate > 5/min (pod instability indicator), clickhouse_query_duration p99 > 2s, active_connections drop > 20% in 5 minutes.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: GKE Autopilot WebSocket pods at steady state (2 pods): ~$180/month. ClickHouse e2-standard-8: ~$380/month. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL db-g1-small: ~$50/month. Redis Memorystore 2GB: ~$60/month. Load balancer and egress: ~$40/month. Total ~$710/month, within $800 budget. Scaling path: add ClickHouse read replica when concurrent buyer sessions exceed 100; shard Redis by buyer_id prefix when buyer count exceeds 500.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): Okta auth flow, watchlist CRUD for top 200 SKUs, live sell-through WebSocket stream, markdown alert desktop notifications, alert history, GitLab CI pipeline, Jamf MDM distribution. Round 2 fixes (alert threshold propagation via Redis Pub/Sub and WebSocket reconnection with exponential backoff) are baseline requirements included in Phase 1. Phase 2 (4 weeks): Store-level drill-down view with hourly ClickHouse bars, reorder signal feed, cross-category manager view. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Alert suppression rules, bulk watchlist CSV import, SOC 2 audit report export from audit_events table.
target_output.development_guidelines: Rust sidecar: clippy with deny(warnings); unit tests for token refresh logic and reconnect backoff intervals. Vue frontend: vue-tsc strict mode; Vitest for Pinia store logic; all WebSocket calls wrapped in a single useAnalyticsSocket composable. All ClickHouse queries parameterized via driver. No credentials stored outside macOS Keychain; GKE workload identity for server-side secrets. ADR required for any change to WebSocket protocol message schema or ClickHouse table schema.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: ClickHouse single-node is a single point of failure for analytics queries. Mitigation: automated disk snapshots every 6 hours; acceptable given internal-only nature and $800 budget constraint. Tradeoff: Redis Pub/Sub for threshold propagation over database polling -- simpler implementation but requires Redis availability for real-time updates; fallback is next WebSocket delta cycle picks up new threshold from PostgreSQL anyway. Risk: Tauri 2 macOS notarization complexity in GitLab CI. Mitigation: Apple Developer Program certificate stored as GitLab CI masked variable; codesign and notarytool steps automated in pipeline. Risk: GKE rolling updates briefly reduce WebSocket capacity; mitigated by PodDisruptionBudget minAvailable=1.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Alert threshold storage resolved: PostgreSQL owns buyer configuration; Redis Pub/Sub propagates changes to active WebSocket subscriptions (ISS-001 closed). WebSocket reconnection resolved: exponential backoff in Rust sidecar with automatic re-subscription on reconnect (ISS-002 closed). Store drill-down confirmed deferred to Phase 2. ClickHouse single-node accepted given budget constraint with snapshot disaster recovery.

metadata.notes: Round 2 revision for macOS fashion buyer analytics app; fixes alert threshold persistence and WebSocket reconnection gaps from round 1.

project_goal.value: TypeScript/JavaScript SDK providing a unified client interface for NetSuite, Brightpearl, and Cin7 inventory management systems so developers can write inventory sync code once and target any backend
project_goal.rationale: Eliminates per-provider integration rewrites for retail developer teams

target_users.value: Developers building inventory sync integrations at retail and e-commerce companies
target_users.rationale: Primary consumers identified through customer interviews

project_class.rationale: Distributed as an npm package; no hosted server component

capabilities.rationale: Library handles API integration logic and data normalization; no frontend or auth surface

complexity_level.rationale: Three provider adapters with divergent API conventions; pagination and retry handling across all

risk_level.rationale: Library itself holds no data; credentials passed in by calling application

data_sensitivity.rationale: Handles inventory and stock data which is internal business data, not personal data

external_exposure.rationale: SDK is consumed by external developer teams integrating with partner inventory backends

access_model.value: OAuth 2.0 per-provider credentials injected by consuming application; SDK never stores credentials
access_model.rationale: Credential lifecycle is the responsibility of the embedding application, not the SDK

feature_scope.value: Unified inventory read (list products, get stock levels, get warehouse locations), unified inventory write (adjust stock, create/update product records), sync cursor management for incremental sync, rate limit retry handling, typed result events
feature_scope.rationale: Core operations covering the inventory sync use case for all three providers

mvp_scope.value: NetSuite and Brightpearl adapters with read and stock adjustment operations; Cin7 adapter deferred to v1.1
mvp_scope.rationale: NetSuite and Brightpearl cover 80% of target customer base

security_baseline.value: Credentials never logged; HTTPS enforced for all provider API calls; no credential storage within library
security_baseline.rationale: Library must not introduce credential leak surface for embedding applications

frontend_stack.value: N/A -- library only, no UI components
frontend_stack.rationale: SDK is a Node.js/browser-compatible library with no visual components

backend_stack.value: TypeScript targeting Node.js 22 and browser-compatible ESM; no runtime framework
backend_stack.rationale: Pure library with no server; compiled to dual CJS/ESM output via tsup

data_platform.value: No database; sync cursors and provider responses are ephemeral objects returned to calling application
data_platform.rationale: Library is stateless; persistence is the consumer's responsibility

hosting_target.value: npm package distribution via npmjs.com; types published to DefinitelyTyped if needed
hosting_target.rationale: Standard npm distribution for TypeScript libraries

privacy_retention_policy.value: Library retains no data; consuming application is responsible for retention of inventory records
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: SDK is stateless and holds no persistent state

future_scope.value: Cin7 adapter, Shopify inventory adapter, webhook subscription helpers for provider event streams
future_scope.rationale: Roadmap items after v1.0 GA

constraints.value: Must support Node.js 18+ and modern browsers; zero runtime dependencies beyond node-fetch polyfill; package size under 80KB minified
constraints.rationale: Broad compatibility requirement; zero-dep policy prevents dependency conflicts in consuming apps

observability_baseline.value: Debug logging via optional logger injection; no telemetry emitted by library itself
observability_baseline.rationale: Library should not impose logging framework on consumers; optional injected logger pattern

execution_preference.value: Semantic versioning with automated changelog; patch releases on bugfixes; minor on new adapters
execution_preference.rationale: Consumer applications need stable semver guarantees for dependency management

project.goal: Unified TypeScript SDK for NetSuite, Brightpearl, and Cin7 inventory APIs
project.target_users: Retail developer teams building inventory sync integrations

security.transport: HTTPS enforced in all provider HTTP clients

data.db: No database; sync cursors are in-memory objects
data.cache: None; stateless library

reasoner_reviews.product: The adapter pattern with a shared interface is exactly the right abstraction for multi-provider inventory sync. Developers in this space have repeatedly built per-provider clients; a unified SDK with cursor-based incremental sync is a clear improvement. The MVP scope covering NetSuite and Brightpearl is realistic for 8 weeks.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Using tsup for dual CJS/ESM output ensures compatibility across Node.js and bundler environments. The injected-credentials pattern is the correct security model for a library -- it avoids storing OAuth tokens while allowing the consumer full control over token refresh. Sync cursors as plain objects returned to the caller is the right stateless design. Provider adapters should be tree-shakeable so consumers only bundle the providers they use.
reasoner_reviews.security: The injected credentials pattern is sound -- the library never touches credential storage or token refresh. The critical control is ensuring HTTP clients in each adapter enforce HTTPS and never log Authorization headers. Credentials passed into adapter constructors must be redacted from any debug log output.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Zero-dependency constraint is achievable; provider APIs all support standard REST over HTTPS with JSON. The 80KB size limit is tight with three adapters but feasible with tree-shaking. Node.js 18+ enables native fetch so no polyfill is needed for server-side usage.

backend.service_design: Core: InventoryClient interface with listProducts(), getStockLevels(), adjustStock(), createProduct(), updateProduct(). Three adapter implementations: NetSuiteAdapter, BrightpearlAdapter (MVP), Cin7Adapter (v1.1). Factory function createInventoryClient(provider, credentials) returns typed adapter.
backend.api_patterns: All adapters implement InventoryClient<TCredentials> generic interface. Responses normalized into shared ProductRecord and StockLevel types. Sync cursor returned as opaque SyncCursor object passed back on next call. Rate limit errors trigger exponential backoff retry (3 attempts) with typed RateLimitError on exhaustion.
backend.failure_handling: Typed error hierarchy: InventoryApiError (base), RateLimitError, AuthenticationError, NotFoundError, ProviderValidationError. Retry logic only on 429 and 5xx; immediate throw on 4xx except 429. All provider HTTP responses normalized to shared error types before surfacing to consumer.

security.auth_design: OAuth 2.0 client credentials or token objects injected via adapter constructor; SDK never initiates token refresh -- consumer provides fresh token. All HTTP clients constructed with Authorization header derived from credentials object; header value never written to logs.
security.secrets_management: No credential storage in library. Credential objects held only in adapter instance memory for the duration of a method call. Debug logger injection interface explicitly masks Authorization and client_secret fields via a redactSensitiveHeaders utility function.
security.abuse_prevention: Rate limit backoff protects consumer from 429 floods. Timeout default of 30s on all provider HTTP calls prevents hanging. No retry on authentication errors to avoid token lockout from provider.

data.entities: ProductRecord (sku, name, description, category, attributes: Record<string, string>, provider_id, updated_at). StockLevel (sku, warehouse_location_id, quantity_on_hand, quantity_reserved, quantity_available, as_of). StockAdjustment (sku, warehouse_location_id, delta, reason, idempotency_key). SyncCursor (provider, cursor_token: string, last_synced_at: Date). WarehouseLocation (id, name, provider_id, is_active).
data.schema_notes: All normalized types use camelCase TypeScript interfaces exported from the library root. Provider-specific response shapes are internal to each adapter and never exposed as public API types. Idempotency key on StockAdjustment prevents duplicate adjustments on retry.
data.data_risks: Provider API schema changes could break adapter response mapping. Mitigation: provider-specific response decoders are isolated per-adapter and version-locked to provider API version in the adapter registry.

devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: typecheck (tsc --noEmit), lint (ESLint with @typescript-eslint), unit tests (Vitest), build (tsup CJS + ESM), size check (bundlesize < 80KB), publish to npm on version tag via npm publish --access public.
devops.environments: No server environments; CI runs all tests in Node.js 18 and 20 matrices. Provider API tests use recorded HTTP fixtures (nock) to avoid live API calls in CI.
devops.devops_risks: npm publish token must be rotated annually and stored as GitHub Actions secret. Provider API breaking changes will require adapter updates; pin tested provider API versions in adapter documentation.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a stateless TypeScript SDK for multi-provider retail inventory sync. Key decisions: adapter pattern with a shared InventoryClient interface so consumers write provider-agnostic code; injected OAuth credentials so the library never manages token lifecycle; tsup dual CJS/ESM output for maximum compatibility; typed error hierarchy with retry only on retriable status codes. Zero-dependency constraint is met by using native fetch on Node.js 18+ and standard REST interactions.
target_output.title: Unified Retail Inventory SDK -- TypeScript Multi-Provider Client
target_output.executive_summary: A TypeScript/JavaScript SDK that provides a single InventoryClient interface for NetSuite, BrightPearl, and Cin7 inventory management systems. Retail developer teams initialize the SDK with provider credentials and call normalized methods like listProducts(), getStockLevels(), and adjustStock() without writing provider-specific API code. The library handles request construction, response normalization, rate limit retry, and sync cursor management. Distributed as a zero-dependency npm package with dual CJS/ESM output targeting Node.js 18+ and modern browsers.
target_output.architecture_overview: Adapter pattern library with a shared generic InventoryClient interface implemented by three provider adapters: NetSuiteAdapter, BrightpearlAdapter, and Cin7Adapter (v1.1). A factory function createInventoryClient(provider, credentials) is the single entry point, returning a fully typed adapter instance. All provider HTTP communication goes through per-adapter HTTP clients that enforce HTTPS, inject Authorization headers from injected credentials, and apply exponential backoff retry on 429 and 5xx responses. Responses are decoded from provider-specific JSON shapes into normalized TypeScript types (ProductRecord, StockLevel, WarehouseLocation) inside each adapter before returning to the caller. Sync cursors are opaque string-backed objects passed back to callers for incremental sync pagination across all providers.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: TypeScript 5.4 targeting ES2022. Build: tsup generating dual CJS and ESM output with declaration maps. Test: Vitest with nock for HTTP fixture recording per provider. Lint: ESLint with @typescript-eslint strict ruleset. Size: bundlesize CI gate enforcing < 80KB minified. Runtime: Node.js 18+ (native fetch), modern browser-compatible ESM. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with Node.js 18 and 20 test matrices. Distribution: npm public package with provenance attestation on publish.
target_output.functional_feature_map: listProducts(cursor?: SyncCursor): AsyncIterable<ProductRecord> -- pages through all products for the provider using provider cursor semantics, yields normalized ProductRecord objects. getStockLevels(skus: string[], locationId?: string): Promise<StockLevel[]> -- fetches current on-hand and reserved quantities per SKU. adjustStock(adjustment: StockAdjustment): Promise<void> -- posts a quantity delta with idempotency key; retries on 5xx. createProduct / updateProduct: writes normalized ProductRecord back to provider API. WarehouseLocation listing: enumerates active warehouse locations for bin assignment. createInventoryClient factory: resolves provider adapter and returns typed InventoryClient instance.
target_output.system_components: InventoryClient interface (shared TypeScript generic interface defining all public methods and return types). NetSuiteAdapter (maps SuiteScript REST API responses to shared types; handles OAuth 1.0a HMAC-SHA256 signing). BrightpearlAdapter (maps Brightpearl REST API v2 to shared types; handles account-id header injection and channel_id scoping). Cin7Adapter (v1.1; maps Cin7 Core API v1 to shared types). HttpClient utility (per-adapter; enforces HTTPS, injects credentials, implements backoff retry, redacts auth headers from debug logs). SyncCursor utility (encodes and decodes provider-specific pagination tokens as opaque strings). TypedErrorMapper (converts provider HTTP error responses to typed InventoryApiError hierarchy).
target_output.workflows: Incremental Product Sync: Consumer initializes NetSuiteAdapter with OAuth token; calls listProducts() which yields ProductRecord objects and internally pages through NetSuite REST API results using LIMIT/OFFSET or link headers; consumer persists each batch and stores the returned SyncCursor; on next run, passes cursor back to listProducts(cursor) which resumes from the last page position, skipping already-synced records. Stock Adjustment with Retry: Consumer calls adjustStock({sku:'SKU-123', warehouseLocationId:'WH-1', delta:-5, reason:'sale', idempotencyKey:'order-456'}) on BrightpearlAdapter; adapter constructs Brightpearl stock correction POST with idempotency key in request body; on 429 response, HttpClient retries with 1s/2s/4s backoff; on 5xx, retries up to 3 times; on provider validation error, throws ProviderValidationError with normalized message.
target_output.data_model: ProductRecord: {sku: string, name: string, description: string, category: string, attributes: Record<string, string>, providerId: string, updatedAt: Date}. StockLevel: {sku: string, warehouseLocationId: string, quantityOnHand: number, quantityReserved: number, quantityAvailable: number, asOf: Date}. StockAdjustment: {sku: string, warehouseLocationId: string, delta: number, reason: string, idempotencyKey: string}. SyncCursor: {provider: 'netsuite' | 'brightpearl' | 'cin7', cursorToken: string, lastSyncedAt: Date}. WarehouseLocation: {id: string, name: string, providerId: string, isActive: boolean}. provider_credentials (ProviderCredentials union type per-adapter; not persisted by library). All types exported as named TypeScript interfaces from package root index.ts.
target_output.api_design: Public API surface: createInventoryClient(provider: 'netsuite' | 'brightpearl' | 'cin7', credentials: ProviderCredentials): InventoryClient. InventoryClient methods: listProducts(cursor?: SyncCursor): AsyncIterable<ProductRecord>, getStockLevels(skus: string[], locationId?: string): Promise<StockLevel[]>, adjustStock(adj: StockAdjustment): Promise<void>, createProduct(product: Omit<ProductRecord, 'providerId' | 'updatedAt'>): Promise<ProductRecord>, updateProduct(sku: string, updates: Partial<ProductRecord>): Promise<ProductRecord>, listWarehouseLocations(): Promise<WarehouseLocation[]>. Errors: InventoryApiError | RateLimitError | AuthenticationError | ProviderValidationError | NotFoundError -- all extend base InventoryApiError with provider, statusCode, and rawResponse fields.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Injected credentials pattern: OAuth tokens or API key objects passed into adapter constructor; never stored beyond method call scope. HTTPS enforced on all provider HTTP calls; HTTP URLs rejected with ConfigurationError at construction time. Authorization and client_secret fields redacted from debug log output via redactSensitiveHeaders utility called before any logger.debug invocation. No telemetry or data transmitted to any endpoint except the configured provider API. npm package published with provenance attestation to allow consumers to verify supply chain integrity. No eval() or dynamic require() usage; strict ESLint rules enforce this.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: No server deployment; library distributed via npm. GitHub Actions CI runs on every PR and push to main: typecheck (tsc --noEmit), ESLint strict, Vitest unit tests with 90% line coverage gate, nock-recorded HTTP fixture tests for NetSuite and Brightpearl adapter response shapes, tsup build, bundlesize check (< 80KB). Release workflow: on version tag push, GitHub Actions runs full CI then publishes to npm with npm publish --access public and --provenance flag. Changelog generated from conventional commits via changelogithub. Breaking changes trigger major semver bump enforced by commitlint in CI.
target_output.observability: Optional logger injection: createInventoryClient accepts optional logger: {debug, info, warn, error} parameter. When provided, adapter logs request URLs (without query params containing tokens), response status codes, retry attempts with backoff delay, and normalized error types. Authorization headers, client secrets, and API keys are redacted before any log call. No metrics emitted by library; consumers can wrap adapter calls to instrument with their own APM. Vitest test coverage report uploaded to Codecov on CI.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: No infrastructure cost; package is stateless and distributed via npm. GitHub Actions free tier covers CI for the expected commit volume. npm registry hosting is free for public packages. Scaling is entirely in the consuming application; the library adds negligible overhead per method call (HTTP round-trip to provider API dominates latency).
target_output.phased_implementation: v1.0 (8 weeks): NetSuiteAdapter (OAuth 1.0a, list products, get stock levels, adjust stock, sync cursors), BrightpearlAdapter (OAuth 2.0 token injection, list products, stock correction, warehouse locations), shared TypeScript types, typed error hierarchy, exponential backoff retry, tsup dual CJS/ESM build, GitHub Actions CI with fixture tests, npm publish workflow, README with adapter quickstart examples. v1.1 (4 weeks): Cin7Adapter (Cin7 Core API v1, product CRUD, stock adjustment), createProduct/updateProduct on all adapters. v1.2 (4 weeks): Shopify inventory adapter, webhook subscription helper types for provider event payloads.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode with noUncheckedIndexedAccess. All public API types documented with JSDoc including param and return descriptions. Each adapter must have dedicated Vitest test file with nock-recorded fixtures covering success, rate limit retry, and authentication error scenarios. Provider-specific response decoder functions are private to each adapter file -- never leak raw provider shapes to public types. No eval(), dynamic require(), or process.env reads inside library code. Conventional commit messages enforced by commitlint; Husky pre-commit hook runs typecheck and lint.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: NetSuite and Brightpearl API schema changes break adapter response decoders. Mitigation: integration tests run against recorded nock fixtures pinned to tested provider API versions; adapter changelogs note provider API version compatibility. Tradeoff: injected credentials require the consumer to manage OAuth refresh -- simpler library but slightly more consumer-side work. Tradeoff: zero-dependency constraint means no Zod validation for provider responses; use hand-written type guards in decoders instead. Risk: tree-shaking may be incomplete in some bundlers for CJS output, pulling in all three adapters. Mitigation: ESM output is preferred for bundler consumption; tsup sideEffects:false flag set in package.json.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: OAuth credential model resolved: injected per-adapter instance, never refreshed by library. Sync cursor format resolved: opaque base64-encoded JSON per provider, decoded only inside adapter. Cin7 adapter deferred to v1.1 as confirmed in MVP scope. Zero-dependency constraint confirmed achievable with native fetch on Node.js 18+.

metadata.notes: First-pass plan for a TypeScript multi-provider retail inventory SDK with adapter pattern, injected credentials, and zero-dependency constraint.

project_goal.value: Research prototype to evaluate computer vision models for automated out-of-stock shelf gap detection using overhead supermarket camera feeds; generates annotated reports and model accuracy metrics
project_goal.rationale: Automates shelf replenishment signal detection to reduce manual store audits

target_users.value: Computer vision researchers and retail operations analysts at the supermarket chain
target_users.rationale: Internal research team plus ops analysts who consume accuracy reports

project_class.rationale: Experimental system for model evaluation; not production-grade shelf management

capabilities.rationale: Streamlit UI for researcher review; FastAPI for inference; PostgreSQL for run tracking; LLM for annotation summarization

complexity_level.rationale: GPU inference pipeline with model versioning, annotation storage, and multi-model comparison evaluation

risk_level.rationale: Internal-only prototype with no PII or financial data; camera feeds contain store aisle images only

data_sensitivity.rationale: Camera frames and detection results are internal operational data

external_exposure.rationale: Prototype accessible only to internal research team on GCP VPC

access_model.value: Custom JWT with researcher and analyst roles; no MFA required for internal prototype
access_model.rationale: Lightweight auth sufficient for internal prototype on VPC

feature_scope.value: Camera frame ingestion from GCS, OOS shelf gap detection inference, detection annotation storage with confidence scores, evaluation run comparison (precision/recall per model version), Streamlit researcher review UI with annotated frame viewer
feature_scope.rationale: Core research workflow for model benchmarking

mvp_scope.value: Single-model inference on pre-uploaded GCS frame batches with annotation storage and basic precision/recall report; live camera feed integration deferred
mvp_scope.rationale: MVP scoped to offline batch evaluation before committing to live feed integration

security_baseline.value: Custom JWT auth, HTTPS on all endpoints, GCS bucket with uniform bucket-level ACL restricted to research VPC service accounts
security_baseline.rationale: Minimal controls appropriate for internal prototype

frontend_stack.rationale: Rapid UI development appropriate for research prototype; no custom frontend needed

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI
backend_stack.rationale: FastAPI enables async inference endpoints; Python ecosystem for CV model integration

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for run metadata, annotations, and evaluation results; GCS for raw frame storage
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL for structured run tracking; GCS for large frame blob storage

hosting_target.value: GCE GPU instances (NVIDIA T4) for inference; Cloud Run for FastAPI serving in eval mode
hosting_target.rationale: GPU instances for model inference; Cloud Run for lightweight API serving between batch runs

privacy_retention_policy.value: Camera frame batches retained 6 months; evaluation run results retained 2 years for model lineage; raw frames purged after annotation extraction
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Research model lineage requires long retention; raw frames have no personal data but storage cost limits retention

future_scope.value: Live camera feed integration, multi-store deployment, automated replenishment alert generation
future_scope.rationale: Production deployment contingent on prototype accuracy meeting 90% precision threshold

constraints.value: GPU budget under $500/month; prototype must run on GCP us-central1 only; inference latency under 2s per frame
constraints.rationale: Research budget constraint; 2s per frame supports 1800 frames/hour batch throughput

observability_baseline.value: MLflow for experiment tracking and model metrics; Weights and Biases for training run visualization; PostgreSQL for inference run logs
observability_baseline.rationale: Standard ML research observability stack for model comparison

execution_preference.value: Exploratory sprints; no fixed release cadence; model evaluation runs triggered manually by researchers
execution_preference.rationale: Research workflow does not follow product sprint cadence

llm_integration.value: GPT-4o used to summarize detection annotation batches into human-readable OOS reports; called via OpenAI API after each evaluation run
llm_integration.rationale: LLM summarization reduces time researchers spend interpreting raw annotation JSON into plain-language gap reports

project.goal: Evaluate CV models for supermarket shelf OOS gap detection with annotated accuracy reports
project.target_users: CV researchers and retail ops analysts

frontend.approach: Researcher review UI with annotated frame viewer and precision/recall plots

backend.api: REST endpoints for run submission and annotation retrieval

security.secrets: GCP Secret Manager for OpenAI API key

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 for run metadata and annotations

reasoner_reviews.product: The batch-first MVP is the right call for a research prototype -- validating model accuracy on labeled GCS frames before building live feed infrastructure avoids expensive integration work on a model that may not meet the 90% precision threshold. The Streamlit UI provides researchers with the annotation review surface they need without front-end engineering overhead.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: FastAPI on Cloud Run for the evaluation API is efficient between batch runs. The GPT-4o summarization step is well-placed as a post-processing stage after annotation extraction -- it adds human-readable context without blocking the inference pipeline. PostgreSQL for annotation and run metadata is appropriate; GCS for raw frames avoids expensive DB blob storage.
reasoner_reviews.security: Internal-only prototype on GCP VPC with custom JWT is acceptable given the low risk level. The critical control is restricting the OpenAI API key to GCP Secret Manager with the Cloud Run service account as the only accessor. Frame batches on GCS should use VPC Service Controls to prevent exfiltration.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: GPU budget of $500/month supports a single NVIDIA T4 instance in us-central1 for batch inference runs. Inference at <2s per frame on T4 with YOLOv8 is achievable for 640x640 frames. MLflow can be self-hosted on a small GCE instance to avoid SaaS cost.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 1 plan was thin on the LLM integration specifics -- it did not describe the prompt structure for GPT-4o summarization or how annotation JSON is formatted before being passed to the LLM. The evaluation_runs entity was missing a model_version field, making cross-version comparison ambiguous.

frontend.app_structure: Streamlit multipage app: Evaluation Run Launcher (submit batch job with GCS path and model version), Run Results (precision/recall metrics per model version side by side), Frame Annotator (grid of annotated frames with bounding boxes and confidence scores), OOS Report Viewer (GPT-4o generated summary text with export to PDF).
frontend.pages_and_flows: Researchers select a GCS frame batch path and model checkpoint, submit evaluation run; Streamlit polls FastAPI /runs/:id/status; on completion renders precision/recall curves and annotated frame sample; LLM summary auto-displays below metrics.
frontend.performance_notes: Annotated frames rendered as base64 PNGs from GCS signed URLs; Streamlit pagination limits frame grid to 50 per page to avoid browser memory issues with high-resolution frames.

backend.service_design: FastAPI app with endpoints for run submission, run status polling, annotation retrieval, and LLM report generation. Inference worker runs as GCE startup script on T4 instance triggered by Pub/Sub message from FastAPI on run submission.
backend.llm_integration_design: After inference completes, annotation records for the run are queried from PostgreSQL and serialized as JSON (sku_id, shelf_position, confidence, oos_flag, frame_id). This JSON is chunked into batches of 100 annotations and passed to GPT-4o with a prompt instructing it to summarize the out-of-stock positions, highlight high-confidence gaps, and suggest priority replenishment zones. The resulting text sections are concatenated and stored in evaluation_runs.llm_report_text.
backend.failure_handling: Inference failures logged to PostgreSQL evaluation_runs.error_message; Streamlit UI shows error state with GCS frame path for manual retry. OpenAI API rate limit errors trigger exponential backoff with 3 retries before marking LLM report as generation_failed.

security.auth_design: Custom JWT issued by FastAPI on researcher login; 8-hour expiry suitable for research session; role claim: researcher or analyst.
security.secrets_management: OpenAI API key stored in GCP Secret Manager; accessed by Cloud Run service account with secretmanager.secretAccessor IAM role. Never written to environment variables or logs.
security.privacy_controls: GCS bucket with uniform ACL restricted to VPC service accounts; no public URLs except signed short-lived URLs for Streamlit frame rendering (15-minute expiry).

data.entities: camera_feeds (id, store_id, camera_position, gcs_prefix, ingestion_active -- PostgreSQL), shelf_frames (id, camera_feed_id, gcs_path, captured_at, frame_width, frame_height -- PostgreSQL), detection_annotations (id, frame_id, model_version_id, shelf_position, bounding_box_json, confidence, oos_flag -- PostgreSQL), oos_events (id, store_id, shelf_position, detected_at, confirmed_by_analyst, model_version_id -- PostgreSQL), model_versions (id, model_name, architecture, checkpoint_gcs_path, trained_at, notes -- PostgreSQL), evaluation_runs (id, model_version_id, frame_batch_gcs_path, submitted_at, completed_at, precision, recall, f1_score, llm_report_text, status ENUM pending/running/completed/failed -- PostgreSQL).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL for all structured metadata. GCS bucket research-frames-prod for raw frame JPEGs organized by store_id/date. GCS bucket model-checkpoints for YOLO/DETR weight files.
data.analytics_events: MLflow experiment per model_version_id; metrics logged per evaluation run: precision, recall, f1, inference_time_ms_p50, oos_detection_rate. Weights and Biases for training visualization if model fine-tuning is performed.

devops.deployment_topology: Cloud Run service (FastAPI, 2 vCPU, 4GB RAM) for API and LLM report generation. GCE n1-standard-4 + NVIDIA T4 (us-central1-a) for inference batch runs; instance started via Pub/Sub trigger and stopped after run completion to control GPU cost. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 (db-f1-micro). GCS for frames and checkpoints.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: pytest with 80% coverage, mypy type check, Docker build + push to Artifact Registry, Cloud Run deploy on main branch merge. GCE inference image rebuilt on inference worker Dockerfile changes only.
devops.cost_controls: GCE T4 instance stopped between runs (Pub/Sub start/stop lifecycle); estimated 2-3 hours/day active = ~$120/month. Cloud Run scales to zero between API calls. Total GCP cost ~$350/month, under $500 budget.

previous_plan.title: Supermarket Shelf OOS Detection Research Prototype -- Round 1

target_output.title: Supermarket Shelf Gap Detection -- Computer Vision Research Prototype
target_output.executive_summary: A research prototype on GCP for evaluating computer vision models that detect out-of-stock shelf gaps in supermarket overhead camera feeds. Researchers submit GCS frame batches for inference on a NVIDIA T4 GPU instance, review annotated detections in a Streamlit UI, and compare precision/recall metrics across model versions. GPT-4o summarizes annotation batches into plain-language OOS reports highlighting priority replenishment zones.
target_output.architecture_overview: FastAPI service on Cloud Run accepts evaluation run submissions and serves annotation data. On run submission, FastAPI publishes to a Pub/Sub topic that starts a GCE n1-standard-4 + T4 GPU instance; the inference worker pulls frames from GCS, runs the detection model, writes detection_annotations to PostgreSQL, then stops the GCE instance to control GPU cost. After inference, FastAPI calls GPT-4o with serialized annotation chunks to generate the OOS summary report stored in evaluation_runs.llm_report_text. Streamlit UI polls run status and renders annotated frames with precision/recall plots and the LLM report.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, Pydantic v2, SQLAlchemy 2 async. CV inference: YOLOv8 (ultralytics) or DETR on PyTorch 2.2 on GCE T4. LLM: GPT-4o via openai-python SDK. Frontend: Streamlit 1.33. Database: Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16. Storage: GCS for frames and model checkpoints. Infra: Cloud Run (API), GCE T4 (inference), Pub/Sub (run lifecycle). Experiment tracking: MLflow (self-hosted on GCE e2-micro). CI: GitHub Actions.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Evaluation Run Submission: researcher provides GCS frame batch path, selects model_version_id, and triggers inference job. Inference Pipeline: GCE T4 worker processes frames through detection model, stores detection_annotations with confidence scores and bounding boxes. Cross-Model Comparison: Streamlit view shows precision/recall/F1 side-by-side for selected model versions using evaluation_runs.model_version_id FK. Annotated Frame Viewer: grid of shelf frames with drawn bounding boxes and OOS confidence scores. LLM OOS Report: GPT-4o generated summary of detected gaps with priority replenishment zone recommendations.
target_output.system_components: Cloud Run FastAPI service (run management, annotation API, LLM report trigger), GCE n1-standard-4 + T4 inference worker (YOLOv8/DETR batch inference), Pub/Sub topic (run lifecycle: start/stop GCE), Cloud SQL PostgreSQL (run metadata, annotations, model versions), GCS bucket research-frames (frame JPEGs), GCS bucket model-checkpoints (YOLO/DETR weight files), MLflow server on GCE e2-micro (experiment metrics), Streamlit app (researcher UI), GPT-4o API (annotation summarization).
target_output.workflows: Inference Batch Run: Researcher submits POST /runs with gcs_frame_batch_path and model_version_id; FastAPI creates evaluation_runs row (status=pending), publishes start_inference Pub/Sub message; GCE startup script pulls selected model checkpoint from GCS, iterates over frame JPEGs, runs detection, writes detection_annotations rows with frame_id, shelf_position, bounding_box_json, confidence, oos_flag, and model_version_id; on completion updates evaluation_runs with precision, recall, f1 metrics and publishes inference_complete message; FastAPI listener triggers LLM report generation step. LLM Report Generation: FastAPI queries detection_annotations WHERE evaluation_run_id=:id; serializes to JSON array [{frame_id, shelf_position, confidence, oos_flag}]; chunks into batches of 100; each chunk sent to GPT-4o with prompt: 'You are analyzing supermarket shelf scan results. The following annotations describe detected shelf positions with confidence scores and out-of-stock flags. Summarize the key OOS gaps, highlight high-confidence detections above 0.85, and identify shelf zones with the most gaps for priority replenishment.'; responses concatenated and stored in evaluation_runs.llm_report_text.
target_output.data_model: model_versions (id UUID PK, model_name VARCHAR, architecture ENUM yolov8/detr, checkpoint_gcs_path, trained_at, notes -- PostgreSQL). evaluation_runs (id UUID PK, model_version_id FK, frame_batch_gcs_path, submitted_at, completed_at, precision FLOAT, recall FLOAT, f1_score FLOAT, llm_report_text TEXT, status ENUM pending/running/completed/failed -- PostgreSQL). shelf_frames (id UUID PK, camera_feed_id FK, gcs_path, captured_at, frame_width, frame_height -- PostgreSQL). detection_annotations (id UUID PK, frame_id FK, evaluation_run_id FK, model_version_id FK, shelf_position VARCHAR, bounding_box_json JSON, confidence FLOAT, oos_flag BOOL -- PostgreSQL, indexed on evaluation_run_id, model_version_id). oos_events (id UUID PK, store_id, shelf_position, detected_at, confirmed_by_analyst BOOL, model_version_id FK -- PostgreSQL). camera_feeds (id UUID PK, store_id, camera_position, gcs_prefix -- PostgreSQL).
target_output.api_design: POST /runs (submit evaluation run with gcs_frame_batch_path, model_version_id), GET /runs/:id (run status with precision/recall/f1 and llm_report_text), GET /runs/:id/annotations?page=1 (paginated detection_annotations for frame viewer), GET /model-versions (list available model checkpoints), POST /model-versions (register new checkpoint with GCS path), GET /runs?model_version_ids=v1,v2 (list runs filtered by model versions for cross-model comparison). All endpoints require custom JWT Bearer token. Signed GCS URLs for frame rendering returned on GET /runs/:id/annotations to avoid proxying frame bytes through Cloud Run.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Custom JWT (HS256) issued by FastAPI login endpoint; 8-hour expiry suitable for research sessions; role claim researcher (submit runs, view all results) or analyst (view only). OpenAI API key in GCP Secret Manager accessed by Cloud Run service account only; never in environment variables or logs. GCS bucket with uniform bucket-level ACL restricted to Cloud Run and GCE service accounts; all Streamlit frame URLs are signed with 15-minute expiry. No PII in system; camera frames contain store aisles only.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Cloud Run FastAPI service: min-instances=0, max-instances=3, 2 vCPU, 4GB RAM. GCE n1-standard-4 + NVIDIA T4 in us-central1-a: started via Pub/Sub Cloud Function on run submission, stopped via Pub/Sub after inference_complete message; instance image pre-baked with ultralytics + PyTorch in Artifact Registry. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 db-f1-micro with daily backups. GCS lifecycle rule: delete raw frames after 6 months. GitHub Actions CI: pytest, mypy, Docker build, Cloud Run deploy on main. No staging environment for prototype; single research environment.
target_output.observability: MLflow experiment per model_version_id tracks precision, recall, F1, and inference_time_ms_p50 per evaluation_run. evaluation_runs table serves as the primary run audit log with status transitions and error_message on failure. Cloud Run request logs in Cloud Logging for API debugging. GCE instance start/stop events logged via Pub/Sub audit trail. No production alerting required for prototype; researchers check MLflow dashboard for run status.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: GCE T4 instance active ~3 hours/day for batch runs: ~$120/month. Cloud Run at low request volume: ~$20/month. Cloud SQL db-f1-micro: ~$25/month. GCS frames (100GB/month): ~$25/month. MLflow GCE e2-micro: ~$10/month. Total ~$200/month, well under $500 GPU budget. If batch volume grows, T4 can be replaced with A100 at ~$3/hour billed per batch run.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): FastAPI inference API, GCE T4 inference worker with YOLOv8, PostgreSQL schema with model_versions and evaluation_runs (with model_version_id), detection_annotations storage, GPT-4o report generation with 100-annotation chunking, Streamlit UI with run submission, frame viewer, and LLM report display, GitHub Actions CI. Phase 2 (4 weeks): DETR model adapter for cross-architecture comparison, precision/recall multi-model chart in Streamlit, analyst role UI. Phase 3 (contingent on accuracy): Live camera feed integration via RTSP ingestion, automated OOS alert generation for store managers.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python strict typing with mypy; Pydantic v2 models for all API request/response shapes. All annotation queries parameterized via SQLAlchemy ORM to prevent injection. OpenAI API key accessed exclusively via Secret Manager client; never read from os.environ. Inference worker Docker image pinned to specific ultralytics and torch versions in requirements.txt. Git tag model_version checkpoints with GCS path in model_versions table before running evaluation to ensure reproducibility.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: GCE T4 unavailability in us-central1-a due to spot capacity fluctuations. Mitigation: use on-demand instance (not preemptible) for prototype batch runs; cost is acceptable at low frequency. Tradeoff: GPT-4o annotation chunking at 100 records may produce fragmented reports for runs with thousands of frames; mitigation is post-concatenation prompt asking GPT-4o to synthesize the full summary. Risk: YOLOv8 fine-tuning not yet done -- base model accuracy may be below 90% threshold for production. This is expected at prototype stage; threshold evaluation is the prototype's purpose.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: LLM prompt structure resolved: 100-annotation JSON chunks with explicit instruction to summarize OOS gaps and priority replenishment zones (ISS-001 closed). evaluation_runs model_version_id FK resolved: model_versions entity added and FK propagated to evaluation_runs and detection_annotations (ISS-002 closed). CV framework narrowed to YOLOv8 as primary with DETR as Phase 2 comparison. Live camera feed deferred to Phase 3 contingent on accuracy threshold.

metadata.notes: Round 2 revision for supermarket shelf gap CV research prototype; fixes LLM prompt gap and data model model_version_id omission from round 1.

project_goal.value: Go CLI tool for retail operations engineers to query and bulk-update product inventory records across multiple store locations via a REST API, with output in JSON, CSV, or formatted table
project_goal.rationale: Replaces ad-hoc curl scripts and spreadsheet-driven bulk updates with a repeatable operations tool

target_users.value: Retail operations engineers who manage inventory records across store locations
target_users.rationale: Technical users comfortable with CLI tooling; not customer-facing

project_class.rationale: Single binary CLI tool; no server component or browser UI

capabilities.rationale: CLI integrates with inventory REST API; no auth surface, frontend, or realtime needs

complexity_level.rationale: CRUD operations over a REST API with output formatting; no complex business logic in the CLI itself

risk_level.rationale: Bulk update capability can cause widespread inventory data corruption if misused or misconfigured

data_sensitivity.rationale: Inventory record data is internal operational data, not personal or financial data

external_exposure.rationale: Tool runs on engineer workstations and CI runners; API endpoint is internal

access_model.value: API key loaded from INVENTORY_API_KEY environment variable; no interactive login flow
access_model.rationale: Standard API key pattern for internal CLI tooling; engineers manage their own keys

feature_scope.value: inventory query (filter by SKU, category, location, status), inventory bulk update (CSV input with SKU/quantity pairs), output formats (JSON, CSV, table), dry-run mode for bulk operations, progress reporting for large batches
feature_scope.rationale: Core operations workflow covering read and write use cases

mvp_scope.value: inventory query with filters and table/JSON/CSV output; bulk update from CSV with dry-run mode and batch progress
mvp_scope.rationale: Both query and bulk-update are equally critical to the operations workflow

project.goal: Go CLI for querying and bulk-updating retail inventory records via REST API
project.target_users: Retail operations engineers

backend.framework: No framework; stdlib + cobra for command parsing
backend.api: REST API calls via net/http with API key auth

security.auth: API key from INVENTORY_API_KEY env var
security.safeguards: --dry-run mode, confirmation prompt on bulk writes

data.cache: None

reasoner_reviews.product: Combining query and bulk-update in a single CLI binary is the right scope for an operations tool at this stage. The dry-run flag and confirmation prompt for bulk operations are essential given the medium risk level -- a bulk update misconfiguration could corrupt inventory across dozens of stores. CSV input for bulk updates matches the workflow engineers already use.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Cobra is the correct choice for a multi-command Go CLI. The parallel batch submission pattern for bulk updates improves throughput significantly vs sequential. Flag-based filtering (--sku, --category, --location) with output format flags (--format json|csv|table) follows standard CLI conventions that engineers expect. No local database or config file simplifies distribution.
reasoner_reviews.security: API key from environment variable is standard and avoids key exposure in shell history. The critical control is the --dry-run flag with a confirmation prompt before any bulk write -- without this, a mis-authored CSV could trigger mass inventory changes. The key must never appear in --verbose log output.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Go 1.22 single binary via goreleaser with CGO_ENABLED=0 will produce a static binary well under 20MB. Cross-compilation to darwin/amd64, darwin/arm64, and linux/amd64 is straightforward. Homebrew tap publication via goreleaser is standard practice.

backend.service_design: cobra CLI with two top-level commands: inventory query and inventory update. inventory query --sku=SKU-123 --category=apparel --location=STORE-7 --format=table pages through inventory API and renders results. inventory update --file=updates.csv --batch-size=50 --dry-run reads CSV, validates rows, previews changes, prompts for confirmation, then submits updates in parallel goroutines.
backend.api_patterns: All API calls use net/http with Accept: application/json; X-API-Key header from INVENTORY_API_KEY env var. Pagination via Link header or cursor query param; CLI follows next links until exhausted. Bulk update uses POST /inventory/bulk-update with array payload; batched at configurable --batch-size (default 50).
backend.failure_handling: HTTP 429 triggers exponential backoff retry (3 attempts). HTTP 4xx errors printed to stderr per-row in bulk update with row number and API error message; remaining rows continue. --fail-fast flag aborts bulk update on first error. Connection timeout default 30s configurable via --timeout flag.

security.auth_design: INVENTORY_API_KEY read from environment at startup; error and exit if not set. API key injected as X-API-Key header on every request. Key value redacted from all --verbose log output via string masking.
security.abuse_prevention: Bulk update requires explicit --confirm flag or interactive y/N prompt when --dry-run is not provided. --dry-run prints affected rows to stdout without making API calls. Batch size cap of 200 per request prevents single oversized payloads.
security.security_risks: Risk: API key in shell history if passed via --api-key flag (not environment variable). Mitigation: only support env var; no --api-key flag in CLI surface.

data.entities: inventory_records (sku_id, store_location_id, quantity_on_hand, quantity_reserved, last_updated -- upstream API schema, read-only in CLI). store_locations (id, name, region -- upstream API). sku_filters (CLI-side: parsed from --sku, --category, --location flags into filter struct for query string construction). bulk_update_jobs (CLI-side in-memory: parsed CSV rows validated as BulkUpdateRow{sku_id, store_location_id, new_quantity, reason} before submission). api_sessions (CLI-side: http.Client with 30s timeout and API key transport wrapper).
data.schema_notes: CSV format for bulk update: headers sku_id,store_location_id,new_quantity,reason. Validation: sku_id and store_location_id are non-empty strings, new_quantity is non-negative integer, reason is non-empty string. Malformed rows reported to stderr with row number.
data.data_risks: Malformed CSV rows that pass validation could set incorrect quantities at scale. Mitigation: --dry-run output shows full row-by-row preview with API-resolved current quantity alongside proposed new_quantity for human review before confirmation.

devops.deployment_topology: No server deployment. GitHub Releases via goreleaser: cross-compiled binaries for darwin/amd64, darwin/arm64, linux/amd64, linux/arm64. Homebrew tap repository auto-updated by goreleaser on release. CI runners install via curl to Homebrew.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: go vet, staticcheck, unit tests (go test ./...), goreleaser build check on every PR. On tag push: goreleaser release with GitHub Release creation, binary upload, and Homebrew tap update.
devops.devops_risks: Homebrew tap formula requires manual update if goreleaser configuration changes. Mitigation: goreleaser automates Homebrew formula generation from release artifacts.
