target_output.title: Retail Inventory Operations CLI -- Go Single Binary
target_output.executive_summary: A Go 1.22 CLI tool for retail operations engineers that queries and bulk-updates product inventory records across store locations via a REST API. The inventory query command filters by SKU, category, and location and renders results as a formatted table, JSON, or CSV. The inventory update command reads a validated CSV of SKU/quantity pairs, previews proposed changes in dry-run mode, prompts for confirmation, and submits updates in parallel batches with per-row error reporting. Distributed as a static binary via GitHub Releases and Homebrew tap.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single static Go binary compiled with goreleaser for darwin/amd64, darwin/arm64, linux/amd64, and linux/arm64. Two cobra subcommands: inventory query (filter, paginate, format) and inventory update (CSV parse, validate, dry-run preview, confirm, parallel batch submit). All inventory API calls use a shared http.Client configured with the INVENTORY_API_KEY from the environment as the X-API-Key header. No local state is written to disk; the tool is fully stateless. Bulk update batches are submitted in parallel goroutines bounded by --batch-size (default 50, max 200) with rate limit retry.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: Go 1.22 with CGO_ENABLED=0. Command parsing: cobra. HTTP: standard library net/http. Output formatting: tablewriter for table output; encoding/json for JSON; encoding/csv for CSV. Build and release: goreleaser with GitHub Releases and Homebrew tap automation. CI: GitHub Actions with go vet, staticcheck, and go test. Binary: single static binary, no runtime dependencies.
target_output.functional_feature_map: inventory query --sku=SKU-123 --category=footwear --location=STORE-42 --format=table|json|csv: pages through inventory API, applies filters, renders output in requested format with totals row for table mode. inventory update --file=updates.csv --batch-size=50 --dry-run: parses and validates CSV, renders preview table (sku_id, store_location_id, current_qty, proposed_qty, change, reason), prompts y/N, then submits batches in parallel with progress bar and per-row error summary. --confirm flag: suppresses y/N prompt for non-interactive CI usage. --verbose flag: enables HTTP debug logging with API key redacted. --timeout flag: configures per-request timeout (default 30s).
target_output.system_components: CLI binary (cobra root command, inventory query subcommand, inventory update subcommand), InventoryAPIClient struct (net/http client with API key transport, pagination follower, exponential backoff retry on 429), CSVParser (reads updates.csv, validates rows, returns []BulkUpdateRow or validation errors), DryRunRenderer (tablewriter table of current vs proposed quantities fetched from API), BatchSubmitter (parallel goroutine pool bounded by batch-size, progress bar via progressbar library, per-row error collector), OutputFormatter (JSON/CSV/table renderers for query results).
target_output.workflows: Inventory Query: engineer runs 'inventory query --sku=BOOT-42 --location=STORE-7 --format=table'; CLI reads INVENTORY_API_KEY from env (exits with error if not set); constructs GET /inventory?sku=BOOT-42&location_id=STORE-7&limit=100 request; follows Link header pagination until exhausted; renders results as aligned table to stdout with column headers sku_id, location, qty_on_hand, qty_reserved, last_updated. Bulk Update with Dry-Run: engineer runs 'inventory update --file=updates.csv --dry-run'; CLI parses updates.csv (headers: sku_id,store_location_id,new_quantity,reason); validates each row (non-empty strings, non-negative integer for new_quantity); fetches current quantities from API for each SKU/location pair; renders preview table with columns sku_id, store_id, current_qty, proposed_qty, change, reason; prints row count and any validation errors to stderr; exits without making write calls. Bulk Update Confirmed: same flow without --dry-run; after preview table, prompts 'Apply 47 updates? [y/N]: '; on y submits batches of 50 in parallel via goroutines; progress bar tracks completed batches; rows that return 4xx errors are collected and printed as a failure summary at the end; exits with code 1 if any rows failed.
target_output.data_model: inventory_records (sku_id, store_location_id, quantity_on_hand, quantity_reserved, last_updated -- upstream API read-only). store_locations (id, name, region -- upstream API). BulkUpdateRow struct (Go): {SkuID string, StoreLocationID string, NewQuantity int, Reason string} -- parsed from CSV row. CSV format: first row must be header 'sku_id,store_location_id,new_quantity,reason'; sku_id: non-empty string; store_location_id: non-empty string; new_quantity: non-negative integer (0 allowed); reason: non-empty string (min 3 chars). Malformed rows: logged to stderr as 'row 14: new_quantity must be a non-negative integer, got: -5' and skipped; remaining valid rows proceed. api_sessions: http.Client with API key transport configured at startup from INVENTORY_API_KEY env var.
target_output.api_design: The CLI consumes (does not expose) the inventory REST API. Calls made by the CLI: GET /inventory?sku=:sku&category=:cat&location_id=:loc&status=:status&limit=100&cursor=:cursor (query with pagination), GET /inventory/:sku?location_id=:loc (single record for dry-run current qty lookup), POST /inventory/bulk-update with body [{sku_id, store_location_id, new_quantity, reason}] array (bulk write, batched at --batch-size). Authentication: X-API-Key header from INVENTORY_API_KEY env var on all requests. Error handling: 401 prints 'Invalid API key -- check INVENTORY_API_KEY environment variable' and exits 1; 429 retries with backoff; 4xx per row logged to stderr as row error; 5xx retried up to 3 times then logged as failure.
target_output.security_and_compliance: API key loaded from INVENTORY_API_KEY at startup; not accepted as a CLI flag to prevent shell history exposure; absent key triggers immediate exit with error message. Key value masked in --verbose HTTP debug log output (replaced with REDACTED). Bulk update safeguards: --dry-run mode fetches current quantities and renders a preview table without writing; interactive y/N confirmation prompt with counts before any write; --confirm flag suppresses prompt for non-interactive CI pipelines and requires explicit opt-in. Batch size capped at 200 per request to prevent oversized payloads. HTTPS enforced: URLs not beginning with https:// rejected with ConfigurationError at startup. No API key or inventory data written to local disk.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: No server deployment. goreleaser configuration produces: darwin/amd64, darwin/arm64, linux/amd64, linux/arm64 static binaries; GitHub Release with asset checksums (SHA-256 .checksums file); Homebrew tap formula auto-generated. GitHub Actions CI on every PR: go vet, staticcheck lint, go test ./..., goreleaser build --snapshot (build check only). On version tag: goreleaser release uploads binaries to GitHub Releases and updates Homebrew tap repository. Engineers install via 'brew install internal-tap/tools/inventory-cli' or curl installer script. CI pipelines use the GitHub Release curl installer.
target_output.observability: --verbose flag: prints HTTP method, URL (without query params containing auth tokens), response status code, and retry attempt count to stderr. API key value replaced with REDACTED in all output. No telemetry or metrics emitted; stateless CLI has no observability infrastructure. Error exit codes: 0 on success, 1 on API error, 2 on configuration error (missing env var, malformed CSV), 3 on partial failure (some bulk update rows failed). Engineers diagnose issues via --verbose output and API server logs.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: No infrastructure cost for the CLI itself. GitHub Actions free tier covers CI. GitHub Releases free for binary hosting. Bulk update throughput scales with --batch-size and network latency to inventory API; parallel goroutines at default batch-size=50 with 30s timeout can process approximately 3000 inventory updates per minute against a typical retail inventory API.
target_output.phased_implementation: v1.0 (4 weeks): inventory query with --sku/--category/--location filters and --format flag, inventory update with CSV parsing, ISS-001 fix (dry-run preview table and y/N confirmation with --confirm flag), ISS-002 fix (CSV validation with per-row error reporting), GitHub Actions CI, goreleaser release pipeline, Homebrew tap. v1.1 (2 weeks): --output-file flag to write query results to file, --filter-file flag for large SKU list queries, bulk update CSV template generation command. v1.2 (future): S3 export command, reorder point calculation, PO creation API integration.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go stdlib only for core logic; cobra for command parsing; tablewriter and progressbar are acceptable lightweight UI dependencies. All API key values must pass through the redactAPIKey() helper before appearing in any log output. Unit tests required for CSVParser validation logic, DryRunRenderer table generation, and BatchSubmitter error collection. integration tests use a mock HTTP server (httptest.NewServer). No init() functions; all initialization explicit in cobra command PreRunE hooks.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: bulk update with --confirm flag in CI pipelines could be triggered unintentionally by automated scripts running against production inventory. Mitigation: require explicit INVENTORY_ENV=production environment variable for any bulk update targeting the production API endpoint; documented in README. Tradeoff: parallel batch submission improves throughput but makes error ordering non-deterministic; per-row errors are collected and sorted by row number in the final failure summary. Risk: inventory API pagination via Link header may differ between environments; mitigation is configurable --pagination-style flag (link_header or cursor_param) for different API versions.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Bulk update confirmation flow resolved: dry-run preview table showing current vs proposed quantities; interactive y/N prompt; --confirm flag for CI usage (ISS-001 closed). CSV schema resolved: headers sku_id,store_location_id,new_quantity,reason; per-field validation; per-row error reporting to stderr without aborting batch (ISS-002 closed). API key exclusively from env var confirmed; no --api-key flag. Homebrew distribution confirmed via goreleaser automation.

metadata.notes: Round 2 revision for retail inventory operations CLI; fixes bulk update confirmation flow and CSV validation schema gaps from round 1.

project_goal.value: Fullstack web application for store associates and managers at a multi-location sporting goods retailer providing real-time inventory lookup across all stores and inter-store transfer request management with instant push notifications
project_goal.rationale: Replaces phone-and-spreadsheet inter-store stock coordination with a real-time digital workflow

target_users.value: Store associates (inventory lookup and transfer request initiation) and store managers (transfer approval and inventory oversight across locations)
target_users.rationale: Two distinct roles with different permissions identified in user research

project_class.rationale: React frontend with Node.js Fastify backend and WebSocket real-time layer

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, realtime, integrations, admin_panel
capabilities.rationale: Real-time WebSocket for transfer notifications; integrations with existing ERP for inventory sync; admin panel for store and user management

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-store real-time inventory state with WebSocket fan-out, stock reservation on transfer approval, and ERP sync integration

risk_level.rationale: Internal store operations data; transfer approval errors could cause inventory discrepancies across locations

data_sensitivity.rationale: Inventory positions and transfer records are internal operational data

external_exposure.rationale: Login required; accessible to store staff on corporate network or VPN

access_model.value: Cognito with associate and manager roles; managers can approve transfers and access cross-store admin panel; associates limited to their assigned store
access_model.rationale: Role separation prevents associates from approving their own transfer requests or viewing financials of other stores

feature_scope.value: Barcode-triggered inventory lookup across all store locations with distance sorting, inter-store transfer request submission, manager approval/rejection with stock reservation, real-time WebSocket push notifications for transfer events, admin panel for store location management and associate provisioning
feature_scope.rationale: Complete transfer workflow from lookup to approval to stock reservation

mvp_scope.value: Barcode inventory lookup, transfer request creation, manager approval with stock reservation, real-time notifications; admin panel and ERP sync in Phase 2
mvp_scope.rationale: Core transfer workflow is the primary pain point; ERP sync can use manual import initially

security_baseline.value: Cognito JWT for all endpoints, TLS 1.3, RBAC enforced at API layer, no cross-store data access for associate role
security_baseline.rationale: Prevents associates from querying inventory at stores outside their assignment or approving their own transfers

frontend_stack.rationale: Next.js App Router for SSR on lookup pages; TypeScript for type safety across barcode scan and transfer form components

backend_stack.value: Node.js 22 with Fastify
backend_stack.rationale: Fastify high-throughput for sub-500ms inventory lookup; native WebSocket plugin for transfer notifications

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for inventory positions and transfer records; Redis 7 for WebSocket session registry and transfer reservation locks
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL transactions for atomic stock reservation on transfer approval; Redis for fast WebSocket client registry lookups

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate in us-east-1; PostgreSQL on RDS Multi-AZ; Redis on ElastiCache
hosting_target.rationale: Managed container platform with Multi-AZ for retail business continuity

privacy_retention_policy.value: Transfer records retained 3 years for audit; inventory snapshot history retained 90 days; associate session logs retained 30 days
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Retail operations audit trail requirements; session logs are operational only

future_scope.value: ERP bi-directional inventory sync, predictive transfer recommendations via ML, mobile companion app for floor associates
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 and 3 roadmap items

constraints.value: Inventory lookup must complete in under 500ms; must support 200 concurrent WebSocket connections across all store locations; AWS us-east-1 only
constraints.rationale: 500ms SLA from barcode scan to stock display; 200 concurrent connections covers all store staff across 30 locations

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM with trace IDs on all inventory lookup and transfer approval requests; structured JSON logging; PagerDuty alert on inventory lookup p99 > 400ms
observability_baseline.rationale: Lookup latency SLA requires active monitoring; Datadog is corporate standard

execution_preference.value: 2-week sprints; blue-green deployments on ECS
execution_preference.rationale: Blue-green ensures zero-downtime for store operations

project.goal: Real-time inter-store inventory lookup and transfer request management for sporting goods retail
project.target_users: Store associates and managers across 30 locations

frontend.state: Zustand for local transfer state, SWR for inventory queries
frontend.approach: Barcode scan input triggers instant API lookup; WebSocket for real-time transfer notifications

backend.api: REST for inventory CRUD + WebSocket for real-time transfer events

security.rbac: Associate scoped to home store; manager sees all stores

data.cache: Redis 7 for WebSocket registry and reservation locks

reasoner_reviews.product: The core workflow -- barcode scan to cross-store stock display to transfer request to manager approval -- directly eliminates the phone-based coordination that burns associate time. The 500ms lookup SLA is critical; customers are waiting while the associate checks. Manager approval with automatic stock reservation is the key workflow improvement over manual tracking. ERP sync can safely defer to Phase 2 given manual import works for launch.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Fastify with the @fastify/websocket plugin is a solid choice for combining REST and WebSocket on one server. PostgreSQL row-level locking on the inventory_positions table is the right mechanism for atomic stock reservation on transfer approval -- it prevents two managers from approving conflicting transfers for the same SKU. Redis is correctly used for the WebSocket session registry to support scaling to multiple Fargate tasks. The multi-column index on inventory_positions(sku_id, store_id) is essential for the sub-500ms lookup SLA.
reasoner_reviews.security: Cognito RBAC is well-suited here. The critical enforcement point is the backend: GET /inventory?store_id=X must verify the requesting associate's home_store_id matches X (or that the user has manager role). Transfer approval endpoint must similarly verify manager role from JWT claims. Never rely on frontend-only role gating for data access.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 200 concurrent WebSocket connections is well within a single Fargate task capacity; Redis Pub/Sub fan-out allows multiple Fargate tasks to serve the same store group. The 500ms SLA for inventory lookup requires the multi-column index on inventory_positions. PostgreSQL RDS Multi-AZ read latency is acceptable at this scale.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 1 plan had two gaps: the stock reservation mechanism on transfer approval was described as 'locking' without specifying whether this was a PostgreSQL row lock or a Redis lock, leading to ambiguity about behavior under concurrent approvals. Additionally, the WebSocket fan-out to store-specific channels was not described -- it was unclear which connected clients receive the transfer notification when a manager approves a request.

frontend.app_structure: Next.js 14 App Router: /lookup (barcode scan input, cross-store availability grid), /transfers (associate's open transfer requests), /transfers/new (create request from lookup result), /manager/approvals (pending transfer queue for managers), /manager/admin (store and associate management). Zustand for transfer form state; SWR for inventory position polling.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Barcode Scan Flow: associate scans barcode on /lookup page; SKU sent to GET /api/inventory/:sku; availability grid renders sorted by distance from associate's home store. Transfer Request Flow: associate selects source store and submits transfer request form; POST /api/transfers; confirmation toast with transfer ID. Manager Approval Flow: manager sees pending requests on /manager/approvals; approves with PATCH /api/transfers/:id/approve; WebSocket emits transfer_approved event to source store staff.
frontend.state_management: Zustand transferStore: pending transfer requests for the current associate, transfer form state (source_store_id, sku_id, quantity, notes). SWR for inventory position data with 30s revalidation as fallback to WebSocket updates. useWebSocket hook manages Cognito JWT-authenticated WebSocket connection and routes transfer events to transferStore.

backend.service_design: Fastify app with REST routes and WebSocket upgrade on /ws path. Modules: inventory (lookup, update), transfers (create, approve, reject, list), stores (location data), auth (Cognito JWT validation middleware). Background task: ERP sync job deferred to Phase 2.
backend.api_patterns: REST: GET /api/inventory/:sku?lat=:lat&lng=:lng (returns store positions sorted by distance), POST /api/transfers (create request), PATCH /api/transfers/:id/approve (manager approves, reserves stock), PATCH /api/transfers/:id/reject. WebSocket /ws: authenticated with Cognito JWT in connection handshake; server pushes {type:transfer_requested, transfer_id, sku_id, source_store_id, quantity} to managers of source store; {type:transfer_approved, transfer_id} to requesting associate.
backend.scaling_notes: Redis Pub/Sub channel per store_id for WebSocket fan-out across multiple Fargate tasks. Each Fastify instance subscribes to channels for stores it has active WebSocket clients for. Inventory lookup uses multi-column index on inventory_positions(sku_id, store_id); query returns all stores in one SELECT with distance calculation in application layer.

security.auth_design: Cognito user pool with associate and manager user groups. JWT contains store_id claim (associate's home store) and role claim. Fastify middleware validates JWT on every request and attaches user context (user_id, role, store_id) to request.
security.authorization_model: Associate role: GET /inventory/:sku (any store, read-only), POST /transfers (only if requesting from a store other than home_store_id), GET /transfers (own transfers only). Manager role: all associate permissions plus PATCH /transfers/:id/approve|reject, GET /manager/transfers (all pending transfers for managed stores), admin panel routes. Role check: middleware enforces role from JWT claims; never trusts client-supplied role.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Transfer lifecycle events (created, approved, rejected, stock_reserved) logged to transfer_audit_log table with user_id, transfer_id, action, timestamp, and client IP. Inventory position changes logged to inventory_change_log. Datadog structured logs include trace_id, user_id, store_id, and role on all requests.

data.entities: store_locations (id UUID PK, name, address, lat DECIMAL, lng DECIMAL, region, active BOOL), inventory_positions (id UUID PK, sku_id, store_id FK, quantity_on_hand INT, quantity_reserved INT, quantity_available GENERATED, last_updated -- multi-column index on sku_id, store_id), transfer_requests (id UUID PK, sku_id, source_store_id FK, destination_store_id FK, requested_by_user_id, quantity, status ENUM pending/approved/rejected/completed/cancelled, approved_by_user_id, requested_at, resolved_at), transfer_audit_log (id UUID PK, transfer_id FK, user_id, action, detail, ip_address, ts), associate_sessions (user_id, store_id, connected_at, last_seen -- Redis HSET per connection, keyed by WebSocket connection ID)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ: all relational data. Row-level lock on inventory_positions row during transfer approval (SELECT ... FOR UPDATE NOWAIT to fail fast on concurrent approval). Redis 7 ElastiCache: associate_sessions hash (WebSocket connection registry per store channel), Pub/Sub channels store:{store_id} for cross-task WebSocket fan-out.
data.consistency_notes: Transfer approval is a PostgreSQL transaction: BEGIN; SELECT inventory_positions WHERE sku_id=:sku AND store_id=:source FOR UPDATE NOWAIT; verify quantity_available >= requested_quantity; UPDATE inventory_positions SET quantity_reserved += quantity; INSERT transfer_audit_log; UPDATE transfer_requests SET status=approved; COMMIT. Concurrent approval attempt receives LockNotAvailable error and returns 409 Conflict to second manager.

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate cluster in us-east-1 with two task definitions: API+WebSocket server (Fastify, 1 vCPU 2GB RAM, 2-6 tasks), admin tasks (migrations, one-off jobs). ALB with sticky sessions disabled (Redis handles WebSocket affinity via Pub/Sub). RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ with read replica for reporting queries. ElastiCache Redis single-node (upgrade to cluster on task scaling beyond 6).
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: ESLint + tsc --noEmit, Jest unit tests (80% coverage gate), integration tests against Dockerized Postgres + Redis, Docker build, push to ECR, ECS blue-green deployment via aws ecs update-service on main branch merge. Migrations: TypeScript migration runner via one-off ECS task before deploy.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM sidecar per ECS task; custom metrics: inventory_lookup_duration_ms (histogram), transfer_request_rate (counter), concurrent_websocket_connections (gauge), stock_reservation_conflict_total (counter). PagerDuty: inventory lookup p99 > 400ms, WebSocket connection count drop > 30% in 5 minutes.

ISS-001.title: Stock reservation mechanism ambiguous (Postgres lock vs Redis lock)
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan described transfer approval as 'locking inventory' without specifying the locking mechanism. Ambiguity between PostgreSQL row lock and Redis distributed lock leaves concurrent approval behavior undefined.

history.note: Architect reviewer flagged in round 1 -- transfer approval section did not specify PostgreSQL FOR UPDATE vs Redis SETNX

ISS-002.title: WebSocket fan-out to store channels not described
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan stated WebSocket pushes transfer notifications but did not specify which connected clients receive each event type, how Redis Pub/Sub is used for multi-task fan-out, or how store-channel routing works.

history.note: Realtime section in round 1 was thin on channel routing and multi-task fan-out

focus_issues.title: Stock reservation mechanism ambiguous
focus_issues.detail: Transfer approval locking mechanism not specified -- PostgreSQL FOR UPDATE vs Redis lock behavior under concurrent approvals undefined.

focus_issues.title: WebSocket fan-out to store channels not described
focus_issues.detail: Which clients receive each event type and how Redis Pub/Sub fans out across Fargate tasks not specified.

latest_recommendations: Specify PostgreSQL SELECT ... FOR UPDATE NOWAIT as the reservation mechanism; describe the 409 Conflict response on lock contention for concurrent manager approvals
Define Redis Pub/Sub channel-per-store routing; specify which event types go to source store channel vs destination store channel vs requesting associate's user channel
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: Sporting Goods Inter-Store Inventory Transfer App -- Round 1

convergence_state.reason: High severity stock reservation ambiguity and medium severity WebSocket routing gap block production confidence

previous_audits.summary: Solid fullstack foundation with correct technology choices but two gaps in the realtime and data consistency sections that need resolution before the transfer approval flow can be implemented safely.
strengths: Fastify WebSocket + PostgreSQL combination is well-suited for this domain
Cognito RBAC with store_id claim is the right associate scoping approach
Datadog APM setup with inventory lookup latency monitoring is appropriate
concerns: Stock reservation uses undefined locking primitive -- PostgreSQL FOR UPDATE vs Redis SETNX behavior differs significantly under concurrent approvals
WebSocket fan-out routing not specified -- unclear if source store, destination store, and requesting associate all receive different event types
blocking_issues: Concurrent manager approval of the same transfer could double-decrement inventory without a clearly specified lock type
WebSocket routing gaps mean notification delivery to wrong client groups cannot be detected until integration testing
recommendations: Use PostgreSQL SELECT...FOR UPDATE NOWAIT; return 409 Conflict to second approver on lock contention; document the transaction boundary
Define Redis Pub/Sub channels: store:{store_id} for managers of source/destination stores; user:{user_id} for direct associate notification

previous_plan.title: Sporting Goods Inter-Store Inventory Transfer App -- Round 1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Next.js 14 frontend with Fastify backend providing real-time inventory lookup and inter-store transfer request management. WebSocket for transfer notifications. PostgreSQL for inventory and transfer data. Cognito auth.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: ECS Fargate running Fastify API + WebSocket server. PostgreSQL RDS Multi-AZ. Redis ElastiCache for WebSocket session registry. Cognito JWT auth with associate and manager roles.
previous_plan.data_model: store_locations, inventory_positions (indexed on sku_id, store_id), transfer_requests with status ENUM. Stock reservation on transfer approval via unspecified locking mechanism.
previous_plan.workflows: Associate scans barcode; lookup returns stock across stores sorted by distance. Transfer request submitted; manager approves triggering stock reservation. WebSocket notifies relevant parties -- channel routing not specified.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 resolves two blocking issues for this advanced multi-location retail transfer system: (1) stock reservation -- PostgreSQL SELECT...FOR UPDATE NOWAIT is the correct mechanism; it keeps the reservation atomic with the approval status update in a single transaction and returns a clear 409 Conflict to concurrent approvers; Redis is not needed for this lock; (2) WebSocket fan-out routing -- define store:{store_id} Pub/Sub channels for manager notifications and user:{user_id} channels for associate direct messages; Redis Pub/Sub enables cross-Fargate-task delivery. Plan quality is strong with detailed entity definitions, endpoint examples, and deployment topology.

fix_report.action_taken: Specified PostgreSQL SELECT...FOR UPDATE NOWAIT as the reservation mechanism in data_model consistency_notes and transfer approval workflow. Transaction boundary: BEGIN; SELECT inventory_positions FOR UPDATE NOWAIT; verify quantity_available >= requested; UPDATE quantity_reserved; INSERT audit log; UPDATE transfer_requests status; COMMIT. Concurrent approval returns 409 Conflict. Updated api_design and workflows sections.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Concurrent manager approval behavior is deterministic; double-reservation impossible; ISS-001 resolved.

fix_report.action_taken: Defined Redis Pub/Sub channel structure: store:{store_id} channels for manager notifications (transfer_requested events published to source store channel); user:{user_id} channels for associate direct notification (transfer_approved/rejected events). Updated system_components, workflows, and deployment_and_operations to describe Fastify instance subscription to relevant store channels on WebSocket client connect.
fix_report.expected_outcome: WebSocket event delivery to correct client groups is fully specified; ISS-002 resolved.

target_output.title: Sporting Goods Multi-Store Inventory Transfer Platform
target_output.executive_summary: A Next.js 14 and Fastify fullstack application enabling store associates at a 30-location sporting goods retailer to scan product barcodes, view live stock availability across all stores sorted by proximity, and initiate inter-store transfer requests. Managers receive real-time WebSocket notifications on pending transfers and approve or reject them; approval atomically reserves stock via a PostgreSQL transaction. Redis Pub/Sub routes transfer events to the correct store and user channels across multiple Fargate tasks. Deployed on AWS ECS Fargate in us-east-1 with Cognito RBAC and Datadog APM monitoring the sub-500ms inventory lookup SLA.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-tier architecture: Next.js 14 App Router frontend on CloudFront; Fastify REST + WebSocket API on ECS Fargate behind an ALB; PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ for inventory and transfer data with Redis 7 ElastiCache for WebSocket session registry and Pub/Sub fan-out. Inventory lookup follows the path: associate barcode scan triggers GET /api/inventory/:sku; Fastify queries inventory_positions with a multi-column index on (sku_id, store_id) returning all store positions; application layer sorts by geo-distance and returns in under 500ms. Transfer approval is a PostgreSQL transaction using SELECT...FOR UPDATE NOWAIT: it atomically verifies available quantity, increments quantity_reserved, updates transfer status to approved, and inserts an audit row. Redis Pub/Sub channels (store:{store_id} and user:{user_id}) fan out transfer lifecycle events to manager and associate WebSocket clients across all Fargate tasks.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Next.js 14 App Router with TypeScript, Zustand, SWR, Tailwind CSS. Backend: Fastify 4 on Node.js 22 with @fastify/websocket plugin, @fastify/jwt for Cognito token validation. Database: RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ with read replica. Cache and Pub/Sub: ElastiCache Redis 7. Auth: AWS Cognito user pool with associate and manager groups. Infra: ECS Fargate, ALB, CloudFront, ECR. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with blue-green ECS deployment. Observability: Datadog APM, PagerDuty.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Barcode Inventory Lookup: associate scans barcode, app calls GET /api/inventory/:sku returning quantity_on_hand and quantity_available per store sorted by geo-distance; response SLA < 500ms. Transfer Request Creation: associate selects source store and submits quantity; POST /api/transfers creates pending request and publishes to source store manager channel. Manager Approval Queue: /manager/approvals shows pending transfer_requests for managed stores; PATCH /api/transfers/:id/approve triggers PostgreSQL reservation transaction. Real-Time Notifications: WebSocket pushes transfer_requested to source store managers, transfer_approved to requesting associate. Admin Panel: store location management (add/edit locations, update lat/lng), associate provisioning (create Cognito users, assign store_id and role).
target_output.system_components: CloudFront distribution (Next.js static assets + SSR edge caching), ALB (routes /api/* and /ws to Fastify tasks), ECS Fargate tasks (Fastify API + WebSocket server, 2-6 tasks, Datadog APM sidecar), RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ (inventory, transfers, audit logs), RDS read replica (admin panel reporting queries), ElastiCache Redis 7 (associate_sessions HSET per WebSocket connection, store:{store_id} Pub/Sub channels for manager notifications, user:{user_id} Pub/Sub channels for associate direct messages), Cognito user pool (associate and manager groups, store_id and role custom attributes), ECR (Docker image registry), GitHub Actions (CI/CD pipeline).
target_output.workflows: Barcode Lookup: associate scans barcode on /lookup; Next.js calls GET /api/inventory/:sku?lat=40.7&lng=-74.0; Fastify validates Cognito JWT, queries SELECT * FROM inventory_positions WHERE sku_id=:sku with index scan (p99 < 150ms for 30 stores), returns stores sorted by haversine distance, renders availability grid in under 500ms total. Transfer Request: associate submits POST /api/transfers {sku_id, source_store_id, quantity, notes}; Fastify validates associate role, inserts transfer_requests row (status=pending), publishes {type:transfer_requested, transfer_id, sku_id, quantity} to Redis channel store:{source_store_id}; all Fastify instances subscribed to that channel push the event to connected manager WebSocket clients. Transfer Approval (atomic): manager submits PATCH /api/transfers/:id/approve; Fastify opens PostgreSQL transaction: SELECT id, quantity_available FROM inventory_positions WHERE sku_id=:sku AND store_id=:source FOR UPDATE NOWAIT; if quantity_available < requested returns 409 Conflict; else UPDATE quantity_reserved += quantity, INSERT transfer_audit_log, UPDATE transfer_requests SET status=approved, COMMIT; publishes {type:transfer_approved, transfer_id} to Redis channel user:{requesting_associate_id}; WebSocket delivers notification to associate's browser.
target_output.data_model: store_locations (id UUID PK, name VARCHAR, address, lat DECIMAL(9,6), lng DECIMAL(9,6), region, active BOOL, created_at). inventory_positions (id UUID PK, sku_id VARCHAR NOT NULL, store_id UUID FK references store_locations, quantity_on_hand INT DEFAULT 0, quantity_reserved INT DEFAULT 0, quantity_available INT GENERATED ALWAYS AS (quantity_on_hand - quantity_reserved) STORED, last_updated TIMESTAMPTZ -- UNIQUE INDEX (sku_id, store_id) for lookup performance). transfer_requests (id UUID PK, sku_id, source_store_id FK, destination_store_id FK, requesting_user_id, quantity INT, notes TEXT, status ENUM pending/approved/rejected/completed/cancelled, approved_by_user_id, requested_at TIMESTAMPTZ, resolved_at TIMESTAMPTZ). transfer_audit_log (id UUID PK, transfer_id FK, user_id, action, detail JSON, ip_address, ts TIMESTAMPTZ -- retained 3 years). associate_sessions (Redis HSET key: session:{connection_id}, fields: user_id, store_id, role, connected_at -- TTL 8h; store membership tracked in Redis SET members:{store_id}).
target_output.api_design: GET /api/inventory/:sku?lat=:lat&lng=:lng -- returns [{store_id, store_name, distance_km, quantity_on_hand, quantity_available}] sorted by distance; requires JWT. GET /api/inventory/:sku/store/:store_id -- single store position detail. POST /api/transfers -- {sku_id, source_store_id, destination_store_id, quantity, notes}; associate role required; returns transfer_id. GET /api/transfers?status=pending -- associate's own transfers; manager sees all for managed stores. PATCH /api/transfers/:id/approve -- manager role required; returns 200 or 409 Conflict on concurrent approval. PATCH /api/transfers/:id/reject -- manager role required with reason. Admin: GET/POST/PATCH /api/admin/stores, GET/POST /api/admin/associates (manager role). WebSocket /ws: handshake includes Cognito JWT in Authorization header; server registers connection in Redis session hash and subscribes to store:{store_id} (managers) or user:{user_id} (associates) channels.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Cognito JWT validated by Fastify @fastify/jwt plugin on every request; JWT contains sub (user_id), custom:store_id, custom:role claims. RBAC enforced at route level: associate routes reject if role != associate or if store_id mismatch; manager routes reject if role != manager. Cross-store data: GET /inventory/:sku returns all stores (read-only acceptable); transfer approval restricted to managers of source store. WebSocket auth: JWT required in handshake Authorization header; unauthenticated upgrade attempts rejected with 401. No plaintext credentials; HTTPS/WSS TLS 1.3. Transfer audit log written within approval transaction to ensure audit completeness. Datadog logs include user_id, store_id, and role for every request; no inventory quantities in debug logs to prevent accidental data exposure.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate cluster in us-east-1a/1b: API task definition (Fastify + Datadog sidecar, 1 vCPU 2GB RAM, min 2 max 6 tasks, scale on CPU 60% and concurrent_websocket_connections > 150). ALB with health check on GET /health. RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ (db.t3.medium, automated backups, 7-day retention). Read replica in us-east-1b for admin reporting queries. ElastiCache Redis 7 (cache.t3.micro, single-node, upgrade to cluster.t3.small when tasks > 4). CloudFront for Next.js static assets with 1-year cache on hashed assets. GitHub Actions CI: ESLint, tsc --noEmit, Jest (80% coverage), integration tests against Docker Postgres + Redis, Docker build, push to ECR, ECS blue-green deployment via CodeDeploy on main branch merge. Migrations: TypeScript migration runner as ECS one-off task before blue-green cutover. Rollback: CodeDeploy automatic rollback on health check failure.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM traces on all Fastify routes; custom spans on PostgreSQL FOR UPDATE transactions and Redis Pub/Sub publish calls. Metrics emitted: inventory_lookup_duration_ms (histogram with sku_id tag), transfer_approval_duration_ms, stock_reservation_conflicts_total (counter for 409s), active_websocket_connections (gauge per task). Dashboards: live transfer request rate, inventory lookup p50/p95/p99 latency, concurrent WebSocket connections, stock reservation conflict rate. PagerDuty pages on: inventory_lookup p99 > 400ms, WebSocket connection count drop > 30% in 5 minutes, ECS task health check failure.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: ECS Fargate 2 tasks baseline: ~$120/month. RDS PostgreSQL db.t3.medium Multi-AZ: ~$180/month. ElastiCache Redis cache.t3.micro: ~$25/month. CloudFront and ALB: ~$30/month. Total ~$355/month at steady state for 30 stores. Scaling path: Fargate tasks autoscale to 6 at peak (lunch + after-school sporting events); Redis upgrade to cluster mode when task count exceeds 4 to ensure Pub/Sub throughput. RDS read replica is optional initially; enable when admin panel queries exceed 10 concurrent sessions.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): Cognito auth, barcode inventory lookup with geo-sort, transfer request creation, PostgreSQL atomic approval with FOR UPDATE NOWAIT (ISS-001 fix), WebSocket notifications with Redis Pub/Sub store/user channel routing (ISS-002 fix), manager approval queue, ECS Fargate deployment, Datadog APM. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Admin panel (store management, associate provisioning), ERP inventory sync integration via nightly batch import, transfer history and reporting. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Predictive transfer recommendations from historical transfer patterns, mobile PWA companion for floor associates, automated reorder triggers.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode on both Next.js and Fastify codebases. All database queries via Drizzle ORM (typed) with no raw SQL except the FOR UPDATE NOWAIT reservation transaction which is written as a parameterized pg query and unit tested with a mock pg client. Fastify route schemas (JSON Schema) enforced for all request/response shapes. Jest unit tests for transfer approval transaction logic, WebSocket channel routing, and geo-distance sort. Integration tests spin up Postgres + Redis via Docker Compose. No JWT claims trusted from the request body; always from verified token only. Feature flags via environment variables for Phase 2 ERP sync enabling.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: PostgreSQL FOR UPDATE NOWAIT returns 409 Conflict to the second manager attempting concurrent approval of the same transfer. This is intentional and correct behavior; the second manager's UI must display a clear error message. Tradeoff: ElastiCache Redis single-node is a single point of failure for WebSocket fan-out. Mitigation: if Redis is unavailable, WebSocket events degrade gracefully -- associates can still use polling via SWR fallback; transfer state is always authoritative in PostgreSQL. Risk: ERP sync deferred to Phase 2 means inventory data may drift from ERP during Phase 1; mitigation is daily manual CSV import of stock counts until automated sync launches.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Stock reservation mechanism resolved: PostgreSQL SELECT...FOR UPDATE NOWAIT with 409 Conflict on contention; Redis not used for this lock (ISS-001 closed). WebSocket fan-out routing resolved: Redis Pub/Sub channels store:{store_id} for manager notifications and user:{user_id} for associate direct messages; Fastify instances subscribe per connected client (ISS-002 closed). ERP sync deferred to Phase 2 confirmed. CloudFront for Next.js static assets confirmed to reduce Fargate egress cost.

metadata.notes: Round 2 revision for sporting goods multi-store inventory transfer platform; resolves stock reservation lock ambiguity and WebSocket channel routing gaps.

profile.domain: DTC Home Goods Order Management & Fulfillment

project_goal.value: Decompose DTC order processing, warehouse picking, carrier shipping, and returns into independently deployable microservices for a home goods brand
project_goal.rationale: Monolith cannot scale fulfillment and returns independently during peak periods

target_users.value: Warehouse staff, shipping coordinators, returns agents, and brand admins
target_users.rationale: Internal operational users across fulfillment functions

project_class.rationale: Requires React operations UI plus multiple Spring Boot microservices

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, integrations, payments, analytics, batch_jobs, admin_panel
capabilities.rationale: Full operational platform with payment capture and analytics

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-service event-driven architecture with peak-load requirements

risk_level.rationale: Handles payment capture and EU customer data under PCI-DSS and GDPR

data_sensitivity.rationale: Payment captures require PCI-DSS Level 1 controls

external_exposure.rationale: All service endpoints require Keycloak JWT; no anonymous access

access_model.value: RBAC roles: warehouse_picker, shipping_coordinator, returns_agent, brand_admin; Keycloak token enforced per service
access_model.rationale: Each service gate-checks claims to prevent cross-function data exposure

feature_scope.value: Order ingestion, fulfillment task assignment, warehouse pick workflow, carrier label generation, shipment tracking, returns initiation, refund processing, admin reporting dashboard
feature_scope.rationale: End-to-end DTC fulfillment lifecycle

mvp_scope.value: Order service, fulfillment service with pick workflow, shipping service with carrier label, returns service with refund event; admin dashboard read-only view
mvp_scope.rationale: Core fulfillment loop needed before Q4 peak

security_baseline.value: TLS 1.3 between all services, Keycloak JWT local JWKS validation, card data tokenized via Stripe never stored, Kubernetes NetworkPolicy isolating service namespaces
security_baseline.rationale: PCI-DSS scoping requires card data isolation and service network segmentation

frontend_stack.rationale: Team expertise; Vite for fast build cycles

backend_stack.value: Java 21 with Spring Boot per service, Spring Security for JWT validation
backend_stack.rationale: Existing Java expertise; Spring Boot standard for microservices at this org

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 per-service schema, Redis 7 for transient state, Kafka for inter-service events
data_platform.rationale: Schema-per-service isolates data ownership; Kafka decouples services

hosting_target.value: AWS EKS Kubernetes with Jenkins CI/CD
hosting_target.rationale: Kubernetes required for independent service scaling and rolling deploys

privacy_retention_policy.value: EU customer PII retained 3 years post-last-order, deleted on GDPR erasure request; order records 7 years for tax compliance
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GDPR and tax record requirements

future_scope.value: Supplier portal for inbound ASN integration, ML-based pick path optimization, customer self-service returns portal
future_scope.rationale: Year 2 roadmap

constraints.value: Must process 10K orders/hour at peak; each service must deploy independently without downtime; Jenkins pipeline per service
constraints.rationale: Q4 peak volume and zero-downtime deployment requirements

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM with distributed tracing across services, structured JSON logs with correlation IDs, PagerDuty for SLO breaches
observability_baseline.rationale: Distributed tracing essential for cross-service debugging

execution_preference.value: Two-week sprints; each service has its own Jenkins pipeline and release cadence
execution_preference.rationale: Independent deployability requires per-service CI/CD

compliance_context.value: PCI-DSS Level 1 for payment capture flows; GDPR for EU customer data; Stripe as PCI-compliant tokenization provider
compliance_context.rationale: Payment card data and EU personal data require formal compliance controls

project.goal: Event-driven microservices platform for DTC order-to-delivery and returns lifecycle
project.target_users: Warehouse pickers, shipping coordinators, returns agents, brand admins

security.card_data: Stripe tokenization, no raw PAN storage

data.cache: Redis 7 for idempotency keys and session state

open_questions.carrier_api: FedEx or UPS primary carrier TBD

reasoner_reviews.product: The event-driven decomposition matches the goal of independent scaling for fulfillment and returns. Pick workflow and carrier label generation are correctly modeled as separate service responsibilities. Admin dashboard scoped as read-only for MVP is appropriate given delivery timeline.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Schema-per-service is the right isolation model. Kafka topic naming strategy and consumer group isolation need explicit definition to prevent cross-service event leakage. The previous plan lacked a saga pattern for the order-to-fulfillment-to-shipment chain, creating distributed transaction risk.
reasoner_reviews.security: PCI-DSS scoping must ensure Kafka payment topics use field-level encryption and the payments service namespace has a strict NetworkPolicy allowing only egress to Stripe. Previous plan did not specify token introspection vs. local JWKS validation per service.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 10K orders/hour translates to roughly 2.8 orders/second sustained, with 5x spikes during flash sales. Fulfillment service is the likely bottleneck and needs KEDA autoscaling on Kafka consumer lag, not CPU.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Previous plan described inter-service communication as event-based without specifying the saga orchestration pattern or compensating transaction strategy for failed shipments. This gap must be closed before peak season.

backend.service_design: Four bounded-context services: order-service (order ingestion, status FSM, saga state log), fulfillment-service (task assignment, pick workflow), shipping-service (carrier label, tracking), returns-service (eligibility check, refund event emission). Each Spring Boot service owns its PostgreSQL schema and Kafka consumer group.
backend.api_patterns: Each service exposes REST endpoints for synchronous queries and emits/consumes Kafka events for state transitions. Choreography saga: order-service emits order.placed, fulfillment-service emits fulfillment.pick-completed, shipping-service emits shipping.label-generated, returns-service emits returns.refund-initiated.
backend.business_modules: Order FSM: pending -> allocated -> picking -> shipped -> delivered. Fulfillment assigns pick tasks round-robin to available pickers. Shipping service integrates with FedEx/UPS via carrier adapter pattern. Returns service validates 30-day return window and condition code.
backend.failure_handling: Kafka consumer idempotency via Redis idempotency keys (TTL 24h); outbox pattern in each service guarantees event publication after DB commit; compensating transactions on saga failures: inventory re-allocation if label generation fails after 3 retries.
backend.scaling_notes: Fulfillment service scales on Kafka consumer lag metric via KEDA. Shipping service scales on pending label queue depth. Order service scales on HTTP request rate via HPA.
backend.backend_risks: Saga choreography complexity may produce hard-to-diagnose stuck orders; saga_state JSONB column in order-service mitigates this. Carrier API rate limits during peak may delay label generation.

frontend.app_structure: Single React SPA with role-scoped lazy loading: WarehouseModule (pick tasks), ShippingModule (label management), ReturnsModule (returns processing), AdminModule (metrics dashboard). Each module loads only for users with matching Keycloak role claims.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Picker: Task Queue -> Pick Task Detail -> Scan Confirm -> Complete. Shipping: Label Queue -> Generate Label -> Print -> Mark Shipped. Returns: Initiate Return -> Validate Eligibility -> Issue Label -> Confirm Receipt. Admin: Order Volume Chart, Fulfillment SLA P95, Returns Rate Trend.
frontend.state_management: React Query for service API calls with 30s stale time. Zustand for picker session state (current task, scan buffer). WebSocket subscription in ShippingModule for real-time label generation status.
frontend.frontend_risks: Role-scoped module loading requires Keycloak role claims to be stable across token refreshes. Recommend E2E Cypress tests covering each role's critical path.

security.auth_design: Keycloak 24 with per-service realm clients; services validate JWT locally using JWKS endpoint with 5-minute key cache. No token introspection at request time to eliminate Keycloak as a synchronous bottleneck.
security.authorization_model: Claims-based RBAC: warehouse_picker can only access fulfillment-service pick endpoints; shipping_coordinator has shipping-service write access; returns_agent scoped to returns-service; brand_admin has read access across all services.
security.secrets_management: AWS Secrets Manager for service credentials; Kubernetes ExternalSecrets operator syncing to pod environment at deploy time. Kafka mTLS certificates rotated quarterly.
security.security_risks: Kafka topic ACL misconfiguration could expose payment events to non-payments services. Stripe webhook signature verification must be enforced in payments-service to prevent spoofed refund events.

data.entities: sales_orders (id UUID PK, customer_id, channel ENUM web/wholesale, status ENUM pending/allocated/picking/shipped/delivered, total_amount DECIMAL, currency CHAR3, eu_resident BOOL, created_at, saga_state JSONB). fulfillment_tasks (id UUID PK, order_id FK, picker_id, status ENUM pending/in_progress/completed/abandoned, assigned_at, completed_at, timeout_at). picking_assignments (id UUID PK, task_id FK, sku VARCHAR, bin_location, quantity INT, scanned_at). shipment_manifests (id UUID PK, order_id FK, carrier ENUM fedex/ups, tracking_number, label_url, shipped_at, label_attempts INT). returns_cases (id UUID PK, order_id FK, reason_code ENUM, status ENUM initiated/label-issued/received/refunded/closed, refund_amount DECIMAL, received_at). payment_captures (id UUID PK, order_id FK, stripe_payment_intent_id VARCHAR encrypted, amount DECIMAL encrypted, captured_at). carrier_labels (id UUID PK, shipment_id FK, format ENUM zpl/pdf, raw_label_base64 TEXT, generated_at).
data.storage_design: Each service has its own PostgreSQL 16 schema with no cross-schema joins. Redis 7 stores idempotency keys (TTL 24h) and pick session state. Kafka topics: order.placed, fulfillment.pick-completed, shipping.label-generated, shipping.label-failed, returns.refund-initiated, payments.capture-completed.
data.retention_and_deletion: EU customer PII purged 3 years post-last-order via scheduled deletion job with GDPR erasure audit trail. Order financial records archived to S3 Parquet after 2 years, retained 7 years total.
data.data_risks: Cross-service joins not possible without API composition; reporting queries must use a read model. Kafka topic retention must exceed service restart windows to prevent message loss.

devops.deployment_topology: EKS cluster with dedicated node groups: high-memory (c6i.xlarge, min 3 max 12) for order-service and fulfillment-service; standard (c6i.large, min 2 max 8) for shipping, returns, payments. AWS ALB Ingress Controller routing to service NodePorts.
devops.ci_cd_design: Jenkins pipeline per service: Checkstyle + SpotBugs lint -> JUnit 5 unit tests -> TestContainers integration tests (PostgreSQL + Kafka) -> Docker build -> ECR push -> helm upgrade rolling deploy. Payments-service uses Argo Rollouts blue-green for zero-risk PCI transitions.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM Java auto-instrumentation with W3C traceparent propagation across Kafka message headers. Custom dashboards: order-to-label P95 latency (SLO < 90 seconds), pick queue depth, Kafka consumer lag, carrier label retry rate, returns processing P90.
devops.rollback_strategy: Helm revision rollback per service (helm rollback service revision). Kafka consumer group offset rollback playbook for event replay. Flyway undo scripts for schema rollback.
devops.devops_risks: Jenkins per-service pipeline sprawl requires shared Groovy library for common stages. EKS node group autoscaling lag during flash sale spikes; recommend Karpenter with warm node pool.

ISS-002.detail: Previous plan did not specify whether services use local JWKS validation or token introspection; introspection at scale makes Keycloak a synchronous bottleneck.

ISS-003.detail: Topics carrying payment capture events were not isolated with ACLs or field-level encryption in the previous plan, creating PCI-DSS scope expansion risk.
