previous_plan.title: DTC Fulfillment Microservices Platform v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Event-driven microservices decomposition for home goods DTC brand covering order ingestion through carrier shipment and returns processing on AWS EKS with Java Spring Boot services and Kafka event bus.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Four Spring Boot services communicating via Kafka topics on EKS, React SPA for warehouse operations, Keycloak authentication, PostgreSQL schema-per-service.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Keycloak JWT for all services; TLS 1.3; Stripe for payment tokenization; PCI-DSS and GDPR noted but isolation controls not detailed.
previous_plan.workflows: Order placement triggers Kafka event chain; picking and shipping handled by separate service consumers; returns initiate refund events.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 revision targeting ISS-001 (saga pattern) and ISS-003 (PCI Kafka isolation). For ISS-001, the choreography saga needs explicit compensating transactions per failure mode: label generation failure triggers inventory re-allocation in fulfillment-service; pick abandonment triggers order FSM reversion in order-service. For ISS-003, payment topics isolated in a dedicated Kafka namespace with MSK ACLs and field-level AES-256 encryption on sensitive payment fields. ISS-002 addressed by specifying local JWKS validation with 5-minute cache across all services.

fix_report.action_taken: Defined choreography saga with compensating transactions for each failure mode: label-failed triggers fulfillment allocation release and order FSM reversion; pick-abandoned triggers re-assignment or manual intervention flag. Added saga_state JSONB column to sales_orders for auditability.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Stuck orders have automated compensation paths; saga state is auditable and queryable in order-service

fix_report.action_taken: Isolated payment Kafka topics in dedicated MSK namespace with ACLs restricting produce/consume to payments-service service account only. Added field-level AES-256 encryption on stripe_payment_intent_id and amount in payment_captures. Documented PCI scope boundary with NetworkPolicy for payments-service namespace.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Payment event topics excluded from PCI scope expansion; no non-payments service can access card-adjacent data

target_output.title: DTC Fulfillment Microservices Platform v2
target_output.executive_summary: Revised event-driven microservices architecture for a DTC home goods brand decomposing order processing, warehouse picking, carrier shipping, and returns into four independently deployable Spring Boot services on AWS EKS. This revision adds choreography saga compensating transactions for each distributed failure mode and isolates PCI-scoped Kafka payment topics with MSK ACLs and field-level encryption, resolving the two high-severity issues from round 1.
target_output.architecture_overview: Four bounded-context Spring Boot 3 services on AWS EKS communicating via Kafka 3.6: order-service (order FSM, saga_state audit, GDPR erasure), fulfillment-service (pick task assignment, KEDA autoscaling on Kafka consumer lag), shipping-service (FedEx/UPS carrier adapter, label retry logic), and returns-service (eligibility validation, refund event emission). React 18 SPA with role-scoped lazy-loaded modules serves warehouse pickers, shipping coordinators, and returns agents. Keycloak 24 issues JWTs validated locally via JWKS with a 5-minute key cache at each service, eliminating synchronous auth bottleneck. PostgreSQL 16 schema-per-service with Kafka outbox pattern guarantees at-least-once event delivery. MSK payment namespace isolated with ACLs; Stripe tokenizes all card data.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: React 18 TypeScript Vite, React Query, Zustand, React Router v6. Backend: Java 21 Spring Boot 3 (four services), Spring Security JWT JWKS validation, Kafka 3.6 clients with outbox pattern. Data: PostgreSQL 16 schema-per-service, Redis 7 idempotency keys and session state, Apache Kafka 3.6 MSK. Auth: Keycloak 24 local JWKS. Infra: AWS EKS, ALB Ingress Controller, KEDA for consumer-lag autoscaling, Argo Rollouts for payments blue-green, Jenkins per-service pipelines, Datadog APM distributed tracing.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Order Service: REST order ingest from storefront, FSM state management (pending/allocated/picking/shipped/delivered), saga_state JSONB audit log, GDPR erasure endpoint. Fulfillment Service: pick task creation round-robin assignment, bin location routing, scan-confirm workflow, KEDA-based pod scaling on Kafka consumer lag. Shipping Service: carrier adapter pattern (FedEx primary, UPS fallback), label generation with 3-retry policy, tracking event ingestion. Returns Service: 30-day return window validation, condition code eligibility check, prepaid label issuance, receipt scan trigger for refund event. Payments Service: Stripe PaymentIntent capture, webhook signature verification, refund processing, PCI-scoped Kafka consumption. Admin Dashboard: order volume trends, fulfillment SLA P95, returns rate, picker productivity metrics.
target_output.system_components: EKS cluster: high-memory node group (c6i.xlarge, min 3 max 12) for order-service and fulfillment-service; standard (c6i.large, min 2 max 8) for shipping, returns, payments. AWS ALB Ingress with WAF. Kafka MSK cluster with two namespaces: ops (order.placed, fulfillment.pick-completed, shipping.label-generated, shipping.label-failed, returns.refund-initiated) and pci (payments.capture-completed, payments.refund-completed) with strict ACLs. PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ per service schema. Redis 7 ElastiCache for idempotency keys (24h TTL) and picker session. Keycloak 24 on EKS with external PostgreSQL. KEDA operator consuming Kafka consumer lag metrics. Argo Rollouts for payments blue-green. Datadog Agent daemonset with APM auto-instrumentation.
target_output.workflows: Order-to-Shipment Choreography Saga: Storefront POSTs to order-service /v1/orders; order-service persists order with saga_state='order_placed', writes Kafka outbox record, emits order.placed to ops namespace. Fulfillment-service consumes order.placed, allocates inventory, creates pick task, updates saga_state='pick_allocated', emits fulfillment.pick-completed. Shipping-service consumes fulfillment.pick-completed, calls FedEx carrier adapter, updates saga_state='label_generated', emits shipping.label-generated. Order-service consumes shipping.label-generated and transitions FSM to 'shipped'. Compensating Transactions: If FedEx label fails after 3 retries, shipping-service emits shipping.label-failed; fulfillment-service consumes and releases pick task allocation, emits fulfillment.allocation-released; order-service consumes and reverts FSM to 'allocated', sets saga_state='label_failed_compensation', alerts ops via SNS. If pick task abandoned (30-minute timeout), fulfillment-service emits fulfillment.task-abandoned; order-service triggers re-assignment queue or flags for manual intervention. Returns Workflow: Customer POSTs to returns-service /v1/returns; service validates 30-day window and condition code; issues prepaid FedEx label; on warehouse receipt scan PATCH /v1/returns/:id/receipt, emits returns.receipt-confirmed; payments-service consumes, calls Stripe Refund API, emits payments.refund-completed; returns-service marks case closed.
target_output.data_model: sales_orders (id UUID PK, customer_id, channel ENUM web/wholesale, status ENUM pending/allocated/picking/shipped/delivered, total_amount DECIMAL, currency CHAR3, eu_resident BOOL, created_at, saga_state JSONB). fulfillment_tasks (id UUID PK, order_id FK, picker_id, status ENUM pending/in_progress/completed/abandoned, assigned_at, completed_at, timeout_at). picking_assignments (id UUID PK, task_id FK, sku VARCHAR, bin_location, quantity INT, scanned_at). shipment_manifests (id UUID PK, order_id FK, carrier ENUM fedex/ups, tracking_number, label_url, shipped_at, label_attempts INT DEFAULT 0). returns_cases (id UUID PK, order_id FK, reason_code ENUM defective/wrong_item/changed_mind, status ENUM initiated/label-issued/received/refunded/closed, refund_amount DECIMAL, received_at). payment_captures (id UUID PK, order_id FK, stripe_payment_intent_id VARCHAR ENCRYPTED, amount DECIMAL ENCRYPTED AES-256, captured_at). carrier_labels (id UUID PK, shipment_id FK, format ENUM zpl/pdf, raw_label_base64 TEXT, generated_at).
target_output.api_design: Order Service: POST /v1/orders (ingest from storefront), GET /v1/orders/:id (FSM status + saga_state), DELETE /v1/orders/:id/customer-data (GDPR erasure, roles: brand_admin). Fulfillment Service: GET /v1/pick-tasks?status=pending (picker queue), PATCH /v1/pick-tasks/:id/scan (record barcode scan), POST /v1/pick-tasks/:id/complete. Shipping Service: POST /v1/labels (generate for order_id), GET /v1/labels/:id (status + attempts), GET /v1/tracking/:tracking_number. Returns Service: POST /v1/returns (initiate, validates eligibility), PATCH /v1/returns/:id/receipt (mark received), GET /v1/returns/:id. All endpoints require Keycloak JWT Bearer; Spring Security method-level @PreAuthorize checks role claims. Rate limiting: 500 req/min per service client.
target_output.security_and_compliance: PCI-DSS: Stripe tokenization eliminates raw PAN from all services. Kafka MSK PCI namespace (topics: payments.capture-completed, payments.refund-completed) protected with IAM-based ACLs restricting produce/consume to payments-service pod IAM role only. Field-level AES-256 encryption on payment_captures.amount and stripe_payment_intent_id; decryption key in AWS Secrets Manager accessible only to payments-service pod IAM role. EKS NetworkPolicy: payments namespace allows egress only to Stripe CIDR range and MSK PCI endpoint. GDPR: EU customer PII (email, shipping address) encrypted at rest via RDS column-level encryption; GDPR erasure endpoint nulls PII and records erasure event in audit log. Keycloak JWT local JWKS validation; 5-minute cache; token TTL 15 minutes with refresh rotation. AWS WAF on ALB: OWASP Top 10 rules, rate limiting 1000 req/min per IP. All Kafka mTLS inter-broker and client-broker encryption.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: EKS node groups: high-memory (c6i.xlarge, min 3 max 12, Karpenter warm pool of 3 nodes) for order-service and fulfillment-service; standard (c6i.large, min 2 max 8) for shipping, returns, payments. KEDA ScaledObject on fulfillment-service: target Kafka consumer lag < 500 messages. Jenkins pipeline per service: Checkstyle + SpotBugs -> JUnit 5 unit tests (80% coverage gate) -> TestContainers integration tests (PostgreSQL + Kafka) -> Docker build -> ECR push -> helm upgrade rolling deploy. Payments-service: Argo Rollouts canary 10%/50%/100% over 15 minutes with automated rollback on error rate >1%. Flyway schema migrations as Kubernetes Job pre-deploy per service. Environments: dev (single AZ, shared MSK dev cluster), staging (multi-AZ, dedicated MSK), production (multi-AZ EKS us-east-1, MSK with PCI namespace, RDS Multi-AZ per service schema). Secrets via AWS Secrets Manager with ExternalSecrets operator; 30-day rotation.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM Java auto-instrumentation across all four services with W3C traceparent propagation in Kafka message headers enabling end-to-end order-to-shipment trace. Custom SLO dashboards: order-to-label P95 latency (target < 90 seconds), pick task queue depth, Kafka consumer lag per service, carrier label retry rate (alert at >5%), returns processing P90. PagerDuty escalations: order-to-label P95 > 120 seconds, fulfillment consumer lag > 2000 messages for 5 minutes, payments Kafka consumer lag > 100 messages, label generation error rate > 2%.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Baseline ~$4800/month: 3 EKS node groups, MSK multi-AZ, RDS Multi-AZ x5 schemas, Redis ElastiCache. Peak flash sale scaling adds ~$1200/hour via Karpenter spot nodes for fulfillment-service. Cost controls: Karpenter spot provisioning for non-payments services, reserved instances for payments-service node group, RDS Aurora Serverless v2 for dev/staging schemas, S3 Intelligent-Tiering for order archive after 2 years.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): order-service with FSM and saga_state log, fulfillment-service with pick workflow, shipping-service with FedEx adapter and label retry, returns-service with refund event emission. Keycloak RBAC, Kafka outbox pattern, choreography saga compensating transactions for label failure and pick abandonment. Jenkins pipelines per service. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Payments-service with PCI Kafka namespace isolation and field-level encryption, KEDA autoscaling, Admin metrics dashboard, GDPR erasure endpoint, Argo Rollouts for payments. Phase 3 (6 weeks): UPS carrier adapter fallback, Karpenter warm pool for flash sale readiness, supplier inbound ASN Kafka integration, advanced SLA analytics and picker productivity reporting.
target_output.development_guidelines: Java 21 with virtual threads for I/O-bound service handlers. Checkstyle + SpotBugs enforced in Jenkins. 80% unit test coverage gate per service. TestContainers for PostgreSQL and Kafka integration tests. No cross-service direct DB queries; API composition or Kafka events only. Flyway versioned migrations with undo scripts. ADR documents required for saga pattern design decisions. PR requires 2 approvals for payments-service; 1 for all other services. Shared Jenkins Groovy library for common pipeline stages.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Choreography saga complexity produces hard-to-diagnose stuck orders at scale. Mitigation: saga_state JSONB in order-service captures each transition with timestamp; ops runbook for manual saga replay from any FSM state. Risk: KEDA lag-based autoscaling may over-provision fulfillment pods during bursty low-volume periods. Mitigation: composite scaling policy combining Kafka lag AND active pick task count. Tradeoff: schema-per-service prevents ad-hoc cross-service joins; reporting queries use Kafka Streams read model materialized in a reporting schema. Tradeoff: local JWKS validation means token revocation is not immediate; mitigated by 15-minute token TTL and short refresh rotation window.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected FedEx as primary carrier with UPS as Phase 3 fallback; confirmed MSK over self-managed Kafka for operational simplicity. Keycloak local JWKS validation chosen over token introspection to prevent auth becoming a synchronous bottleneck at 10K orders/hour. GDPR erasure implemented at order-service level with cascading null-out across related service schemas via compensation events.

metadata.notes: Round 2 revision resolving PCI-DSS Kafka isolation and choreography saga compensating transaction gaps for DTC home goods microservices platform.

project_goal.value: Provide a Go library for high-throughput batched write clients embedding in POS and WMS systems to feed a central ClickHouse analytics store with stock movement, price change, and promotional activation events
project_goal.rationale: Embedding systems need a zero-overhead typed client to avoid ad-hoc HTTP calls that drop events under load

target_users.value: Go developers at retail POS and WMS teams embedding the library into their services
target_users.rationale: Library consumer is the embedding application developer, not an end user

project_class.rationale: Distributed as a Go module; no hosted runtime of its own

capabilities.rationale: Pure library providing data ingestion and integration with ClickHouse; no frontend or auth surface

complexity_level.rationale: Concurrent batching, disk-based fallback, mTLS, and ClickHouse protocol integration require careful design

risk_level.rationale: Internal analytics data; no PII, no payments, no external exposure

data_sensitivity.rationale: Stock movement and pricing data is business-confidential internal data, not regulated

external_exposure.rationale: ClickHouse write destination is on internal network; library never exposes public endpoints

access_model.value: mTLS client certificates per embedding application; no user-facing auth
access_model.rationale: Library-to-ClickHouse auth via mutual TLS; each POS system has its own client certificate

feature_scope.value: Typed event constructors for StockMovementEvent, PriceChangeEvent, PromoActivationEvent; configurable batch size and flush interval; exponential backoff retry; disk-based fallback buffer on flush failure; schema version tagging; mTLS embedded client
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set for reliable high-throughput analytics ingestion

mvp_scope.value: StockMovementEvent and PriceChangeEvent typed constructors, batch flushing to ClickHouse, exponential backoff retry, basic disk fallback buffer
mvp_scope.rationale: Stock and price events are the highest-volume and highest-priority analytics streams

security_baseline.value: mTLS between library and ClickHouse; client certificates stored in embedding application's secret store; no plaintext credentials in library config
security_baseline.rationale: Network-level encryption and mutual authentication sufficient for internal analytics transport

frontend_stack.rationale: No user interface; library distributes as a Go module

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22, standard library only for core logic; ClickHouse Go driver (clickhouse-go/v2)
backend_stack.rationale: Minimal dependency surface reduces embedding application conflict risk

data_platform.value: ClickHouse as write destination; local disk buffer for fallback (OS filesystem)
data_platform.rationale: ClickHouse is the central analytics store; disk buffer prevents data loss during network partitions

hosting_target.value: Go module distribution via Go module proxy; no hosted infrastructure
hosting_target.rationale: Library ships as source, consumed via go get

privacy_retention_policy.value: Library does not own data retention; embedding application and ClickHouse cluster own retention policies
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Library is a transport layer; data lifecycle managed by downstream

future_scope.value: PromoActivationEvent type, configurable schema versioning, OpenTelemetry trace propagation in batch headers, async acknowledgement callbacks
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 features after POS and WMS systems validate core ingestion reliability

constraints.value: Zero CGO; no global state; goroutine-safe; must not panic under any input; Go 1.22 minimum
constraints.rationale: Embedding application requirements: pure Go for cross-compilation, goroutine safety for concurrent use

observability_baseline.value: Library exposes a Metrics interface for embedding application to hook into (flush counts, error rates, buffer depth, batch sizes); no built-in metrics backend
observability_baseline.rationale: Embedding applications own their monitoring stack; library provides hooks, not opinions

execution_preference.value: Trunk-based development on main; semantic versioning; GitHub Actions CI on every PR
execution_preference.rationale: Library consumers need stable versioned releases

project.goal: Typed batched write library for retail analytics events targeting ClickHouse
project.target_users: Go developers embedding in POS and WMS services

backend.concurrency: goroutine-safe with internal mutex on batch buffer

constraints.panics: Must not panic on any input
constraints.goroutine_safety: All exported types must be safe for concurrent use

open_questions.schema_evolution: How to handle ClickHouse schema changes without breaking embedded callers

reasoner_reviews.product: The library addresses a real pain point: POS and WMS teams writing ad-hoc HTTP clients that drop events under load. The typed constructors and batch flushing design is correct. PromoActivationEvent is lower priority than StockMovement and PriceChange which are needed by reporting pipelines first.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The previous plan's disk fallback buffer design was underspecified: it did not define the file format, maximum disk size, or replay ordering guarantees. Under a 10-minute network partition with 50K events/minute throughput, the disk buffer could consume 30GB without a size cap. This must be addressed.
reasoner_reviews.security: mTLS certificate rotation procedure is missing from the previous plan. Embedding applications need a documented process for rotating client certificates without dropping in-flight batches. The library should accept a tls.Config factory function rather than a static certificate path to support hot rotation.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Zero CGO constraint is satisfied by the chosen stack. The goroutine-safety requirement means the internal batch buffer must use a sync.Mutex or channel-based design; the previous plan's use of a plain slice was not goroutine-safe.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The previous plan's retry logic used a fixed 5-second backoff, which causes thundering herd when multiple POS systems recover simultaneously from a network partition. Jittered exponential backoff is required.

backend.service_design: Library exposes three top-level types: StockMovementWriter, PriceChangeWriter, PromoActivationWriter. Each embeds a shared BatchFlusher with configurable MaxBatchSize (default 500 events), FlushInterval (default 2 seconds), and MaxDiskBufferBytes (default 512MB). All types implement io.Closer for graceful shutdown.
backend.api_patterns: Public API: NewStockMovementWriter(cfg Config) (*StockMovementWriter, error), writer.Write(ctx, StockMovementEvent) error, writer.Flush(ctx) error, writer.Close() error. Config struct: ClickHouseAddr, TLSConfigFn func() (*tls.Config, error), MaxBatchSize int, FlushInterval time.Duration, MaxDiskBufferBytes int64, MetricsHook MetricsInterface.
backend.business_modules: BatchFlusher: internal goroutine accumulates events in a ring buffer; flushes to ClickHouse on size or timer trigger. DiskBuffer: WAL-style append-only file with CRC32 checksums per record; replay on startup drains to ClickHouse before accepting new events. RetryPolicy: jittered exponential backoff starting at 100ms, max 30 seconds, max 10 attempts.
backend.failure_handling: On ClickHouse write failure, BatchFlusher moves batch to DiskBuffer. DiskBuffer caps at MaxDiskBufferBytes; if cap reached, library invokes MetricsHook.OnBufferFull() and drops oldest records with a warning log. Startup replay: drains disk buffer to ClickHouse before normal operation; replay failures re-queue to disk.
backend.backend_risks: ClickHouse schema changes (column additions) may cause clickhouse-go/v2 to fail batch inserts if strict column matching is enforced. Library should use INSERT with explicit column list to tolerate additive schema changes.

security.auth_design: Library accepts TLSConfigFn func() (*tls.Config, error) rather than a static tls.Config, enabling embedding application to hot-rotate client certificates by providing a new factory. Library calls TLSConfigFn before each new ClickHouse connection.
security.secrets_management: Library never reads certificates from disk directly; the TLSConfigFn factory pattern delegates certificate loading to the embedding application's secret store (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, etc.).
security.security_risks: If TLSConfigFn returns an error, BatchFlusher must fall back to DiskBuffer rather than attempting plaintext connection. Library must not log certificate content or TLS handshake details at any log level.

data.entities: StockMovementEvent (sku_id STRING, location_id STRING, delta_qty INT32, movement_type ENUM sale/adjustment/receipt, event_time DateTime64, schema_version UINT8). PriceChangeEvent (sku_id STRING, location_id STRING, old_price DECIMAL64, new_price DECIMAL64, price_type ENUM regular/promo/clearance, effective_at DateTime64, schema_version UINT8). PromoActivationEvent (promo_id STRING, sku_id STRING, location_id STRING, discount_pct DECIMAL32, start_at DateTime64, end_at DateTime64, schema_version UINT8). batch_write_buffers (in-memory ring buffer, max MaxBatchSize events). write_acknowledgements (ClickHouse insert response, rows_written UINT64). schema_versions (embedded UINT8 in each event; library validates against known set on Write call).
data.schema_notes: All ClickHouse INSERT statements use explicit column lists to tolerate additive schema migrations. schema_version field allows analytics layer to route events to versioned materialized views.
data.data_risks: DiskBuffer file corruption on abrupt OS termination; mitigated by CRC32 checksum per record and skip-corrupt-record replay mode. ClickHouse server-side deduplication via ReplacingMergeTree requires embedding application to ensure event_time uniqueness per sku_id and location_id.

devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions on every PR: go vet, staticcheck, golangci-lint, go test -race -count=3 (race detector, 3 runs for flakiness), go test -bench=. for regression benchmarks. Release workflow on tag push: go build for linux/amd64, linux/arm64, darwin/arm64; publish module to pkg.go.dev via GOPROXY.
devops.observability_stack: MetricsInterface: OnFlushSuccess(batchSize int, duration time.Duration), OnFlushError(err error, retryCount int), OnDiskBufferWrite(byteCount int64), OnDiskReplayComplete(eventsReplayed int), OnBufferFull(droppedCount int). Embedding application wires this to Prometheus, Datadog, or any backend.
devops.devops_risks: Go module proxy caching means a yanked version remains available to callers; document yank procedure in SECURITY.md. Major version bumps (v2) require import path change which breaks all embedding applications simultaneously.

ISS-001.title: DiskBuffer size cap and eviction policy not defined
ISS-001.detail: Previous plan mentioned disk fallback but did not specify maximum disk buffer size, record format, or eviction policy. Without a cap, a 10-minute partition at 50K events/minute could consume 30GB of disk.

history.note: Architect reasoner flagged missing disk buffer specification

ISS-002.title: Goroutine-unsafe batch buffer in previous plan
ISS-002.detail: Previous plan's BatchFlusher used a plain []Event slice for the internal buffer without synchronization, which would cause data races when multiple goroutines call Write concurrently.

history.note: Raised during constraints review; zero-CGO and goroutine-safety both required

ISS-003.title: Fixed backoff causes thundering herd on partition recovery
ISS-003.detail: Previous plan used a fixed 5-second retry interval. When multiple POS systems recover simultaneously from a network partition, synchronized retries overwhelm ClickHouse.

history.note: Critic identified thundering herd risk

focus_issues.title: DiskBuffer size cap and eviction policy not defined
focus_issues.detail: No disk buffer size cap or record format defined; risk of unbounded disk consumption

focus_issues.title: Goroutine-unsafe batch buffer in previous plan

previous_audits.summary: Library API surface and ClickHouse integration design are sound, but the internal batch buffer and disk fallback have reliability and correctness defects that would cause data loss and data races in production.
strengths: Typed event constructors match the analytics schema
mTLS design is appropriate for internal analytics transport
MetricsInterface hook pattern is the right abstraction for embedding applications
concerns: Disk fallback buffer undefined: no size cap, no record format, no replay ordering guarantee
Plain slice buffer is not goroutine-safe; violates stated constraint
Fixed backoff creates thundering herd risk on partition recovery
blocking_issues: Data race on concurrent Write calls due to unsynchronized batch buffer
Unbounded disk buffer could fill POS system disk during extended outage
recommendations: Implement DiskBuffer as WAL file with CRC32 checksums and MaxDiskBufferBytes cap
Use sync.Mutex-protected ring buffer for batch accumulation
Implement jittered exponential backoff in RetryPolicy

previous_plan.title: Retail Analytics Ingestion Library v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Go library providing typed batch write clients for StockMovementEvent and PriceChangeEvent to ClickHouse with disk fallback on failure and mTLS authentication.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Three writer types sharing a BatchFlusher; ClickHouse writes via clickhouse-go/v2; disk fallback for network failures; MetricsInterface for embedding application hooks.
previous_plan.data_model: StockMovementEvent and PriceChangeEvent typed structs; batch buffer as plain slice; disk fallback as unstructured file.
previous_plan.risks_and_tradeoffs: Disk fallback may grow large during outages; retry uses fixed 5s interval; goroutine safety mentioned but not implemented.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 revision targeting ISS-001 (disk buffer design) and ISS-002 (goroutine safety). DiskBuffer redesigned as a WAL-style append-only file with per-record CRC32 checksums, MaxDiskBufferBytes cap (default 512MB), and oldest-record eviction when cap reached. Batch buffer replaced with sync.Mutex-protected ring buffer (circular array of fixed capacity) to guarantee goroutine safety without CGO. ISS-003 addressed with full-jitter exponential backoff (100ms to 30s). TLSConfigFn factory pattern added to enable hot certificate rotation.

fix_report.action_taken: Redesigned DiskBuffer as WAL-style append-only file with 4-byte CRC32 checksum per record, explicit MaxDiskBufferBytes cap (default 512MB), oldest-record eviction when cap reached, and structured replay with skip-corrupt-record mode on startup.
fix_report.expected_outcome: POS system disk usage bounded at 512MB maximum; corrupted records skipped with metrics hook notification rather than causing panic

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced plain []Event slice with sync.Mutex-protected fixed-capacity ring buffer (EventRingBuffer). All Write, Flush, and Close paths acquire the mutex before accessing the buffer. Verified goroutine safety with go test -race -count=3 in CI.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Zero data races under concurrent Write calls from multiple goroutines; go test -race passes in CI

target_output.title: Retail Analytics Ingestion Library v2
target_output.executive_summary: Revised Go library providing goroutine-safe typed batch write clients for StockMovementEvent, PriceChangeEvent, and PromoActivationEvent to ClickHouse, with a WAL-style disk fallback buffer capped at 512MB, jittered exponential backoff retry, and a TLSConfigFn factory for hot certificate rotation. This revision resolves the goroutine-safety data race and the unbounded disk buffer defects from round 1.
target_output.architecture_overview: Pure Go 1.22 library with zero CGO distributed as a Go module. Three writer types (StockMovementWriter, PriceChangeWriter, PromoActivationWriter) each embed a shared BatchFlusher. BatchFlusher accumulates events in an EventRingBuffer (sync.Mutex-protected, fixed-capacity ring buffer) and flushes to ClickHouse via clickhouse-go/v2 native protocol with mTLS. On flush failure, DiskBuffer writes events to a WAL-style append-only file with CRC32 checksums per record and enforces MaxDiskBufferBytes cap by evicting oldest records. RetryPolicy implements jittered exponential backoff (100ms base, 2x multiplier, 30s max, full jitter). TLSConfigFn factory function called per new ClickHouse connection to support hot certificate rotation without dropping in-flight batches.
target_output.technology_stack: Go 1.22 standard library for core logic (sync, io, os, crypto/tls). clickhouse-go/v2 for ClickHouse native protocol INSERT. encoding/binary for WAL record serialization. hash/crc32 for disk buffer record integrity. No CGO; no global state; all exported types goroutine-safe. GitHub Actions CI with go test -race -count=3 and golangci-lint. Distribution via Go module proxy.
target_output.functional_feature_map: StockMovementWriter: Write(ctx, StockMovementEvent) error with schema_version validation; Flush(ctx) error for immediate flush; Close() error for graceful drain and shutdown. PriceChangeWriter: identical interface for PriceChangeEvent. PromoActivationWriter: Phase 2. BatchFlusher: EventRingBuffer accumulation, size and timer-triggered flush, DiskBuffer fallback on failure, startup replay drain, MetricsInterface hook invocations. DiskBuffer: WAL append, CRC32 per record, MaxDiskBufferBytes cap, oldest-record eviction, skip-corrupt-record replay. RetryPolicy: jittered exponential backoff, configurable max attempts.
target_output.system_components: EventRingBuffer: fixed-capacity circular array with sync.Mutex; Write appends to head, flush reads from tail, wraps around on capacity; capacity = MaxBatchSize * 4 to absorb bursts. BatchFlusher goroutine: timer (FlushInterval default 2s) and size trigger (MaxBatchSize default 500) drive flush; context-aware for graceful shutdown. DiskBuffer: single WAL file at configurable path; file format: [4-byte record length][4-byte CRC32][record bytes]; eviction scans from file head to free space when MaxDiskBufferBytes exceeded. ClickHouseConn: wraps clickhouse-go/v2 connection; calls TLSConfigFn on each new Dial to pick up rotated certificates; INSERT with explicit column list for schema-additive tolerance. MetricsInterface: OnFlushSuccess, OnFlushError, OnDiskBufferWrite, OnDiskReplayComplete, OnBufferFull, OnRecordSkipped callbacks.
target_output.workflows: Normal Write Path: Caller calls writer.Write(ctx, event); event validated against known schema_version set; EventRingBuffer mutex acquired, event appended; mutex released. BatchFlusher timer or size trigger fires; mutex acquired, batch copied from ring buffer, mutex released; ClickHouseConn.Insert(ctx, batch) called with explicit column list; on success MetricsHook.OnFlushSuccess invoked. Failure and Recovery Path: ClickHouseConn.Insert fails; RetryPolicy applies jittered exponential backoff (100ms * 2^n + rand(0,100ms), max 30s, max 10 attempts); after max attempts, batch serialized to DiskBuffer WAL; CRC32 computed per record; if DiskBuffer size >= MaxDiskBufferBytes, oldest records evicted and MetricsHook.OnBufferFull invoked with dropped count. Startup Replay: On writer initialization, DiskBuffer scanned from tail; each record CRC32 verified; corrupt records skipped with MetricsHook.OnRecordSkipped; valid records requeued to EventRingBuffer for immediate flush before normal operation resumes.
target_output.data_model: StockMovementEvent{SkuID string, LocationID string, DeltaQty int32, MovementType StockMovementType (ENUM: sale/adjustment/receipt), EventTime time.Time, SchemaVersion uint8}. PriceChangeEvent{SkuID string, LocationID string, OldPrice decimal.Decimal, NewPrice decimal.Decimal, PriceType PriceType (ENUM: regular/promo/clearance), EffectiveAt time.Time, SchemaVersion uint8}. PromoActivationEvent{PromoID string, SkuID string, LocationID string, DiscountPct decimal.Decimal, StartAt time.Time, EndAt time.Time, SchemaVersion uint8}. Internal types: batch_write_buffers (EventRingBuffer, cap = MaxBatchSize*4, sync.Mutex protected). write_acknowledgements (clickhouse-go/v2 insert result: RowsWritten uint64, Duration time.Duration). schema_versions (library-internal map[uint8]bool of known valid versions per event type). DiskBuffer WAL record: [uint32 length][uint32 CRC32][encoded event bytes].
target_output.api_design: NewStockMovementWriter(cfg Config) (*StockMovementWriter, error) - validates config, initializes EventRingBuffer, starts BatchFlusher goroutine, replays DiskBuffer if non-empty. writer.Write(ctx context.Context, e StockMovementEvent) error - validates schema_version, acquires mutex, appends to ring buffer, releases mutex; returns ErrBufferFull if ring at capacity. writer.Flush(ctx context.Context) error - forces immediate flush of current ring buffer contents; blocks until ClickHouse INSERT completes or DiskBuffer fallback completes. writer.Close() error - signals BatchFlusher to drain, waits for in-flight flush, closes ClickHouse connection, closes DiskBuffer file. Config{ClickHouseAddr string, TLSConfigFn func() (*tls.Config, error), MaxBatchSize int, FlushInterval time.Duration, MaxDiskBufferBytes int64, DiskBufferPath string, MetricsHook MetricsInterface, MaxRetryAttempts int}.
target_output.security_and_compliance: mTLS: TLSConfigFn factory called on each new ClickHouse connection; embedding application controls certificate loading from its secret store (Vault, Kubernetes secrets, AWS Secrets Manager). Library never logs certificate content or TLS error details beyond error type. All ClickHouse INSERT statements use explicit column lists, preventing injection via schema drift. No global state; each writer instance is fully isolated, enabling multi-tenant embedding in the same process with different mTLS identities. go vet and staticcheck in CI enforce no unsafe pointer usage.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Distribution: Go module published to pkg.go.dev via GOPROXY. Semantic versioning: v1.x for stable API; breaking changes increment major version with updated import path. GitHub Actions CI pipeline: go vet -> staticcheck -> golangci-lint (errcheck, gosec, exhaustive) -> go test ./... -race -count=3 -timeout=120s -> go test -bench=. -benchmem -run=^$ (benchmark regression check, fail if >10% regression vs main). Release workflow triggered on tag v*.*.* - cross-compile for linux/amd64, linux/arm64, darwin/arm64; attach binaries to GitHub release. CHANGELOG.md maintained per release for embedding application teams.
target_output.observability: MetricsInterface callbacks provide embedding application full observability: OnFlushSuccess(batchSize int, duration time.Duration) for throughput tracking, OnFlushError(err error, retryCount int) for alert on sustained retry storms, OnDiskBufferWrite(byteCount int64) for disk usage trend, OnDiskReplayComplete(eventsReplayed int) for post-partition recovery audit, OnBufferFull(droppedCount int) for data loss alerting, OnRecordSkipped(reason string) for disk corruption events. Embedding applications wire these to Prometheus counters or Datadog DogStatsD as appropriate.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: No hosted infrastructure cost; library is source-distributed. Memory footprint: EventRingBuffer fixed at MaxBatchSize*4 events (default ~800KB for 500-event batches of StockMovementEvent). Disk footprint: bounded at MaxDiskBufferBytes (default 512MB). ClickHouse throughput scales linearly with flush batch size; benchmark target 50K events/second sustained on ClickHouse cluster with 3 shards.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 v1.0 (6 weeks): StockMovementWriter and PriceChangeWriter, EventRingBuffer goroutine-safe batch accumulation, DiskBuffer WAL with CRC32 and 512MB cap, jittered exponential backoff, TLSConfigFn factory, MetricsInterface, GitHub Actions CI with race detector. Phase 2 v1.1 (4 weeks): PromoActivationWriter, schema_version validation against known set, startup DiskBuffer replay drain, OpenTelemetry trace context propagation in batch headers. Phase 3 v2.0 (if needed): Async acknowledgement callbacks, pluggable serialization format (native vs Arrow), multi-destination fan-out.
target_output.development_guidelines: Zero CGO enforced via CGO_ENABLED=0 in CI build. All exported types must have godoc comments with example usage. go test -race -count=3 must pass before any merge. Benchmark regression gate: CI fails if BenchmarkStockMovementWrite regresses >10% vs main branch. Error handling: all errors wrapped with fmt.Errorf with %w for upstream unwrapping. No global variables; all state encapsulated in writer instances. golangci-lint ruleset checked into .golangci.yml in repo root.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: ClickHouse schema column additions cause INSERT failures if column list is not explicitly specified. Mitigation: always use INSERT INTO table (col1, col2, ...) syntax in clickhouse-go/v2 to tolerate additive changes. Risk: DiskBuffer WAL file grows to MaxDiskBufferBytes during extended ClickHouse outage, then drops oldest records. Mitigation: MetricsHook.OnBufferFull gives embedding application opportunity to alert ops before data loss accumulates. Tradeoff: fixed-capacity EventRingBuffer returns ErrBufferFull rather than blocking callers; this prevents backpressure deadlocks in high-throughput POS systems but requires caller to handle the error. Tradeoff: jittered full exponential backoff may delay post-partition recovery by up to 30s for the last retry; acceptable given DiskBuffer absorbs events during retry window.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Chose full-jitter exponential backoff (decorrelated jitter) over equal-jitter after reviewing AWS architecture blog benchmarks showing full jitter minimizes ClickHouse server lock contention during coordinated recovery. DiskBuffer eviction uses oldest-record-first to preserve most recent events, which are more actionable for real-time analytics. Confirmed clickhouse-go/v2 native protocol over HTTP interface for lower per-batch latency.

metadata.notes: Round 2 revision fixing goroutine-unsafe batch buffer and unbounded disk fallback in a Go ClickHouse analytics ingestion library.

profile.domain: Retail Pharmacy Controlled Substance Compliance Automation

project_goal.value: Automate reconciliation of dispensed controlled substance quantities against received stock, flag discrepancies, and generate DEA Form 222-compliant audit reports for a retail pharmacy chain
project_goal.rationale: Manual reconciliation process takes 4 hours per pharmacy per month and introduces human error in DEA reporting

target_users.value: Pharmacy compliance officers, DEA-licensed pharmacists, and regional compliance managers
target_users.rationale: Operational users who review discrepancy flags and sign off on DEA reports

project_class.rationale: Scheduled batch reconciliation with no interactive UI; outputs reports and flags for human review

capabilities.rationale: Batch reconciliation jobs, pharmacy system integrations, data persistence, no frontend needed

complexity_level.rationale: DEA schedule-specific reconciliation logic, signed PDF generation, multi-pharmacy chain coordination

risk_level.rationale: Handles controlled substance records subject to DEA 21 CFR Part 1304 and incidental PHI in dispensing records

data_sensitivity.rationale: Dispensing records contain patient identifiers and prescription details constituting PHI under HIPAA

external_exposure.rationale: Tool runs on-premises or in pharmacy chain's private Azure environment; no internet-facing endpoints

access_model.value: Azure AD with RBAC: pharmacist_reviewer can view discrepancy flags; compliance_officer can sign off reports; regional_admin can view all pharmacies in region; no anonymous access
access_model.rationale: DEA audit requirements mandate individual user sign-off traceability

feature_scope.value: Nightly reconciliation job per DEA schedule (II, III, IV, V), discrepancy flagging with threshold configuration per schedule, pharmacist digital sign-off workflow, DEA Form 222-compliant PDF report generation, compliance document archive, integration with pharmacy dispensing system and receipt management system
feature_scope.rationale: Complete DEA compliance workflow from data ingestion to signed archived report

mvp_scope.value: Schedule II reconciliation, discrepancy flagging, pharmacist sign-off, DEA report PDF generation, Azure Blob Storage archive
mvp_scope.rationale: Schedule II (opioids) is highest-risk and highest regulatory scrutiny; MVP focuses there

security_baseline.value: Azure AD authentication, TLS 1.3 for all API calls, PostgreSQL column-level encryption for patient_id and prescription_id in dispensing records, Azure Key Vault for signing keys used in PDF reports, immutable audit log for all reconciliation actions
security_baseline.rationale: HIPAA minimum controls for PHI and DEA audit trail requirements

frontend_stack.value: none - automation tool with REST API for sign-off callbacks only
frontend_stack.rationale: No interactive UI; sign-off workflow triggered via API called by pharmacy staff portal

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI for sign-off API; Prefect for batch job orchestration
backend_stack.rationale: Python ecosystem for PDF generation (ReportLab) and pharmacy system integrations; Prefect for observable scheduled flows

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Database for PostgreSQL; Azure Blob Storage for PDF archive
data_platform.rationale: Managed PostgreSQL with Azure AD auth and encryption at rest; Blob for durable PDF archive

hosting_target.value: Azure Container Apps for API and worker containers; Azure DevOps Pipelines for CI/CD
hosting_target.rationale: Managed containers without Kubernetes overhead; Azure ecosystem aligns with pharmacy chain's existing Azure tenancy

privacy_retention_policy.value: Dispensing records retained 2 years per DEA 21 CFR 1304.04; DEA audit reports retained 2 years; PHI-containing records purged after retention period via scheduled deletion job
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Federal DEA retention requirements and HIPAA minimum necessary principle

future_scope.value: State pharmacy board reporting integration, Schedule III-V reconciliation, anomaly detection ML model for unusual dispensing patterns, pharmacist mobile sign-off app
future_scope.rationale: Year 2 expansion after DEA compliance baseline established

constraints.value: Must run within pharmacy chain's Azure subscription; no data leaves Azure boundary; report PDFs must be digitally signed with pharmacist certificate; must complete nightly reconciliation before 6am pharmacy opening
constraints.rationale: Data residency, regulatory, and operational timing requirements

observability_baseline.value: Azure Monitor + Log Analytics for container logs; Prefect UI for job run history; email alerts to compliance_officer role on reconciliation failure or unresolved discrepancies at report generation time
observability_baseline.rationale: Compliance officers need visibility into job failures before business hours

execution_preference.value: Two-week sprints; Azure DevOps Pipelines; manual approval gate before deploying to production pharmacy environments
execution_preference.rationale: Pharmacy compliance tooling requires manual sign-off before production changes

compliance_context.value: DEA 21 CFR Part 1304 (controlled substance records), HIPAA (incidental PHI in dispensing records), state pharmacy board regulations for digital signatures on controlled substance records
compliance_context.rationale: Primary DEA compliance requirement with HIPAA overlay for patient-adjacent data

project.goal: Automated DEA-compliant controlled substance reconciliation and reporting tool
project.target_users: Pharmacists, compliance officers, regional managers

backend.framework: FastAPI for sign-off API; Prefect 2 for flow orchestration
backend.pdf: ReportLab for DEA Form 222 PDF generation

security.signing: Azure Key Vault for PDF digital signatures
security.phi: Column-level encryption for patient_id and prescription_id

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Database for PostgreSQL
data.archive: Azure Blob Storage for signed PDFs

open_questions.dispensing_system: HL7 v2 or proprietary API for dispensing system integration

reasoner_reviews.product: The scope is well-bounded: nightly reconciliation with discrepancy flagging and report generation is the core DEA compliance workflow. The sign-off API design is the right approach to avoid building a UI while still capturing individual pharmacist accountability. Schedule II priority for MVP is correct given DEA audit focus.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: After two rounds, the main outstanding issue is the reconciliation tolerance threshold configuration. Rounds 1 and 2 hardcoded thresholds, but pharmacy chains require per-DEA-schedule and per-pharmacy configurable tolerances. A database-driven threshold config table is needed. The PDF signing mechanism using Azure Key Vault PKCS#7 was added in round 2 but the certificate rotation procedure is still missing.
reasoner_reviews.security: HIPAA minimum necessary principle requires that dispensing records loaded into the reconciliation job only expose the fields needed for quantity comparison (NDC, DEA schedule, quantity, dates) and never the full patient prescription detail. The round 2 plan loaded entire dispensing records into memory, unnecessarily expanding PHI exposure.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 6am timing constraint requires the nightly reconciliation flow to complete within a bounded window. Round 2 did not include a job timeout or alerting on approaching deadline. Prefect flow timeout and a 4:30am alert if reconciliation is not complete by that time are needed.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The compliance_document_store integration for archiving signed PDFs was described in round 2 but lacked blob naming convention and lifecycle management details. DEA auditors need predictable file paths for spot audits.

backend.service_design: Two Prefect deployment flows: ReconciliationFlow (nightly, per pharmacy, per DEA schedule) and ReportGenerationFlow (monthly, triggered by compliance_officer sign-off). FastAPI app with three endpoints: POST /sign-offs (pharmacist sign-off submission), GET /discrepancies (filtered by pharmacy_id, schedule, status), GET /reconciliation-runs (job history). All FastAPI endpoints protected by Azure AD token validation.
backend.business_modules: ReconciliationEngine: loads dispensing_records (NDC, schedule, quantity, date range only - PHI fields excluded) and receipt_records for a pharmacy and schedule; computes expected_on_hand = prior_balance + sum(receipt_qty) - sum(dispensed_qty); flags if |actual_on_hand - expected_on_hand| > threshold[schedule][pharmacy]. ThresholdConfig: per-DEA-schedule and per-pharmacy configurable tolerance table in PostgreSQL. PDFGenerator: uses ReportLab to render DEA Form 222 layout; embeds digital signature via Azure Key Vault PKCS#7 sign call with pharmacist certificate.
backend.background_jobs: ReconciliationFlow scheduled via Prefect cron (11pm nightly); flow_run_timeout=5h to ensure completion before 6am; on-failure hook emails compliance_officer. MonthlyReportFlow triggered by compliance_officer API call after all discrepancies resolved; generates and archives signed PDF. RetentionPurgeJob: monthly Prefect flow deletes dispensing_records and receipt_records older than 2 years per DEA 1304.04.
backend.failure_handling: ReconciliationFlow failure sends Azure Monitor alert and emails compliance_officer with flow run URL. Partial reconciliation (some pharmacies succeed, some fail) records individual pharmacy status; next run retries failed pharmacies only. PDF signing failure (Azure Key Vault unavailable) blocks report generation and alerts compliance team.

security.auth_design: Azure AD app registration with RBAC roles assigned in Azure Portal: pharmacist_reviewer (read discrepancies for their pharmacy), compliance_officer (sign off, trigger reports, view all pharmacies), regional_admin (read across region). FastAPI dependency validates Azure AD JWT and extracts role claims.
security.privacy_controls: ReconciliationEngine SELECT query includes only: pharmacy_id, ndc_code, dea_schedule, quantity, dispensed_at, receipt_at. Columns patient_id and prescription_id never loaded into application memory during reconciliation. PHI fields only accessible via separate audit endpoint requiring compliance_officer role.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Immutable reconciliation_audit_log table: every reconciliation run, discrepancy creation, sign-off action, and PDF generation logged with user_id, timestamp, action, and resource_id. Azure Log Analytics captures all FastAPI access logs with correlation IDs.
security.security_risks: Azure Key Vault certificate used for PDF signing must have 2-year validity with automated rotation 90 days before expiry; expired certificate blocks monthly report generation. Document rotation procedure in runbook.

data.entities: controlled_substance_inventory (id UUID PK, pharmacy_id FK, ndc_code, dea_schedule ENUM II/III/IV/V, on_hand_qty DECIMAL, as_of_date, last_reconciled_at). dispensing_records (id UUID PK, pharmacy_id FK, ndc_code, dea_schedule, quantity_dispensed DECIMAL, dispensed_at, patient_id_encrypted VARCHAR, prescription_id_encrypted VARCHAR, pharmacist_id FK). receipt_records (id UUID PK, pharmacy_id FK, ndc_code, dea_schedule, quantity_received DECIMAL, received_at, supplier_dea_number, po_number). reconciliation_runs (id UUID PK, pharmacy_id FK, dea_schedule, run_date, status ENUM running/completed/failed, expected_on_hand DECIMAL, actual_on_hand DECIMAL, variance DECIMAL, completed_at). discrepancy_flags (id UUID PK, run_id FK, ndc_code, variance_qty DECIMAL, threshold_used DECIMAL, status ENUM open/reviewed/resolved/escalated, flagged_at). dea_audit_reports (id UUID PK, pharmacy_id FK, report_period, status ENUM draft/signed/archived, blob_path VARCHAR, signed_by FK, signed_at). pharmacist_sign_offs (id UUID PK, report_id FK, pharmacist_id FK, sign_off_at, certificate_thumbprint). reconciliation_thresholds (id UUID PK, pharmacy_id FK nullable, dea_schedule ENUM, tolerance_qty DECIMAL, tolerance_pct DECIMAL, effective_from DATE).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Database for PostgreSQL with Azure AD authentication. Column-level encryption via pgcrypto for patient_id_encrypted and prescription_id_encrypted (AES-256, keys in Azure Key Vault). Azure Blob Storage container 'dea-audit-reports' with naming convention: {pharmacy_id}/{YYYY-MM}/{report_id}.pdf. Blob container has immutable storage policy (WORM, 2-year retention lock) to satisfy DEA retention requirements.
data.retention_and_deletion: RetentionPurgeJob runs monthly: deletes dispensing_records and receipt_records with dispensed_at/received_at older than 2 years. dea_audit_reports blob retention governed by Azure Blob immutable WORM policy (cannot be deleted before 2-year lock expires). reconciliation_audit_log retained 7 years for general audit purposes.

devops.deployment_topology: Azure Container Apps environment with two apps: reconciliation-api (FastAPI, min 1 max 3 replicas, triggered by HTTP) and reconciliation-worker (Prefect worker, min 1 max 2 replicas, long-running). Azure Container Registry for images. Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server (General Purpose, 4 vCores). Azure Blob Storage for PDF archive. Azure Key Vault for signing certificates and DB credentials.
devops.ci_cd_design: Azure DevOps Pipeline: pytest + mypy + ruff lint -> Docker build -> ACR push -> Container Apps deploy to dev (auto) -> staging (auto with smoke tests) -> production (manual approval gate). Pipeline gate: all DEA reconciliation unit tests must pass including mock Schedule II discrepancy scenarios.
devops.observability_stack: Azure Monitor Container Insights for CPU/memory. Azure Log Analytics workspace receives FastAPI structured JSON logs with correlation IDs. Prefect Cloud UI for flow run history and failure inspection. Azure Monitor alert rule: ReconciliationFlow not completed by 4:30am -> email to compliance_officer distribution list.
devops.backup_and_recovery: PostgreSQL automated backups (7-day retention, geo-redundant). Azure Blob WORM policy prevents report deletion. Disaster recovery: Prefect flow definitions stored in Git; worker redeployment restores job scheduling within 30 minutes.

ISS-001.title: Reconciliation tolerance thresholds hardcoded, not configurable
ISS-001.detail: Rounds 1 and 2 hardcoded Schedule II tolerance at 0 units. Pharmacy chains require per-schedule and per-pharmacy configurable thresholds to account for counting variance in high-volume dispensing environments.

history.note: Raised in round 1 audit
Still not addressed in round 2 revision

ISS-002.title: Full dispensing records loaded into memory during reconciliation (PHI overexposure)
ISS-002.detail: ReconciliationEngine SELECT query pulled entire dispensing_records rows including patient_id and prescription_id into memory, violating HIPAA minimum necessary principle.

history.note: Security review flagged
Round 2 plan did not restrict the SELECT column list

ISS-003.title: No job completion timeout or pre-deadline alert
ISS-003.detail: ReconciliationFlow has no timeout; a runaway job could still be running when pharmacies open at 6am. No alert fires if job is not complete by 4:30am.

history.note: Added to ledger in round 2 audit

Set Prefect flow_run_timeout=5h on ReconciliationFlow; add Azure Monitor alert rule if flow not marked complete by 4:30am
