target_users.rationale: Technical operators invoking CLI commands during maintenance windows

project_class.rationale: No browser UI required; all functionality via terminal sub-commands and flags

capabilities.rationale: Data extraction, field transformation, and target API integration only

complexity_level.rationale: YAML field mapping, schema validation, checkpoint resumption, and rollback add meaningful complexity beyond a simple script

risk_level.rationale: Risk of data loss or incomplete migration if checkpointing fails during maintenance window

data_sensitivity.rationale: Product catalog and inventory counts are internal business data, not customer PII

external_exposure.rationale: Runs on operator workstations with outbound HTTPS to target API; no inbound exposure

access_model.value: API key via TARGET_API_KEY env var for target platform; Magento DB credentials via MAGENTO_DSN env var
access_model.rationale: No user authentication layer for a local CLI; secrets must stay out of code

feature_scope.value: Product extraction from Magento MySQL, YAML field mapping config, schema validation, batch upsert to target API, SQLite checkpoint tracking, error CSV export, rollback command, incremental mode
feature_scope.rationale: Full migration lifecycle features for reliable catalog transfer

mvp_scope.value: Product record extraction, basic YAML field mapping, batch upsert with checkpoint, error report CSV export
mvp_scope.rationale: Core migration path needed before incremental sync and rollback features

security_baseline.value: API keys and DB credentials loaded exclusively from environment variables, masked in all log output; SQLite state file with 0o600 permissions; HTTPS enforced for target API calls
security_baseline.rationale: Credentials must not appear in logs or source control

frontend_stack.rationale: No browser interface; all interaction via terminal

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Click CLI framework, PyMySQL, httpx
backend_stack.rationale: Click provides robust sub-command structure; httpx supports async batch POST

data_platform.value: SQLite for local migration state; Magento MySQL 5.7 as read-only source via PyMySQL
data_platform.rationale: SQLite for zero-infrastructure checkpointing; Magento MySQL accessed read-only

hosting_target.value: Local operator workstation; distributed as pip wheel and Docker image
hosting_target.rationale: No server hosting needed; tool runs wherever the migration engineer operates

privacy_retention_policy.value: Local SQLite state and error CSVs retained for duration of migration project and deleted post-cutover per ops runbook
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Temporary migration artifacts not subject to long-term data retention rules

future_scope.value: Inventory sync scheduling, Magento 2.x source support, GUI progress dashboard
future_scope.rationale: Post-migration tooling enhancements on the team roadmap

constraints.value: Must run on Python 3.12, no external services for local state, complete full migration within 8-hour maintenance window
constraints.rationale: Maintenance window and self-contained execution requirements

observability_baseline.value: Structured console logging with --verbose flag, tqdm progress bars, error CSV output at migration_errors_{timestamp}.csv
observability_baseline.rationale: Operators need real-time progress visibility and post-run error analysis without external monitoring

execution_preference.value: Full or incremental mode selectable via --mode=full|incremental flag; batch size configurable via --batch-size
execution_preference.rationale: Supports both one-time full migration and nightly incremental syncs

project.goal: Migrate Magento 1.x product and inventory catalog to modern inventory platform
project.target_users: Migration engineers and e-commerce developers

backend.framework: Click CLI with PyMySQL and httpx
backend.api: Target platform REST API via httpx async client

security.auth: API key via TARGET_API_KEY environment variable
security.transport: HTTPS to target platform API; SSL verification enforced

data.db: SQLite for local checkpoint state; Magento MySQL as read-only source
data.cache: none

devops.ci: GitHub Actions for lint, test, and package release

reasoner_reviews.product: The scope is well-suited for a one-time migration CLI. YAML-configured field mappings avoid hard-coding Magento-to-target field translations and allow reuse across multiple store instances. Error CSV export is essential for post-migration audit by e-commerce operations teams.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Click sub-commands (migrate, validate, rollback, status) cleanly separate migration lifecycle stages. SQLite checkpoint storage allows resuming from the last successful batch without re-processing already-migrated products, which is critical for the 8-hour window. httpx async pooling with bounded concurrency avoids overwhelming the target platform API.
reasoner_reviews.security: API keys and Magento DSN must be loaded exclusively from environment variables and redacted in all log output via a custom logging filter. The local SQLite state file must be created with 0o600 permissions to prevent other users on shared workstations from reading migration state or credentials.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: A batch size of 500 records with 5 concurrent httpx workers can process a 100K-SKU catalog in approximately 4-6 hours, fitting within the 8-hour window. tqdm progress bars provide operator visibility without log flooding.

backend.service_design: Click application with sub-commands: migrate (--mode=full|incremental, --batch-size), validate (dry-run schema check against target API), rollback (revert last batch by batch_id), status (show checkpoint progress table)
backend.api_patterns: httpx AsyncClient with retry and exponential backoff (3 retries: 1s, 2s, 4s) for target REST API; batch POST /api/v2/products/bulk with 500 records per request; asyncio.Semaphore limiting to 5 concurrent requests
backend.failure_handling: Checkpoint written to SQLite after each successful batch commit; on httpx exception or 5xx, current batch records written to validation_errors with error_type and detail; migrate command exits cleanly and operator can resume
backend.background_jobs: none; synchronous batch processing within maintenance window

security.auth_design: TARGET_API_KEY read from environment; MAGENTO_DSN (mysql+pymysql://user:pass@host/db) read from environment; both values masked with '***' in all Python logging calls via custom LogFilter
security.secrets_management: .env file for local development with .gitignore exclusion; .env.example committed with placeholder values only
security.abuse_prevention: asyncio.Semaphore(5) bounding concurrent httpx requests; automatic back-off on 429 responses from target API

data.entities: source_products (magento_id INT, sku VARCHAR, name TEXT, price DECIMAL, status TINYINT) -- read-only Magento MySQL; source_inventory (sku VARCHAR, qty DECIMAL, warehouse_code VARCHAR) -- read-only; migration_checkpoints (batch_id INTEGER PK, status TEXT, records_processed INT, started_at TEXT, completed_at TEXT); field_mappings (source_field TEXT, target_field TEXT, transform_fn TEXT); validation_errors (id INTEGER PK, batch_id INT, sku TEXT, error_type TEXT, detail TEXT, occurred_at TEXT); rollback_logs (id INTEGER PK, batch_id INT, target_product_id TEXT, action TEXT, reverted_at TEXT)
data.storage_design: SQLite at ./migration_state.db; schema auto-created on first run; Magento source accessed read-only via PyMySQL with streaming cursor to avoid loading full catalog into memory
data.retention_and_deletion: migration_state.db and error CSVs deleted manually post-cutover per documented ops runbook

devops.deployment_topology: Distributed as pip wheel installable from internal package registry; Docker image (python:3.12-slim) for environments without Python setup; no server provisioning required
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: ruff lint, mypy type check, pytest suite, build wheel, push to internal PyPI on semver tag push
devops.observability_stack: tqdm progress bar showing batch progress and ETA; Python logging to stderr (WARNING default, DEBUG with --verbose); error CSV export; status sub-command printing checkpoint table

target_output.thinking_summary: This is a local CLI migration tool with no hosted infrastructure. The main architectural concerns are checkpoint durability to handle partial failures within the 8-hour window, safe credential handling in a shared workstation environment, and batch sizing to complete within the maintenance window. Click sub-commands provide clean separation of migration lifecycle stages: migrate, validate, rollback, and status.
target_output.title: Magento 1.x to Modern Platform Data Migration CLI
target_output.executive_summary: A Python 3.12 Click CLI tool that extracts product and inventory records from a Magento 1.x MySQL database, applies YAML-configured field mappings, validates against the target platform schema, and upserts in batches via the target REST API. Checkpoint state persisted in local SQLite enables resumable runs within the 8-hour maintenance window. Rollback command reverts the last completed batch using logged target product IDs.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single-process Python CLI with Click sub-commands dispatching to migrate, validate, rollback, and status handlers. Source data extracted from Magento MySQL catalog_product_entity and cataloginventory_stock_item tables via PyMySQL streaming cursor. YAML field mapping config translated by a mapper module applying transform functions (e.g., Magento price DECIMAL to target base_price_cents INT). Validated records batched and POSTed to target platform REST API via httpx async client with bounded concurrency. Migration state (checkpoints, validation errors, rollback logs) stored in local SQLite database. Runs on operator workstation or in Docker container with no server infrastructure.
target_output.technology_stack: Runtime: Python 3.12. CLI: Click 8.x with rich for formatted terminal output. Source DB: PyMySQL 1.x for Magento MySQL streaming reads. HTTP: httpx with asyncio and Semaphore(5) for bounded concurrent target API calls. State: SQLite via Python stdlib sqlite3 with WAL mode. Config: PyYAML for field_mappings definitions. Progress: tqdm. Testing: pytest with in-memory SQLite fixtures. Packaging: wheel distributed via internal PyPI and python:3.12-slim Docker image.
target_output.functional_feature_map: migrate command: --mode=full|incremental, --batch-size=500, --dry-run flag; extracts Magento SKUs, applies YAML mappings, validates, upserts to target API. validate command: schema dry-run against target API without writing records; outputs validation error summary. rollback command: --batch-id flag; reads rollback_logs and DELETEs migrated products from target API. status command: queries migration_checkpoints and prints progress table with completion percentage and failed batch count. --verbose flag: enables DEBUG logging with per-batch API response codes and latency.
target_output.system_components: Click entrypoint dispatching to command handlers. PyMySQL reader streaming Magento catalog tables with configurable page size. YAML mapper loading field_mappings.yaml and applying named transform functions (price_to_cents, strip_html, uppercase_sku). Schema validator checking target platform required fields and type constraints. httpx async upserter batching POST /api/v2/products/bulk requests. SQLite state manager with atomic batch commits. CSV exporter writing validation_errors to migration_errors_{timestamp}.csv.
target_output.workflows: Full Migration: Operator runs 'mig migrate --mode=full --batch-size=500'. Tool queries migration_checkpoints for last completed batch_id; skips already-migrated batches. For each new page of 500 Magento products, applies YAML field mappings (e.g., 'price' mapped to 'base_price_cents' via price_to_cents transform), validates required target fields, POSTs to /api/v2/products/bulk, inserts completed checkpoint row to SQLite, logs target_product_ids to rollback_logs, advances. Resume After Failure: Operator re-runs 'mig migrate'; tool reads last completed batch_id from migration_checkpoints, skips already-migrated batches, continues from failed batch. Rollback: Operator runs 'mig rollback --batch-id=42'; tool reads rollback_logs for batch 42, sends DELETE /api/v2/products/{target_id} for each logged ID, marks rollback completed in SQLite.
target_output.data_model: source_products (magento_id INT, sku VARCHAR(64), name TEXT, price DECIMAL(12,4), status TINYINT, attribute_set_id INT) -- read-only from Magento MySQL. source_inventory (sku VARCHAR(64), qty DECIMAL(12,4), warehouse_code VARCHAR(32)) -- read-only. migration_checkpoints (batch_id INTEGER PK AUTOINCREMENT, status TEXT CHECK IN ('pending','running','completed','failed'), records_processed INT, started_at TEXT, completed_at TEXT). field_mappings (source_field TEXT, target_field TEXT, transform_fn TEXT). validation_errors (id INTEGER PK AUTOINCREMENT, batch_id INT REFERENCES migration_checkpoints, sku TEXT, error_type TEXT, detail TEXT, occurred_at TEXT). rollback_logs (id INTEGER PK AUTOINCREMENT, batch_id INT, target_product_id TEXT, action TEXT DEFAULT 'created', reverted_at TEXT).
target_output.api_design: Target platform REST calls via httpx. POST /api/v2/products/bulk: body is JSON array of up to 500 product objects; returns {success_count, failed_ids[]}. DELETE /api/v2/products/{target_id}: used by rollback command per logged target_product_id. GET /api/v2/products?sku={sku}: used by validate command to detect duplicate SKUs before upsert. All requests: Authorization: Bearer {TARGET_API_KEY} header. Retry policy: 3 retries with 1s/2s/4s exponential backoff on 429 or 5xx; 4xx non-429 logged as validation_errors without retry.
target_output.security_and_compliance: TARGET_API_KEY and MAGENTO_DSN read exclusively from environment variables; custom Python LogFilter replaces their values with '***' in all log records. .env file excluded by .gitignore; .env.example committed with placeholders. SQLite migration_state.db created with os.chmod(path, 0o600) on first run. HTTPS enforced for all target API calls; httpx SSL verification enabled by default. No customer PII in scope; migrating internal product catalog only.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Distributed as pip wheel installable via 'pip install mig-tool --index-url https://internal-pypi.company.com/simple'. Docker image built from python:3.12-slim with tool pre-installed; operator mounts .env file as volume. GitHub Actions pipeline: ruff lint check, mypy --strict type check, pytest suite with in-memory SQLite, build wheel, push to internal PyPI on semver tag. Operator workflow: set TARGET_API_KEY and MAGENTO_DSN env vars, run 'mig status' to confirm clean state, run 'mig migrate --mode=full', monitor tqdm progress, run 'mig status' post-migration to verify all batches completed.
target_output.observability: tqdm progress bar on stderr showing 'Batch 42/200 [SKUs: 20500/100000] ETA: 2h14m'. Python logging to stderr: WARNING by default; --verbose enables DEBUG with per-batch fields: batch_id, records_processed, api_latency_ms, http_status. Structured log format: '{timestamp} {level} batch={batch_id} records={n} latency_ms={ms}'. Error CSV at ./migration_errors_{timestamp}.csv with columns: sku, error_type, detail, batch_id, occurred_at. status sub-command prints checkpoint table: batch_id, status, records_processed, started_at, completed_at.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero infrastructure cost; runs on operator workstation. Batch size 500 with Semaphore(5) concurrent workers processes a 100K-SKU catalog in approximately 4-6 hours. For large catalogs exceeding 500K SKUs, run on a dedicated EC2 instance in the same region as the target API to reduce network latency. Docker image enables consistent execution environment across migration team members.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (2 weeks): migrate command with --mode=full, YAML field mapping loader with price_to_cents and strip_html transforms, SQLite checkpoint and rollback_logs, error CSV export, basic logging. Phase 2 (1 week): validate dry-run command with target API schema check, rollback command, status command with progress table, --verbose flag. Phase 3 (1 week): --mode=incremental comparing Magento updated_at against last checkpoint timestamp, tqdm progress bars, pip wheel and Docker image packaging.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python type hints throughout; mypy --strict in CI. ruff for linting and import sorting. pytest with 85% coverage minimum; use sqlite3 in-memory database for all checkpoint tests. No external network calls in unit tests; mock httpx with respx. Click CliRunner for command integration tests. All secrets via environment variables; never assert credential values in logs.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Magento MySQL table scans on catalog_product_entity (potentially millions of rows) could impact store performance if run during business hours. Mitigation: run only during maintenance window; use PyMySQL SSCursor for streaming to avoid full-table memory load. Risk: Target platform API rate limits (e.g., 1000 req/min) causing migration to exceed 8-hour window. Mitigation: configurable --workers flag reduces concurrency; automatic back-off on 429 responses. Tradeoff: SQLite chosen over PostgreSQL for zero-infrastructure portability at the cost of no concurrent multi-process writes, which is acceptable since the CLI is single-process.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Click over argparse for structured sub-command routing. Chose SQLite over flat-file JSON checkpoints to enable atomic batch transactions. httpx chosen over requests for native asyncio support in batch POSTing. PyMySQL selected over SQLAlchemy to avoid ORM overhead for read-only Magento source queries.

metadata.notes: CLI migration tool for Magento 1.x product catalog; weak quality tier with acceptable but thin sections.

project_goal.value: Migrate a large specialty retailer's on-premise inventory and ERP data stores from bare-metal SQL Server to Azure SQL Managed Instance, containerize inventory microservices, and deploy to AKS while maintaining SOC 2 Type II and ISO 27001 certifications
project_goal.rationale: Legacy bare-metal SQL Server reaching hardware EOL; retailer needs cloud elasticity for seasonal inventory spikes

target_users.value: Platform engineering team, infrastructure SREs, and inventory application teams consuming the modernized platform
target_users.rationale: Internal engineering teams are the primary consumers of this infrastructure migration

project_class.rationale: This is a platform and environment migration; no application UI is being built

capabilities.rationale: Terraform/Helm IaC, containerized microservices, and database migration are the core capability domains

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-terabyte SQL Server migration, multi-cluster AKS topology, VNet peering, and SOC 2 compliance controls make this high-scale

risk_level.rationale: Live ERP and inventory data migration with compliance certification continuity risk

data_sensitivity.rationale: ERP data contains supplier contracts, cost prices, and proprietary inventory planning data

external_exposure.rationale: AKS services exposed only on private VNet; no public internet endpoints

access_model.value: Azure AD workload identities for AKS pods; RBAC on Azure SQL MI; privileged access via Azure PIM with just-in-time elevation
access_model.rationale: Zero standing privilege for database and cluster access enforced by Azure AD

feature_scope.value: Terraform modules for AKS node pools, Azure SQL MI, VNet peering, and private endpoints; Helm charts for inventory microservices; Azure DMS replication jobs; canary rollout runbooks; cutover automation; rollback checkpoints
feature_scope.rationale: Full IaC and migration automation scope for repeatable, auditable deployments

mvp_scope.value: Dev AKS cluster provisioned via Terraform, inventory-service Helm chart deployed, Azure DMS replication job validating schema parity
mvp_scope.rationale: Prove end-to-end IaC and DMS pipeline before migrating staging and production

security_baseline.value: Private endpoints for Azure SQL MI, AKS network policies (deny-all default), Azure Key Vault for secrets, Azure Defender for Containers and SQL, TLS 1.3 for all inter-service communication
security_baseline.rationale: Compliance and security baseline required for SOC 2 and ISO 27001 continuity

frontend_stack.rationale: No browser UI; platform consumed by application teams

backend_stack.value: Terraform 1.7 + Helm 3 for IaC; inventory microservices containerized as Docker images
backend_stack.rationale: Terraform for Azure resource provisioning; Helm for Kubernetes workload deployment

data_platform.value: Azure SQL Managed Instance (migration target); on-premise SQL Server 2016 (source); Azure DMS for continuous replication
data_platform.rationale: Azure SQL MI provides near-full SQL Server compatibility for ERP and inventory schemas

hosting_target.value: AKS (Azure Kubernetes Service) in Azure West Europe region; system node pool (D4s v3) and inventory node pool (E8s v3 memory-optimized)
hosting_target.rationale: AKS for container orchestration; region matches existing Azure tenant

privacy_retention_policy.value: ERP and inventory data retained per existing on-premise policy (7 years); Azure SQL MI backups retained 35 days; Azure Monitor logs retained 90 days
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Retention policies must match existing compliance commitments

future_scope.value: Migrate remaining ERP modules (procurement, financials), enable Azure Autoscale for AKS node pools during seasonal peaks, implement GitOps with Flux v2
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 roadmap items after inventory migration stabilizes

constraints.value: Zero downtime cutover required for inventory service; all resources in Azure West Europe; SOC 2 and ISO 27001 certifications must be maintained throughout migration
constraints.rationale: Business continuity and compliance certification continuity are non-negotiable

observability_baseline.value: Azure Monitor + Grafana dashboards for AKS cluster and SQL MI metrics; PagerDuty alerts for DMS replication lag >5min; Azure Log Analytics for audit logs
observability_baseline.rationale: Platform team requires unified observability during migration and steady state

execution_preference.value: Staged migration: dev first, then staging with load testing, then production canary cutover
execution_preference.rationale: Progressive risk reduction through environment staging

compliance_context.value: SOC 2 Type II and ISO 27001; retailer's existing certifications must be maintained through migration; Azure Policy assignments enforce compliance guardrails
compliance_context.rationale: Certification continuity is a contractual obligation with the retailer's enterprise customers

project.goal: Migrate on-premise SQL Server ERP and inventory to Azure SQL MI; containerize inventory microservices on AKS
project.target_users: Platform SREs and inventory application teams

backend.framework: Azure provider for Terraform; Helm charts for inventory microservices
backend.api: Internal AKS services on private VNet

security.auth: Azure AD workload identities; Azure PIM for privileged access
security.transport: TLS 1.3; private endpoints; AKS network policies

data.db: Azure SQL Managed Instance; source: SQL Server 2016
data.cache: Azure Cache for Redis for inventory session state

devops.ci: Azure DevOps Pipelines for Terraform plan/apply and Helm release

reasoner_reviews.product: The scope is well-defined for an infrastructure migration program. Terraform modules for AKS and SQL MI ensure repeatable provisioning. The staged migration approach (dev -> staging -> production canary) appropriately manages risk for a live ERP system.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Azure DMS continuous replication mode for SQL Server to SQL MI supports near-zero downtime cutover, which is the core constraint. Helm canary rollout (10% traffic to new pods, monitor, promote) for inventory microservices reduces cutover risk. Network policies with deny-all default and private endpoints are the right security posture for an internal-only platform.
reasoner_reviews.security: SOC 2 Type II continuity requires that Azure Policy assignments enforce encryption-at-rest, private endpoint usage, and audit log forwarding to Log Analytics. Azure Defender for SQL must be enabled on SQL MI. Azure PIM just-in-time elevation must replace all standing privileged accounts used on the on-premise SQL Server.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Zero-downtime cutover is achievable with DMS continuous replication: replicate to SQL MI, validate row counts and integrity checks, switch application connection strings via Azure App Configuration, confirm replication lag reaches zero, then stop DMS job. Rollback is reverting App Configuration connection string values.

backend.service_design: Terraform modules: aks-cluster (node pools, RBAC, add-ons), sql-mi (vCores, storage, HA pair), vnet-peering (on-prem ExpressRoute to Azure VNet), key-vault (secrets, rotation policies). Helm charts: inventory-service (Deployment, HPA, PodDisruptionBudget, NetworkPolicy), redis-session (StatefulSet)
backend.api_patterns: AKS inventory-service pods communicate with Azure SQL MI via private endpoint on port 1433; Redis session cache on port 6380 TLS; no public endpoints
backend.failure_handling: Helm rollback: 'helm rollback inventory-service' reverts to last deployed chart version; Azure SQL MI auto-failover group provides HA within West Europe; DMS rollback: revert App Configuration connection string to on-premise SQL Server DSN
backend.scaling_notes: AKS Cluster Autoscaler on inventory node pool: min 3, max 20 E8s v3 nodes; HPA on inventory-service: min 3, max 50 replicas on CPU 70% target

security.auth_design: AKS pods use Azure AD Workload Identity (federated credentials) to authenticate to Azure SQL MI and Key Vault; no service account passwords in Kubernetes secrets
security.authorization_model: Azure RBAC on AKS and SQL MI; Azure PIM for Contributor-level access with 8-hour maximum elevation and approval workflow; network policies: inventory-service pods allowed egress only to sql-mi private endpoint and redis private endpoint
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Azure Policy: audit log forwarding to Log Analytics Workspace enforced; SQL MI Auditing to Storage Account with 90-day retention; AKS audit logs to Log Analytics; Azure Monitor alerts on policy compliance drift
security.security_risks: DMS service requires inbound connectivity from Azure DMS IP ranges to on-premise SQL Server; mitigated by firewall allowlist for DMS IPs only with ExpressRoute private peering

data.entities: database_migration_plans (migration_id, source_instance, target_mi, schema_list, status, validated_at); data_replication_jobs (job_id, dms_activity_id, replication_lag_seconds, last_row_count_check, parity_status); cutover_runbooks (runbook_id, step_number, action, expected_duration_min, rollback_action); rollback_checkpoints (checkpoint_id, migration_phase, connection_string_snapshot, created_at)
data.storage_design: Azure SQL MI Business Critical tier (16 vCores, 280GB RAM) for inventory and ERP schemas; Azure Cache for Redis 6 (C4 Standard) for inventory session state; all storage encrypted with Customer Managed Keys in Azure Key Vault
data.migration_notes: Azure DMS online migration mode: full database load then continuous change data capture until cutover; row count validation queries run every 15 minutes; cutover approved only when replication lag <10 seconds and row counts match within 0.01%

devops.deployment_topology: Azure DevOps Pipeline: stage 1 Terraform plan (PR validation), stage 2 Terraform apply (main branch merge), stage 3 Helm diff review, stage 4 Helm upgrade with canary annotation. Environments: dev (single-AZ AKS, General Purpose SQL MI), staging (multi-AZ AKS, Business Critical SQL MI, load test with k6), production (multi-AZ AKS, Business Critical SQL MI with auto-failover group)
devops.environments: dev, staging, production each as separate Terraform workspaces with separate Azure resource groups and Azure AD app registrations
devops.rollback_strategy: Application: helm rollback inventory-service [revision]. Database: revert App Configuration connection string to on-premise DSN (validated within 30 seconds). Infrastructure: Terraform state rollback to previous approved plan via Azure DevOps pipeline gate
devops.observability_stack: Azure Monitor Container Insights for AKS node and pod metrics; Grafana dashboards for DMS replication lag, SQL MI DTU utilization, AKS HPA replica counts; PagerDuty alerts: replication lag >5min, pod CrashLoopBackOff, SQL MI CPU >85%

ISS-001.title: DMS replication lag monitoring not wired to PagerDuty
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan described Azure Monitor alerts but did not specify the DMS replication lag metric name or PagerDuty routing key; observability gap during cutover window

ISS-002.title: AKS network policies missing egress allow for SQL MI private endpoint
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 NetworkPolicy spec used deny-all egress without explicitly allowing TCP 1433 to the sql-mi private endpoint CIDR, which would block all inventory-service database connections

ISS-003.title: Rollback checkpoint does not snapshot App Configuration connection string
ISS-003.detail: Rollback runbook describes reverting connection strings but rollback_checkpoints entity does not store the pre-cutover connection string value, making automated rollback unreliable

focus_issues.title: Rollback checkpoint does not snapshot App Configuration connection string
focus_issues.detail: Rollback runbook describes reverting connection strings but rollback_checkpoints entity does not store the pre-cutover connection string value, making automated rollback unreliable

latest_recommendations: Add connection_string_snapshot field to rollback_checkpoints and populate it in the cutover automation script before switching App Configuration values
Document the rollback test procedure in the cutover runbook with a go/no-go decision point

previous_plan.executive_summary: Terraform-managed AKS and Azure SQL MI platform for migrating bare-metal SQL Server ERP and inventory to Azure, with Azure DMS continuous replication and Helm canary rollout for inventory microservices. ISS-001 (DMS lag alerting) and ISS-002 (NetworkPolicy egress) resolved; ISS-003 (rollback checkpoint gap) pending.
previous_plan.data_model: rollback_checkpoints (checkpoint_id, migration_phase, created_at) -- missing connection_string_snapshot field.
previous_plan.deployment_and_operations: Cutover runbook step 4: switch App Configuration connection string to Azure SQL MI. Rollback step: revert connection string -- value sourced from undocumented location.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 focus is resolving ISS-003: the rollback_checkpoints entity must store the pre-cutover App Configuration connection string snapshot so automated rollback can reliably revert without operator lookup. target_output.title: Specialty Retailer On-Premise to Azure Infrastructure Migration
target_output.executive_summary: Terraform-managed AKS and Azure SQL MI platform migrating a specialty retailer's bare-metal SQL Server 2016 ERP and inventory databases to Azure West Europe with zero-downtime cutover. Azure DMS continuous replication with automated row-count validation drives the database migration. Inventory microservices containerized and deployed via Helm canary rollout on AKS. SOC 2 Type II and ISO 27001 certification continuity enforced via Azure Policy assignments and Azure Defender. ISS-003 resolved in this round by adding pre-cutover connection string snapshots to the rollback automation.
target_output.architecture_overview: Hub-and-spoke Azure VNet architecture with ExpressRoute private peering to the retailer's on-premise data center. AKS cluster in West Europe with system node pool (D4s v3, min 3 nodes) and inventory node pool (E8s v3 memory-optimized, min 3 max 20 nodes with Cluster Autoscaler). Azure SQL Managed Instance Business Critical tier (16 vCores) accessed via private endpoint from inventory-service pods using Azure AD Workload Identity. Azure DMS online migration mode maintains continuous replication from on-premise SQL Server 2016 until cutover. Azure Cache for Redis (C4 Standard) for inventory session state. Azure Key Vault for all secrets with Customer Managed Keys. Azure DevOps Pipelines for Terraform apply and Helm release with environment gates.
target_output.technology_stack: IaC: Terraform 1.7 with Azure provider 3.x; modules for aks-cluster, sql-mi, vnet-peering, key-vault, private-endpoints, policy-assignments. Container orchestration: AKS 1.29 with Helm 3.14 for inventory microservices. Database: Azure SQL Managed Instance Business Critical (source: SQL Server 2016 on-premise). Migration: Azure Database Migration Service online mode. Cache: Azure Cache for Redis 6. CI/CD: Azure DevOps Pipelines with Terraform Cloud state backend. Observability: Azure Monitor, Container Insights, Grafana, Log Analytics Workspace, PagerDuty.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Terraform aks-cluster module provisioning node pools, Azure AD RBAC, Container Insights add-on, and Cluster Autoscaler. Terraform sql-mi module provisioning Business Critical instance, auto-failover group, private endpoint, and Defender for SQL. Azure DMS online migration job with continuous CDC replication and automated row-count validation queries. Helm inventory-service chart with Deployment, HPA (min 3, max 50 replicas), PodDisruptionBudget, and NetworkPolicy. Cutover automation script: snapshot connection string to rollback_checkpoints, switch App Configuration, validate response times, promote or rollback. Rollback script: read connection_string_snapshot from rollback_checkpoints, revert App Configuration, notify PagerDuty.
target_output.system_components: ExpressRoute private peering gateway connecting on-premise SQL Server to Azure VNet. Azure DMS service replicating inventory and ERP schemas via CDC. Azure SQL MI Business Critical instance with auto-failover group (primary West Europe, geo-replica North Europe). AKS cluster with inventory-service Deployment (3-50 pods), Redis StatefulSet, and deny-all NetworkPolicy with targeted egress allows. Azure Key Vault storing SQL MI connection strings and Redis TLS certificates. Azure App Configuration storing environment-specific connection strings switched during cutover. Azure DevOps Pipelines with Terraform Cloud state and environment approval gates. Azure Monitor + Grafana for DMS lag, SQL MI DTU, and AKS HPA dashboards.
target_output.workflows: DMS Continuous Replication to Cutover: Azure DMS online migration job starts full load of inventory and ERP schemas to SQL MI. After full load, CDC captures all on-premise SQL Server transactions and applies to SQL MI. Automated validation queries run every 15 minutes: SELECT COUNT(*) on 12 key tables; parity confirmed when counts match within 0.01% and replication lag <10 seconds. Cutover: automation script inserts row into rollback_checkpoints with current App Configuration connection string snapshot, then calls 'az appconfig kv set --name retailer-appconfig --key inventory-db-dsn --value {sql-mi-dsn}'. Inventory-service pods pick up new DSN on next config refresh (30-second poll). DMS replication stopped. Rollback Execution: If post-cutover health checks fail (p99 latency >500ms or error rate >2% within 5 minutes), rollback script reads connection_string_snapshot from rollback_checkpoints, calls 'az appconfig kv set' to revert DSN, PagerDuty incident auto-resolved.
target_output.data_model: database_migration_plans (migration_id UUID PK, source_instance VARCHAR, target_mi VARCHAR, schema_list TEXT, status TEXT CHECK IN ('planned','replicating','validated','cut_over','complete'), validated_at DATETIME). data_replication_jobs (job_id UUID PK, dms_activity_id VARCHAR, replication_lag_seconds INT, last_row_count_check DATETIME, parity_status TEXT). cutover_runbooks (runbook_id UUID PK, step_number INT, action TEXT, expected_duration_min INT, rollback_action TEXT, completed_at DATETIME). rollback_checkpoints (checkpoint_id UUID PK, migration_phase TEXT, connection_string_snapshot TEXT NOT NULL, created_at DATETIME) -- connection_string_snapshot populated by cutover automation before App Configuration switch; used by rollback script to revert DSN without manual lookup. container_manifests (manifest_id UUID PK, service_name TEXT, helm_chart_version VARCHAR, deployed_at DATETIME, rollback_revision INT). network_policies (policy_id UUID PK, namespace TEXT, policy_yaml TEXT, applied_at DATETIME).
target_output.api_design: No public API surface; infrastructure-only project. Internal integration points: Azure App Configuration REST API (az appconfig kv set/get) used by cutover and rollback scripts. Azure DMS REST API (GET /subscriptions/{sub}/providers/Microsoft.DataMigration/services/{name}/projects/{proj}/tasks/{task}) polled every 60 seconds for replication_lag_seconds. AKS API Server accessed via Azure AD kubeconfig for Helm operations. Azure Resource Manager API used by Terraform for all resource CRUD. All API calls authenticated via Azure AD Workload Identity or Azure DevOps service connection.
target_output.security_and_compliance: SOC 2 Type II and ISO 27001 continuity enforced via Azure Policy assignments: deny-if-no-private-endpoint for SQL MI, deny-if-encryption-at-rest-disabled, audit-if-log-analytics-not-configured. Azure Defender for Containers enabled on AKS; Defender for SQL on SQL MI. AKS NetworkPolicy: deny-all default with explicit egress allows for inventory-service to sql-mi private endpoint (TCP 1433) and redis private endpoint (TCP 6380). Azure AD Workload Identity for pod-to-Azure-resource authentication; no Kubernetes secrets storing database passwords. Azure PIM: 8-hour maximum elevation for Contributor role; approval required; all activations logged to Log Analytics. Azure Key Vault Customer Managed Keys for SQL MI encryption at rest. TLS 1.3 enforced for all inter-service communication.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Azure DevOps Pipeline stages: 1) Terraform plan on PR with plan output as PR comment; 2) Terraform apply on main merge with environment approval gate (platform-lead approval required for staging and production); 3) Helm diff review job generating kubectl diff output; 4) Helm upgrade with --atomic flag and canary annotation (10% traffic via Nginx ingress weight). Environments: dev (single-AZ AKS, General Purpose SQL MI 4 vCores), staging (multi-AZ AKS, Business Critical SQL MI 8 vCores, k6 load test at 1000 RPS), production (multi-AZ AKS, Business Critical SQL MI 16 vCores with auto-failover group). Cutover rehearsal run on staging environment 2 weeks before production cutover, including rollback test verifying connection_string_snapshot retrieval and App Configuration revert.
target_output.observability: Azure Monitor Container Insights: AKS node CPU/memory, pod restart counts, HPA replica count. Grafana dashboards: DMS replication lag (Microsoft.DataMigration/services/replicationLag metric), SQL MI DTU utilization and connection count, inventory-service p50/p99 latency and error rate, Redis cache hit ratio. PagerDuty alerts: DMS replication lag >5 minutes (P1), AKS pod CrashLoopBackOff >2 times (P2), SQL MI DTU >85% sustained 10 minutes (P2), Azure Policy compliance drift (P3). Log Analytics Workspace: AKS audit logs, SQL MI audit logs (90-day retention), Azure Policy compliance logs.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated Azure spend: AKS nodes (3x D4s v3 system + 3-20x E8s v3 inventory) ~$4,200-$18,000/month variable with Cluster Autoscaler. Azure SQL MI Business Critical 16 vCores ~$6,800/month. Redis C4 Standard ~$400/month. ExpressRoute Standard ~$800/month. Azure DMS (during migration only) ~$300/month. Total steady-state ~$12,200/month vs estimated $15,000/month on-premise hardware/licensing. Azure Reserved Instances for AKS baseline and SQL MI provide 40% cost reduction on 3-year term.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - Dev Environment (6 weeks): Terraform modules for AKS, SQL MI, VNet, and private endpoints in dev environment. Helm chart for inventory-service with NetworkPolicy. Azure DMS job replicating dev schema from on-premise snapshot. Phase 2 - Staging Migration (8 weeks): Staging environment with full Business Critical SQL MI. DMS continuous replication from production SQL Server clone. k6 load test at 1000 RPS validating p99 <200ms. Cutover rehearsal with rollback test verifying rollback_checkpoints. Phase 3 - Production Cutover (4 weeks): Production DMS replication, row-count validation, maintenance-window cutover execution with 5-minute rollback SLA, post-cutover stabilization. Phase 4 - Decommission (2 weeks): On-premise SQL Server instances decommissioned after 30-day parallel run, ExpressRoute resized.
target_output.development_guidelines: Terraform: modules in /modules, environments in /environments/{dev,staging,prod}; all resources tagged with env, team, cost-center. Terraform fmt and tflint in CI. No hardcoded values; all via variable files and Key Vault references. Helm: chart version bumped on every change; values overrides per environment in /helm/values/{dev,staging,prod}.yaml. Rollback revision must be set in container_manifests after every successful deploy. Cutover scripts in /scripts/cutover/ with unit tests (bats). Runbook steps documented in /runbooks/ as Markdown with explicit rollback_action per step.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Azure DMS CDC replication falling behind during high transaction volume on on-premise SQL Server before cutover. Mitigation: DMS job monitored by PagerDuty alert at 5-minute lag; cutover blocked if lag exceeds threshold at time-of-cutover. Risk: AKS pod restarts during connection string refresh causing brief inventory-service downtime. Mitigation: Pod graceful termination period 60 seconds; HPA minimum 3 replicas ensures availability during rolling restart. Risk ISS-003 (resolved): automated rollback was unreliable without stored connection string snapshot. Fix: rollback_checkpoints now stores pre-cutover DSN; verified in staging cutover rehearsal. Tradeoff: Azure SQL MI Business Critical tier chosen over General Purpose for in-memory OLTP and faster failover (20-30 seconds vs 60-120 seconds), accepting higher cost.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed Azure DMS online mode over offline migration to achieve zero-downtime cutover. Selected App Configuration for connection string switching over Kubernetes ConfigMap to enable instant revert without pod restart. Chose Business Critical SQL MI tier for faster auto-failover SLA. Confirmed ExpressRoute private peering over VPN Gateway for consistent low-latency DMS replication.

metadata.notes: Infrastructure modernization: bare-metal SQL Server to Azure SQL MI with AKS; round 3 resolves ISS-003 rollback checkpoint gap.

project_goal.value: High-scale web application enabling thousands of independent sellers to manage product listings, pricing rules, and inventory levels with real-time flash sale support and seller payout processing
project_goal.rationale: Core marketplace platform replacing legacy monolith unable to handle flash sale traffic spikes

target_users.rationale: Three distinct user roles with different access patterns

project_class.rationale: Browser-based marketplace with real-time features and seller dashboard

capabilities.rationale: Full marketplace capability set including real-time inventory and seller payouts

mvp_scope.rationale: Core marketplace loop before flash sale and dispute features

project.goal: Build high-scale seller marketplace with flash sale inventory and payout processing
project.target_users: Independent sellers, buyers, marketplace admins

data.cache: ElastiCache Redis 7 cluster mode

reasoner_reviews.product: The marketplace scope is well-defined with clear differentiation between seller, buyer, and admin roles. Flash sale as a first-class feature is a strong differentiator. The real-time inventory reservation SLA (sub-100ms) is aggressive and needs careful architecture to avoid Redis becoming a bottleneck during peak flash sale events.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Redis atomic INCR/DECR operations on inventory_reservations:{sku_id} counters with Lua scripting for compare-and-decrement are the right approach for sub-100ms reservation under flash sale load. Fastify with cluster mode on EKS handles high concurrency. However, the plan should clarify the split between Stripe for card tokenization and the internal payout ledger for seller settlements.
reasoner_reviews.security: PCI-DSS Level 1 requires quarterly external ASV scans, annual on-site QSA assessment, and network segmentation of the cardholder data environment. Card numbers must never touch the application servers -- Stripe.js handles tokenization client-side. Seller payout bank account details are sensitive financial data requiring field-level encryption in the database.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Sub-100ms inventory reservation is achievable with Redis Lua scripts but requires Redis cluster mode with read replicas to avoid single-shard bottlenecks during flash sales. EKS HPA should scale on custom inventory reservation RPS metric, not just CPU, to preemptively scale before flash sale events begin.

frontend.app_structure: React SPA with route-based code splitting; seller dashboard, buyer catalog, and admin panel as separate lazy-loaded route chunks
frontend.pages_and_flows: Seller: ListingManager, PricingRuleEditor, InventoryOverview, FlashSaleCreator, PayoutHistory. Buyer: ProductCatalog, FlashSaleCountdown, CartCheckout. Admin: SellerVerification, DisputeQueue, PayoutApprovals
frontend.state_management: React Query for server state (listings, inventory levels); Zustand for flash sale countdown timer and cart state; WebSocket connection for real-time inventory reservation updates
frontend.performance_notes: FlashSaleCountdown component uses requestAnimationFrame for countdown ticker; ProductCatalog virtualizes seller listing grids with react-window for catalogs >1000 SKUs

backend.service_design: Fastify app with domain plugins: listings (CRUD, pricing rules), inventory (Redis-backed reservation), flash-sales (event lifecycle), payouts (Stripe Connect), disputes (workflow), admin (verification queue)
backend.api_patterns: REST with OpenAPI 3.0; versioned at /api/v1; webhook receiver for Stripe Connect payout events at /webhooks/stripe
backend.business_modules: Inventory reservation: Lua script atomically checks flash_sale_events.remaining_qty Redis key and decrements if >0; returns reservation token or SOLD_OUT. Pricing rule engine evaluates seller_accounts.pricing_rules JSONB against buyer segment and quantity. Payout processor: batch job reconciles seller_payouts with Stripe Connect transfer statuses nightly.
backend.failure_handling: Inventory reservation timeout (>100ms Redis op): return HTTP 503 to buyer with retry-after header. Stripe payout webhook failure: SQS dead-letter queue for manual retry. Idempotency keys on all payment endpoints.

security.auth_design: Auth0 Universal Login with RBAC; seller and admin MFA required; buyer MFA optional. JWT validation on every Fastify route via auth0-fastify-plugin.
security.authorization_model: Sellers can only mutate their own product_listings and pricing_rules (row-level policy via seller_id claim). Admins can read all seller data. Buyers cannot access seller financial data.
security.abuse_prevention: WAF on ALB: AWS WAF with managed rule groups for OWASP Top 10 and rate limiting 500 req/min per IP. Flash sale abuse: limit 1 reservation per buyer per flash_sale_event_id via Redis SET NX with 5-minute TTL.
security.privacy_controls: GDPR Article 17: account deletion job anonymizes seller_accounts and buyer_profiles within 30 days; retains seller_payouts and dispute_cases for 7-year financial audit. Stripe.js client-side tokenization; card numbers never reach application servers.

data.entities: seller_accounts (id, auth0_sub, business_name, verification_status, pricing_rules JSONB, bank_account_token_encrypted, created_at). product_listings (id, seller_id FK, sku, title, description, base_price_cents, inventory_qty, status ENUM active/paused/sold_out). pricing_rules (id, listing_id FK, rule_type ENUM volume/flash/segment, conditions JSONB, discount_pct, valid_from, valid_until). inventory_reservations (id, listing_id FK, buyer_id, quantity, reservation_token UUID, expires_at, status ENUM held/confirmed/released). flash_sale_events (id, seller_id FK, listing_id FK, sale_price_cents, start_at, end_at, total_qty, remaining_qty_key VARCHAR). seller_payouts (id, seller_id FK, amount_cents, currency, stripe_transfer_id, status ENUM pending/processing/paid/failed, initiated_at). dispute_cases (id, listing_id FK, buyer_id, seller_id FK, reason TEXT, status ENUM open/under_review/resolved, resolution TEXT, created_at).
data.storage_design: RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ for transactional data; ElastiCache Redis 7 cluster mode with 6 shards for inventory reservation counters and session tokens; S3 for product listing images
data.analytics_events: listing_viewed, flash_sale_started, inventory_reserved, inventory_reservation_failed, payout_initiated, dispute_opened -- published to Kinesis Data Streams for real-time analytics

devops.deployment_topology: EKS cluster with marketplace-api Deployment (HPA min 5, max 100 pods), marketplace-web Deployment, and payout-worker Deployment. RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ in private subnets. ElastiCache Redis cluster mode in private subnets. ALB with WAF for public traffic.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: ESLint + TypeScript check, Jest unit tests (85% coverage gate), integration tests against ephemeral PostgreSQL/Redis, Docker build, ECR push, Helm upgrade on EKS
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM: trace every /api/v1/inventory/reserve call with flash_sale_id tag; custom metric marketplace.inventory.reservation_latency_ms; alert on p99 >80ms. PagerDuty: inventory reservation error rate >1%, Stripe webhook failure rate >5%.

target_output.thinking_summary: This is an intentionally flawed plan for training purposes. The plan introduces two deliberate inconsistencies: (1) the deployment_and_operations section describes a serverless AWS Lambda architecture that contradicts the contract's EKS/Kubernetes hosting target, and (2) the data_model uses generic Users, Orders, and Products entities instead of the domain-specific seller_accounts, product_listings, flash_sale_events, and inventory_reservations entities from the specialist subplans. These flaws represent the kinds of contract violations and internal inconsistencies the auditor should flag.
target_output.title: High-Scale Seller Marketplace Platform Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A public marketplace platform enabling independent sellers to manage product listings, pricing rules, and inventory levels, with real-time flash sale inventory reservation and seller payout processing. Built with React 18, Node.js 22 Fastify, PostgreSQL 16, and Redis 7 on AWS with Auth0 authentication and Stripe Connect for payouts.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-tier web application with React 18 SPA served via CloudFront, Node.js 22 Fastify API cluster behind an ALB with WAF, and RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ for persistence. ElastiCache Redis 7 cluster mode provides sub-100ms inventory reservation via atomic Lua scripts on flash_sale counters. Auth0 handles seller, buyer, and admin authentication with RBAC JWT validation. Stripe.js tokenizes payment cards client-side; Stripe Connect manages seller payout transfers. WebSocket connections (via API Gateway WebSocket) push real-time inventory level updates to buyer catalog pages during flash sales.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: React 18 TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, React Query, Zustand, react-window for virtualized listing grids. Backend: Node.js 22, Fastify 5.x, Prisma ORM, auth0-fastify-plugin, Stripe SDK. Database: RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ; ElastiCache Redis 7 cluster (6 shards). Auth: Auth0 RBAC with MFA. Payments: Stripe Connect for seller payouts; Stripe.js client-side tokenization. Analytics: Kinesis Data Streams, Datadog custom metrics. CI/CD: GitHub Actions, ECR, Helm on EKS.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Seller Listing Manager: create/edit/pause product_listings with base_price_cents and inventory_qty. Pricing Rule Editor: configure volume discounts, flash sale prices, and buyer segment rules stored as pricing_rules JSONB. Flash Sale Creator: define flash_sale_events with time window, sale_price_cents, and total_qty; Redis key initialized on event start. Real-Time Inventory Reservation: Lua script atomically decrements flash_sale_events.remaining_qty in Redis; returns reservation_token or SOLD_OUT. Payout Dashboard: seller views seller_payouts history and initiates bank transfers via Stripe Connect. Dispute Queue: admin reviews dispute_cases with buyer and seller context.
target_output.system_components: CloudFront distribution for React SPA static assets. ALB with AWS WAF (OWASP managed rules, 500 req/min rate limit per IP). EKS cluster with marketplace-api HPA (min 5, max 100 pods), marketplace-web Deployment, and payout-worker Deployment. RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ in private VPC subnets. ElastiCache Redis 7 cluster mode (6 shards) for inventory reservation counters and session tokens. S3 bucket for product listing images. Kinesis Data Streams for analytics event pipeline. Stripe Connect for seller payout transfers.
target_output.workflows: Flash Sale Inventory Reservation: Buyer requests POST /api/v1/inventory/reserve with {listing_id, flash_sale_event_id, quantity}. Fastify handler executes Redis Lua script: EVAL 'local remaining = redis.call(GET, KEYS[1]) if remaining >= ARGV[1] then redis.call(DECRBY, KEYS[1], ARGV[1]) return 1 else return 0 end'. If script returns 1, inserts inventory_reservations row with 5-minute expiry and returns reservation_token. If 0, returns HTTP 409 SOLD_OUT. Reservation expiry job (every 60s): queries inventory_reservations WHERE expires_at < NOW() AND status = 'held', increments Redis counter back, sets status = 'released'. Seller Pricing Rule Propagation: Seller updates pricing rule via PATCH /api/v1/listings/{id}/pricing-rules. Fastify validates rule conditions JSONB schema. Prisma writes pricing_rules row. Cache invalidation: DELETE Redis key listings:{listing_id}:active_rules. Elasticsearch index updated within 30 seconds via CDC connector for buyer search.
target_output.data_model: Users (id UUID PK, email VARCHAR, password_hash VARCHAR, role ENUM user/admin, created_at TIMESTAMP). Orders (id UUID PK, user_id UUID FK REFERENCES Users, total_amount DECIMAL, status ENUM pending/paid/shipped/cancelled, created_at TIMESTAMP). Products (id UUID PK, name VARCHAR, description TEXT, price DECIMAL, stock_qty INT, seller_id UUID FK REFERENCES Users, created_at TIMESTAMP). OrderItems (id UUID PK, order_id UUID FK REFERENCES Orders, product_id UUID FK REFERENCES Products, quantity INT, unit_price DECIMAL). Payments (id UUID PK, order_id UUID FK REFERENCES Orders, stripe_payment_intent_id VARCHAR, amount_cents INT, status ENUM pending/succeeded/failed, processed_at TIMESTAMP).
target_output.api_design: REST API at /api/v1 with OpenAPI 3.0 spec. Seller endpoints: POST /listings (create listing), PATCH /listings/{id} (update price/inventory), POST /listings/{id}/flash-sales (create flash sale event), GET /payouts (seller payout history), POST /payouts/initiate (trigger Stripe Connect transfer). Inventory: POST /inventory/reserve (atomic reservation with Lua script, returns reservation_token). Buyer: GET /listings?flash_sale=active (active flash sales feed). Admin: GET /admin/disputes (dispute queue), PATCH /admin/disputes/{id} (resolve dispute), GET /admin/sellers/pending-verification (verification queue). Webhook: POST /webhooks/stripe (Stripe Connect payout status updates, HMAC-verified).
target_output.security_and_compliance: PCI-DSS Level 1: Stripe.js client-side tokenization prevents card data from touching application servers. Network segmentation: RDS and Redis in private VPC subnets inaccessible from internet. Quarterly ASV scans and annual QSA assessment documented. GDPR: account deletion job anonymizes seller_accounts and buyer_profiles within 30 days; financial records (seller_payouts, dispute_cases) retained 7 years. Auth0 RBAC: seller/buyer/admin roles enforced via JWT claims on every Fastify route. WAF rate limiting: 500 req/min per IP on all public endpoints. Flash sale abuse: Redis SET NX key buyers:{buyer_id}:flash_sale:{event_id} with 5-minute TTL limits each buyer to one reservation per event.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Deployed as serverless AWS Lambda functions with API Gateway HTTP API. Each domain module (listings, inventory, flash-sales, payouts, disputes) deployed as a separate Lambda function with 512MB memory. Lambda cold starts mitigated via provisioned concurrency on inventory Lambda (10 instances always warm). RDS PostgreSQL connects via RDS Proxy to manage Lambda connection pooling. ElastiCache Redis accessed from Lambda in the same VPC. GitHub Actions: ESLint, TypeScript build, Jest tests, Serverless Framework deploy to dev/staging/prod. Auto-scaling handled by Lambda concurrency limits per function. No Kubernetes or Helm involved.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM: Lambda traces for inventory reservation with flash_sale_id tag; custom metric marketplace.inventory.reservation_latency_ms with p99 alert at 80ms. marketplace.flash_sale.sold_out_rate metric alerting when >20% of reservations return SOLD_OUT within 1 minute (indicates undersized inventory). Seller payout success rate metric with PagerDuty alert on <95% success. CloudWatch Logs for Lambda function logs with structured JSON and correlation IDs. Kinesis analytics events drive real-time seller dashboard charts via Datadog dashboards.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: EKS node group estimated $3,500-$12,000/month depending on flash sale frequency. RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ db.r6g.2xlarge ~$800/month. ElastiCache Redis cluster (6 shards) ~$1,200/month. CloudFront ~$200/month. Total ~$6,000-$14,000/month. During flash sales, HPA scales marketplace-api from 5 to 100 pods within 90 seconds based on custom inventory_reservation_rps metric. Reserved instances for baseline EKS nodes and RDS provide 30% savings on 1-year term.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (8 weeks): Seller listing management, pricing rules, Auth0 RBAC, basic inventory tracking, Stripe Connect seller onboarding, payout initiation. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Flash sale event creation, real-time inventory reservation with Redis Lua scripts, WebSocket inventory updates, flash sale countdown UI. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Dispute management workflow, admin verification queue, analytics event pipeline to Kinesis, seller payout history dashboard. Phase 4 (4 weeks): GDPR deletion automation, PCI-DSS audit preparation, Elasticsearch integration for buyer search with pricing rule propagation.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode for frontend and backend. ESLint + Prettier enforced in CI. 85% test coverage gate on Jest unit tests. Integration tests use ephemeral PostgreSQL 16 and Redis 7 containers via Docker Compose. Prisma migrations versioned in /prisma/migrations; never edit migration files after merge. Idempotency keys required on all payment and payout endpoints. Feature flags via LaunchDarkly for flash sale and payout feature rollout.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Redis single-shard hotspot if all flash sale inventory counters land on same shard. Mitigation: prefix flash_sale_events.remaining_qty key with shard hint {listing_id} to distribute across Redis cluster shards. Risk: Stripe Connect payout delays during high volume. Mitigation: SQS dead-letter queue for failed webhook processing; nightly reconciliation job compares seller_payouts against Stripe transfer API. Tradeoff: Stripe.js tokenization means no server-side card number handling but limits payment flexibility to Stripe-supported payment methods. Tradeoff: PostgreSQL row-level security for seller data isolation over application-layer filtering for defense-in-depth.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed Stripe Connect over Adyen for seller payouts based on Stripe's marketplace payout API maturity. Chose Redis Lua scripts over database pessimistic locking for inventory reservation to meet sub-100ms SLA. Selected ElastiCache Redis cluster mode over single-node for flash sale shard distribution. Confirmed EKS over ECS for HPA custom metrics on inventory reservation RPS.

project.goal: Pre-rendered static catalog for wholesale hardware SKUs with volume pricing and availability
project.target_users: Trade buyers and procurement managers

data.cache: CDN edge caching at Vercel

reasoner_reviews.product: The static catalog approach is ideal for a public product browser with no transactional features. Pre-rendering all SKU pages at build time ensures instant load times for trade buyers comparing specifications across hundreds of hardware SKUs. Nightly rebuilds keep pricing tiers and availability indicators current without any runtime infrastructure.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Next.js 14 static export (next export) with getStaticPaths for all SKU slugs is the correct pattern. Build-time inventory API calls should be parallelized with Promise.all to keep build time under 5 minutes for large catalogs. Volume pricing tier components should be pre-rendered as static HTML tables, not client-side rendered, to ensure search engine indexability.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The round 1 plan attempted to introduce a server-side data-fetching layer, which contradicts the static_website project class and the contract's explicit no-runtime-server constraint. This must be corrected in round 2.
reasoner_reviews.security: Static catalog has no authentication surface but must enforce CSP headers to prevent XSS via search parameter injection in the client-side filter UI. Next.js static export served via CDN should set strict cache-control headers to prevent stale SKU pricing data from being served after a rebuild. No PII collected; no cookie consent banner required under GDPR for this purely informational catalog.

frontend.app_structure: Next.js 14 pages directory with dynamic SKU routes at /products/[sku]; category index pages at /categories/[slug]; all pages generated via getStaticPaths/getStaticProps at build time
frontend.pages_and_flows: Home (featured categories), Category Index (SKU grid with filter by specification), SKU Detail (spec table, volume_pricing_tiers table, availability_indicator, spec sheet download link), About/Contact (static markdown content)
frontend.performance_notes: next/image for automatic WebP conversion of product photos; next/font for self-hosted Inter typeface; Tailwind CSS purge for minimal CSS bundle; target LCP <1.5s on Vercel Edge CDN
frontend.accessibility_notes: Spec tables use proper <table><th scope='col'> markup; availability indicators use aria-label describing stock level text; all product images have descriptive alt text from SKU specification data

security.auth_design: none; fully public static pages
security.abuse_prevention: Vercel Edge automatic DDoS protection; no input forms to abuse on catalog pages; CSP header: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' va.vercel-scripts.com
security.privacy_controls: Vercel Analytics uses privacy-first anonymized metrics with no cookies; no third-party tracking pixels; privacy policy page explaining analytics approach

data.entities: product_sku_pages (sku_id, product_name, category_slug, specifications_json, image_urls[], availability_indicator ENUM in_stock/low_stock/out_of_stock). volume_pricing_tiers (sku_id, min_qty, max_qty, unit_price_gbp). category_taxonomy (category_id, name, parent_category_id, slug). specification_sheets (sku_id, pdf_url, last_updated)
data.storage_design: no database; all catalog data fetched from inventory system REST API during Next.js build process and serialized into static page props

devops.deployment_topology: Vercel project connected to GitHub repo; automatic deployment on push to main; Vercel Edge Network distributes to 100+ PoPs globally

devops.observability_stack: Vercel Analytics dashboard: page views, unique visitors, Core Web Vitals (LCP, FID, CLS) per SKU page and category page. Build time monitoring in GitHub Actions logs.

target_output.title: Wholesale Hardware Supplier Product Catalog
target_output.executive_summary: A fully static Next.js 14 product catalog pre-rendering all hardware SKU pages with specifications, volume pricing tiers, and availability indicators at build time. Deployed to Vercel Edge Network with nightly GitHub Actions rebuilds to refresh catalog data from the inventory system API. No server runtime, no database, no user authentication required.
target_output.architecture_overview: Fully static Next.js 14 application using static export mode with no server runtime. All product_sku_pages generated at build time via getStaticPaths over all active SKU slugs and getStaticProps fetching spec data, volume_pricing_tiers, and availability_indicators from the inventory system REST API. Generated HTML/CSS/JS bundle deployed to Vercel Edge Network distributing from 100+ global PoPs. Nightly GitHub Actions workflow triggers Vercel deploy hook to rebuild with latest pricing and stock data. No database, no application server, no authentication layer.
target_output.technology_stack: Framework: Next.js 14 with TypeScript in static export mode (output: 'export' in next.config.ts). Styling: Tailwind CSS 3.x with custom hardware supplier design tokens. Build-time data: fetch() calls to inventory system REST API in getStaticProps. Images: next/image for WebP optimization at build time. Fonts: next/font with self-hosted Inter. Analytics: Vercel Analytics (privacy-first, no cookies). Hosting: Vercel Edge Network. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with Vercel CLI deploy.
target_output.functional_feature_map: SKU Detail Pages (/products/[sku]): product name, category breadcrumb, specification table (dimensions, material, thread standard, load rating), volume_pricing_tiers table (1-9 units, 10-49 units, 50-249 units, 250+ units prices in GBP), availability_indicator badge (in_stock/low_stock/out_of_stock from inventory API), specification sheet PDF download link. Category Index Pages (/categories/[slug]): filterable SKU grid by specification attribute. Search: client-side static search index built at build time via Pagefind over all SKU content.
target_output.system_components: Next.js build process fetching catalog data from inventory system API and generating static HTML for all SKU and category pages. Vercel Edge Network serving pre-rendered HTML from global CDN PoPs. GitHub Actions nightly cron job triggering Vercel deploy hook for data refresh. Pagefind static search index generated during build and served as static assets. Vercel Analytics tracking page views and Core Web Vitals. No Node.js application server, no database, no API gateway.
target_output.workflows: Build-Time Data Refresh: GitHub Actions cron (0 2 * * *) calls Vercel deploy hook URL. Vercel clones repo, runs next build. Build script calls inventory system GET /api/v1/products?active=true (returns all active SKUs). For each SKU, getStaticProps calls GET /api/v1/products/{sku}/pricing-tiers and GET /api/v1/products/{sku}/availability. All responses serialized into page props and rendered as static HTML. next export generates /out directory. Vercel deploys /out to Edge CDN. Total build time target <5 minutes for catalogs up to 5,000 SKUs. New SKU Page: Product manager adds SKU to inventory system. Next nightly build picks up new SKU via getStaticPaths returning updated SKU list. New /products/{sku} page generated and deployed automatically without code changes.
target_output.data_model: No database. Build-time data shapes: product_sku_pages {sku_id: string, product_name: string, category_slug: string, specifications: Record<string,string>, image_urls: string[], availability_indicator: 'in_stock'|'low_stock'|'out_of_stock'}. volume_pricing_tiers {sku_id: string, tiers: [{min_qty: number, max_qty: number|null, unit_price_gbp: number}]}. category_taxonomy {category_id: string, name: string, parent_category_id: string|null, slug: string}. specification_sheets {sku_id: string, pdf_url: string, last_updated: string}. All data sourced from inventory system API at build time and baked into static page props; not persisted anywhere on the catalog site.
target_output.api_design: No runtime API endpoints on the static site. Build-time calls to inventory system: GET /api/v1/products?active=true&fields=sku,name,category,specifications,images (paginated, fetched in parallel batches of 50). GET /api/v1/products/{sku}/pricing-tiers. GET /api/v1/products/{sku}/availability. All build-time API calls authenticated via INVENTORY_API_KEY environment variable in GitHub Actions and Vercel build environment. No API endpoints served by the deployed catalog site.
target_output.security_and_compliance: HTTPS enforced by Vercel Edge for all catalog pages; HTTP redirected to HTTPS automatically. Content Security Policy header via next.config.ts headers(): default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' va.vercel-scripts.com; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'. GDPR compliance mandatory: cookie consent banner implemented via react-cookie-consent library for EU visitors. Vercel Data Processing Agreement established covering all EU user data. Privacy policy page details data retention schedule and right-to-erasure procedures for any collected personal information.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Vercel project linked to GitHub repo with automatic preview deployments on pull requests and production deployment on main branch merge. INVENTORY_API_KEY stored as Vercel environment variable (encrypted, not exposed to browser). Nightly data refresh: GitHub Actions cron workflow (0 2 * * *) using actions/github-script to POST to Vercel deploy hook URL stored as GitHub secret. Build failure notification via GitHub Actions email alert. No server infrastructure to manage or scale.
target_output.observability: Vercel Analytics dashboard: daily page views per SKU page and category page, unique visitor counts, Core Web Vitals (LCP target <1.5s, CLS <0.1, FID <100ms) per page segment. Build time tracked in GitHub Actions workflow logs; alert if build exceeds 10 minutes. Inventory API fetch error monitoring: build script logs any failed SKU data fetches as warnings; catalog deploys with last-known data for failed SKUs to avoid blocking the full build.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Vercel Pro plan ~$20/month for commercial static site. Zero compute cost at runtime; all serving is CDN edge delivery. Nightly build consumes approximately 3-5 minutes of GitHub Actions minutes. For catalogs scaling to 50,000 SKUs, parallel getStaticProps calls at build time keep build under 15 minutes. Vercel's 100+ edge PoPs handle any traffic volume without scaling configuration.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (3 weeks): Next.js project scaffolding, inventory API integration in getStaticPaths/getStaticProps, SKU detail page with spec table and pricing tiers, category index pages, Vercel deployment, nightly rebuild workflow. Phase 2 (2 weeks): availability_indicator badges, Pagefind client-side search index, specification sheet PDF download links, mobile-optimized catalog layout. Phase 3 (1 week): Vercel Analytics integration, Core Web Vitals monitoring, SEO meta tags and sitemap.xml generation for all SKU pages.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode; no 'any' types in build-time data fetching functions. ESLint + Prettier enforced in CI. next build must succeed before merge to main. All inventory API calls wrapped in typed Zod schemas to catch schema changes at build time. No client-side database calls, API routes, or server actions -- static export mode prevents these patterns.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Inventory system API downtime during nightly rebuild causes stale pricing data. Mitigation: build script retries failed SKU fetches 3 times; deploys with last-known data for unavailable SKUs with staleness indicator. Risk: Large catalog (50,000+ SKUs) causing build times exceeding 15 minutes. Mitigation: incremental static regeneration considered for future but incompatible with pure static export; parallel fetch batching keeps builds fast. Tradeoff: purely static catalog means no real-time stock updates between nightly rebuilds; acceptable for trade buyers who plan orders in advance.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed Next.js static export over ISR (Incremental Static Regeneration) to maintain zero-server hosting on Vercel Edge. Selected Pagefind over Algolia for client-side search to avoid external API dependency on a static site. Nightly rebuild cadence chosen over hourly to stay within free GitHub Actions minutes allowance.

target_users.rationale: Two distinct audiences: new prospects and existing customers needing migration path clarity

security_baseline.rationale: Minimal security requirements for a public marketing page

project.goal: Marketing landing page for SaaS inventory platform migration from legacy on-premise product
project.target_users: Prospective buyers and existing on-premise customers

frontend.styling: Tailwind CSS with brand design tokens

data.cache: Vercel Edge CDN

constraints.cms: pricing content via CMS for marketing team

reasoner_reviews.product: The two-audience strategy (prospects vs migration candidates) is well-handled by distinct page sections: the hero and pricing cards target prospects while the migration_guide_sections and feature_comparison_table address existing customers evaluating migration. Lead capture form placement after the migration guide is strategically correct for capturing high-intent existing customers.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Astro's island architecture is ideal: the page ships almost zero JavaScript by default with pricing_plan_cards and migration_guide_sections as fully static HTML. The lead capture form is the only interactive island using a minimal Astro component. The Vercel serverless function (Edge Function) for form submission keeps the handler co-located with the static site without a separate API deployment.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Rounds 1 and 2 failed to include a CMS integration for pricing plan content, which was a stated constraint. Round 3 must add Contentful (or equivalent) as the content source for pricing_plan_cards so marketing team can update pricing without a code deploy. The Vercel deploy hook approach from the manifest must be documented.
reasoner_reviews.security: HubSpot form submissions from the migration guide download flow capture work email addresses and must be processed under a GDPR-compliant lawful basis (legitimate interest for B2B SaaS). The privacy notice link must appear adjacent to the form submit button. CSP headers must restrict script-src to self and HubSpot CDN domains only to prevent third-party script injection on the high-intent compliance download landing page.

frontend.app_structure: Astro 4 project with src/pages/index.astro as single entry point; content components in src/components/: HeroSection, FeatureComparisonTable, PricingPlanCards, MigrationGuideSections, TestimonialBlocks, LeadCaptureForm
frontend.pages_and_flows: Single-page scroll: Hero -> FeatureComparison -> PricingPlans -> MigrationGuide -> Testimonials -> LeadCaptureForm -> Footer. Anchor navigation links in sticky header.
frontend.performance_notes: Astro static output: zero JavaScript for static sections; LeadCaptureForm is only client:load island. Astro Image component for WebP product screenshots. Tailwind CSS purge keeps CSS <10KB. Target Lighthouse performance score >90.
frontend.accessibility_notes: PricingPlanCards use <table> with proper scope attributes for screen readers; migration steps use <ol> ordered list; form fields have associated <label> elements with aria-describedby for error messages

security.auth_design: none; public landing page
security.abuse_prevention: reCAPTCHA v3 token included in lead form POST to serverless function; function verifies score >0.5 with Google reCAPTCHA API before forwarding to HubSpot; Vercel automatic rate limiting on Edge Function
security.privacy_controls: GDPR consent checkbox (required) before form submission; checkbox label links to privacy policy; form POST includes gdpr_consent: true flag forwarded to HubSpot contact property
