target_output.title: SaaS Inventory Platform Migration Landing Page
target_output.executive_summary: An Astro 4 marketing landing page on Vercel communicating the new cloud SaaS inventory management platform's benefits, pricing plans, and migration path from the legacy on-premise version. Pricing plan content managed in Contentful CMS with Vercel deploy hook for instant marketing team updates. Lead capture form submission handled by a Vercel Edge Function with reCAPTCHA v3 protection, forwarding migration interest leads to HubSpot CRM. GA4 and HotJar provide conversion analytics.
target_output.architecture_overview: Astro 4 static site with minimal JavaScript islands deployed to Vercel Edge Network. Pricing plan and migration guide content fetched from Contentful CMS at build time via Contentful Delivery API; content changes trigger Vercel rebuild via webhook. Lead capture form is the only client:load island; submission POSTs to /api/lead-capture Vercel Edge Function which verifies reCAPTCHA v3 score (>0.5 threshold), then creates a HubSpot contact with gdpr_consent flag. GA4 tracks form_submitted, pricing_plan_clicked, and migration_guide_section_viewed events. HotJar records lead form sessions for UX analysis.
target_output.technology_stack: Framework: Astro 4 with TypeScript. Styling: Tailwind CSS 3.x. CMS: Contentful Delivery API for pricing_plan_cards and migration_guide_sections. Lead form: Vercel Edge Function (TypeScript) with reCAPTCHA v3 and HubSpot Contacts API. Analytics: Google Analytics 4 with custom events, HotJar for session recordings. Hosting: Vercel Edge Network. CI/CD: GitHub Actions + Vercel CLI deploy.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Hero section: headline, subheadline, and primary CTA linking to lead capture form. FeatureComparisonTable: static Astro component comparing on-premise vs cloud SaaS on 12 dimensions (uptime SLA, auto-updates, multi-warehouse support, API access, mobile app). PricingPlanCards: Starter/Professional/Enterprise plan cards sourced from Contentful; marketing team updates pricing in Contentful CMS and Vercel rebuilds automatically. MigrationGuideSections: step-by-step migration from on-premise to SaaS sourced from Contentful; 5-step guide with estimated time per step. TestimonialBlocks: customer quotes from Contentful. LeadCaptureForm: name, email, company, current_platform select, GDPR consent checkbox, reCAPTCHA v3.
target_output.system_components: Astro 4 static build process fetching pricing_plan_cards, migration_guide_sections, and testimonial_blocks from Contentful Delivery API. Vercel Edge Network serving generated HTML/CSS from global PoPs. Vercel Edge Function at /api/lead-capture: verifies reCAPTCHA v3 token (score threshold 0.5), creates HubSpot contact via HubSpot Contacts API v3 with gdpr_consent property. Contentful webhook sending POST to Vercel deploy hook URL on pricing plan content publish. GA4 gtag.js with custom event definitions. HotJar session recording script (deferred loading to avoid Lighthouse penalty).
target_output.workflows: Marketing Pricing Update: Marketing manager edits pricing_plan_cards entry in Contentful and clicks Publish. Contentful webhook fires POST to Vercel deploy hook. Vercel triggers astro build fetching updated Contentful entries. New HTML deployed to Edge CDN within 2-3 minutes. Lead Capture: Visitor fills LeadCaptureForm with name, email, company, current_platform, and GDPR consent checkbox. On submit, Astro island POSTs JSON to /api/lead-capture with reCAPTCHA token. Edge Function calls reCAPTCHA verify API: score <0.5 returns HTTP 429. Score >=0.5: function calls HubSpot POST /crm/v3/objects/contacts with {email, firstname, company, hs_lead_status: 'NEW', gdpr_consent: true, lead_source: 'migration_landing_page'}. Function returns 201 to client. GA4 event form_submitted fires. Welcome email sequence triggered by HubSpot workflow.
target_output.data_model: No database on landing page. Contentful content types: pricing_plan_cards {plan_name: String, price_monthly: Number, price_annual: Number, features: Array<String>, highlight_badge: String, cta_label: String}. migration_guide_sections {step_number: Number, title: String, description: RichText, estimated_time: String, icon_name: String}. testimonial_blocks {company_name: String, contact_role: String, quote_text: String, logo_url: String}. Lead form data shape (transient, forwarded to HubSpot): {name: string, email: string, company: string, current_platform: 'on_premise'|'competitor'|'none', gdpr_consent: boolean}. No data persisted on landing page infrastructure.
target_output.api_design: Single internal endpoint: POST /api/lead-capture (Vercel Edge Function). Request body: {name, email, company, current_platform, gdpr_consent, recaptcha_token}. Response: 201 {success: true, lead_id: string} | 429 {error: 'Bot submission detected'} | 422 {error: 'GDPR consent required'} | 500 {error: 'Submission failed'}. External API calls from Edge Function: POST https://www.google.com/recaptcha/api/siteverify (reCAPTCHA score check). POST https://api.hubapi.com/crm/v3/objects/contacts (HubSpot lead creation, authenticated via HUBSPOT_API_KEY env var). Build-time API calls: Contentful Delivery API GET /spaces/{space_id}/entries?content_type=pricingPlanCard.
target_output.security_and_compliance: HTTPS enforced via Vercel Edge for all requests. reCAPTCHA v3 on lead form: Edge Function rejects submissions with score <0.5 with HTTP 429; score threshold and bot rejection behavior documented. CSP headers via vercel.json: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' www.googletagmanager.com www.google.com www.gstatic.com static.hotjar.com. GDPR consent checkbox required before form submission; gdpr_consent: true forwarded to HubSpot contact property; privacy policy linked from checkbox label. No personal data stored on Vercel infrastructure; all lead data lives in HubSpot CRM. HUBSPOT_API_KEY and RECAPTCHA_SECRET_KEY stored as encrypted Vercel environment variables.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Vercel project connected to GitHub repo with Astro framework preset. Environment variables: CONTENTFUL_SPACE_ID, CONTENTFUL_ACCESS_TOKEN, HUBSPOT_API_KEY, RECAPTCHA_SECRET_KEY, RECAPTCHA_SITE_KEY stored as encrypted Vercel env vars. GitHub Actions pipeline: astro check (TypeScript), ESLint, astro build (with Contentful env vars), Vercel deploy on merge to main. Preview deployments on pull requests with preview Contentful environment. Contentful webhook URL configured in Contentful Settings > Webhooks pointing to Vercel deploy hook; triggers rebuild on pricing_plan_cards and migration_guide_sections publish events.
target_output.observability: GA4 custom events: form_started (first field focus), form_submitted (successful 201), pricing_plan_clicked (plan name in event params), migration_guide_section_viewed (section title via Intersection Observer). HotJar recordings filtered to sessions with lead form interactions. Vercel Function Logs for /api/lead-capture: log reCAPTCHA score, HubSpot response status, and any errors. Vercel Analytics for Core Web Vitals.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Vercel Pro ~$20/month. Contentful free tier (10K API calls/month) sufficient for build-time fetching. HubSpot Starter CRM free for lead storage. GA4 and HotJar free tier for analytics. Edge Function invocations for lead capture negligible cost. Total ~$20/month.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (2 weeks): Astro project setup, Contentful space with pricing_plan_cards and migration_guide_sections content types, Hero/PricingPlanCards/MigrationGuideSections/LeadCaptureForm components, Vercel Edge Function with reCAPTCHA and HubSpot integration, GA4 base tracking. Phase 2 (1 week): FeatureComparisonTable, TestimonialBlocks from Contentful, Contentful webhook deploy hook, HotJar integration, all GA4 custom events. Phase 3 (0.5 week): Lighthouse audit and performance optimizations, accessibility review, QA on all reCAPTCHA and GDPR consent flows.
target_output.development_guidelines: Astro TypeScript strict mode; Astro content collections for Contentful data shapes. ESLint + Prettier. astro check in CI blocks merge on type errors. No client:load islands beyond LeadCaptureForm; use server-only Astro components for all static sections. All secrets via Vercel environment variables; never in source code. reCAPTCHA site key is public and safe to commit to source; secret key stays server-side in Edge Function only.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Contentful API downtime during Vercel build causing missing pricing data. Mitigation: astro build fails safe with error on missing Contentful entries; Vercel retries failed builds twice. Risk: reCAPTCHA v3 false positives blocking legitimate leads from enterprise networks. Mitigation: threshold tunable via RECAPTCHA_THRESHOLD env var (default 0.5); flagged submissions logged for manual review rather than hard-rejected if score >0.3 and <0.5 (soft challenge). Tradeoff: Contentful CMS adds a third-party dependency for content but removes developer bottleneck on pricing updates, which was a constraint violation in prior rounds.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Contentful chosen over Sanity for CMS due to Astro integration maturity and existing company Contentful account. reCAPTCHA v3 chosen over Cloudflare Turnstile for broader browser compatibility. HubSpot Contacts API v3 chosen over legacy v1 for GDPR contact property support. Vercel Edge Function chosen over Netlify Functions to co-locate with Vercel hosting.

metadata.notes: Landing page for SaaS inventory platform migration marketing; round 3 resolves all three issues including Contentful CMS integration and reCAPTCHA threshold. Moderate quality.

project_goal.value: Python SDK exposing pre-trained retail demand forecasting and stockout prediction models as callable functions, abstracting ONNX model loading, feature engineering, and inference for embedding in inventory planning scripts and notebooks.
project_goal.rationale: Core library objective: zero-ceremony inference for data science consumers.

target_users.value: Data scientists and inventory planning engineers embedding forecasting into Python scripts and Jupyter notebooks.
target_users.rationale: Primary consumers identified in internal tooling survey.

project_class.rationale: Distributable Python package; no hosted runtime or browser UI.

capabilities.rationale: ONNX inference, feature engineering, and data transformation are core capabilities.

complexity_level.rationale: ONNX runtime integration, lazy model loading, typed feature contracts, and confidence interval math are non-trivial.

risk_level.rationale: Internal library; no external exposure, no user data, no compliance requirements.

data_sensitivity.rationale: Handles internal sales time-series and inventory data, not personal or financial data.

external_exposure.rationale: Distributed as internal PyPI package; not public.

access_model.value: No auth; consumers install via internal PyPI and import directly.
access_model.rationale: Library pattern; access control is at the distribution channel level.

feature_scope.value: forecast() and stockout_risk() callable functions, typed ForecastRequest/ForecastResult objects, lazy ONNX model loading with in-process cache, automated feature engineering from raw sales and inventory inputs, confidence interval computation.
feature_scope.rationale: Complete API surface for MVP distribution.

mvp_scope.value: forecast() function for 7/14/30-day horizon, stockout_risk() binary predictor, model artifact loading from local path or S3 URI, typed output with p10/p50/p90 quantiles.
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum viable for integration into current quarterly planning workflow.

security_baseline.value: No secrets in package; model registry credentials supplied by caller via env vars. Artifact checksums validated on load.
security_baseline.rationale: Library must not bundle credentials; consumers own their auth.

frontend_stack.rationale: Not applicable for a Python library.

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with ONNX Runtime 1.17, NumPy, Pandas, Pydantic v2 for typed contracts.
backend_stack.rationale: Team expertise; ONNX Runtime gives hardware-agnostic inference without PyTorch/TensorFlow.

data_platform.value: No persistent database; model artifacts sourced from local filesystem or S3 via configurable model_registry_uri.
data_platform.rationale: Library should have no hard infrastructure dependencies.

hosting_target.value: Internal PyPI registry (Nexus); installable via pip or uv.
hosting_target.rationale: Standard internal package distribution channel.

privacy_retention_policy.value: No data persisted by the library; input data ownership remains with the calling application.
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Library does not store inputs or outputs.

future_scope.value: Streaming inference for real-time POS events, multi-model ensemble support, automatic feature drift detection between training and serving distributions.
future_scope.rationale: Post-MVP roadmap once adoption is established.

constraints.value: Must support Python 3.10+; ONNX Runtime only (no PyTorch/TF dependency); package install must complete in under 30 seconds on typical CI runners; wheel size under 50 MB.
constraints.rationale: Existing CI environments and storage quotas.

observability_baseline.value: Structured Python logging (structlog) with latency and model_version fields emitted per inference call; consumers attach their own handlers.
observability_baseline.rationale: Library should emit telemetry without owning the sink.

execution_preference.value: Iterative releases with semantic versioning; consumers pin major versions.
execution_preference.rationale: Breaking changes in forecast contracts must be gated behind major version bumps.

llm_integration.value: Optional LLM-based forecast explanation module: calls a configurable LLM endpoint to generate natural-language summaries of forecast drivers from feature importance scores. Disabled by default; enabled via explain=True flag.
llm_integration.rationale: Business stakeholders want plain-English summaries alongside numeric forecasts.

project.goal: Distributable Python SDK for retail demand forecasting and stockout risk prediction using ONNX models
project.target_users: Data scientists and inventory planning engineers
project.timeline: Q2 initial release, Q3 drift detection add-on

backend.framework: ONNX Runtime 1.17, Pydantic v2, NumPy, Pandas, structlog

security.auth: N/A - library; callers supply model registry credentials via environment
security.artifact_integrity: SHA-256 checksums validated on model artifact load

data.model_source: Local filesystem or S3 URI via RETAIL_FORECAST_MODEL_REGISTRY env var
data.cache: In-process LRU cache keyed by model_name + version hash

devops.ci: GitHub Actions: lint (ruff), type-check (mypy strict), unit tests (pytest), build wheel, publish to Nexus

confirmed_decisions.serialization: Pydantic v2 for all public contracts
confirmed_decisions.versioning: SemVer with major version for breaking changes

reasoner_reviews.product: The explain=True LLM path is a useful stakeholder feature but must be clearly opt-in to avoid adding LLM latency to every forecast call in production pipelines. The forecast() and stockout_risk() separation is clean; resist merging them into a single overloaded function.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Lazy model loading with in-process LRU cache is the right default but requires thread-safety consideration for multi-threaded pipeline workers. A threading.RLock per model slot prevents double-loading. The S3 URI support should use a thin boto3 download abstraction so consumers on GCS or Azure Blob can swap in their own fetcher.
reasoner_reviews.security: Artifact checksum validation on load is a good integrity control for an internal SDK. Ensure the checksum manifest is fetched from a trusted source (same registry, not bundled in the wheel) so a corrupted artifact cannot also supply a forged checksum.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Wheel size constraint of 50 MB is achievable if ONNX Runtime is declared as a dependency (not bundled). The 30-second install time on CI is the tighter constraint; pin ONNX Runtime to a specific minor version to avoid resolver backtracking.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The previous plan's feature engineering module lacked explicit validation of input DataFrame column names and dtypes before passing to ONNX. Missing or mistyped columns currently produce cryptic ONNX runtime errors rather than informative SDK exceptions. This must be addressed in this revision.

backend.service_design: Public module surface: retail_forecast.forecast(request: ForecastRequest) -> ForecastResult, retail_forecast.stockout_risk(request: StockoutRequest) -> StockoutResult, retail_forecast.explain(result: ForecastResult) -> ForecastExplanation (optional). Internal modules: model_loader (lazy load + LRU cache), feature_engineer (raw sales/inventory DataFrame to ONNX input tensor), inference_runner (ONNX session.run wrapper), explainer (LLM call with feature importance payload).
backend.api_patterns: All public functions accept Pydantic v2 models for inputs; return Pydantic v2 models for outputs. Validation errors surface as ForecastValidationError with field-level messages. Model loading errors surface as ModelLoadError. ONNX inference errors surface as InferenceError.
backend.failure_handling: Input validation before model load; ForecastValidationError on missing or wrong-dtype columns. ModelLoadError if artifact checksum fails or S3 URI unreachable. Inference timeout (configurable, default 5s) raises InferenceTimeoutError. LLM explain path has independent timeout; failure returns explain=None without raising.
backend.scaling_notes: Thread-safe LRU cache using threading.RLock for model slot access. GIL releases during ONNX inference allow true parallelism with multi-threading in pipeline frameworks. For multi-process workers, each process holds its own in-process cache; no shared memory required.

security.auth_design: No SDK-level auth. Model registry credentials (AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID/SECRET for S3, RETAIL_FORECAST_REGISTRY_TOKEN for Nexus artifact pull) consumed from environment; never logged or included in error messages.
security.secrets_management: SDK never reads secrets from files or hardcoded paths. structlog scrubs any field named *token*, *key*, *secret* before emitting log events.
security.abuse_prevention: Artifact integrity: SHA-256 checksum of each .onnx file validated against a checksum manifest fetched separately from the registry. Mismatched checksum raises ModelIntegrityError and refuses to load.

data.entities: ForecastRequest (sku_id, historical_sales: List[float], stock_level: float, horizon_days: int, model_version: Optional[str]). ForecastResult (sku_id, horizon_days, p10: List[float], p50: List[float], p90: List[float], model_version: str, latency_ms: float). StockoutRequest (sku_id, forecast: ForecastResult, lead_time_days: int). StockoutResult (sku_id, stockout_probability: float, days_until_risk: int, confidence: float). model_metadata (model_name, version, onnx_path, checksum_sha256, feature_schema_json, loaded_at).
data.schema_notes: feature_schema_json in model_metadata defines expected input column names and dtypes; feature_engineer validates caller DataFrame against this schema before tensor construction, raising ForecastValidationError with column-level diff on mismatch.
data.analytics_events: structlog events emitted per call: {event: 'forecast_complete', sku_id, horizon_days, model_version, latency_ms, cache_hit: bool}. Consumers attach handlers for Datadog, CloudWatch, or stdout.

devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions workflow: ruff lint -> mypy strict -> pytest with coverage gate (90%) -> bumpversion -> build wheel -> twine upload to Nexus on tag push. PR checks run lint + test only; publish requires maintainer tag.
devops.deployment_topology: No deployed service. Package published to internal Nexus PyPI. Version tags trigger publish job. Consumers install with pip install retail-forecast-sdk==<version> from internal index.
devops.rollback_strategy: SemVer: breaking changes bump major version; consumers pin retail-forecast-sdk~=1.0. Yanked versions removed from Nexus index within 1 hour of defect report.

ISS-001.title: Feature engineering lacks input schema validation
ISS-001.detail: The feature_engineer module accepts arbitrary DataFrames without validating column names or dtypes against the model's expected feature schema. A caller passing a DataFrame with a renamed or missing column receives a cryptic onnxruntime.OrtException rather than an informative SDK error.

history.note: Identified during internal dogfooding; affects every caller on first integration.

ISS-002.title: LRU cache not thread-safe under concurrent pipeline workers
ISS-002.detail: The model LRU cache uses a plain dict with no locking. When two threads simultaneously request a model that is not yet cached, both threads trigger an S3 download, wasting bandwidth and causing a race on the dict write.

history.note: Reproduced in Airflow multi-threaded operator context.

focus_issues.title: Feature engineering lacks input schema validation
focus_issues.detail: The feature_engineer module accepts arbitrary DataFrames without validating column names or dtypes against the model's expected feature schema.

focus_issues.title: LRU cache not thread-safe under concurrent pipeline workers
focus_issues.detail: Plain dict LRU cache causes double-download race condition when multiple threads request an uncached model simultaneously.

latest_recommendations: Add explicit DataFrame schema validation in feature_engineer against model_metadata.feature_schema_json before tensor construction
Replace plain dict cache with threading.RLock-protected LRU to prevent concurrent download races
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: Retail Demand Forecasting SDK - Initial Architecture

convergence_state.reason: Two unresolved issues blocking safe internal rollout.

previous_audits.summary: Solid library structure with clean public API and good ONNX integration design. Two implementation gaps undermine reliability for multi-threaded pipeline consumers.
strengths: Clear Pydantic v2 typed contracts for ForecastRequest/ForecastResult
Artifact checksum validation on model load
Separation of forecast() and stockout_risk() functions avoids API overloading
concerns: feature_engineer validates nothing before passing to ONNX session; callers get unreadable runtime errors
In-process cache is not thread-safe; concurrent workers trigger redundant S3 downloads
blocking_issues: ISS-001: no input schema validation causes opaque ONNX errors for any mismatch
ISS-002: dict-based cache race condition under concurrent Airflow tasks
recommendations: Load feature_schema_json from model_metadata and validate caller DataFrame columns and dtypes before tensor construction; raise ForecastValidationError with a column-level diff
Protect model slot writes with threading.RLock; use a double-checked lock pattern to avoid redundant downloads

issue_updates.title: Feature engineering lacks input schema validation
issue_updates.detail: feature_engineer passes DataFrame directly to ONNX without column/dtype checks

issue_updates.title: LRU cache not thread-safe
issue_updates.detail: Plain dict write race under concurrent thread access

previous_plan.title: Retail Demand Forecasting SDK - Initial Architecture
previous_plan.executive_summary: A Python SDK distributing ONNX-based demand forecasting and stockout prediction as typed callable functions. Lazy model loading from local or S3 sources. Initial plan did not address thread-safe caching or input schema validation.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Module-based library with public forecast() and stockout_risk() entry points backed by ONNX Runtime. Model loading was described as lazy but without thread-safety controls.
previous_plan.feature_engineering: feature_engineer module accepted raw sales DataFrames without validating column names or dtypes against model feature schema.
previous_plan.model_loading: LRU cache implemented as plain Python dict; no locking described.
previous_plan.api_design: Pydantic v2 ForecastRequest/ForecastResult contracts documented but validation error handling not specified for feature mismatches.

target_output.thinking_summary: Addressing two blocking issues from round 1: ISS-001 requires injecting schema validation into feature_engineer using feature_schema_json from model_metadata before ONNX tensor construction; ISS-002 requires replacing the plain dict cache with a threading.RLock-protected double-checked locking pattern. Both fixes are localized to internal modules and do not change the public Pydantic API surface.

fix_report.action_taken: Added _validate_dataframe_schema() in feature_engineer that deserializes feature_schema_json from model_metadata and checks caller DataFrame for missing columns, extra columns, and dtype mismatches. On failure raises ForecastValidationError with a structured diff listing each offending column and its expected vs received dtype.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Callers receive a ForecastValidationError with field-level detail instead of a cryptic OrtException; integration errors are diagnosed in minutes rather than hours.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced plain dict LRU with a ModelCache class using threading.RLock and double-checked locking: acquire lock, check slot, download only if still absent, write slot, release. Slot is keyed by (model_name, version_hash).
fix_report.expected_outcome: Under concurrent Airflow multi-thread workers, each model artifact is downloaded exactly once per process lifetime; no race conditions on cache write.

target_output.title: Retail Demand Forecasting SDK - Revised Architecture v2
target_output.executive_summary: A Python 3.12 SDK distributing ONNX-based retail demand forecasting and stockout prediction as typed callable functions installable from an internal PyPI registry. This revision adds explicit DataFrame schema validation in the feature engineering layer and thread-safe model caching, resolving both blocking issues from round 1. The optional LLM explain module generates natural-language forecast summaries from feature importance scores when called with explain=True.
target_output.architecture_overview: Pure Python library with no hosted runtime. Three entry-point functions (forecast, stockout_risk, explain) accept Pydantic v2 request models and return typed result models. The inference pipeline runs as: input validation then DataFrame schema check against model_metadata.feature_schema_json then feature tensor construction then thread-safe ONNX session execution via ModelCache then quantile extraction then ForecastResult assembly. Model artifacts loaded lazily from local filesystem or S3 URI on first call, cached in a threading.RLock-protected ModelCache per process. The optional explain path calls a configurable LLM endpoint with feature importance JSON and returns a ForecastExplanation; it is isolated from the main inference path and never raises exceptions that propagate to the caller.
target_output.technology_stack: Python 3.12, ONNX Runtime 1.17 (CPU/CUDA provider auto-detected), Pydantic v2 for typed contracts and validation, NumPy 1.26 for tensor operations, Pandas 2.1 for DataFrame feature engineering, structlog 24 for structured per-call telemetry, boto3 for S3 artifact fetching (optional extra), httpx for LLM explain HTTP calls (optional extra). Build: pyproject.toml with hatchling, ruff for linting, mypy strict for type checking, pytest with pytest-cov for unit tests.
target_output.functional_feature_map: forecast(request: ForecastRequest) -> ForecastResult: accepts sku_id, historical_sales list, stock_level, horizon_days (7/14/30), optional model_version; validates input schema; returns p10/p50/p90 quantile arrays with latency_ms. stockout_risk(request: StockoutRequest) -> StockoutResult: accepts a completed ForecastResult and lead_time_days; returns stockout_probability and days_until_risk with confidence score. explain(result: ForecastResult, llm_config: LLMConfig) -> ForecastExplanation: extracts top-5 feature importances from ONNX session metadata; sends to LLM endpoint; returns plain-English summary of demand drivers. Lazy model loading with ModelCache; in-process LRU keyed by (model_name, version_hash).
target_output.system_components: Public API module (retail_forecast/__init__.py): exports forecast, stockout_risk, explain, and all Pydantic types. ModelCache (retail_forecast/model_loader.py): threading.RLock-protected LRU dict; double-checked locking prevents concurrent S3 downloads; validates SHA-256 checksum against registry manifest on load. FeatureEngineer (retail_forecast/feature_engineer.py): deserializes feature_schema_json from ModelMetadata; calls _validate_dataframe_schema() which diffs caller DataFrame columns and dtypes against schema; raises ForecastValidationError with column-level diff on mismatch; constructs float32 ONNX input tensor. InferenceRunner (retail_forecast/inference_runner.py): wraps onnxruntime.InferenceSession.run(); enforces configurable inference timeout via threading.Timer. Explainer (retail_forecast/explainer.py): reads ONNX session metadata for feature importance; calls LLM endpoint via httpx with 10s timeout; returns None on any HTTP or timeout error without propagating.
target_output.workflows: Forecast Inference Workflow: caller constructs ForecastRequest(sku_id='SKU-4821', historical_sales=[120,135,...], stock_level=48.0, horizon_days=14); forecast() calls ModelCache.get('demand_v3', version='latest') which acquires RLock, checks slot, downloads .onnx from s3://model-registry/demand/v3.2.onnx if absent, validates checksum, releases lock; FeatureEngineer._validate_dataframe_schema() compares caller columns against model_metadata.feature_schema_json and raises ForecastValidationError if 'promo_flag' column is missing; on valid input constructs float32 tensor; InferenceRunner executes ONNX session returning quantile arrays; ForecastResult assembled with p10/p50/p90 and latency_ms. Schema Validation Error Workflow: caller passes DataFrame missing 'days_since_last_promo' feature; _validate_dataframe_schema() builds diff {missing: ['days_since_last_promo'], dtype_mismatches: {'stock_level': {expected: 'float32', got: 'int64'}}}; raises ForecastValidationError with actionable column list rather than OrtException.
target_output.data_model: ForecastRequest (sku_id: str, historical_sales: List[float] min_length=4, stock_level: float ge=0, horizon_days: Literal[7,14,30], model_version: Optional[str]='latest'). ForecastResult (sku_id: str, horizon_days: int, p10: List[float], p50: List[float], p90: List[float], model_version: str, latency_ms: float). StockoutRequest (sku_id: str, forecast: ForecastResult, lead_time_days: int ge=1). StockoutResult (sku_id: str, stockout_probability: float, days_until_risk: int, confidence: float). ModelMetadata (model_name: str, version: str, onnx_path: str, checksum_sha256: str, feature_schema_json: str, loaded_at: datetime). FeatureColumnDef (name: str, dtype: str, required: bool). ForecastValidationError (message: str, column_diff: dict with missing and dtype_mismatches keys).
target_output.api_design: No HTTP API; pure Python function calls. Public surface: from retail_forecast import forecast, stockout_risk, explain. forecast(ForecastRequest) -> ForecastResult. stockout_risk(StockoutRequest) -> StockoutResult. explain(ForecastResult, LLMConfig) -> Optional[ForecastExplanation]. Configuration: RetailForecastConfig(model_registry_uri: str, inference_timeout_s: float=5.0, cache_max_models: int=10, llm_endpoint: Optional[str]=None). Error hierarchy: ForecastSDKError (base) -> ForecastValidationError, ModelLoadError, ModelIntegrityError, InferenceTimeoutError.
target_output.security_and_compliance: No secrets bundled in wheel; model registry credentials consumed exclusively from environment variables (AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID/SECRET for S3, RETAIL_FORECAST_REGISTRY_TOKEN for Nexus). structlog scrubs fields matching *token*, *key*, *secret* patterns before emitting events. SHA-256 checksum of each .onnx artifact validated against registry manifest fetched separately from artifact; mismatched checksum raises ModelIntegrityError and aborts load. Artifact checksum manifest is fetched from the same Nexus registry path as the artifact, not bundled in the wheel, preventing a compromised wheel from supplying a forged checksum.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Distribution: wheel built with hatchling via python -m build; published to internal Nexus PyPI on version tag push via GitHub Actions twine upload. Consumers install with pip install retail-forecast-sdk~=2.0 pointing to internal index URL. CI pipeline: ruff lint -> mypy strict mode -> pytest (90% coverage gate, including concurrent cache tests with ThreadPoolExecutor) -> build wheel -> publish to Nexus. Release process: maintainer bumps version in pyproject.toml, pushes tag vX.Y.Z, GitHub Actions publish job fires. Breaking changes require major version bump with migration guide in CHANGELOG.md. Yanked versions removed from Nexus within 1 hour of defect confirmation.
target_output.observability: structlog emits one structured JSON event per inference call: {event: 'forecast_complete', sku_id, horizon_days, model_version, latency_ms, cache_hit: bool, schema_valid: bool}. Separate event on validation failure: {event: 'forecast_validation_error', sku_id, column_diff}. Consumers attach handlers for Datadog custom metrics, CloudWatch Embedded Metrics, or stdout. No SDK-owned sink. LLM explain calls emit {event: 'explain_complete', latency_ms, llm_endpoint_host, success: bool}.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: No infrastructure cost; library executes in caller's process. ONNX Runtime CPU provider runs on any hardware; CUDA provider auto-detected for GPU acceleration with zero code change. Model artifact S3 download cost is one-time per process boot per model version. Wheel size target under 50 MB achieved by declaring ONNX Runtime as install dependency rather than bundling.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (complete - rounds 1-2): Public forecast() and stockout_risk() API with Pydantic v2 contracts, ONNX Runtime inference, thread-safe ModelCache with double-checked locking, DataFrame schema validation with ForecastValidationError, SHA-256 artifact integrity, CI pipeline publishing to Nexus, 90% unit test coverage. Phase 2 (Q3): explain() module with LLM integration for plain-English forecast summaries, multi-model ensemble support combining outputs of multiple model versions. Phase 3 (Q4): Feature drift detection comparing caller input distributions against training set statistics stored in model_metadata; automatic warning when distribution shift exceeds configured threshold.
target_output.development_guidelines: mypy strict mode enforced in CI; all public functions fully annotated. ruff replaces flake8/isort/black; configured in pyproject.toml. pytest with pytest-cov; 90% coverage gate includes concurrent cache tests with ThreadPoolExecutor and 8 workers triggering simultaneous forecast calls on cold cache. SemVer strictly followed: any change to Pydantic model field names, types, or error codes is a major version bump. All public exceptions documented in docstrings with example raise scenarios.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: ONNX model format versioning - a model retrained with new features changes feature_schema_json, breaking callers on the old SDK version. Mitigation: model version is part of the cache key; old SDK versions continue loading old model versions; migration guide published with each feature schema change. Risk: boto3 optional dependency adds ~15 MB to install if S3 is used. Mitigation: boto3 declared as optional extra (pip install retail-forecast-sdk[s3]). Tradeoff: in-process LRU means each Airflow worker process holds its own model copy in RAM; for large ONNX models (>500 MB) this multiplies memory usage. Mitigation: document recommended cache_max_models=1 for memory-constrained workers.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected ONNX Runtime over TorchScript for hardware portability and lighter dependency footprint. Confirmed S3 URI support as optional extra to avoid mandatory cloud dependency. Chose Pydantic v2 over dataclasses for built-in validation. Thread-safe RLock cache selected over multiprocessing.Manager to avoid IPC overhead in Airflow local executor.

metadata.notes: Library SDK for retail ONNX forecasting; round 2 fixes thread-safe caching and input schema validation.

profile.domain: Retail ML Model Performance Reporting Microsite

project_goal.value: Static reporting microsite publishing weekly ML model performance reports for demand forecasting and dynamic pricing models, displayed as auto-generated charts and summary tables for business stakeholders.
project_goal.rationale: Replaces manual weekly email distribution of model accuracy spreadsheets.

target_users.value: Retail analytics business stakeholders and product managers who need weekly model accuracy summaries without accessing data science tooling.
target_users.rationale: Primary audience identified in analytics team survey.

project_class.rationale: No server-side runtime required; pre-generated HTML/charts served from S3/CloudFront.

capabilities.rationale: Static chart rendering and session analytics are the only runtime capabilities needed.

complexity_level.rationale: Static site with CI-driven weekly regeneration; no dynamic server logic.

risk_level.rationale: Internal-only, no user input, read-only content.

data_sensitivity.rationale: Reports contain internal model performance metrics, not customer PII.

external_exposure.rationale: IP allowlist via CloudFront restricts access to corporate network.

access_model.value: IP allowlist enforced at CloudFront distribution; no user login required within the corporate network.
access_model.rationale: Simplest viable access control for internal read-only site.

feature_scope.value: Weekly model performance reports for demand forecasting accuracy (MAPE, RMSE by SKU category) and dynamic pricing model performance (price elasticity accuracy, revenue impact); historical archive of prior weekly reports; data drift alert banners.
feature_scope.rationale: Full reporting scope for business stakeholders.

mvp_scope.value: Current week demand forecasting accuracy charts (MAPE by category) and pricing model summary table; archive of last 12 weekly reports.
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum viable for replacing manual email distribution.

security_baseline.value: CloudFront IP allowlist; HTTPS-only with ACM certificate; S3 bucket not publicly accessible (CloudFront OAC).
security_baseline.rationale: Static site security standard for internal tools.

frontend_stack.value: Gatsby 5 with React 18 for static site generation; Recharts for accuracy charts; Tailwind CSS for layout.
frontend_stack.rationale: Team familiarity with React; Gatsby provides static export with page-level pre-rendering.

backend_stack.value: N/A - static site; no server-side runtime.
backend_stack.rationale: All data is pre-rendered at build time from S3 evaluation JSON files.

data_platform.value: Model evaluation JSON files generated by data science pipelines and stored in s3://retail-mlops/weekly-reports/; Gatsby build reads these at build time.
data_platform.rationale: No database required; evaluation outputs are the data source.

hosting_target.value: AWS S3 static hosting behind CloudFront distribution with IP allowlist and OAC.
hosting_target.rationale: Cost-effective static delivery; CloudFront provides IP restriction and HTTPS.

privacy_retention_policy.value: Weekly report archives retained for 52 weeks on CloudFront/S3; older reports moved to S3 Glacier after 52 weeks.
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Internal reporting retention standard.

future_scope.value: Model comparison views showing accuracy trends across model versions; email digest integration sending report links to Slack; search across historical report archives.
future_scope.rationale: Post-MVP stakeholder requests.

constraints.value: No server-side compute; all rendering must happen at CI build time. GitLab CI for all pipeline work (not GitHub Actions). Reports must be available by 8 AM Monday.
constraints.rationale: Platform and timing requirements from operations team.

observability_baseline.value: CloudFront access logs to S3; no application-level monitoring required for a static site.
observability_baseline.rationale: Read-only static site; page load analytics via CloudFront logs is sufficient.

execution_preference.value: Weekly automated CI regeneration every Monday at 5 AM UTC; manual trigger available for out-of-cycle updates.
execution_preference.rationale: Aligned with data science team's weekly evaluation cadence.

project.goal: Auto-generated static ML model performance reports for retail analytics stakeholders
project.target_users: Business stakeholders and product managers on corporate network
project.timeline: Q2 launch, weekly automated refresh thereafter

frontend.charts: Recharts for MAPE/RMSE line charts and pricing model bar charts

data.build_time: Gatsby reads evaluation JSON at build; no runtime data fetch

devops.ci: GitLab CI scheduled pipeline every Monday 5 AM UTC

constraints.no_server: Static only; no Lambda, no server-side runtime

reasoner_reviews.product: The weekly auto-regeneration cadence is well-matched to the data science team's evaluation schedule. The MVP scope of demand forecasting MAPE charts and pricing model tables is achievable in one sprint. Stakeholders should have clear visibility into which model version each week's report reflects.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Static site generation at build time is the correct pattern here. Gatsby's graphQL data layer can read local JSON files downloaded from S3 during CI; no Lambda@Edge or server-side component is appropriate. The archive pattern of generating a new static page per weekly report keeps historical data accessible without a database.
reasoner_reviews.security: CloudFront IP allowlist is the right access control for a read-only internal site. Ensure the S3 bucket blocks all public access and uses Origin Access Control so objects are not directly accessible via S3 URL. CloudFront access logs should be retained to detect any unauthorized access attempts.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: GitLab CI scheduled pipeline on Monday at 5 AM UTC gives a 3-hour window before the 8 AM deadline. The main risk is S3 download latency for large evaluation JSON files; if the full evaluation output exceeds 50 MB, consider splitting into per-model-type files.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The previous plan did not specify how Gatsby handles the case where the Monday S3 evaluation files are not yet available at pipeline trigger time. A missing or stale data file should cause the CI build to fail with a clear error rather than silently deploying charts with last week's data labeled as current.

frontend.app_structure: Gatsby static site with page-per-week-report generation using gatsby-node.js createPages. Page templates: WeeklyReportPage (demand forecasting section + pricing model section + drift alert banner), ArchivePage (list of past 52 weekly reports with links). Data sourced from local JSON files downloaded from S3 at build start.
frontend.pages_and_flows: / (current week report): MAPE by SKU category Recharts LineChart, RMSE trend Recharts BarChart, pricing model accuracy table, data drift alert banner if drift_flag=true. /archive (list of past reports): table of week dates with links to /report/YYYY-WW/. /report/YYYY-WW (historical report): same layout as current week, rendered as static page.
frontend.performance_notes: Recharts SVG charts are pre-rendered at build time; no client-side chart library load for static content. Gatsby image plugin for any screenshot assets. Tailwind CSS purged to minimal bundle. CloudFront caching with 7-day TTL for archived report pages; 1-hour TTL for current week.

security.auth_design: No application-level auth. Access controlled at CloudFront layer via IP allowlist (corporate VPN and office CIDR ranges configured in WAF IP set associated with distribution).
security.abuse_prevention: S3 bucket policy denies all s3:GetObject except from CloudFront OAC principal. No presigned URL generation needed.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: CloudFront standard logging enabled; access logs delivered to s3://retail-mlops/cloudfront-logs/. Log retention 90 days per S3 lifecycle rule.

data.entities: model_performance_reports (week_id, model_type ENUM demand_forecasting/dynamic_pricing, mape_by_category: dict, rmse_overall: float, evaluation_date, model_version). forecast_accuracy_charts (derived from model_performance_reports at build time). pricing_model_summaries (week_id, elasticity_accuracy: float, revenue_impact_pct: float, model_version). data_drift_alerts (week_id, drift_flag: bool, affected_features: list). weekly_report_archives (week_id, s3_key, generated_at).
data.retention_and_deletion: 52 weeks of static report pages retained in S3 standard; older pages transitioned to Glacier after 52 weeks via S3 lifecycle rule. CloudFront cache invalidated on new deploy.

devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI scheduled pipeline: [download_evaluation_data] aws s3 sync s3://retail-mlops/weekly-reports/current/ ./data/ --only-show-errors; validate JSON schema; [build] gatsby build; [deploy] aws s3 sync public/ s3://retail-ml-reports-site/; aws cloudfront create-invalidation. Build fails if evaluation JSON missing or schema invalid.
devops.environments: Single production environment; no staging. Dev testing uses mock evaluation JSON fixtures checked into the repo.
devops.deployment_topology: S3 bucket (retail-ml-reports-site, versioning enabled, public access blocked) -> CloudFront distribution with OAC, IP allowlist WAF rule, ACM HTTPS certificate, 7-day cache for archive pages.

backend.service_design: No backend service. Build-time data pipeline: Gatsby reads evaluation JSON from ./data/ directory (populated by CI S3 sync). gatsby-node.js createPages iterates report JSON files and creates one static page per weekly report.
backend.failure_handling: CI pipeline step validates evaluation JSON against schema before Gatsby build; missing required fields (mape_by_category, model_version, evaluation_date) cause pipeline failure with explicit error message, preventing silent deploy of incomplete data.

ISS-001.title: No validation of S3 evaluation data before Gatsby build
ISS-001.detail: The CI pipeline triggers Gatsby build immediately after S3 sync without validating that evaluation JSON files are complete and match the expected schema. If the data science pipeline is delayed or produces partial output, the site builds and deploys silently with missing or stale chart data.

history.note: Identified as gap in CI pipeline spec.

focus_issues.title: No validation of S3 evaluation data before Gatsby build
focus_issues.detail: CI triggers Gatsby build without validating evaluation JSON completeness; partial data deploys silently.

latest_recommendations: Add a CI validation step between S3 sync and gatsby build that checks each evaluation JSON file against a JSON Schema spec; fail the pipeline with a descriptive error if required fields are missing or evaluation_date is more than 8 days old
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: Retail ML Model Performance Reporting Microsite - Initial Architecture

convergence_state.reason: Data validation gap must be closed before production deployment.

previous_audits.summary: Correct static site pattern with Gatsby and CloudFront IP allowlist. CI pipeline lacks an evaluation data validation gate, which is a reliability risk for Monday morning stakeholder deadlines.
strengths: Gatsby static generation is the right pattern for pre-rendered weekly reports
CloudFront IP allowlist with OAC is appropriate access control
Archive-per-week-page pattern scales without a database
concerns: No schema validation of S3 evaluation JSON before build; stale or partial data deploys silently
blocking_issues: ISS-001: missing evaluation data validation step in CI
recommendations: Add jsonschema validation step in CI after S3 sync; check for required fields and that evaluation_date is within expected window; fail pipeline with clear error message on mismatch

issue_updates.title: No validation of S3 evaluation data before Gatsby build
issue_updates.detail: CI pipeline goes straight from S3 sync to gatsby build with no data quality gate

previous_plan.title: Retail ML Model Performance Reporting Microsite - Initial Architecture
previous_plan.executive_summary: Static Gatsby site auto-generated weekly from S3 evaluation JSON files. CloudFront with IP allowlist. No server-side runtime. Initial plan did not include evaluation data validation in CI.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Gatsby 5 static site reading evaluation JSON from S3 at build time. CloudFront distribution with IP allowlist for corporate access. Weekly GitLab CI pipeline regenerates the site.
previous_plan.deployment_and_operations: GitLab CI: aws s3 sync then gatsby build then aws s3 sync to deploy bucket. No validation step between S3 sync and Gatsby build.
previous_plan.workflows: Monday 5 AM CI pipeline: S3 sync then gatsby build then CloudFront deploy. No data quality gate.

target_output.thinking_summary: Addressing ISS-001 by adding a JSON schema validation step between S3 sync and gatsby build in the GitLab CI pipeline. The validator checks each evaluation file for required fields (mape_by_category, model_version, evaluation_date, drift_flag) and verifies evaluation_date is within 8 days of the pipeline trigger. On failure the pipeline exits non-zero with a structured error message, preventing silent deploy of stale or incomplete model performance data.

fix_report.action_taken: Added validate_evaluation_data.py script executed as a CI step between S3 sync and gatsby build. Script uses jsonschema to validate each weekly report JSON against a checked-in schema spec; also checks that evaluation_date is within 8 days of the current date. Pipeline fails with exit code 1 and prints which files failed validation and why.
fix_report.expected_outcome: CI pipeline fails fast with a clear error message rather than deploying incomplete or stale demand forecasting charts to stakeholders.

target_output.title: Retail ML Model Performance Reporting Microsite - Revised Architecture v2
target_output.executive_summary: A static Gatsby 5 site auto-generated every Monday by a GitLab CI pipeline from S3 model evaluation JSON files. Displays weekly demand forecasting accuracy (MAPE by SKU category, RMSE trend) and dynamic pricing model performance summaries with Recharts. Served from S3 via CloudFront with IP allowlist restricting access to corporate network. This revision adds a JSON schema validation gate in CI to prevent silent deployment of incomplete or stale evaluation data.
target_output.architecture_overview: Fully static architecture with no server-side runtime. GitLab CI pipeline runs every Monday at 5 AM UTC: syncs current week evaluation JSON from s3://retail-mlops/weekly-reports/current/, runs validate_evaluation_data.py against JSON Schema spec, and on pass invokes gatsby build which reads local JSON files to pre-render all chart pages. Built artifacts deployed to S3 static hosting bucket behind CloudFront distribution. IP allowlist WAF rule restricts CloudFront access to corporate VPN and office CIDR ranges. Historical weekly report pages archived as static files; no database or server-side compute at any layer.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Gatsby 5, React 18, Recharts 2 for MAPE/RMSE line charts and pricing accuracy bar charts, Tailwind CSS 3. Build tooling: Node 20 LTS, gatsby-cli. CI: GitLab CI with scheduled pipeline. Validation: Python 3.12 with jsonschema for evaluation data quality gate. Infrastructure: AWS S3 (static hosting), CloudFront (OAC, IP allowlist WAF, ACM HTTPS), GitLab CI runner with AWS IAM role for S3 access.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Current Week Report Page (/): demand forecasting section with MAPE by SKU category Recharts LineChart (Electronics, Apparel, Home Goods, Grocery categories), RMSE 13-week trend chart, model_version badge, data drift alert banner rendered if drift_flag=true in evaluation JSON. Dynamic Pricing Model Section: price elasticity accuracy percentage and revenue impact bar chart from pricing_model_summaries JSON. Report Archive Page (/archive): table of past 52 weeks with ISO week identifiers, generation timestamps, and links to /report/YYYY-WW/. Historical Report Pages (/report/YYYY-WW/): identical layout to current week, pre-rendered as static pages.
target_output.system_components: GitLab CI Pipeline: scheduled job with aws s3 sync stage, validate_evaluation_data.py validation stage, gatsby build stage, aws s3 sync deploy stage, CloudFront invalidation stage. validate_evaluation_data.py: loads weekly_report_schema.json spec; validates each evaluation file; checks evaluation_date freshness (<=8 days); exits 1 with field-level error on failure. Gatsby Build: gatsby-node.js reads ./data/*.json; calls createPages for each weekly report file creating /report/YYYY-WW/ pages; WeeklyReportPage template renders Recharts charts from page context data. S3 Hosting Bucket: public access blocked, versioning enabled, OAC-only access policy. CloudFront Distribution: OAC, WAF IP set allowlist, ACM cert, 7-day cache TTL for archive pages, 1-hour TTL for current week page.
target_output.workflows: Weekly Report Generation Workflow: GitLab scheduled pipeline triggers 5 AM UTC Monday; aws s3 sync downloads current/ evaluation JSON to ./data/; validate_evaluation_data.py checks each file against schema - if evaluation_date for demand_forecasting report is 2026-W12 but current week is 2026-W13, pipeline exits 1 with 'demand_forecasting_2026-W12.json: evaluation_date 2026-03-17 is 10 days old, expected within 8 days'; on pass, gatsby build pre-renders WeeklyReportPage with MAPE Recharts LineChart data from JSON; aws s3 sync deploys public/ to S3; CloudFront invalidation clears current week page cache. Archive Access Workflow: stakeholder opens corporate browser, CloudFront WAF checks source IP against allowlist, serves /archive/ static page listing past 52 weekly reports; stakeholder clicks 2025-W48 link to view historical demand forecasting accuracy for Black Friday week.
target_output.data_model: model_performance_reports JSON schema: {week_id: str ISO-week format YYYY-WW, model_type: enum demand_forecasting|dynamic_pricing, evaluation_date: date ISO8601, model_version: str, mape_by_category: {Electronics: float, Apparel: float, Home_Goods: float, Grocery: float}, rmse_overall: float, drift_flag: bool, affected_features: list[str]}. pricing_model_summaries JSON schema: {week_id: str, evaluation_date: date, model_version: str, elasticity_accuracy: float, revenue_impact_pct: float}. weekly_report_archives: Gatsby page metadata keyed by week_id with s3_key and generated_at fields for archive listing page.
target_output.api_design: No runtime API. Build-time data access: Gatsby reads ./data/demand_forecasting_YYYY-WW.json and ./data/pricing_YYYY-WW.json via filesystem. gatsby-node.js createPages iterates matching files and passes JSON as pageContext to WeeklyReportPage template. No client-side fetch calls in deployed static site; all data embedded in pre-rendered HTML and JS bundles.
target_output.security_and_compliance: CloudFront WAF IP set allowlist configured with corporate VPN CIDR and office IP ranges; all requests from non-listed IPs receive 403. S3 bucket policy denies all s3:GetObject except from CloudFront OAC service principal; direct S3 URL access returns 403. HTTPS enforced via CloudFront with ACM certificate; HTTP redirected to HTTPS. CI IAM role has s3:GetObject on s3://retail-mlops/weekly-reports/ and s3:PutObject on s3://retail-ml-reports-site/ only; no other permissions. CloudFront access logs retained 90 days.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitLab CI pipeline stages: (1) download_data: aws s3 sync s3://retail-mlops/weekly-reports/current/ ./data/ using CI IAM role; (2) validate_data: python3 validate_evaluation_data.py --schema weekly_report_schema.json --max-age-days 8 --data-dir ./data/; exits 1 on validation failure with field-level error output; (3) build: gatsby build; (4) deploy: aws s3 sync public/ s3://retail-ml-reports-site/ --delete; (5) invalidate: aws cloudfront create-invalidation --distribution-id $CF_DIST_ID --paths '/index.html' '/archive/*'. Scheduled trigger: Monday 5 AM UTC. Manual trigger available for out-of-cycle model evaluation updates. On pipeline failure, Slack webhook notification sent to #retail-analytics-ops channel.
target_output.observability: CloudFront standard access logs delivered to s3://retail-mlops/cloudfront-logs/ with 90-day S3 lifecycle retention. GitLab CI pipeline status visible in GitLab dashboard; Slack webhook on failure. No application-level APM required for a static site. Weekly report generation success tracked via GitLab CI pipeline pass/fail rate.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Near-zero hosting cost: S3 static hosting ($0.023/GB/month for ~5 MB weekly site), CloudFront (~$0.01/GB transfer on corporate internal traffic). GitLab CI runner minutes: ~10 minutes per weekly pipeline. Gatsby build scales linearly with archive size; 52-week archive builds in under 3 minutes on standard CI runner.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (complete - round 1): Gatsby site structure, WeeklyReportPage template, Recharts MAPE and pricing charts, S3/CloudFront IP-restricted hosting, GitLab CI weekly pipeline, archive page. Phase 2 (this revision - round 2): validate_evaluation_data.py CI validation gate with JSON Schema check and evaluation_date freshness check; Slack failure notifications. Phase 3 (Q3): model version comparison view showing MAPE trend across model versions; email digest with report link sent to stakeholder Slack channel on successful Monday deploy.
target_output.development_guidelines: Evaluation JSON schema spec (weekly_report_schema.json) is checked into the repo and versioned alongside the site; schema changes require PR review from the data science team. Gatsby pageContext types defined in TypeScript interfaces matching the JSON schema. Local development uses mock evaluation JSON fixtures in ./data/fixtures/. All CI pipeline scripts are Python 3.12 with type hints; validated by mypy in CI.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: data science pipeline delayed past 5 AM UTC; validate_evaluation_data.py freshness check fails and Monday report is not published. Mitigation: Slack alert to #retail-analytics-ops; manual pipeline trigger available once data is ready. Tradeoff: static generation means the site reflects the state of evaluation JSON at build time; intra-week model updates are not reflected until the next weekly build or manual trigger. Risk: CloudFront IP allowlist requires manual update when office CIDR ranges change; undocumented update process causes access outage. Mitigation: IP allowlist managed as Terraform resource with change documented in runbook.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Gatsby over Next.js because static export is the primary use case and no server-side rendering is needed. Confirmed Recharts over D3 for simpler React integration with pre-processed JSON data. GitLab CI selected over GitHub Actions per engineering platform constraint. IP allowlist confirmed as sufficient access control for internal-only site.

metadata.notes: Static reporting site for retail ML model performance; round 2 adds CI evaluation data validation gate.

profile.domain: Retail ML Feature Store and Model Serving Platform

project_goal.value: Provision a scalable feature store and model serving platform for real-time inventory optimization models on GCP, supporting sub-5ms feature retrieval during online inference and batch feature computation for model training across a large retail chain's SKU catalogue.
project_goal.rationale: Existing ad-hoc model serving is blocking ML team velocity; a dedicated platform reduces deployment overhead for new inventory optimization models.

target_users.value: ML engineers deploying inventory optimization models and downstream inventory services consuming real-time replenishment recommendations.
target_users.rationale: Two distinct internal consumer groups: ML team producing models and engineering teams consuming predictions.

project_class.rationale: Terraform-managed GCP platform; no application UI or end-user product.

capabilities.rationale: Platform spans infrastructure provisioning, model serving API, offline/online feature stores, and batch feature computation jobs.

complexity_level.rationale: Serving 50K+ SKUs, sub-5ms online feature retrieval SLA, multi-tenant model registry, large batch feature computation windows.

risk_level.rationale: Internal platform; no customer data at risk, but inventory decision quality depends on platform availability and feature freshness.

data_sensitivity.rationale: Features are internal inventory and sales metrics, not customer PII or financial transaction data.

external_exposure.rationale: Platform APIs accessible only within GCP VPC via internal load balancer; no public endpoint.

access_model.value: GCP IAM with Workload Identity Federation; GKE service accounts bound to IAM roles; model serving API requires caller service account with roles/mlplatform.featureReader.
access_model.rationale: Workload Identity eliminates long-lived credentials in container environments.

feature_scope.value: Online feature store (Redis 7) for sub-5ms retrieval of pre-computed SKU features during inference; offline feature store (ClickHouse) for training dataset generation; feature definition registry with schema versioning; batch feature computation jobs on Dataflow; model serving layer (FastAPI on GKE) with model version routing; model registry backed by Vertex AI Model Registry.
feature_scope.rationale: Complete feature store and serving platform specification.

mvp_scope.value: Single feature group (inventory_optimization_features) with 15 features, Redis online store, ClickHouse offline store, one Dataflow batch job for daily feature computation, FastAPI serving endpoint for replenishment_recommendation model.
mvp_scope.rationale: MVP scoped to unblock one production model with highest business impact.

security_baseline.value: Workload Identity for all GKE workloads; no long-lived service account keys. VPC Service Controls around BigQuery and ClickHouse. Encryption at rest via CMEK. TLS 1.3 for all internal service communication.
security_baseline.rationale: GCP security standard for internal ML platform.

frontend_stack.rationale: Platform is headless; ML engineers interact via Python SDK and Terraform.

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI for model serving layer; Terraform 1.7 for infrastructure provisioning; GKE Autopilot for container orchestration; Vertex AI for model training jobs.
backend_stack.rationale: Team expertise; Terraform for reproducible GCP infrastructure; GKE Autopilot reduces node management overhead.

data_platform.value: ClickHouse (offline feature store, batch feature materialization, training dataset queries); Redis 7 Cluster (online feature store, sub-5ms retrieval); BigQuery (raw event source for Dataflow batch jobs); GCS (feature snapshot archives, model artifacts).
data_platform.rationale: ClickHouse chosen over BigQuery for offline store due to columnar performance on time-windowed feature queries. Redis for online store sub-ms reads.

hosting_target.value: GKE Autopilot (model serving, feature store SDK server); Cloud Memorystore Redis (online store); Cloud SQL for feature registry metadata; Dataflow managed (batch feature jobs); Vertex AI Pipelines (model training); GCS (artifacts).
hosting_target.rationale: Fully managed GCP services minimize operational burden.

privacy_retention_policy.value: Feature data retained for 365 days in ClickHouse; online store (Redis) TTL 48 hours per feature group. Model artifacts retained indefinitely in GCS with version metadata in Vertex AI Registry.
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Internal data retention standard; 365 days covers trailing-year training window requirements.

future_scope.value: Real-time feature computation via Pub/Sub streaming pipeline (replace daily Dataflow batch with near-real-time); multi-region online store replica for disaster recovery; feature lineage tracking; automated feature drift alerting.
future_scope.rationale: Post-MVP scalability and resilience roadmap.

constraints.value: All resources in GCP us-central1. Cloud Build only (no external CI systems). Monthly budget cap $15,000 for platform infrastructure. ClickHouse must be self-managed on GKE (no managed ClickHouse available on GCP). Online store p99 retrieval latency under 5ms.
constraints.rationale: Regional data residency, cost, and latency constraints from platform governance.

observability_baseline.value: Prometheus + Grafana on GKE for platform metrics; Vertex AI Model Monitoring for prediction drift; Cloud Logging for structured logs; PagerDuty for online store latency SLA breaches and model serving error rate spikes.
observability_baseline.rationale: Standard GCP ML platform observability stack.

execution_preference.value: Infrastructure provisioned via Terraform with GitOps review; platform components deployed via Cloud Build pipelines on Git tag.
execution_preference.rationale: Reproducible infrastructure and auditable deployment history.

llm_integration.value: Vertex AI Gemini integration for automated feature importance narrative generation: after each model training run, a Vertex AI Pipeline step calls Gemini to summarize the top-10 feature importances in plain English for the model card registry entry.
llm_integration.rationale: ML engineers need human-readable model card summaries without manual writing.

project.goal: GCP ML feature store and model serving platform for retail inventory optimization
project.target_users: ML engineers and inventory service consumers
project.timeline: Q1 MVP (replenishment model), Q2 multi-model expansion

backend.framework: FastAPI for model serving, Terraform 1.7 for infrastructure

security.auth: GCP IAM + Workload Identity Federation; no long-lived keys
security.network: VPC internal load balancer only; VPC Service Controls on data stores

data.source: BigQuery raw events via Dataflow batch jobs

reasoner_reviews.product: The platform correctly scopes to one feature group and one model for MVP, which is the right way to validate platform patterns before expanding to the full SKU catalogue. The Gemini model card narrative feature is a good force-multiplier for ML engineer productivity but should be clearly non-blocking for the model deployment critical path.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Self-managed ClickHouse on GKE is the highest-risk infrastructure component given GCP lacks a managed offering. The ClickHouse cluster must have a dedicated node pool with local SSD for columnar I/O performance. Redis Cluster mode is essential for horizontal sharding across the full SKU catalogue; a single-node Memorystore instance would become a bottleneck above 100K SKUs. The Dataflow batch job must handle schema evolution in BigQuery source tables without breaking feature computation.
reasoner_reviews.security: Workload Identity is correctly specified. VPC Service Controls must extend to Cloud Memorystore to prevent unauthorized cross-project Redis access. The Terraform service account used in Cloud Build must have scoped permissions and the Terraform state bucket must use CMEK encryption.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The $15,000/month budget is tight for ClickHouse on GKE with local SSD plus Redis Cluster plus GKE Autopilot. A cost breakdown should be included to validate feasibility. The sub-5ms Redis p99 SLA is achievable with Memorystore within the same VPC region but requires connection pooling in the serving layer.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 2 addressed the ClickHouse node pool specification and Redis connection pooling. The remaining gap is the Dataflow job schema evolution strategy: the current plan still does not specify how feature computation handles new columns added to BigQuery source tables without requiring a full pipeline redeploy.

backend.service_design: Feature Store Server (GKE Autopilot): exposes gRPC and REST APIs for online feature retrieval (Redis) and offline feature snapshot export (ClickHouse). Model Serving Layer (GKE Autopilot): FastAPI service per model family; accepts InferenceRequest with sku_id and context_features; calls Feature Store Server to hydrate missing features; runs model inference via ONNX Runtime or Vertex AI endpoint; returns InferenceResponse with recommendation and confidence. Model Registry Client: wraps Vertex AI Model Registry API; resolves model_alias to model_version at serving startup.
backend.api_patterns: gRPC: GetOnlineFeatures(entity_ids: list[str], feature_refs: list[str]) -> FeatureVector. REST: POST /v1/features/online (same semantics). POST /v1/infer/{model_name} (model serving). GET /v1/models/{model_name}/versions (registry query). All internal; served on GKE internal load balancer.
backend.failure_handling: Redis retrieval timeout (>5ms p99): serving layer falls back to ClickHouse point-query with 50ms timeout; PagerDuty alert fired. ClickHouse fallback timeout: serving returns InferenceResponse with features_source='degraded' and uses cached feature values from last successful retrieval (TTL 10 minutes). ONNX inference error: 500 with structured error code; circuit breaker trips after 5 consecutive failures.
backend.scaling_notes: GKE Autopilot scales Feature Store Server pods on CPU 70% and request rate. Redis Cluster configured with 3 shards and 1 replica each for horizontal scale and HA. Dataflow batch jobs autoscale worker count based on BigQuery read throughput.

security.auth_design: Workload Identity: GKE service account retail-mlplatform-sa@PROJECT.iam.gserviceaccount.com bound to Kubernetes service account mlplatform/feature-store-sa. IAM roles: roles/redis.editor for Memorystore, roles/bigquery.dataViewer for BigQuery source datasets, roles/storage.objectAdmin for GCS model artifacts.
security.secrets_management: No long-lived service account keys. ClickHouse admin credentials stored in Secret Manager; mounted as Kubernetes secret via External Secrets Operator. Terraform state bucket encrypted with CMEK.
security.abuse_prevention: VPC Service Controls perimeter includes BigQuery, GCS, and Memorystore; cross-project access denied. GKE network policy restricts pod-to-pod communication to allowed service pairs only.

data.entities: feature_definitions (feature_name, feature_group, dtype, description, owner_team, created_at, schema_version). feature_groups (group_name, entity_type ENUM sku/store/sku_store, online_ttl_hours, offline_retention_days, batch_schedule_cron). training_datasets (dataset_id, feature_group_ids, entity_filter, start_date, end_date, clickhouse_table, created_at). model_versions (model_name, version, artifact_gcs_uri, feature_group_ids, performance_metrics_json, model_card_text, registered_at). serving_endpoints (endpoint_name, model_name, model_version, gke_deployment, created_at). inference_logs (request_id, endpoint_name, entity_id, features_source, latency_ms, predicted_at).
data.storage_design: Online store: Cloud Memorystore Redis 7 Cluster (3 shards, 1 replica each); key pattern entity_type:entity_id:feature_group:schema_version; value: MessagePack-serialized feature vector; TTL 48 hours. Offline store: ClickHouse on GKE with local SSD node pool; tables partitioned by feature_group and event_date; MergeTree engine with TTL 365 days. Feature registry metadata: Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 15.
data.consistency_notes: Batch Dataflow job writes to ClickHouse first, then materializes to Redis after ClickHouse write is confirmed; ensures offline and online stores are always in consistent state. Schema version included in Redis key to allow gradual rollout of feature schema changes without cache invalidation.

devops.deployment_topology: GKE Autopilot cluster (us-central1): namespaces mlplatform (feature store server, model serving), mlops (Dataflow launcher, Cloud Build agents). ClickHouse StatefulSet on dedicated node pool with local SSD (n2-highmem-8 with 375GB local SSD). Cloud Memorystore Redis Cluster (3 shards, us-central1). Cloud SQL (feature registry). Vertex AI Pipelines for training. GCS buckets: model artifacts, Dataflow staging, feature snapshots.
devops.ci_cd_design: Cloud Build triggers: (1) Terraform plan on PR to infra/ directory; (2) Terraform apply on merge to main. (3) Docker image build + push to Artifact Registry on serving/ or feature_store/ directory changes; (4) GKE rolling deployment via kubectl apply. Terraform state in GCS bucket with state locking. Cloud Build service account uses Workload Identity.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus operator on GKE scrapes Feature Store Server (online_retrieval_latency_ms histogram, cache_hit_rate gauge, batch_write_duration_ms histogram) and Model Serving (inference_latency_ms histogram, error_rate counter). Grafana dashboards: Feature Store SLA (p50/p99 online retrieval latency), Model Serving Health, Batch Job Success Rate. Vertex AI Model Monitoring for prediction distribution drift on replenishment_recommendation model.
devops.backup_and_recovery: ClickHouse: daily full backup via clickhouse-backup to GCS; point-in-time recovery to 24-hour window. Redis: Memorystore daily snapshot to GCS. Cloud SQL: automated daily backup with 7-day retention. Model artifacts in GCS with versioning enabled; never deleted.

ISS-001.title: ClickHouse node pool missing local SSD specification
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan specified ClickHouse on GKE without defining the node pool machine type or storage configuration. Without local SSD, ClickHouse columnar queries on the offline feature store will not meet training dataset generation performance targets.

history.note: Identified in round 1 audit.
Added n2-highmem-8 node pool with 375GB local SSD in Terraform spec and GKE node pool definition.

ISS-002.title: Redis serving layer missing connection pooling
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan did not specify Redis connection pooling in the FastAPI serving layer. Without pooling, each inference request opens a new Redis connection, blowing through the sub-5ms latency budget on connection establishment alone.

history.note: Identified in round 1 audit.
Added redis-py ConnectionPool with max_connections=50 per pod and async client for non-blocking retrieval.

ISS-003.title: Dataflow job schema evolution strategy not specified
ISS-003.detail: The Dataflow batch feature computation job reads from BigQuery source tables. When new columns are added to the source schema, the current plan does not specify how the job handles schema changes without requiring a full pipeline redeploy or causing feature computation errors.

history.note: Identified in round 2 audit as remaining gap after ISS-001 and ISS-002 resolved.

focus_issues.title: Dataflow job schema evolution strategy not specified
focus_issues.detail: Dataflow batch feature computation job has no specified strategy for handling new columns added to BigQuery source tables without requiring full pipeline redeploy.

latest_recommendations: Define a schema evolution strategy for the Dataflow batch job: use BigQuery schema detection mode, define an explicit feature_column_map in Dataflow job config so new source columns are ignored unless added to the map, and add a schema_version field to feature_definitions to gate new features
