previous_plan.title: Retail ML Feature Store and Model Serving Platform - Revised Architecture v2
previous_plan.executive_summary: GCP-native ML feature store platform with Redis online store, ClickHouse offline store on GKE with local SSD, and FastAPI model serving on GKE Autopilot. ISS-001 (ClickHouse storage) and ISS-002 (Redis connection pooling) resolved in round 2. ISS-003 (Dataflow schema evolution) remains.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: ClickHouse StatefulSet on n2-highmem-8 local SSD node pool for offline feature queries. Redis Cluster on Memorystore for sub-5ms online retrieval. FastAPI serving with async redis-py ConnectionPool. Dataflow batch jobs still read BigQuery without schema guard.
previous_plan.deployment_and_operations: Terraform 1.7 provisions GKE Autopilot, ClickHouse node pool (n2-highmem-8, 375GB local SSD), Memorystore Redis Cluster, Cloud SQL feature registry. Cloud Build CI/CD on Git tag.
previous_plan.data_model: feature_definitions, feature_groups, training_datasets, model_versions, serving_endpoints, inference_logs entities defined. No schema_version field on feature_definitions yet.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 resolves ISS-003 by adding an explicit feature_column_map contract in Dataflow PipelineOptions that whitelists the exact BigQuery column names contributing to each feature. New upstream columns are silently ignored unless added to the map (requiring a schema_version bump in feature_definitions). A BigQuery schema change alerting step in Cloud Build monitors source table schemas and fires a PagerDuty alert when new columns appear, giving ML engineers time to update the map before the next batch run.

fix_report.action_taken: Added feature_column_map as an explicit Dataflow PipelineOptions field defining the BigQuery column-to-feature mapping for each feature group. Dataflow job reads BigQuery with SCHEMA_CHANGE_MODE=IGNORE_UNKNOWN_COLUMNS and maps only columns present in the map. schema_version added to feature_definitions entity; incremented when column map changes. Cloud Build step added: bq show --schema SOURCE_TABLE compared against checked-in schema snapshot; PagerDuty alert fired on diff.
fix_report.expected_outcome: New columns added to upstream BigQuery inventory tables are silently ignored by Dataflow feature computation until explicitly added to the map; feature vectors remain stable across source schema changes; ML engineers are proactively alerted to schema changes before they affect training runs.

target_output.title: Retail ML Feature Store and Model Serving Platform - Final Architecture v3
target_output.executive_summary: A GCP-native ML infrastructure platform provisioned via Terraform 1.7 on GKE Autopilot, providing a dual-layer feature store (Cloud Memorystore Redis 7 Cluster for sub-5ms online retrieval, ClickHouse on local-SSD GKE node pool for offline training dataset generation) and a FastAPI model serving layer for retail inventory optimization models. This revision resolves the final blocking issue by introducing an explicit feature_column_map contract in Dataflow batch jobs, ensuring stable feature vectors despite upstream BigQuery schema changes. All three identified issues from prior rounds are now resolved.
target_output.architecture_overview: Dual-layer feature store architecture on GCP us-central1. Offline layer: ClickHouse StatefulSet on n2-highmem-8 GKE node pool with 375GB local SSD per node, MergeTree tables partitioned by feature_group and event_date, 365-day TTL. Online layer: Cloud Memorystore Redis 7 Cluster with 3 shards and 1 replica each; keys use entity_type:entity_id:feature_group:schema_version pattern; 48-hour TTL. Batch feature computation: Dataflow jobs read BigQuery raw inventory and sales event tables using explicit feature_column_map to isolate feature extraction from source schema evolution. Serving layer: FastAPI on GKE Autopilot with async redis-py ConnectionPool; falls back to ClickHouse point-query on Redis miss. Model registry: Vertex AI Model Registry with Gemini-generated model card narratives. All workloads use Workload Identity; no long-lived service account keys. Platform provisioned entirely via Terraform with Cloud Build GitOps.
target_output.technology_stack: Infrastructure: Terraform 1.7, GKE Autopilot (us-central1), Cloud Build for CI/CD. Online store: Cloud Memorystore Redis 7 Cluster (3 shards x 1 replica). Offline store: ClickHouse 24.x on GKE (n2-highmem-8 nodes, 375GB local SSD, 3-node cluster). Feature registry: Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 15. Batch compute: Dataflow (Apache Beam Python 2.55). Serving: Python 3.12, FastAPI, ONNX Runtime 1.17, redis-py async with ConnectionPool. Model registry: Vertex AI Model Registry. Monitoring: Prometheus operator, Grafana, Vertex AI Model Monitoring, PagerDuty. LLM: Vertex AI Gemini 1.5 Pro for model card narrative generation.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Online Feature Retrieval: Feature Store Server GetOnlineFeatures gRPC/REST API retrieves pre-computed sku_id features from Redis Cluster in under 5ms p99. Offline Feature Queries: ClickHouse HTTP interface serves training dataset SQL queries joining feature_groups on (sku_id, event_date) for Vertex AI training jobs. Batch Feature Materialization: Dataflow jobs compute daily SKU features from BigQuery inventory_events and sales_transactions tables using feature_column_map; write to ClickHouse then materialize to Redis. Model Serving: FastAPI InferenceRequest endpoint hydrates features from Feature Store Server, runs ONNX inference, returns replenishment_recommendation with quantity and confidence. Model Card Generation: Vertex AI Pipeline step calls Gemini 1.5 Pro with top-10 SHAP feature importances JSON to generate model card narrative text registered in Vertex AI Model Registry.
target_output.system_components: Feature Store Server (GKE Autopilot, mlplatform namespace): gRPC + REST; reads Redis Cluster via async ConnectionPool (max_connections=50/pod); falls back to ClickHouse on Redis miss; emits online_retrieval_latency_ms histogram. ClickHouse Cluster (GKE StatefulSet, 3 nodes, n2-highmem-8 + 375GB local SSD): MergeTree tables for offline features; ClickHouseOperator for cluster management; daily backup via clickhouse-backup to GCS. Cloud Memorystore Redis Cluster (3 shards, 1 replica each, 60GB total): online feature store; CMEK encrypted. Dataflow Feature Computation Jobs: Apache Beam pipelines parameterized by feature_group and feature_column_map; BigQuery read with IGNORE_UNKNOWN_COLUMNS; ClickHouse write then Redis materialization; daily schedule via Cloud Scheduler. FastAPI Model Serving (GKE Autopilot): per-model-family deployment; ONNX Runtime inference; circuit breaker on consecutive errors. Vertex AI Model Registry: model version storage with performance metrics and Gemini model card narrative. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 15: feature_definitions and feature_groups metadata registry. Schema Change Monitor (Cloud Build step): bq show --schema compared against schema snapshots in GCS; PagerDuty alert on diff.
target_output.workflows: Batch Feature Computation Workflow: Cloud Scheduler triggers Dataflow job at 2 AM UTC daily; job reads feature_column_map from feature_group config (e.g., inventory_optimization_features maps BigQuery columns inventory_qty, days_of_supply, sell_through_7d, promo_flag to feature slots 0-3); Beam pipeline reads BigQuery inventory_events with ReadFromBigQuery(schema_update_options=['ALLOW_FIELD_ADDITION']) but maps only whitelisted columns; computes 15 SKU features via sliding windows; writes to ClickHouse MergeTree partition event_date=today; materializes to Redis keys sku:SKU-4821:inventory_optimization_features:v3 with MessagePack encoding; job emits batch_write_duration_ms metric. Real-Time Inference Workflow: inventory service calls POST /v1/infer/replenishment_recommendation with {sku_id: 'SKU-4821', store_id: 'STR-012'}; FastAPI handler calls GetOnlineFeatures gRPC with entity_ids=['SKU-4821:STR-012'] and feature_refs=['inventory_optimization_features:*']; Redis returns 15-feature MessagePack vector in 2ms p50; ONNX Runtime runs replenishment model (demand_v3.onnx) returning quantity=48 confidence=0.87; response returned in under 10ms total. Schema Change Alert Workflow: Cloud Build step runs on schedule; calls bq show --schema retail-data.inventory.inventory_events and compares against GCS-stored snapshot; detects new column 'supplier_lead_time_days'; fires PagerDuty alert 'BigQuery schema change detected: inventory_events +supplier_lead_time_days'; ML engineer reviews and adds to feature_column_map if relevant.
target_output.data_model: feature_definitions (feature_name: str PK, feature_group: str FK, dtype: ENUM float32/int32/bool, description: str, owner_team: str, schema_version: int, created_at: timestamp). feature_groups (group_name: str PK, entity_type: ENUM sku/store/sku_store, online_ttl_hours: int, offline_retention_days: int, batch_schedule_cron: str, feature_column_map: jsonb). training_datasets (dataset_id: uuid PK, feature_group_ids: text[], entity_filter: jsonb, start_date: date, end_date: date, clickhouse_table: str, row_count: bigint, created_at: timestamp). model_versions (model_name: str, version: str, artifact_gcs_uri: str, feature_group_ids: text[], performance_metrics: jsonb, model_card_text: text, shap_importances: jsonb, registered_at: timestamp). serving_endpoints (endpoint_name: str PK, model_name: str FK, model_version: str, gke_deployment: str, created_at: timestamp). inference_logs (request_id: uuid PK, endpoint_name: str, entity_id: str, features_source: ENUM redis/clickhouse/degraded, latency_ms: float, predicted_at: timestamp).
target_output.api_design: gRPC: GetOnlineFeatures(request: {entity_ids: list[str], feature_refs: list[str]}) -> FeatureVector(features: dict[str, Any], metadata: {source: str, latency_ms: float, schema_version: int}). REST equivalent: POST /v1/features/online. Model Serving REST: POST /v1/infer/{model_name} body: {entity_id: str, context_features: dict[str, Any]} response: {recommendation: Any, confidence: float, model_version: str, features_source: str, latency_ms: float}. Feature Registry REST: GET /v1/feature-groups (list groups), GET /v1/feature-groups/{name} (schema + column map), POST /v1/feature-groups (register new group). All APIs on GKE internal load balancer; no public endpoint.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Workload Identity: GKE service account retail-mlplatform-sa bound to K8s service account mlplatform/feature-store-sa; IAM roles scoped to redis.editor, bigquery.dataViewer on source datasets, storage.objectAdmin on artifact buckets. VPC Service Controls perimeter includes Memorystore, BigQuery, GCS, and Cloud SQL; cross-project access denied. ClickHouse cluster credentials in Secret Manager; mounted via External Secrets Operator. Terraform state bucket encrypted with CMEK. TLS 1.3 for all gRPC and REST communication within GKE. GKE network policy restricts pod-to-pod to mlplatform namespace service pairs only. No long-lived service account key files anywhere in CI or container images.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Terraform 1.7 manages: GKE Autopilot cluster, ClickHouse node pool (n2-highmem-8, local-ssd-375gb), Cloud Memorystore Redis Cluster (3 shards), Cloud SQL (feature registry), GCS buckets (artifacts, Dataflow staging, backups), IAM bindings, VPC Service Controls. Cloud Build triggers: (1) PR to infra/: terraform plan, post diff as PR comment; (2) merge to main: terraform apply with state lock; (3) serving/ or feature_store/ directory change: docker build + push to Artifact Registry + kubectl apply rolling deploy; (4) scheduled schema monitor: bq schema diff + PagerDuty alert on change. Environments: production only (budget constraint); Dataflow staging uses dev BigQuery dataset. Rollback: previous GKE deployment revision via kubectl rollout undo; Terraform state rollback via state pull from GCS versioned bucket.
target_output.observability: Prometheus operator on GKE: Feature Store Server emits online_retrieval_latency_ms (histogram, labels: feature_group, source), cache_hit_rate (gauge), batch_write_duration_ms. Model Serving emits inference_latency_ms (histogram, label: model_name), feature_hydration_failures (counter). Grafana dashboards: Feature Store SLA (p50/p99 online latency vs 5ms target, cache hit rate), Model Serving Health (throughput, error rate, p99 latency), Batch Job Status (last run time, row count, duration). Vertex AI Model Monitoring: prediction distribution drift on replenishment_recommendation model; alert on Jensen-Shannon divergence > 0.1. PagerDuty escalation: online_retrieval_latency p99 > 5ms for 3 consecutive minutes; inference error rate > 2%; batch job failure; BigQuery schema change detected.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated monthly cost: GKE Autopilot workloads ~$3,200 (feature store server + model serving pods), ClickHouse node pool (3x n2-highmem-8 + local SSD) ~$4,800, Cloud Memorystore Redis Cluster (60GB) ~$1,800, Cloud SQL ~$400, Dataflow batch jobs ~$600, Vertex AI Pipelines ~$800, GCS/egress ~$300. Total ~$11,900/month, within $15,000 budget with ~$3,100 headroom for model expansion. Scaling: GKE Autopilot autoscales serving pods on CPU 70%; Redis Cluster shards can be expanded without downtime; ClickHouse node pool scale-up requires rolling StatefulSet update.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (rounds 1-2, complete): Core platform provisioned - ClickHouse cluster with local SSD, Redis Cluster, Feature Store Server gRPC/REST, Dataflow batch job for inventory_optimization_features group, FastAPI serving endpoint for replenishment_recommendation model, Prometheus/Grafana observability, Cloud Build CI/CD. Phase 2 (this revision, round 3): feature_column_map in Dataflow PipelineOptions, schema_version in feature_definitions, BigQuery schema change monitor in Cloud Build, ISS-003 resolved. Phase 3 (Q2): second feature group (pricing_features) and pricing_model serving endpoint; multi-model A/B routing in serving layer. Phase 4 (Q3): real-time feature computation via Pub/Sub streaming pipeline replacing daily Dataflow batch; streaming Beam job with 5-minute tumbling windows for near-real-time inventory feature updates.
target_output.development_guidelines: Terraform modules for reusable GKE node pool, Memorystore, and Cloud SQL patterns; module versions pinned in tfvars. feature_column_map changes require PR review from the feature store platform team and must bump schema_version in feature_definitions. Dataflow job changes require integration test run against dev BigQuery dataset before merge. All Python serving and feature store code: mypy strict, ruff lint, pytest with 85% coverage gate. Schema snapshots for BigQuery source tables stored in GCS under infra/schema-snapshots/; updated as part of PR process when intentional schema changes are made.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: ClickHouse self-managed on GKE adds operational overhead absent from a managed offering; GCP has no managed ClickHouse. Mitigation: ClickHouseOperator automates rolling upgrades; clickhouse-backup to GCS provides daily recovery point. Tradeoff: feature_column_map approach means new upstream features require explicit ML engineer action to include; this prevents accidental feature leakage but adds a manual step. Risk: Redis Cluster capacity (60GB) may be insufficient if feature group count grows; adding shards requires coordinated redistribution. Mitigation: Redis keyspace monitoring alert at 80% memory; shard addition tested in staging before production. Risk: Vertex AI Gemini model card generation cost grows with model registration frequency; at $0.00025/1K tokens for Gemini 1.5 Pro and ~5K tokens per card, cost is negligible at current model registration rate.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed ClickHouse over BigQuery for offline store due to columnar query performance on windowed feature aggregations. Selected Cloud Memorystore over self-managed Redis for operational simplicity. Confirmed Dataflow over Spark for batch jobs due to native GCP integration and serverless scaling. feature_column_map approach chosen over dynamic schema introspection to maintain feature vector stability.

metadata.notes: GCP ML feature store platform; round 3 resolves final Dataflow schema evolution issue; all prior issues closed.

profile.domain: Supply Chain Visibility SaaS Landing Page Rebrand

project_goal.value: Redesigned landing page for a supply chain visibility SaaS repositioning from EDI-focused messaging to real-time inventory intelligence platform, targeting retail operations and supply chain director personas.
project_goal.rationale: Marketing repositioning needed to address new ICP after product pivot.

target_users.value: Retail operations managers and supply chain directors evaluating real-time inventory visibility solutions.
target_users.rationale: ICP defined in updated GTM strategy.

project_class.rationale: Marketing landing page only; no application logic, no database, no server-side runtime.

capabilities.rationale: Static page with client-side analytics tracking; no backend capability.

complexity_level.rationale: Static marketing page; no dynamic content or user authentication.

risk_level.rationale: Public marketing page; no sensitive data stored.

data_sensitivity.rationale: No user data stored by the static site itself; form data routed to HubSpot.

external_exposure.rationale: Public marketing page; no access restriction.

access_model.value: Fully public; no authentication required.
access_model.rationale: Landing page must be accessible to all inbound traffic.

feature_scope.value: Hero section with real-time inventory intelligence messaging, persona-specific content blocks (retail ops and supply chain director), feature highlight cards for live shipment tracking and stockout prediction, social proof section with logos and case study links, demo request form routing to HubSpot with Slack notification.
feature_scope.rationale: Full marketing page content scope.

mvp_scope.value: Hero section, 3 feature highlight cards, demo request form, basic GA4 tracking.
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum to support campaign launch.

security_baseline.value: HTTPS via Vercel Edge; no server-side secrets on the static page; HubSpot form API key managed in Vercel environment variables.
security_baseline.rationale: Minimal security footprint appropriate for a public static page.

frontend_stack.value: Astro 4 with Tailwind CSS 3; TypeScript for component logic; no client-side framework hydration on static sections.
frontend_stack.rationale: Astro islands for minimal JavaScript; excellent Core Web Vitals performance.

backend_stack.value: N/A - landing_page; no server-side runtime. HubSpot form submission via client-side HubSpot Forms API.
backend_stack.rationale: Static-only architecture; form data handled by HubSpot, not a custom backend.

data_platform.value: No database. Form submissions go to HubSpot CRM via client-side API. Analytics events to GA4 and Segment.
data_platform.rationale: No data persistence on the landing page infrastructure.

hosting_target.value: Vercel Edge Network with automatic HTTPS and global CDN.
hosting_target.rationale: Zero-config deployment for Astro; Vercel Edge provides global low-latency delivery.

privacy_retention_policy.value: No data retained by the landing page infrastructure. HubSpot data retention governed by HubSpot DPA. GA4 data retention set to 14 months.
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Static site stores nothing; third-party systems govern their own retention.

future_scope.value: Personalized landing page variants by UTM source, multi-language support for EMEA expansion, video demo embed with Wistia tracking.
future_scope.rationale: Post-launch personalization and localization roadmap.

constraints.value: Lighthouse performance score >= 90 on mobile. No server-side runtime allowed. GitHub Actions for CI. Form submissions must reach HubSpot within 2 seconds.
constraints.rationale: SEO and performance targets from marketing team; no backend budget.

observability_baseline.value: Vercel Analytics for Core Web Vitals; GA4 for page engagement and demo form conversion funnel; Segment for lead event forwarding to HubSpot and Slack.
observability_baseline.rationale: Marketing analytics standard stack.

execution_preference.value: Deploy on merge to main; preview deployments on PR branches.
execution_preference.rationale: Marketing team needs fast iteration on copy and layout changes.

project.goal: Public marketing landing page for supply chain SaaS repositioning
project.target_users: Retail ops and supply chain directors

frontend.approach: Static site generation with Astro islands; no SPA routing

security.secrets: HubSpot API key in Vercel env vars; never in client bundle

data.persistence: None on landing page; HubSpot for leads, GA4 for analytics

devops.ci: GitHub Actions for lint and build check on PR

reasoner_reviews.product: The repositioning from EDI messaging to real-time inventory intelligence is a meaningful brand shift. The persona-specific content blocks for retail ops vs supply chain directors are the right approach - these two audiences have different pain points (operational vs strategic) and need different value propositions. Demo request form is the primary conversion goal; keep the form above the fold on mobile.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Astro with no client-side hydration on static sections is the correct pattern for a marketing landing page optimizing Core Web Vitals. The HubSpot Forms API client-side integration is appropriate; the API key used is a public portal ID, not a secret. A/B testing via CMS feature flag with edge cookie is architecturally sound but adds complexity - consider a simpler approach for the first version.
reasoner_reviews.security: The HubSpot portal ID is public by design; it should still not appear in plain text in the repo. Vercel environment variables are the right location. The demo request form captures name, email, and company from visitors including EU residents - GDPR cookie consent and a clear privacy policy link are required before form submission.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Lighthouse mobile >= 90 is achievable with Astro's minimal JS output. The 2-second HubSpot form submission SLA is client-side dependent; on slow connections this may fail. The A/B testing via edge cookie approach should be validated against Vercel Edge Middleware capabilities.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The previous plan's data_model section incorrectly described a database schema for leads with SQL table definitions. This is a landing_page with no database; the plan must not include any database entities or server-side data persistence. The architecture section also mentioned a Node.js API route for form handling which contradicts the no-backend constraint.

frontend.app_structure: Astro project structure: src/pages/index.astro (main landing page), src/components/ (HeroSection, PersonaBlock, FeatureCard, SocialProof, DemoRequestForm as Astro components). DemoRequestForm uses a React island (client:visible) for form state and HubSpot API submission. All other sections are static Astro HTML with zero JavaScript.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Single page: Hero (real-time inventory intelligence headline, CTA button to demo form anchor), Persona Blocks (retail ops: stockout prevention messaging; supply chain director: supply chain resilience ROI messaging), Feature Highlight Cards (3 cards: live shipment tracking, automated stockout alerts, demand forecast integration), Social Proof (customer logos, 2 case study excerpt quotes), Demo Request Form (name, email, company, job title, submit button).
frontend.performance_notes: Astro generates zero JS for static sections; only DemoRequestForm island hydrates (~8KB React runtime). Images use Astro Image component with WebP conversion and responsive srcset. Tailwind CSS purged to ~5KB. Lighthouse mobile target: LCP < 2.5s, CLS < 0.1, FID < 100ms.
frontend.frontend_security_notes: HubSpot portal ID passed as Astro env var (PUBLIC_HUBSPOT_PORTAL_ID); safe to expose client-side as it is a public identifier. Form submission uses HTTPS to HubSpot Forms API endpoint. No PII stored in client-side state beyond the active form session.

security.auth_design: No authentication. Public page.
security.abuse_prevention: HubSpot bot protection on form submissions (reCAPTCHA via HubSpot's built-in protection). Vercel rate limiting on Edge for repeated form submission attempts from same IP.
security.privacy_controls: GDPR: cookie consent banner (Cookiebot or similar) shown on first visit to EU visitors before GA4 and Segment analytics load. Privacy policy page linked from demo request form. Form data processed by HubSpot under their DPA.

data.entities: No database entities. Lead data captured by HubSpot: {firstname, lastname, email, company, jobtitle, utm_source, utm_medium, utm_campaign, page_url, submitted_at}. Analytics events in GA4: {demo_form_viewed, demo_form_submitted, cta_clicked, persona_block_scrolled (retail_ops|supply_chain_director)}. Segment event schema: {event: 'Demo Requested', properties: {email, company, job_title, persona_inferred}}.
data.analytics_events: Segment track events: 'Demo Requested' (on form submit), 'CTA Clicked' (hero and feature cards), 'Persona Content Viewed' (scroll depth on persona blocks). Segment routes to HubSpot (lead creation), Slack webhook (sales notification), and GA4 (via Segment destination).

devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions on PR: astro build (confirms build succeeds), Lighthouse CI check (mobile score >= 90 gate). Vercel auto-deploy: push to main triggers production deploy; PR branches get Vercel preview URL for stakeholder review.
devops.deployment_topology: Vercel Edge Network: global CDN with automatic HTTPS via Let's Encrypt, Edge Middleware for A/B variant cookie assignment. No server functions; no serverless runtime. Custom domain configured in Vercel project settings.
devops.cost_controls: Vercel Pro plan (~$20/month). No compute costs for static hosting. HubSpot Marketing Starter for lead CRM. GA4 free tier. Segment free tier (1000 MTU).

backend.service_design: No backend service. This section is incorrectly included. The landing page has no server-side runtime; all integration is client-side via HubSpot Forms API and Segment analytics library.
backend.api_patterns: N/A. HubSpot Forms API called client-side: POST https://api.hsforms.com/submissions/v3/integration/submit/{portalId}/{formGuid} with form field JSON body.

ISS-001.title: Plan includes database schema and Node.js API route contradicting no-backend constraint
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan's data_model section defined a SQL leads table with PostgreSQL schema and the deployment section described a Node.js API route for form handling. Both contradict the contract's backend_stack=N/A and constraints=no server-side runtime. The plan must be corrected to reflect a purely static architecture.

history.note: Plan incorrectly included server-side components for a landing_page project_class.

focus_issues.title: Plan includes database schema and Node.js API route contradicting no-backend constraint
focus_issues.detail: data_model had SQL table definitions and deployment described a Node.js API route; both contradict landing_page project_class with no backend.

latest_recommendations: Remove all SQL table definitions and database references from data_model section; replace with HubSpot lead field schema and Segment event schema
Remove Node.js API route from deployment; confirm HubSpot Forms API is called client-side from the React island

convergence_state.reason: Critical plan-contract mismatch unresolved; plan described a backend for a landing_page.

strengths: Astro with React island for form is correct minimal-JS approach
Segment + HubSpot + GA4 analytics stack is appropriate
Lighthouse CI gate in GitHub Actions is good practice
concerns: data_model section defines a SQL leads table with PostgreSQL - this is a static landing page with no database
Deployment section describes a Node.js serverless function for form handling - contradicts backend_stack=N/A
blocking_issues: ISS-001: plan describes a database and Node.js backend for a no-backend landing page project

Remove Node.js form handler from deployment; confirm HubSpot Forms API client-side call from React island

previous_plan.title: Supply Chain SaaS Landing Page Rebrand - Initial Architecture
previous_plan.executive_summary: Astro 4 landing page for supply chain SaaS repositioning. Round 1 incorrectly included a PostgreSQL leads database and Node.js API route.
previous_plan.data_model: leads (id SERIAL PK, email TEXT, company TEXT, submitted_at TIMESTAMP). FLAWED: this is a static landing page with no database.
previous_plan.deployment_and_operations: Vercel deploy with Node.js serverless function at /api/submit-form for HubSpot proxy. FLAWED: contradicts no-backend constraint.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Astro static site with React form island. Incorrectly included server-side form handler.

target_output.thinking_summary: ISS-001 requires removing the erroneously included database schema and Node.js API route from the round 1 plan. This revision corrects the plan to be purely static: data_model becomes HubSpot lead field schema and Segment event schema; deployment has no server-side functions. The plan intentionally retains a subtle flaw: the backend specialist subplan is included in the output despite landing_page project_class having no backend capability - this tests the auditor's detection of inappropriate specialist subplan inclusion.

fix_report.action_taken: Removed all SQL table definitions and PostgreSQL references from data_model section. Replaced with HubSpot CRM lead field schema and Segment analytics event schema. Removed Node.js API serverless function from deployment section; HubSpot Forms API now documented as client-side call from DemoRequestForm React island. Corrected architecture_overview to describe purely static architecture.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Plan accurately reflects a no-backend landing page architecture; no contradiction between project_class=landing_page and described components.

target_output.title: Supply Chain SaaS Landing Page Rebrand - Revised Architecture v2
target_output.executive_summary: An Astro 4 static landing page deployed on Vercel Edge Network, repositioning a supply chain visibility SaaS from EDI-focused messaging to real-time inventory intelligence for retail operations and supply chain director personas. Demo request form submissions are handled client-side via HubSpot Forms API and Segment, routing leads to HubSpot CRM with Slack sales notifications. No server-side runtime, no database. This revision corrects the round 1 plan which incorrectly included a PostgreSQL database and Node.js API route.
target_output.architecture_overview: Fully static Astro 4 site with zero server-side compute. Astro islands pattern: all sections (hero, persona blocks, feature cards, social proof) are static HTML rendered at build time with no client JavaScript. DemoRequestForm is the sole React island (client:visible lazy hydration, ~8KB React runtime). Form submission calls HubSpot Forms API directly from the browser via fetch POST. Segment analytics library tracks conversion events and routes to HubSpot, GA4, and Slack webhook. Deployed to Vercel Edge Network; globally distributed static assets with automatic HTTPS. GitHub Actions CI validates Lighthouse mobile score >= 90 on PR. No Node.js API routes, no serverless functions, no database anywhere in this architecture.
target_output.technology_stack: Astro 4 for static site generation with islands architecture. React 18 for DemoRequestForm island (client:visible). Tailwind CSS 3 with JIT purging. TypeScript for all component logic. Astro Image component for WebP conversion and responsive images. Vercel for hosting and Edge Network CDN. GitHub Actions for CI Lighthouse check. Analytics: Segment (analytics.js 2.0), GA4, Vercel Analytics. HubSpot Forms API (client-side, v3 submission endpoint). Cookiebot for GDPR cookie consent.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Hero Section: full-width headline 'Real-time inventory intelligence, not EDI plumbing' with sub-headline targeting supply chain resilience, CTA button anchoring to demo form, background supply chain network visualization graphic. Persona Content Blocks: Retail Ops block (stockout prevention, automated reorder triggers, 99.2% in-stock rate customer stat), Supply Chain Director block (end-to-end visibility, carrier performance scorecards, inventory cost reduction ROI stat). Feature Highlight Cards (3): Live Shipment Tracking (real-time carrier data, ETA variance alerts), Automated Stockout Alerts (ML-powered stockout risk with 14-day lead time), Demand Forecast Integration (connect to existing forecast outputs). Social Proof: 6 retail customer logos, 2 case study quote excerpts with attribution. Demo Request Form: first name, last name, email, company name, job title (dropdown: VP Operations/Supply Chain Director/Logistics Manager/Other), submit button. Post-submit: inline success message with 'Check your email for next steps'.
target_output.system_components: Astro Build Pipeline: gatsby-node equivalent in Astro = src/pages/index.astro; no dynamic page generation needed. React Island: DemoRequestForm.tsx (client:visible); manages form state with useState; calls HubSpot Forms API via fetch; fires Segment track('Demo Requested') on success. Segment Analytics: analytics.js 2.0 loaded conditionally after cookie consent; destinations: HubSpot (lead object creation), Slack webhook (sales notification via Segment function), GA4 (via Segment destination). Cookiebot: cookie consent script injected in Astro head; blocks Segment and GA4 until consent given for EU visitors. Vercel Edge Middleware: reads utm_variant cookie for A/B headline copy; serves same static build but passes variant to Astro island via meta tag. GitHub Actions CI: astro build verification + Lighthouse CI with mobile score >= 90 gate on every PR.
target_output.workflows: Demo Request Submission Workflow: visitor scrolls to demo form; DemoRequestForm React island hydrates on viewport entry; visitor fills in name, email, company, job title; clicks 'Request Demo'; React handler calls POST https://api.hsforms.com/submissions/v3/integration/submit/{HUBSPOT_PORTAL_ID}/{FORM_GUID} with JSON body; on 200 response, fires Segment track('Demo Requested', {email, company, job_title}); Segment routes event to HubSpot (creates/updates contact), Slack webhook (posts lead card to #sales-leads), GA4 (conversion event). Form shows inline success message. A/B Headline Variant Workflow: Vercel Edge Middleware reads utm_source query param on first visit; assigns variant A or B cookie; sets x-variant header; Astro island reads PUBLIC_VARIANT meta tag injected at Edge; renders correct headline copy. Both variants are the same static build; no separate deployment per variant.
target_output.data_model: No database. HubSpot CRM lead fields captured by DemoRequestForm: firstname (string), lastname (string), email (string, primary identifier), company (string), jobtitle (string from dropdown). Additional HubSpot properties set by Segment: utm_source, utm_medium, utm_campaign (from URL params), hs_page_url, hs_submission_date. Segment event schema: {event: 'Demo Requested', anonymousId: string, properties: {email: string, company: string, job_title: string, variant: 'A'|'B', utm_source: string|null}}. GA4 custom event: demo_form_submitted with parameters {job_title_group: 'executive'|'manager'|'other'}.
target_output.api_design: No server-side API. Client-side integrations: HubSpot Forms API: POST https://api.hsforms.com/submissions/v3/integration/submit/{portalId}/{formGuid} body: {fields: [{name: 'email', value: '...'}, ...], context: {pageUri: window.location.href}}. HubSpot portal ID (PUBLIC_HUBSPOT_PORTAL_ID) and form GUID (PUBLIC_HUBSPOT_FORM_GUID) are Astro PUBLIC_ env vars; safe to expose client-side as HubSpot public identifiers. Segment: analytics.track() and analytics.identify() calls from React island. No custom API endpoints. No Vercel serverless functions.
target_output.security_and_compliance: HTTPS enforced via Vercel Edge (automatic TLS). HubSpot portal ID and form GUID are public identifiers; set as Astro PUBLIC_ env vars, not secrets. No server-side secrets in the static bundle. HubSpot bot protection (reCAPTCHA) enabled on form. GDPR: Cookiebot consent banner blocks Segment and GA4 analytics scripts until EU visitor grants consent; privacy policy page linked in form footer and cookie banner. Form submission includes consent timestamp in HubSpot lead record. Note: compliance_context contract field was not included in this plan despite GDPR-relevant form data capture from EU visitors - this is an intentional omission representing a plan flaw.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Vercel project connected to GitHub repo; auto-deploys on push to main branch. PR branches receive Vercel preview URLs for stakeholder review of copy and layout changes. GitHub Actions CI on PR: (1) bun install + astro build to verify build succeeds; (2) Lighthouse CI action against Vercel preview URL; fails PR if mobile Lighthouse score < 90. Custom domain configured in Vercel project settings with automatic ACM certificate. No server functions, no Edge Functions beyond the A/B variant middleware. Deployments are atomic and instant via Vercel's CDN propagation. Rollback via Vercel dashboard one-click previous deployment promotion.
target_output.observability: Vercel Analytics: Core Web Vitals (LCP, CLS, FID) tracked per page visit; visible in Vercel dashboard. GA4: page views, session duration, demo_form_submitted conversion event, cta_clicked event. Segment: event stream to HubSpot and Slack. No server-side monitoring needed for static site. Lighthouse CI score tracked in GitHub Actions history for performance regression detection.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Vercel Pro plan $20/month for custom domain and preview deployments. HubSpot Marketing Starter ~$50/month for CRM and form handling. GA4 and Segment free tier sufficient for landing page traffic. No compute or database costs. Static site scales to any traffic volume via Vercel Edge CDN with no configuration.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (complete - round 1 attempt): Astro project scaffolding, hero section, feature cards, demo request form with HubSpot client-side integration, GA4 and Segment tracking, Vercel deploy. Phase 2 (this revision - round 2): ISS-001 corrected; removed database and Node.js API route; data_model replaced with HubSpot lead schema and Segment events; Cookiebot GDPR consent added; Lighthouse CI gate added. Phase 3 (post-launch): A/B headline variant testing via Edge Middleware; social proof section with logos and case study quotes; persona content blocks expansion; video demo embed with Wistia tracking.
target_output.development_guidelines: Astro PUBLIC_ env vars for any client-side integration IDs (HubSpot portal ID, form GUID, Segment write key, GA4 measurement ID). No server-only secrets in the codebase. TypeScript strict mode for React island components. Tailwind CSS classes audited via PurgeCSS output size check in CI. Copy changes (hero headline, persona blocks) go through marketing team review before merge to main. Lighthouse CI score >= 90 is a hard PR gate; no exceptions.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: HubSpot Forms API client-side rate limiting on high traffic; form submissions from same IP may be throttled. Mitigation: HubSpot portal-level rate limits are generous for a B2B landing page volume; monitor for 429 responses in Segment error events. Risk: Cookiebot adds ~15KB script weight and consent dialog may reduce conversion rate for EU visitors. Tradeoff: GDPR compliance vs conversion friction; accepted. Risk: A/B variant via Edge Middleware cookie means returning visitors may switch variants if cookie expires; acceptable for simple headline test. Note: this plan omits a formal compliance_context despite GDPR-applicable form data capture - an architectural governance gap.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed Astro over Next.js because static export is the only requirement and Next.js adds unnecessary server-side complexity. Confirmed HubSpot Forms API client-side over a serverless proxy because the portal ID is a public identifier. Selected Cookiebot over custom consent implementation for GDPR compliance speed.

metadata.notes: Landing page rebrand; round 2 fixes database/backend contract violation from round 1; residual flaws: compliance_context absent despite GDPR form data, backend specialist subplan included for no-backend project.

project_goal.value: Ingest raw order and inventory events from a Shopify store via Airbyte, transform and enrich with dbt, load into BigQuery for cohort analysis and reporting dashboards.
project_goal.rationale: Founders need reliable data foundation for investor reporting and product decisions.

target_users.value: E-commerce startup founders and analysts consuming BigQuery-backed BI dashboards for cohort and inventory reporting.
target_users.rationale: Primary consumers identified in data strategy kickoff.

project_class.rationale: End-to-end ELT pipeline with no user-facing application.

capabilities.rationale: Airbyte integration, BigQuery storage, Airflow batch orchestration, and analytics reporting.

complexity_level.rationale: Standard ELT pattern with Airbyte/dbt/BigQuery but requires incremental sync and cohort logic.

risk_level.rationale: Internal analytics pipeline; no real-time decisions depend on it.

data_sensitivity.rationale: Customer order data is business-internal; not exposed externally.

external_exposure.rationale: Pipeline runs entirely within GCP; no external API endpoints.

access_model.value: GCP IAM service accounts; Airflow with Google OAuth for team access to DAG UI; BigQuery dataset-level IAM for analyst read access.
access_model.rationale: Standard GCP IAM access controls for internal data team.

feature_scope.value: Airbyte incremental sync of Shopify orders, inventory_levels, products, customers, refunds tables into BigQuery staging dataset; dbt models for clean reporting layer (shopify_orders, inventory_snapshots, customer_cohorts, product_performance, refund_events); Airflow DAG for daily orchestration; Looker Studio or Metabase dashboards for cohort retention and sell-through reporting.
feature_scope.rationale: Full analytics data platform scope for the startup.

mvp_scope.value: Airbyte Shopify sync to BigQuery staging, dbt orders and customer_cohorts models, daily Airflow DAG, basic Looker Studio dashboard for order volume and cohort week-1 retention.
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum viable for monthly investor reporting.

security_baseline.value: IAM service accounts with least-privilege roles; BigQuery data encrypted at rest by default; Shopify API key stored in Airflow connections (not in code); no public BigQuery datasets.
security_baseline.rationale: Minimal but correct security for an internal analytics pipeline.

frontend_stack.value: N/A - data_pipeline; no UI. Looker Studio for dashboards (external, not part of this build).
frontend_stack.rationale: Dashboards are consumer tools, not part of the pipeline architecture.

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 for dbt model development and Airflow DAG scripts. dbt Core 1.7 with BigQuery adapter. Airbyte 0.50.x self-hosted or Cloud.
backend_stack.rationale: dbt Core chosen over dbt Cloud to minimize cost; Airbyte for Shopify connector.

data_platform.value: BigQuery (GCP us-central1) as data warehouse; Airbyte for EL from Shopify; dbt for T; Cloud Composer (managed Airflow) for orchestration.
data_platform.rationale: Standard modern data stack on GCP; team has existing GCP account.

hosting_target.value: Cloud Composer 2 (managed Airflow) for orchestration; Airbyte Cloud for managed connector; BigQuery for storage and compute.
hosting_target.rationale: Managed services minimize infrastructure management for a small startup team.

privacy_retention_policy.value: Order data retained indefinitely in BigQuery (startup needs full history for cohort analysis). Raw staging tables partitioned by ingestion date with 90-day partition expiry; clean reporting tables retain all history.
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Analytics requires full historical order data; staging tables can expire after 90 days once clean models are materialized.

future_scope.value: Real-time Shopify webhook ingestion for faster inventory alerts; Stripe payment data integration; automated cohort retention email triggers via Klaviyo.
future_scope.rationale: Post-MVP expansion once pipeline is stable.

constraints.rationale: Startup cost and team size constraints.

observability_baseline.rationale: Basic alerting sufficient for a low-risk analytics pipeline.

project.goal: ELT pipeline ingesting Shopify orders and inventory into BigQuery for cohort analysis

backend.framework: dbt Core 1.7, Airbyte 0.50.x, Apache Airflow 2.8 on Cloud Composer

security.auth: IAM service accounts with least privilege

devops.ci: GitHub Actions: dbt compile + dbt test on PR

constraints.team: Generalist developer, no dedicated data engineer

reasoner_reviews.product: The MVP scope of order volume and cohort week-1 retention is the right starting point for investor reporting. The daily Airflow DAG schedule is sufficient; the 15-minute Airbyte sync is more frequent than the daily dbt runs require, but incremental syncs are cheap and provide fresher staging data. Customer cohort definition (first order date as cohort week assignment) must be locked down before dbt model build starts.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The Airbyte-dbt-BigQuery stack is well-matched to startup scale and budget. Cloud Composer 2 may be over-engineered for a single daily DAG; consider starting with Cloud Scheduler + Cloud Run job to stay under the $300/month budget, since Cloud Composer starts at ~$300/month alone. dbt incremental models with merge strategy on order_id are essential to avoid full table scans on daily runs.
reasoner_reviews.security: Shopify API key in Airflow connections is acceptable for MVP but credentials should be migrated to Secret Manager. BigQuery dataset IAM should explicitly deny public access and restrict analyst roles to read-only on reporting dataset only.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Cloud Composer 2 at $300/month alone will breach the budget cap before BigQuery or Airbyte costs. This was flagged in round 1 and must be resolved. Alternatives: Cloud Composer 2 Small environment (~$200/month) or replace with Cloud Scheduler + dbt Cloud (~$50/month).
reasoner_reviews.critic: The orchestration cost issue from round 1 was noted but not fully resolved in the previous plan. The current plan still shows Cloud Composer without a cost breakdown demonstrating it fits within $300/month total for all infrastructure.

backend.service_design: ELT pipeline components: Airbyte connection (Shopify source -> BigQuery destination, incremental deduped sync on order_id, customer_id, inventory_item_id). dbt project with staging models (stg_shopify_orders, stg_shopify_customers, stg_shopify_inventory_levels, stg_shopify_refunds) and reporting models (shopify_orders, customer_cohorts, product_performance, inventory_snapshots, refund_events). Airflow DAG: trigger_airbyte_sync -> wait_for_sync_complete -> dbt_run_staging -> dbt_run_reporting -> dbt_test_reporting.
backend.business_modules: customer_cohorts dbt model: assigns each customer to cohort_week (DATE_TRUNC(first_order_date, WEEK)), computes orders_per_cohort_week array for retention calculation. product_performance model: sell_through_rate = total_units_sold / (units_sold + ending_inventory) by product_id and week. inventory_snapshots: point-in-time inventory level per SKU from Airbyte inventory_levels sync.
backend.failure_handling: Airflow task retries=2 on Airbyte sync trigger; retry_delay=10 minutes. dbt test failures email alert to data@startup.com; DAG continues but tags run as warning. Airbyte sync failure stops downstream dbt tasks via Airflow upstream_failed cascade.

security.auth_design: Cloud Composer service account with roles/bigquery.dataEditor on staging dataset and roles/bigquery.dataViewer on reporting dataset during dbt runs. Airbyte service account with roles/bigquery.dataEditor on staging dataset. Analyst IAM group with roles/bigquery.dataViewer on reporting dataset only.
security.secrets_management: Shopify API key in Airflow UI Connections (encrypted at rest by Cloud Composer). Migration to Secret Manager planned for Q2. dbt profiles.yml uses service account key-less authentication via Application Default Credentials.

data.entities: shopify_orders (order_id BIGINT PK, customer_id BIGINT FK, order_number STRING, financial_status STRING, fulfillment_status STRING, total_price NUMERIC, subtotal_price NUMERIC, total_tax NUMERIC, currency STRING, created_at TIMESTAMP, updated_at TIMESTAMP, processed_at TIMESTAMP). inventory_snapshots (inventory_item_id BIGINT, location_id BIGINT, available INT64, snapshot_date DATE, synced_at TIMESTAMP). customer_cohorts (customer_id BIGINT PK, first_order_date DATE, cohort_week DATE, total_orders INT64, total_revenue NUMERIC, last_order_date DATE). product_performance (product_id BIGINT, variant_id BIGINT, title STRING, week DATE, units_sold INT64, revenue NUMERIC, ending_inventory INT64, sell_through_rate FLOAT64). refund_events (refund_id BIGINT PK, order_id BIGINT FK, amount NUMERIC, reason STRING, created_at TIMESTAMP).
data.retention_and_deletion: BigQuery staging tables (raw_shopify.*) partitioned by _airbyte_emitted_at with 90-day partition expiry. Reporting tables (analytics.*) retain all history; no expiry. BigQuery column-level encryption not required for internal analytics data.
data.migration_notes: Initial Airbyte full refresh loads all historical Shopify orders (full refresh sync mode for first run). After initial load, switch connection to incremental+deduped mode using cursor field updated_at.

devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions on PR: pip install dbt-bigquery, dbt compile (schema validation), dbt test --select staging (data quality tests on staging models). Merge to main: Airflow DAG file sync to Cloud Composer GCS bucket via gcloud storage cp. dbt project directory synced to GCS on merge.
devops.deployment_topology: Cloud Composer 2 Small environment (us-central1, 1 scheduler, autoscaling workers 1-3). Airbyte Cloud with Shopify connector. BigQuery datasets: raw_shopify (staging, 90-day partition expiry), analytics (reporting, no expiry), dbt_test_results (CI validation).
devops.cost_controls: Cloud Composer 2 Small ~$200/month. Airbyte Cloud free tier (1 connection, <1M rows/month). BigQuery: estimated $30/month for startup order volume. Total ~$230/month within $300 budget. BigQuery slot reservations not needed at startup scale; on-demand pricing sufficient.
