target_output.title: E-Commerce Shopify Data Pipeline - Revised Architecture v2
target_output.executive_summary: An ELT data pipeline for an e-commerce startup ingesting raw Shopify order, inventory, customer, and refund data via Airbyte into BigQuery staging tables, transforming with dbt into clean reporting models, and orchestrating daily with Cloud Composer 2 Small. Outputs cohort retention, product sell-through, and inventory snapshot tables for Looker Studio dashboards. Total infrastructure cost $235/month, within the $300 budget. This revision downsizes Cloud Composer to the Small environment to resolve the round 1 budget breach.
target_output.architecture_overview: ELT architecture on GCP us-central1. Extract and Load: Airbyte Cloud Shopify connector syncs orders, customers, inventory_levels, products, and refunds tables to BigQuery raw_shopify staging dataset on 15-minute incremental schedule using deduped merge strategy on primary keys. Transform: dbt Core 1.7 project runs staging models (type casting, null handling, renamed columns) then reporting models (customer_cohorts, product_performance, inventory_snapshots, refund_events) against BigQuery. Orchestrate: Cloud Composer 2 Small runs a daily Airflow DAG at 2 AM UTC: trigger Airbyte sync, wait for completion, run dbt staging, run dbt reporting, run dbt tests, alert on test failures. All components in GCP us-central1; no external compute.
target_output.technology_stack: Airbyte Cloud 0.50.x with Shopify source connector and BigQuery destination connector. dbt Core 1.7 with dbt-bigquery adapter. Apache Airflow 2.8 on Cloud Composer 2 Small. BigQuery as data warehouse. Python 3.12 for Airflow DAG definitions. GitHub Actions for CI (dbt compile and test on PR). Looker Studio for dashboards (consumer, not part of pipeline build). GCP services: Cloud Composer, BigQuery, GCS (Airflow DAGs bucket), IAM.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Shopify Order Ingestion: Airbyte incremental sync of shopify.orders table to raw_shopify.orders in BigQuery; deduped merge on order_id with cursor field updated_at ensures idempotent loads. Customer Cohort Model: dbt customer_cohorts model assigns first_order_date as cohort anchor; computes cohort_week via DATE_TRUNC; calculates orders and revenue per customer per week for retention funnel. Product Sell-Through Model: dbt product_performance model joins shopify_orders and inventory_snapshots to compute sell_through_rate = units_sold / (units_sold + ending_inventory) by product_id and week. Inventory Snapshot Model: point-in-time inventory_levels by SKU and location from Airbyte sync. Refund Event Model: refund_events with join to parent order for refund rate by product and cohort calculations.
target_output.system_components: Airbyte Cloud: Shopify source (API key auth, shop domain configured), BigQuery destination (service account auth, raw_shopify dataset). Cloud Composer 2 Small: 1 scheduler, 1-3 autoscaling workers, GCS DAGs bucket, Airflow connections for Airbyte API token and BigQuery service account. dbt Core project: profiles.yml using Application Default Credentials on Cloud Composer; models/ directory with staging/ and reporting/ subdirectories; schema.yml with source definitions and model tests. BigQuery: raw_shopify dataset (staging, 90-day partition expiry on _airbyte_emitted_at), analytics dataset (reporting, no expiry). GitHub Actions: dbt compile and dbt test on PR.
target_output.workflows: Daily Pipeline Run: Cloud Composer triggers shopify_pipeline DAG at 2 AM UTC; AirbyteTriggerSyncOperator starts Shopify sync connection; AirbyteJobSensor polls until sync complete (typically 5-15 minutes for incremental); BashOperator runs dbt run --select staging.*; BashOperator runs dbt run --select reporting.*; BashOperator runs dbt test --select reporting.*; on test failure, Airflow email alert to data@startup.com. Airbyte Incremental Sync: Shopify connector reads orders updated since last sync cursor (updated_at); appends new records to BigQuery staging; deduplication merge on order_id removes duplicates from Shopify webhook retries. Initial Historical Load: first Airbyte run uses full_refresh mode to load all historical Shopify orders; after initial load, connection switched to incremental+deduped mode.
target_output.data_model: shopify_orders (order_id BIGINT PK, customer_id BIGINT FK, order_number STRING, financial_status STRING, fulfillment_status STRING, total_price NUMERIC, subtotal_price NUMERIC, total_tax NUMERIC, currency STRING, created_at TIMESTAMP, updated_at TIMESTAMP, processed_at TIMESTAMP). inventory_snapshots (inventory_item_id BIGINT, location_id BIGINT, available INT64, snapshot_date DATE). customer_cohorts (customer_id BIGINT PK, first_order_date DATE, cohort_week DATE, total_orders INT64, total_revenue NUMERIC). product_performance (product_id BIGINT, variant_id BIGINT, title STRING, week DATE, units_sold INT64, revenue NUMERIC, ending_inventory INT64, sell_through_rate FLOAT64). refund_events (refund_id BIGINT PK, order_id BIGINT FK, amount NUMERIC, reason STRING, created_at TIMESTAMP). warehouse_tables: raw_shopify.* (staging, Airbyte-managed schema), analytics.* (dbt-managed reporting tables).
target_output.api_design: No external API. Internal pipeline interfaces: Airflow to Airbyte via Airbyte REST API (POST /api/v1/connections/{connectionId}/sync, GET /api/v1/jobs/{jobId}). Airflow to dbt via BashOperator invoking dbt CLI. Analysts access BigQuery analytics dataset via BigQuery console, Looker Studio data source connector, or bq CLI. No custom HTTP endpoints.
target_output.security_and_compliance: IAM service accounts: airbyte-sa with roles/bigquery.dataEditor on raw_shopify dataset only; composer-sa with roles/bigquery.dataEditor on raw_shopify and analytics datasets. Analyst IAM group with roles/bigquery.dataViewer on analytics dataset only. Shopify API key stored in Airflow Connections UI (encrypted by Cloud Composer). BigQuery datasets have allUsers access denied via explicit IAM deny. All BigQuery data encrypted at rest by Google-managed keys by default. No public BigQuery datasets.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Cloud Composer 2 Small environment provisioned manually via GCP Console (single environment; no Terraform for MVP given team size). DAG files deployed via GitHub Actions: on merge to main, gcloud storage cp dags/ gs://COMPOSER_BUCKET/dags/ syncs DAG Python files. dbt project deployed via same GitHub Actions step: gcloud storage cp dbt/ gs://COMPOSER_BUCKET/dbt/. Airbyte Cloud connections configured via Airbyte UI; connection IDs stored as Airflow variables. Rollback: previous DAG version restored from GCS versioned bucket. No staging environment; dev testing via manual DAG trigger with --conf overrides on test BigQuery dataset.
target_output.observability: Airflow task logs in Cloud Composer UI. dbt test results logged to Airflow task stdout; email alert on test failure. Google Cloud Monitoring: BigQuery on-demand slot usage alert if daily query cost exceeds $5. No dedicated APM. Airbyte Cloud dashboard shows sync status and row counts per run.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Monthly cost breakdown: Cloud Composer 2 Small $200, BigQuery on-demand queries ~$30 (estimated 500GB scanned/month at $5/TB), Airbyte Cloud free tier (1 connection, under 1M rows/month) $0, GCS storage ~$5. Total: $235/month, within $300 budget with $65 headroom. Scaling path: if order volume exceeds 1M rows/month, upgrade to Airbyte Cloud Growth tier (~$100/month); add BigQuery slot reservation at 500GB/month threshold.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (complete - round 1): Airbyte Shopify connection to BigQuery staging, dbt staging models, customer_cohorts and product_performance reporting models, daily Airflow DAG, basic Looker Studio order volume dashboard. Phase 2 (this revision - round 2): Cloud Composer 2 Small replacing Standard, cost breakdown validated, ISS-001 resolved. Phase 3 (Q2): inventory_snapshots and refund_events models; dbt incremental model optimization for orders table; Stripe payments Airbyte connection added; automated cohort retention Looker Studio dashboard with week-over-week comparison.
target_output.development_guidelines: dbt models use incremental strategy with unique_key=order_id for orders; full table refresh only for customer_cohorts (small table). dbt schema.yml tests: not_null and unique on all primary keys; accepted_values on financial_status and fulfillment_status columns. GitHub Actions CI: dbt compile on every PR; dbt test --select staging on every PR. Shopify API key never committed to repo; always in Airflow Connections. BigQuery dataset names and project ID stored as Airflow variables, not hardcoded in DAG files.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Airbyte free tier (1M row/month limit) may be exceeded if Shopify store grows rapidly; 429 errors cause sync failures. Mitigation: Cloud Monitoring alert on Airbyte sync row count; upgrade to Growth tier before hitting limit. Risk: Cloud Composer 2 Small has limited worker capacity; if DAG complexity grows (more models, more sources), Small environment may queue tasks. Mitigation: upgrade to Medium environment at ~$300/month if daily DAG runtime exceeds 2 hours. Tradeoff: no staging environment to save cost; dev testing done against dedicated BigQuery test dataset with reduced data volume.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Cloud Composer Small over Cloud Scheduler + Cloud Run to retain Airflow DAG visibility and retry controls. Confirmed Airbyte Cloud over self-hosted Airbyte to eliminate infrastructure management. Chose dbt Core over dbt Cloud to minimize cost; dbt Cloud Teams would add ~$100/month. Confirmed BigQuery over Snowflake due to existing GCP account and free BigQuery storage within startup data volumes.

metadata.notes: Shopify ELT pipeline for e-commerce startup; round 2 resolves Cloud Composer cost budget breach by downsizing to Small environment.

profile.domain: Retail Operations - Inventory Health & Cycle Counting

project_goal.value: Centralized inventory health dashboard with automated cycle count scheduling and variance reporting for a 50+ store retail chain, replacing manual spreadsheet workflows
project_goal.rationale: Core business need to eliminate spreadsheet-driven cycle counting and enable real-time variance visibility

target_users.value: Store associates, store managers, area managers, and retail ops administrators
target_users.rationale: Multi-tier user base with different data access levels and workflow responsibilities

project_class.rationale: Browser-based application serving store-level and regional operations users

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, analytics, admin_panel, batch_jobs
capabilities.rationale: Full dashboard with scheduled batch jobs for cycle count generation and analytics for shrinkage reporting

complexity_level.rationale: 50+ store footprint, ABC classification engine, multi-tier approval workflow, and automated scheduling add meaningful complexity

risk_level.rationale: Internal operations data; incorrect inventory adjustments can cause financial loss but no external customer exposure

data_sensitivity.rationale: Inventory and shrinkage data is sensitive internally but not regulated externally

external_exposure.rationale: All users must authenticate; no public access to inventory data

access_model.value: RBAC with roles: associate (count submission own store), manager (store-level approval), area_manager (variance approval multi-store), admin (full configuration access)
access_model.rationale: Multi-tier approval workflow requires distinct permission boundaries per role

feature_scope.value: Inventory health dashboard per store, ABC-based cycle count schedule generation, count task assignment, variance calculation and approval workflow, shrinkage trend reporting, admin config for count rules
feature_scope.rationale: Complete feature set replacing spreadsheet processes

mvp_scope.value: Cycle count task assignment, basic variance reporting, and store-level inventory health summary for 10 pilot stores
mvp_scope.rationale: Pilot with 10 stores validates core workflow before full chain rollout

security_baseline.value: Okta SSO with MFA, TLS 1.3, encrypted data at rest, role-based data scoping by store cluster
security_baseline.rationale: Corporate identity provider mandate and internal data protection standards

frontend_stack.rationale: SSR for dashboard performance and team familiarity with the stack

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with Gin
backend_stack.rationale: High-concurrency batch job scheduling and fast API response times for 50+ store real-time queries

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 with Redis 7 cache
data_platform.rationale: Relational model fits hierarchical store/region/count data; Redis for session and real-time count status caching

hosting_target.value: GKE Autopilot on GCP
hosting_target.rationale: Kubernetes managed service reduces ops burden; GCP chosen for existing enterprise agreement

privacy_retention_policy.value: Inventory adjustment records retained 7 years per internal audit policy; cycle count task logs purged after 3 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Internal audit requirements and SOC 2 evidence retention

future_scope.value: RFID integration for automated counting, predictive shrinkage alerts using ML, mobile native app for store associates
future_scope.rationale: Roadmap items post-launch

constraints.value: Must integrate with existing ERP via REST API; deployment within GCP us-central1; no third-party analytics SaaS allowed
constraints.rationale: ERP integration is non-negotiable; data residency and budget limits apply

observability_baseline.value: Grafana dashboards with Prometheus metrics; structured JSON logs to Cloud Logging; alerting on batch job failures and API error rate spikes
observability_baseline.rationale: Existing GCP-native monitoring stack

execution_preference.value: Two-week sprints with monthly store rollout milestones
execution_preference.rationale: Incremental store onboarding requires milestone-gated delivery

compliance_context.value: SOC 2 Type II audit evidence required; internal change management controls for inventory adjustment approvals
compliance_context.rationale: Retail chain internal audit requirements mandate SOC 2 compliance for financial-adjacent systems

project.goal: Replace spreadsheet-based cycle counting with automated scheduling, real-time variance tracking, and multi-tier approval workflows across 50+ stores
project.target_users: Store associates, store managers, area managers, ops admins
project.timeline: MVP in 12 weeks, full chain rollout in 6 months

frontend.state: React Query for server state, Zustand for UI state

data.cache: Redis 7 for session and count status

reasoner_reviews.product: The ABC inventory classification engine is the most differentiating feature -- without accurate A/B/C tiering per store, cycle count schedules will be ineffective. Recommend building the classification refresh as a daily batch job rather than on-demand to ensure consistent scheduling behavior across stores.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Go with Gin is well-suited for the concurrent batch scheduling workload. The cycle count generation job needs to handle 50+ stores simultaneously without blocking the API. Design the scheduler as a separate Gin worker pool with a job queue backed by PostgreSQL advisory locks to prevent duplicate schedule generation.
reasoner_reviews.security: RBAC scoping by store cluster is critical. An area manager must only see variance data for their assigned stores. Implement row-level security in PostgreSQL to enforce store cluster boundaries at the data layer, not just application layer, to prevent privilege escalation bugs.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The ERP REST integration needs a resilient retry mechanism. Design the inventory sync adapter with circuit breaker and dead-letter queue to prevent stale on-hand quantities from corrupting cycle count variance calculations.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The variance approval workflow needs a clear audit trail. Every approval and rejection event must be immutably logged with user ID, timestamp, and store context for SOC 2 evidence.

backend.service_design: Gin server with domain modules: counts (scheduling, task management), inventory (ERP sync, on-hand cache), variances (calculation, approval workflow), reports (shrinkage analytics), admin (store config, rule management)
backend.api_patterns: REST API versioned at /api/v1; paginated list endpoints for count tasks and variance queues; bulk status update for batch operations
backend.background_jobs: Nightly ABC classification refresh per store, daily cycle count schedule generator using advisory locks, ERP inventory sync every 15 minutes, weekly shrinkage report pre-computation
backend.failure_handling: Circuit breaker on ERP REST calls with 3-retry exponential backoff; dead-letter table for failed sync events; batch job idempotency via run_id deduplication

frontend.app_structure: Next.js SSR pages for dashboard and reports; client-side React for interactive count task list and variance approval queue
frontend.pages_and_flows: Store Dashboard (health KPIs, pending count tasks), Count Task View (item list, enter counts), Variance Queue (pending approvals, drill-down), Shrinkage Reports (trend charts), Admin Panel (store config, ABC rules, user management)
frontend.state_management: React Query for count task lists and variance data with 30-second polling; Zustand for active count session state
frontend.performance_notes: SSR for initial dashboard load; incremental static regeneration for shrinkage reports; lazy-load variance drill-down components

security.auth_design: Okta OIDC integration with mandatory MFA; Gin middleware validates JWT on every request; 8-hour token expiry
security.authorization_model: RBAC enforced at API and PostgreSQL row-level: associate (own store tasks only), manager (all tasks in assigned store), area_manager (assigned cluster), admin (all stores)
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Append-only audit_events table logging every variance approval, adjustment submission, and admin config change with user_id, store_id, action, before/after values, and timestamp

data.entities: store_locations (id, name, region_id, cluster_id, erp_store_code), cycle_count_schedules (id, store_id, sku_class ENUM A/B/C, frequency_days, next_due_date), count_tasks (id, schedule_id, store_id, sku_id, assigned_to, status ENUM pending/in_progress/submitted/approved/rejected, on_hand_qty, counted_qty, variance_qty, variance_value), inventory_variances (id, task_id, store_id, sku_id, variance_value, approval_status ENUM pending/approved/rejected, approver_id), shrinkage_reports (id, store_id, period_start, period_end, total_variance_value, by_category_json), audit_events (id, user_id, store_id, action, entity_type, entity_id, before_json, after_json, created_at)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 with RLS policies on store_cluster_id; Redis 7 for active count session state and ERP on-hand cache with 15-minute TTL

devops.deployment_topology: GKE Autopilot cluster in us-central1 with separate deployments for API server and batch worker; Cloud Load Balancing with IAP for internal access
devops.ci_cd_design: Cloud Build: golangci-lint, unit tests 80% gate, Docker build, Artifact Registry push, Helm deploy to staging, manual approval for production
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scraping /metrics from API and worker pods; Grafana dashboards for batch job duration, ERP sync lag, variance queue depth; Cloud Logging for structured JSON
devops.rollback_strategy: Helm chart versioning with one-command rollback; golang-migrate rollback scripts validated in CI

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a multi-store inventory cycle count platform on GKE with Go backend. Key decisions: PostgreSQL row-level security for store cluster data scoping avoids complex application-layer guards and provides SOC 2-auditable boundaries. Go goroutine scheduler with advisory locks handles 50+ concurrent store schedule generation without a managed job service. Redis caches ERP on-hand quantities so associates can complete counts during ERP downtime.
target_output.title: Retail Inventory Health & Cycle Count Platform
target_output.executive_summary: A GKE-hosted web application replacing spreadsheet-based inventory cycle counting for a 50+ store retail chain. Built with Next.js 14 and Go 1.22 on GCP, it automates ABC-based count schedule generation, manages count task workflows from assignment through variance approval, and provides shrinkage trend analytics by store and region. Okta RBAC with PostgreSQL row-level security enforces strict store cluster data boundaries required for SOC 2 compliance.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-tier architecture: Next.js 14 SSR frontend served via Cloud CDN communicating with a Go/Gin REST API on GKE Autopilot. A separate batch worker deployment handles nightly ABC classification, daily cycle count schedule generation, and 15-minute ERP inventory sync using PostgreSQL advisory locks for distributed concurrency control. PostgreSQL 16 with row-level security policies enforces store cluster boundaries at the data layer. Redis 7 caches ERP on-hand quantities with a 15-minute TTL to decouple ERP availability from live count operations. All user actions flow through an append-only audit_events table for SOC 2 evidence collection.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Next.js 14, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, React Query, Zustand. Backend: Go 1.22, Gin, golang-migrate, golang-jwt, otelgin for tracing. Database: PostgreSQL 16 with RLS policies. Cache: Redis 7 for ERP on-hand cache and count session state. Infrastructure: GKE Autopilot, Cloud Load Balancing with IAP, Artifact Registry, Cloud Build, Cloud Logging, Prometheus, Grafana.
target_output.functional_feature_map: ABC Inventory Classification Engine: daily batch scores each SKU per store into A/B/C tiers by velocity and value, driving count frequency rules (A=weekly, B=monthly, C=quarterly). Cycle Count Scheduler: generates daily count task lists per store based on ABC tier rules and last-counted dates. Count Task Workflow: associates submit counted quantities; system calculates variance against cached ERP on-hand; variances exceeding configurable tolerance route to manager approval queue. Area Manager Variance Dashboard: aggregated variance view across assigned store cluster with SKU-level drill-down. Shrinkage Analytics: weekly pre-computed reports showing variance trends by store, department, and SKU class with YoY comparisons. Admin Panel: configures count rules, ABC thresholds, store cluster assignments, and RBAC role mappings.
target_output.system_components: GKE Autopilot cluster with api-server deployment (3-6 pods, HPA on CPU 60%) and batch-worker deployment (2 fixed pods). Cloud Load Balancing with Identity-Aware Proxy enforcing Okta authentication upstream of the API. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 with HA and read replica for analytics queries. Redis Memorystore instance for ERP on-hand cache and count session state. Cloud Build pipeline with Artifact Registry for container versioning. Prometheus sidecars with Grafana Cloud dashboards. ERP REST adapter with circuit breaker and dead-letter PostgreSQL table.
target_output.workflows: Cycle Count Execution: Morning scheduler generates count tasks for each store per ABC schedule; associates log in, see their pending tasks, select a task, enter counted quantities per SKU, and submit. System computes variance = counted_qty minus erp_on_hand and flags variances exceeding store-configured tolerance. Variance Approval: Flagged variances enter manager queue; store manager reviews SKU, location, counted vs expected, dollar impact; approves to trigger inventory adjustment and audit event, or rejects to return task for recount with mandatory note. ERP Inventory Sync: Every 15 minutes the sync worker calls the ERP REST API for on-hand quantities per store; results written to Redis TTL cache and asynchronously persisted to inventory_snapshots for historical tracking.
target_output.data_model: store_locations (id UUID PK, name, region_id, cluster_id, erp_store_code VARCHAR, active BOOL). cycle_count_schedules (id UUID PK, store_id FK, sku_class ENUM A/B/C, frequency_days INT, last_generated DATE, next_due_date DATE, active BOOL). count_tasks (id UUID PK, schedule_id FK, store_id FK, sku_id VARCHAR, assigned_to UUID, status ENUM pending/in_progress/submitted/approved/rejected, on_hand_qty DECIMAL, counted_qty DECIMAL, variance_qty DECIMAL, variance_value DECIMAL, submitted_at TIMESTAMPTZ, approved_by UUID, approved_at TIMESTAMPTZ). inventory_variances (id UUID PK, task_id FK UNIQUE, store_id FK, sku_id, variance_value DECIMAL, requires_approval BOOL, approval_status ENUM pending/approved/rejected, approver_id UUID, approval_note TEXT, created_at). shrinkage_reports (id UUID PK, store_id FK, period_start DATE, period_end DATE, total_variance_value DECIMAL, by_department_json JSONB). audit_events (id UUID PK, user_id FK, store_id FK, action VARCHAR, entity_type, entity_id, before_json JSONB, after_json JSONB, created_at) -- append-only, no updates or deletes permitted.
target_output.api_design: REST at /api/v1. GET /stores (admin and area_manager; scoped by cluster_id claim in JWT). GET /stores/:id/count-tasks?status=pending (paginated, store-scoped). POST /count-tasks/:id/submit body {counted_quantities: [{sku_id, qty}]}. GET /variances?store_id=&status=pending (manager approval queue, paginated). PATCH /variances/:id/approve body {note}. PATCH /variances/:id/reject body {note}. GET /reports/shrinkage?store_id=&period= (pre-computed weekly). GET /admin/schedule-config/:store_id. PUT /admin/schedule-config/:store_id body {abc_rules}. All endpoints require Okta JWT; PostgreSQL RLS enforces store_cluster_id boundaries automatically.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Okta OIDC with mandatory MFA for all roles. GKE IAP as outer authentication layer. JWT tokens carry store_cluster_id claim used by PostgreSQL RLS policies on count_tasks and inventory_variances tables -- every query is automatically filtered to the user's cluster, preventing cross-cluster data leakage even from application bugs. SOC 2 Type II evidence: append-only audit_events table captures every variance approval, inventory adjustment, and config change with before/after JSON. Cloud Logging retention 7 years for audit records. TLS 1.3 at load balancer. Secret Manager for ERP API credentials with 90-day rotation schedule.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Cloud Build CI pipeline: golangci-lint + eslint, unit tests with 80% coverage gate, integration tests against ephemeral PostgreSQL via Cloud Build workers, Docker build and Artifact Registry push, Helm upgrade to staging namespace with smoke tests, manual approval gate before production Helm upgrade. GKE Autopilot: api-server HPA CPU 60% min 2 max 6 pods; batch-worker fixed 2 pods with PodDisruptionBudget allowing 1 unavailable. Cloud SQL with automated point-in-time backups and daily export to GCS. golang-migrate runs as a Kubernetes Job pre-deployment; rollback SQL scripts tested in CI. Blue-green deploy for major schema migrations via feature-flagged code paths.
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics from API and worker /metrics: api_request_duration_seconds (p50/p95/p99 by endpoint and store_cluster), batch_job_duration_seconds by job_name, erp_sync_lag_seconds per store (time since last successful sync), variance_approval_queue_depth by store_cluster, abc_classification_last_run_age_hours. Grafana dashboards: Operations Health (sync lag, queue depth, batch job status by store), API Performance (latency percentiles, error rates by endpoint), Store Rollout Tracker (count tasks submitted and approved per store per week). PagerDuty alerts: ERP sync lag >20 min, any batch job failure, API error rate >2%, approval queue depth >100.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $650-900/month for 50 stores: Cloud SQL HA ~$220, Memorystore ~$80, GKE Autopilot scales to near-zero outside business hours. Read replica offloads shrinkage report aggregation from primary. At 200+ stores add partition on count_tasks by store_cluster_id and Cloud SQL read replicas per region for area manager dashboards.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (12 weeks, 10 pilot stores): ABC classification batch, daily cycle count scheduler, count task submission and variance calculation, manager approval workflow, Okta RBAC, ERP sync adapter with circuit breaker, Cloud Build CI/CD, SOC 2 audit logging. Phase 2 (8 weeks, full 50-store rollout): Area manager multi-store variance dashboard, shrinkage trend analytics with weekly pre-computed reports, store-cluster RLS hardening, bulk count task management. Phase 3 (8 weeks): Admin panel for self-service count rule configuration, barcode scan input via browser media API, RFID integration interface hooks, predictive shrinkage alerting prototype.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go: golangci-lint in CI, table-driven unit tests for ABC classification and variance calculation logic, 80% coverage gate, no ad-hoc DDL outside golang-migrate migrations. Frontend: ESLint + Prettier, Playwright E2E for count submission and variance approval flows. ADRs for PostgreSQL RLS policy design, ERP adapter circuit breaker approach, and Go advisory lock scheduler pattern. Feature flags via environment config variables for new store onboarding rollout gating.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: ERP API instability during active count operations leaves associates with stale on-hand data. Mitigation: Redis ERP cache allows count completion during ERP downtime; sync reconciles differences on recovery. Risk: PostgreSQL RLS policy misconfiguration could expose cross-cluster variance data. Mitigation: automated integration tests assert that area_manager tokens cannot retrieve out-of-cluster store records. Tradeoff: Go goroutine scheduler over Cloud Scheduler per-store jobs adds implementation complexity but eliminates per-invocation cost at 50+ store scale. Tradeoff: monolithic API with Go module boundaries over microservices fits the team size while preserving future extraction paths.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: PostgreSQL RLS selected over application-layer store filtering for stronger SOC 2-defensible data boundary guarantees. Redis ERP cache chosen over direct ERP calls during count submissions to decouple ERP SLA from associate workflows. Cloud Build confirmed over third-party CI per ops cost constraint. Pre-computed shrinkage reports chosen over on-demand aggregation given 3-year historical query range requirements.

metadata.notes: 50-store retail cycle count platform on GKE with PostgreSQL RLS for store cluster access scoping and Go batch scheduler with advisory locks.

profile.domain: Retail Data Engineering - Inventory Data Quality Auditing

project_goal.value: CLI tool for retail data engineers to run large-scale inventory data quality audits detecting duplicate SKUs, orphaned warehouse records, price inconsistencies across channels, and stale stock data, outputting structured reports for remediation
project_goal.rationale: Data quality issues in inventory systems cause pricing errors and fulfillment failures; engineers need automated audit tooling

target_users.value: Retail data engineers and platform engineers running audits locally or in CI pipelines
target_users.rationale: CLI designed for technical users needing machine-readable output and CI gate integration

project_class.rationale: No UI needed; data engineers operate via terminal and CI runners

capabilities.rationale: Read-only database access, audit rule execution, structured report output; no auth system or UI required

complexity_level.rationale: Parallel audit rule execution, configurable rule suite, multi-format output, and CI exit code integration add moderate complexity

risk_level.rationale: Read-only access to internal inventory database; no writes, no external exposure

data_sensitivity.rationale: Inventory pricing and stock data is internal business information, not regulated

external_exposure.rationale: Runs on local workstations and CI runners with no network-exposed endpoints

access_model.value: Database read-only credentials via INVENTORY_DB_URL env var; no user authentication system; read-only PostgreSQL role enforced at DB level
access_model.rationale: CLI accessed by authenticated engineers; DB credentials managed via vault or CI secrets

feature_scope.value: Configurable audit rule suite (duplicate SKUs, orphaned warehouse records, cross-channel price inconsistencies, stale stock), parallel rule execution, JSON/CSV/table output, severity tagging, CI exit codes
feature_scope.rationale: Full audit capability with CI integration is the core product

mvp_scope.value: Duplicate SKU detection and stale stock audit with JSON and CSV output; single-database target
mvp_scope.rationale: Two highest-priority audit types validated first before extending rule suite

security_baseline.value: Read-only PostgreSQL role enforced at DB level; credentials from environment variables only; no credentials in config files or CLI flags
security_baseline.rationale: Prevents accidental writes and credential leakage in shell history

frontend_stack.value: N/A -- CLI tool with no UI; output to stdout/stderr only
frontend_stack.rationale: CLI tool does not require any frontend component

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Typer CLI framework
backend_stack.rationale: Typer provides typed command definitions and automatic --help generation; team Python expertise

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 read-only connection to source inventory database
data_platform.rationale: Audit queries run directly against the inventory PostgreSQL instance via read-only credentials

hosting_target.value: Local workstation and CI runners (GitHub Actions); distributed as PyPI package or Docker image
hosting_target.rationale: No hosting infrastructure required; pip install or Docker pull

privacy_retention_policy.value: Audit report files retained locally by engineer; no centralized storage of report outputs
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Reports contain internal inventory data; engineer responsible for secure local handling

future_scope.value: Supplier lead time anomaly rule, scheduled runs with Slack report delivery, multi-database comparison audits
future_scope.rationale: Extensible rule architecture supports future additions

constraints.value: Must run without system dependencies beyond Python 3.12 + pip; read-only DB access enforced; full audit must complete within 5 minutes for 500K SKU database
constraints.rationale: CI runner time limits and minimal install footprint for broad engineer adoption

observability_baseline.value: Structured stderr logging during audit execution; exit codes: 0 clean, 1 warnings only, 2 high/critical findings, 3 execution error
observability_baseline.rationale: CI pipelines interpret exit codes; structured logs enable audit run debugging

execution_preference.value: Deliver as PyPI package with pinned dependencies; CI-first development with tests on every PR
execution_preference.rationale: PyPI distribution maximizes engineer adoption; CI testing prevents regression in audit rules

project.goal: Automated inventory data quality audit CLI with configurable rules, parallel execution, and CI-friendly output
project.target_users: Retail data engineers and platform engineers
project.timeline: MVP in 4 weeks, full rule suite in 8 weeks

backend.async_db: asyncpg with asyncio.gather for parallel rule execution

security.auth: Read-only DB credentials via INVENTORY_DB_URL env var
security.authorization: PostgreSQL read-only role enforced at DB level

data.cache: None

constraints.performance: Full audit under 5 minutes for 500K SKU DB

reasoner_reviews.product: The CI gate use case -- non-zero exit on critical findings -- is the highest-value feature for the platform team. It prevents bad inventory data from reaching production. Prioritize the exit code contract and severity tagging in the MVP alongside the first two audit rules.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: asyncio with asyncpg for parallel audit rule execution is the right call. Running 8 audit rules sequentially against a 500K SKU table would exceed the 5-minute CI budget. Each audit rule should be a coroutine issuing a single optimized SQL query; asyncio.gather() with semaphore(4) keeps DB read concurrency manageable.
reasoner_reviews.security: Read-only PostgreSQL role enforcement at the DB layer is essential -- not just a Typer guard. The PreflightChecker should verify no INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE grants on audit target tables before running any queries. Credentials must come exclusively from env vars; the --db-url flag should not exist to prevent process list leakage.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 5-minute budget for 500K SKUs is achievable with indexed queries. Document the expected indexes (sku_id, warehouse_id, updated_at) in the package README and add a check-prereqs command that validates index presence via pg_indexes before full audit execution.

backend.service_design: Typer application with commands: audit (run full or targeted suite), report (re-render saved JSON), check-prereqs (validate DB connectivity and indexes). Audit rules as pluggable Python classes implementing AuditRule ABC with async execute(conn) -> List[AuditFinding]
backend.api_patterns: CLI interface only. Command: audit [--rules duplicate-sku,stale-stock,...] [--output-format json|csv|table] [--output-file PATH] [--fail-on low|medium|high|critical] [--dry-run]. Exit codes: 0 clean, 1 warnings, 2 high/critical findings, 3 execution error
backend.business_modules: AuditRuleRegistry (discovers rule classes from rules/ package), AuditRunner (asyncio.gather with semaphore(4)), ReportFormatter (JSON/CSV/rich table), PreflightChecker (DB connectivity, role permissions, index presence validation)
backend.failure_handling: DB connection errors cause immediate exit code 3 with diagnostic message; individual rule failures logged to stderr and marked as rule_error in output rather than crashing the full run

security.auth_design: No user auth. DB credentials via INVENTORY_DB_URL env var exclusively; Typer pre-run hook validates env var before opening any DB connection
security.secrets_management: PreflightChecker issues pg_roles query to verify connected role has no INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on products, warehouse_inventory, or channel_pricing; aborts with exit code 3 if write privileges detected
security.security_risks: Risk: credentials in CI logs if --db-url flag existed. Mitigation: flag omitted entirely, env var only enforced

data.entities: sku_records (source: products with sku_id, channel_id, price, updated_at), warehouse_records (source: warehouse_inventory with sku_id, warehouse_id, quantity, last_updated), channel_prices (source: channel_pricing with sku_id, channel_id, listed_price, sync_at). In-memory: AuditFinding (rule_name, severity, entity_id, description, context_dict), AuditResult (rule_name, status, findings, query_duration_ms, rows_scanned), ReportSummary (total_findings, by_severity, by_rule, run_at)
data.schema_notes: Duplicate SKU rule uses COUNT(*)>1 GROUP BY LOWER(TRIM(sku_id)); stale stock rule filters warehouse_inventory WHERE last_updated < NOW() - INTERVAL '{threshold} days'; all queries validated via EXPLAIN ANALYZE in --dry-run mode

devops.deployment_topology: No server deployment. PyPI package 'inventory-audit-cli' and Docker image ghcr.io/org/inventory-audit:latest
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions matrix on Python 3.11/3.12; pytest-asyncio with testcontainers PostgreSQL 16 fixture seeded with known-bad inventory data; 90% coverage gate; publish to PyPI on version tag
devops.devops_risks: Audit queries on production DB during peak hours risk read contention. Mitigation: 30-second statement timeout default and documentation recommending off-peak scheduling

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a read-only inventory data quality CLI in Python/Typer. Key decisions: asyncio + asyncpg for parallel rule execution to meet the 5-minute CI budget at 500K SKU scale; pluggable AuditRule ABC enables new rules without modifying the runner; exit code contract 0/1/2/3 for CI gate integration; environment-variable-only credentials prevent leakage in process lists and shell history.
target_output.title: Inventory Data Quality Audit CLI
target_output.executive_summary: A Python 3.12 CLI built with Typer that connects read-only to a PostgreSQL inventory database and runs a configurable suite of audit rules -- duplicate SKU detection, orphaned warehouse record identification, cross-channel price inconsistency scanning, and stale stock flagging -- in parallel via asyncio. Outputs structured JSON, CSV, or rich terminal tables and exits with severity-coded exit codes for CI gate integration. Distributed as a pip-installable PyPI package and Docker image.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single-process Python CLI with no network-exposed endpoints. Typer defines the command surface (audit, report, check-prereqs). An asyncio event loop drives parallel AuditRule coroutines via asyncio.gather() with semaphore(4) limiting DB concurrency. Each rule issues one optimized SQL query via asyncpg against a read-only PostgreSQL 16 connection. Results are collected as typed AuditFinding objects and passed to a ReportFormatter rendering JSON, CSV, or rich terminal tables. PreflightChecker validates DB connectivity, role write-permission absence, and expected index presence before any audit queries execute.
target_output.technology_stack: Runtime: Python 3.12. CLI: Typer 0.12, rich for terminal table rendering and progress display. Async DB: asyncpg 0.29 with connection pool max 5 read-only connections. Data models: Python dataclasses (AuditFinding, AuditResult, ReportSummary). Output: stdlib json, csv; rich.table for terminal. Testing: pytest, pytest-asyncio, testcontainers-python (PostgreSQL 16 fixture). Linting: ruff, mypy strict. Distribution: PyPI via pyproject.toml, Docker image via GitHub Actions.
target_output.functional_feature_map: audit command: runs full or targeted rule suite via --rules flag, emits findings by severity, exits 0 (clean) / 1 (medium/low only) / 2 (high/critical present) / 3 (execution error). Duplicate SKU Rule: detects sku_id values in products table appearing multiple times with collation-insensitive grouping. Orphaned Warehouse Rule: finds warehouse_inventory rows referencing sku_ids absent from products. Price Inconsistency Rule: identifies SKUs where listed_price across channel_pricing rows varies by more than configurable tolerance (default 1%). Stale Stock Rule: flags warehouse_inventory rows not updated in over --stale-threshold-days (default 90) days. check-prereqs command: validates env var, DB connectivity, read-only role, and index presence. report command: re-renders saved JSON audit result in CSV or table format without re-querying.
target_output.system_components: Typer CLI entrypoint registering audit, report, check-prereqs app commands. AuditRuleRegistry loading rule classes from rules/ package. AuditRunner with asyncio.gather() and asyncio.Semaphore(4). PreflightChecker issuing pg_roles and pg_indexes verification queries. ReportFormatter with JSON (stdlib), CSV (DictWriter), and rich.table renderers. asyncpg connection pool (1-5 read-only connections, 30-second statement_timeout). Docker image for CI runners that cannot pip install.
target_output.workflows: Full Audit Run: engineer sets INVENTORY_DB_URL, runs 'inventory-audit audit --output-format json --output-file findings.json'; PreflightChecker verifies credentials, role permissions, and indexes; AuditRunner launches 4 concurrent rule coroutines; each rule executes its SQL and returns AuditFinding objects; ReportFormatter writes findings.json; CLI prints summary table to stdout and exits with code 2 if any high/critical findings. Targeted CI Gate: GitHub Actions step runs 'inventory-audit audit --rules duplicate-sku,price-inconsistency --fail-on critical'; workflow fails only on critical-severity inventory data quality regressions after a data migration. check-prereqs Validation: before first use on a new DB, engineer runs check-prereqs to verify DB connectivity, confirm read-only role has no write grants on inventory tables, and validate that sku_id, warehouse_id, and last_updated indexes exist.
target_output.data_model: AuditFinding (dataclass): rule_name str, severity Literal['critical','high','medium','low'], entity_type str, entity_id str, description str, context dict. AuditResult (dataclass): rule_name, status Literal['passed','failed','error'], findings List[AuditFinding], query_duration_ms float, rows_scanned int. ReportSummary (dataclass): total_findings int, by_severity dict[str,int], by_rule dict[str,int], audit_duration_ms float, db_host str (hostname only, no credentials), run_at datetime. Source tables (read-only): products (sku_id VARCHAR, channel_id, name, price NUMERIC, active BOOL, updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ), warehouse_inventory (sku_id, warehouse_id, quantity INT, last_updated TIMESTAMPTZ), channel_pricing (sku_id, channel_id, listed_price NUMERIC, sync_at TIMESTAMPTZ). Required indexes: products(sku_id), warehouse_inventory(sku_id), warehouse_inventory(last_updated), channel_pricing(sku_id, channel_id).
target_output.api_design: CLI interface, not HTTP. Command: inventory-audit audit. Flags: --rules TEXT (comma-separated, default all). --output-format [json|csv|table] (default table). --output-file PATH (default stdout). --fail-on [low|medium|high|critical] (default high). --stale-threshold-days INT (default 90). --dry-run FLAG (EXPLAIN ANALYZE only, no result rows). Command: inventory-audit check-prereqs (no flags). Command: inventory-audit report --input-file PATH --output-format [csv|table]. All commands load INVENTORY_DB_URL from environment; reject with exit code 3 and clear error message if not set. No --db-url flag exists to prevent credential exposure in process lists.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Read-only PostgreSQL role enforced at DB layer -- the connected role is granted SELECT on inventory tables only. PreflightChecker verifies no INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE grants on products, warehouse_inventory, or channel_pricing before any audit query executes; aborts with exit code 3 if write privileges detected. Credentials loaded exclusively from INVENTORY_DB_URL env var; --db-url CLI flag intentionally omitted. TLS via sslmode=require in asyncpg DSN. Statement timeout 30 seconds per query prevents long-running queries from degrading DB performance. No audit data transmitted externally; output files remain local.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: No server deployment. PyPI package 'inventory-audit-cli' published via GitHub Actions on version tag push using pyproject.toml with pinned dependencies. Docker image ghcr.io/org/inventory-audit:latest for CI runners without Python. GitHub Actions CI: matrix tests Python 3.11 and 3.12, pytest-asyncio against a testcontainers PostgreSQL 16 fixture seeded with 3 duplicate SKU pairs, 50 orphaned warehouse records, 10 price inconsistencies, and 25 stale stock rows; 90% coverage gate on rules/ and formatters/ packages. Pre-commit hooks: ruff and mypy strict.
target_output.observability: Structured stderr logging: rule start/complete with timestamps, query duration in ms, rows scanned, connection pool utilization. rich.progress bar for interactive runs showing N of M rules completed. Exit codes 0/1/2/3 consumed by CI orchestrators. --verbose flag adds per-finding detail to stderr. No external monitoring; CLI is a local tool with no persistent process.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: No infrastructure cost. PyPI hosting free. GitHub Actions CI ~2 minutes per PR run. At 5M+ SKU scale, audit SQL may exceed 5-minute budget; future --sample-mode flag will use TABLESAMPLE SYSTEM(10) for quick scans. --dry-run EXPLAIN ANALYZE mode lets engineers validate query plans before full execution.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (4 weeks): Typer CLI with audit and check-prereqs commands, asyncpg read-only connection, PreflightChecker, duplicate SKU rule and stale stock rule, JSON and CSV output formatters, exit code contract, pytest-asyncio integration tests with testcontainers fixture, PyPI publish workflow. Phase 2 (4 weeks): Orphaned warehouse record rule, cross-channel price inconsistency rule, rich terminal table renderer, --rules targeted execution, --dry-run mode, Docker image. Phase 3 (future): Slack report delivery, multi-database comparison mode, supplier lead time anomaly rule, scheduled nightly run with findings delta diffing.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 with mypy strict enforced in CI. ruff for linting and formatting. All audit rules must implement AuditRule ABC; no direct asyncpg calls outside rule execute() methods. New rules require pytest-asyncio integration test with testcontainers fixture containing at least 5 true-positive and 5 true-negative cases. 90% coverage gate on rules/ and formatters/ packages. Semantic versioning with CHANGELOG entry on each release.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: audit queries causing read contention on production DB during peak hours. Mitigation: 30-second statement timeout default, --dry-run mode for query plan validation, documentation recommending off-peak scheduling. Risk: false positives in duplicate SKU detection from case or whitespace variations. Mitigation: LOWER(TRIM(sku_id)) normalization in grouping with configurable normalization function. Tradeoff: asyncpg over SQLAlchemy async -- lower overhead for single-query rules but more verbose connection management code. Tradeoff: PyPI over internal registry -- broader adoption at cost of public publish credentials in CI.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: asyncpg chosen over psycopg3 async for lower per-query overhead on read-heavy patterns. Typer selected over Click for typed command definitions and automatic rich --help output. Exit code 2 for high/critical and exit code 1 for warnings-only enables differentiated CI behavior. testcontainers-python selected over a shared test PostgreSQL instance to keep CI self-contained.

metadata.notes: Inventory data quality audit CLI in Python/Typer with async parallel rule execution, CI exit code contract, and read-only PostgreSQL enforcement.

profile.domain: Retail Category Management - AI-Driven Assortment Optimization

project_goal.value: Web application for retail category managers surfacing AI-driven assortment optimization recommendations -- which SKUs to add, discontinue, or reposition -- based on demand signals, margin analysis, and competitor pricing data
project_goal.rationale: Category managers currently rely on manual spreadsheet analysis; AI recommendations can process 10x more signals simultaneously

target_users.value: Retail category managers and merchandising directors
target_users.rationale: Primary decision makers for SKU-level assortment choices

project_class.rationale: Browser-based dashboard for category management workflows

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, ai_llm, analytics, admin_panel
capabilities.rationale: Full-stack app with ML recommendation pipeline, analytics dashboards, and admin panel for model configuration

complexity_level.rationale: Weekly ML pipeline, model explainability, human-in-the-loop override feedback loop, competitor data ingestion

risk_level.rationale: Confidential competitive pricing and margin data; no payment processing but sensitive financial data

data_sensitivity.rationale: Competitor pricing intelligence and internal margin data is commercially sensitive

external_exposure.rationale: Internal tool; all access requires Cognito authentication

access_model.value: RBAC: category_manager (view recommendations, accept/override in assigned categories), director (all categories, model config view), admin (full access including model retraining triggers)
access_model.rationale: Category managers should only see and act on their assigned category recommendations

feature_scope.value: Weekly ML-scored recommendation feed, SKU-level accept/override workflow with mandatory reason capture, model explanation viewer, competitor price trend charts, margin analysis dashboard, admin panel for category assignments and model config
feature_scope.rationale: Complete feature set for category management decision support

mvp_scope.value: Recommendation feed for 3 pilot categories, accept/override workflow, basic model explanation display, Cognito auth
mvp_scope.rationale: Pilot validates core recommendation quality and UX before full category rollout

security_baseline.value: Cognito with MFA, TLS 1.3, AES-256 at rest, category-scoped data access, SOC 2 access logging
security_baseline.rationale: Confidential competitive data requires strong access controls and auditability

frontend_stack.rationale: Fast build tooling for chart-heavy dashboard; team expertise

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI
backend_stack.rationale: Python ecosystem fits ML pipeline integration; FastAPI for async API performance

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 with Redis 7 cache
data_platform.rationale: Relational model for assortment decisions and feedback; Redis for recommendation cache

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate
hosting_target.rationale: Managed containers; existing AWS infrastructure and ML team familiarity

privacy_retention_policy.value: Assortment decisions and model explanations retained 5 years; competitor pricing snapshots retained 2 years; override reasons retained indefinitely for model feedback
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Business audit trail and model improvement history

future_scope.value: Real-time competitor price monitoring, automated assortment testing via A/B price experiments, mobile app for directors
future_scope.rationale: Roadmap items after MVP validation

constraints.value: ML pipeline must complete within 4 hours on Sunday nights; competitor price data sourced from existing third-party feed (no new data contracts); budget $2000/month AWS
constraints.rationale: Sunday batch window and existing data contracts are fixed constraints

observability_baseline.value: New Relic APM, structured JSON logging, PagerDuty for ML pipeline failures and recommendation loading errors
observability_baseline.rationale: Corporate monitoring standard; ML pipeline failure must page on-call immediately

execution_preference.value: Two-week sprints; ML pipeline and UI developed in parallel tracks
execution_preference.rationale: Parallel tracks reduce dependency blocking between data science and frontend teams

compliance_context.value: SOC 2 Type II required; access to competitive pricing intelligence must be logged and auditable
compliance_context.rationale: Commercially sensitive data access requires SOC 2 audit trail

llm_integration.value: GPT-4o used for natural-language model explanation generation; explanations cached in Redis for 7 days; no raw competitor data sent to OpenAI
llm_integration.rationale: LLM generates human-readable explanations from structured model output without exposing raw sensitive data

project.goal: AI-driven assortment optimization recommendations with human-in-the-loop accept/override and model explainability
project.target_users: Category managers and merchandising directors
project.timeline: MVP in 10 weeks, full category rollout in 5 months

frontend.state: TanStack Query for server state, Zustand for UI state
frontend.charts: Recharts for demand and margin trend visualizations

backend.ml_pipeline: Weekly ECS Fargate batch task with scikit-learn + SHAP for explanations

data.cache: Redis 7 for recommendation cache and LLM explanation cache

reasoner_reviews.product: The override feedback loop is the most strategically important feature for long-term model quality. Every manager override with a documented reason is a training signal. Ensure the override reason is a structured enum (price_too_high, wrong_category, supplier_issue, seasonal_exception, other) plus a free-text field rather than free-text only -- structured reasons enable automated model retraining feedback.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The weekly ML pipeline running in a 4-hour window needs checkpoint logging at each stage (feature extraction, scoring, SHAP explanation, load to DB) so failures can resume from the last checkpoint rather than restart from scratch. Design the pipeline as a DAG of ECS tasks with S3 intermediate artifacts.
reasoner_reviews.security: Competitive pricing data is the most sensitive asset. Ensure the LLM explanation generation never includes raw competitor prices in the prompt -- only derived signals (price_gap_percentile, competitive_rank). The OpenAI API call must use a sanitized prompt schema validated before dispatch.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 4-hour Sunday ML window is tight if the catalog grows beyond 50K SKUs. Design the scoring pipeline to process categories in parallel ECS subtasks with a coordinator task tracking completion. At 50K SKUs, parallel category scoring should complete in under 2 hours leaving buffer for explanation generation.

backend.service_design: FastAPI with domain routers: recommendations (feed, accept, override, explain), categories (assignment, config), analytics (margin trends, demand signals), admin (model config, retraining triggers). ML pipeline as separate ECS Fargate task triggered by EventBridge weekly schedule
backend.api_patterns: REST at /api/v1; recommendation feed paginated by category and recommendation_type; PATCH /recommendations/:id/decision for accept/override; GET /recommendations/:id/explanation for LLM explanation fetch with Redis cache check
backend.llm_integration_design: GPT-4o invoked only for explanation generation; prompt template injects structured signals (demand_percentile, margin_rank, competitive_gap_level, trend_direction) without raw prices; response cached in Redis with 7-day TTL; fallback to rule-based template explanation on OpenAI error
backend.background_jobs: Weekly ECS Fargate batch: (1) feature extraction from PostgreSQL, (2) scikit-learn scoring per SKU per category, (3) SHAP value computation for top recommendations, (4) LLM explanation batch for top 200 recommendations, (5) load results to product_assortments and optimization_recommendations tables

frontend.app_structure: React SPA with route-based code splitting; chart-heavy pages use React.lazy() for Recharts bundle isolation
frontend.pages_and_flows: Recommendation Feed (category-filtered ranked list with accept/override actions), SKU Detail (demand chart, margin history, competitor gap chart, SHAP explanation), Override Dialog (structured reason enum + free text), Analytics Dashboard (category-level margin trends), Admin Panel (category assignments, model config)
frontend.state_management: TanStack Query for recommendation lists, SKU detail data, and explanation fetch with stale-while-revalidate; Zustand for active category filter and override draft state
frontend.performance_notes: Recommendation feed virtualized with TanStack Virtual for 1000+ SKU lists; Recharts charts lazy-loaded; explanation fetch deferred until SKU detail panel is opened

security.auth_design: Cognito user pool with MFA required; FastAPI JWT middleware validates token and extracts category_assignments claim
security.authorization_model: Category-scoped RBAC: category_manager JWT claim contains list of assigned category_ids; API queries filter optimization_recommendations by category_id intersection with claim; director role receives all categories
security.audit_and_logging_controls: audit_log table captures every recommendation accept/override with user_id, recommendation_id, decision, reason, category_id, timestamp for SOC 2 evidence

data.entities: product_assortments (sku_id, category_id, current_status ENUM active/discontinued/trial, margin_pct, demand_percentile, last_scored_at), optimization_recommendations (id, sku_id, category_id, recommendation_type ENUM add/discontinue/reposition, confidence_score, demand_signal_json, margin_analysis_json, competitor_gap_json, shap_values_json, manager_decision ENUM pending/accepted/overridden, decision_reason, decided_by, decided_at, pipeline_run_id), model_explanations (recommendation_id PK, explanation_text, generated_at, model_version), competitor_prices (sku_id, competitor_id, price, captured_at), audit_log (id, user_id, recommendation_id, action, reason_enum, reason_text, created_at)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 primary for assortment decisions and audit log; Redis 7 recommendation list cache (1-hour TTL) and LLM explanation cache (7-day TTL); S3 for ML pipeline intermediate artifacts and SHAP value archives

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate service for FastAPI API (2-4 tasks, ALB); separate ECS Fargate task definition for weekly ML pipeline triggered by EventBridge; RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ; ElastiCache Redis
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: pytest 80% coverage gate, mypy strict, Docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling deploy to staging, integration smoke tests, manual production approval gate
devops.observability_stack: New Relic APM agent in FastAPI; structured JSON logs to CloudWatch; custom metrics: recommendation_feed_load_ms, pipeline_stage_duration_seconds, override_rate_by_category, llm_explanation_cache_hit_rate
devops.rollback_strategy: ECS rolling deploy with minimum healthy percent 50%; prior task definition retained for one-command rollback; ML pipeline outputs versioned by pipeline_run_id so rollback restores previous recommendation set

ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan sent raw competitor_prices rows directly to GPT-4o prompt, risking competitive data leakage to OpenAI training corpus

previous_audits.summary: Solid overall architecture with strong recommendation UX design. Two blocking issues: LLM prompt design exposes sensitive competitor pricing data, and the ML pipeline lacks checkpoint resume capability creating Sunday window risk.
strengths: Category-scoped RBAC design is well-thought-out
Override feedback loop with structured reasons is production-quality
Recharts virtualization approach handles large SKU lists
concerns: Raw competitor prices in LLM prompt is a data governance and legal risk
4-hour ML pipeline without checkpoints could fail and leave recommendations stale for a week

Add pipeline_stage_checkpoints table tracking completion of each ECS subtask; allow resume from last completed stage

issue_updates.detail: Prompt template in round 1 included raw SELECT * from competitor_prices output
